title: 弱衰变因子化近似与拓扑振幅分析

recall_papers.title:
Updated analysis of $D\to PP, V\!P$ and $VV$ decays: Implications for
  $K_S^0-K_L^0$ asymmetries and $D^0$-$\overline {D}^0$ mixing
recall_papers.abstract:
An updated analysis of the two-body $D\to PP, V\!P$ and $VV$ decays within
the framework of the topological diagram approach is performed. A global fit to
the Cabibbo-favored (CF) modes in the $V\!P$ sector gives many solutions with
similarly small local minima in $\chi^2$. The solution degeneracy is lifted
once we use them to predict for the singly Cabibbo-suppressed (SCS) modes.
Topological amplitudes are extracted for the $\eta-\eta'$ mixing angles
$\phi=40.4^\circ$ and $43.5^\circ$. The $K_S^0-K_L^0$ asymmetries in $D\to
K_{S,L}^0M$ decays denoted by $R(D,M)$ are studied. While the predicted
$R(D^0,P)$ for $P=\pi^0, \eta$ and $\eta'$ agree with experiment, the
calculated $R(D^+,\pi^+)$, $R(D_s^+, K^+)$, $R(D^0,\omega)$ and $R(D^0,\phi)$
deviate from the data. We conjecture that the relative phase between the
topological amplitudes $(C+A)$ and $(T+C)$ should be slightly smaller than
$90^\circ$ in order to explain the first two discrepancies and that additional
singlet contributions due to the SU(3)-singlet nature of $\omega$ and $\phi$
are needed to account for the last two. For doubly Cabibbo-suppressed (DCS)
$D\to V\!P$ decays, their topological amplitudes (double-primed) cannot be all
the same as the corresponding ones in the CF modes. The assumption of
$E_{V,P}''=E_{V,P}$ for the $W$-exchange amplitude leads to some
inconsistencies with the experiment. Through the measured relative phases
between CF and DCS channels, the relations of $E_{V,P}''$ with $E_{V,P}$ are
determined. Long-distance contributions to the $D^0$-$\overline {D}^0$ mixing
parameter $y$ are evaluated in the exclusive approach. In particular, we focus
on $D\to PP$ and $V\!P$ decays where $y$ can be reliably estimated. We conclude
that $y_{_{P\!P}}\sim (0.110\pm 0.011)\%$ and the lower bound on $y_{_{V\!P}}$
is $(0.220\pm 0.071)\%$.

sections.18: Ambrosino et al., “A global fit to determine the pseudoscalar mixing angle and the gluonium content of the η′superscript𝜂′\eta^{\prime} meson,” JHEP 0907, 105 (2009) [arXiv:0906.3819 [hep-ph]].

authors: Hai-Yang Cheng, Cheng-Wei Chiang

recall_papers.title:
Analysis of three-body decays $B \to D (V \to ) PP $ under the
  factorization-assisted topological-amplitude approach
recall_papers.abstract:
Motived by the accumulated experimental results on three-body charmed $B$
decays with resonance contributions in Babar, LHCb and Belle (II), we
systematically analyze $B_{(s)} \to D_{(s)} (V \to) P_1 P_2 $ decays with $V$
representing a vector resonance ($\rho, K^*, \omega$ or $\phi$) and $P_{1,2}$
as a light pseudoscalar meson (pion or kaon). The intermediate subprocesses
$B_{(s)} \to D_{(s)} V$ are calculated with the factorization-assisted
topological-amplitude (FAT) approach and the intermediate resonant states $V$
described by the relativistic Breit-Wigner distribution successively decay to
$P_1 P_2$ via strong interaction. Taking all lowest resonance states ($\rho,
K^*, \omega, \phi$) into account, we calculate the branching fractions of these
decay modes as well as the Breit-Wigner-tail effects for $B_{(s)} \to D_{(s)}
(\rho ,\omega \to) KK$. Our results agree with the data by Babar, LHCb and
Belle (II). Among the predictions that are still not observed, there are some
branching ratios of order $10^{-6}-10^{-4}$ which are hopeful to be measured by
LHCb and Belle II. Our approach and the perturbative QCD approach (PQCD) adopt
the compatible theme to deal with the resonance contributions. What's more, our
data for the intermediate two-body charmed $B$-meson decays in FAT approach are
more precise. As a result, our results for branching fractions have smaller
uncertainties, especially for color-suppressed emission diagram dominated
modes.

authors: Si-Hong Zhou, Run-Hui Li and Xiao-Yao L\"u

qualitative: The factorization-assisted topological-amplitude (FAT) approach is employed to systematically analyze three-body $B_{(s)} \to D_{(s)} (V \to) P_1 P_2$ decays, where $V$ denotes a vector resonance ($\rho, K^*, \omega, \phi$) and $P_{1,2}$ are light pseudoscalar mesons (pions or kaons).
The intermediate subprocess $B_{(s)} \to D_{(s)} V$ is calculated using the FAT approach, which incorporates topological amplitude decomposition and factorization assumptions.
The resonant vector states $V$ are described by relativistic Breit-Wigner distributions, and their subsequent decays to $P_1 P_2$ are governed by strong interactions.
The analysis includes all lowest-lying vector resonances ($\rho, K^*, \omega, \phi$) and accounts for Breit-Wigner tail effects, particularly in $B_{(s)} \to D_{(s)} (\rho, \omega \to) KK$ decays.
The FAT approach provides more precise results for intermediate two-body charmed $B$-meson decays compared to other methods, leading to smaller uncertainties in branching fraction predictions.
The FAT approach and the perturbative QCD (PQCD) approach share a compatible framework for handling resonance contributions in three-body decays.
The predictions for branching fractions in unobserved modes are in the range $10^{-6} - 10^{-4}$, which are within reach of future measurements by LHCb and Belle II.
Color-suppressed emission diagram-dominated modes benefit significantly from the reduced uncertainties in the FAT approach due to improved precision in the underlying two-body decay amplitudes.
quantitative: Branching fractions for $B_{(s)} \to D_{(s)} (V \to) P_1 P_2$ decays are calculated with the FAT approach, showing agreement with experimental data from Babar, LHCb, and Belle (II).
Predicted branching ratios for unobserved modes are in the range $\mathcal{B} \sim 10^{-6} - 10^{-4}$.
The relativistic Breit-Wigner distribution is used to model resonant states: $\rho, K^*, \omega, \phi$, with their decay widths and masses taken from experimental inputs.
The FAT approach yields smaller uncertainties in branching fractions, especially for color-suppressed emission diagram-dominated modes, due to more precise input amplitudes.
The Breit-Wigner-tail effects are explicitly included in the analysis of $B_{(s)} \to D_{(s)} (\rho, \omega \to) KK$ decays, contributing to the shape of the invariant mass distributions.

recall_papers.title:
Analysis of three-body charmed B meson decays under the
  factorization-assisted topological-amplitude approach
recall_papers.abstract:
We analyze the quasi-two-body charmed $B$ decays $B^{+,0}_{(s)} \to D_{(s)}^*
P_2 \to D_{(s)} P_1 P_2$ with $P_{1,2}$ as a pion or kaon. The intermediate
processes $B_{(s)} \to D_{(s)}^* P_2 $ are calculated with the
factorization-assisted topological-amplitude approach and the resonant effects
are calculated with the Breit-Wigner formalism. Taking all p-wave resonance
states $ \bar D_{(s)}^*$ into consideration, we present the related branching
fractions, calculate the Breit-Wigner-Tail effects, and investigate the flavor
$SU(3)$ breaking effects. Most of our branching fractions are consistent with
the perturbative QCD approach's predictions as well as the current experimental
data. With more precision calculation of the intermediate two body charmed B
meson decays, our quasi-two-body B decays calculation has significantly less
theoretical uncertainty than the perturbative QCD approach. Many of those
channels without any experimental data will be confronted with the future more
accurate experiment measurements. Our results of the Breit-Wigner-tail effects
also agree with the experimental very well. In $B^0$ decays this effect can
reach approximately to $5\%$. It is also found that the Breit-Wigner-tail
effects are not sensitive to the widths of their corresponding resonances. The
flavor $SU(3)$ symmetry breaking effect is also investigated.

sections.35: Aubert et al.(BABAR Collaboration), “Measurement of branching fractions and resonance contributions for B0→D¯0​K+​π−→superscript𝐵0superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝐾superscript𝜋B^{0}\to\bar{D}^{0}K^{+}\pi^{-} and search for B0→D0​K+​π−→superscript𝐵0superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾superscript𝜋B^{0}\to D^{0}K^{+}\pi^{-} decays,” Phys
sections.37: Abe et al.(BELLE Collaboration), “Observation of Cabibbo suppressed B→D(∗)​K−→𝐵superscript𝐷superscript𝐾B\to D^{(*)}K^{-} decays at BELLE,” Phys

authors: Si-Hong Zhou, Run-Hui Li, Zheng-Yi Wei, Cai-Dian L\" u

qualitative: The factorization-assisted topological-amplitude (FAT) approach is used to analyze quasi-two-body charmed B meson decays, providing more precise decay amplitudes for intermediate two-body decays B(s) → D(s)*P2 compared to other approaches.
The quasi-two-body framework treats three-body decays B(s) → D(s)*P2 → D(s)P1P2 as sequential two-body processes, where the intermediate D(s)* resonance decays into D(s)P1.

Resonant contributions are modeled using the Breit-Wigner formalism, while non-resonant effects are accounted for through the Breit-Wigner-tail (BWT) effect, which captures off-shell contributions when the invariant mass of D(s)P1 is below the resonance pole mass.

The FAT approach significantly reduces theoretical uncertainty in three-body decay calculations by leveraging precise amplitudes from two-body decays, especially for non-factorizable QCD contributions.
Flavor SU(3) symmetry breaking effects are investigated, and the Breit-Wigner-tail effects are found to be insensitive to the widths of the corresponding resonances.
The BWT effect is non-negligible, reaching up to approximately 5% in B⁰ decays, and is in good agreement with experimental observations.

Theoretical uncertainties in the perturbative QCD (PQCD) approach are dominated by uncertainties in the two-body B → D*P decays, which the FAT approach mitigates through a global fit to experimental data.
quantitative: Breit-Wigner-tail (BWT) effects reach approximately 5% in B⁰ decays.
Off-shell contributions from D* resonances are about 5% in three-body B → Dππ(K) decays.
Contributions from Ds* resonances are found to be tiny, less than 1%.
Theoretical uncertainty in the quasi-two-body decay calculation is significantly reduced compared to the perturbative QCD approach due to improved precision in intermediate two-body decay amplitudes.
The Breit-Wigner-tail effects are not sensitive to the widths of the corresponding resonances.

recall_papers.title:
Analysis of three-body charmless $B$-meson decays under the
  factorization-assisted topological-amplitude approach
recall_papers.abstract:
We analyze quasi-two-body charmless $B$ decays $B_{(s)} \to P_1 V \to P_1 P_2
P_3$ with $V$ representing a vector resonant, and $P_{1,2,3}$ as a light
pseudo-scalar meson, pion, kaon or $\eta^{(\prime)}$. The intermediate
processes $B_{(s)} \to P_1 V $ are calculated in the factorization-assisted
topological-amplitude approach and the vector resonant effects are described by
the Breit-Wigner propagator, which successively decay to $P_1 P_2$ via strong
interaction. Taking into account of all vector resonances in ground state,
$\rho, K^*, \omega, \phi$, we present the related branching fractions, and
calculate the virtual effects for $B_{(s)} \to \pi, K (\rho ,\omega \to) KK$.
We also predict direct $\it{CP}$ asymmetries of three body B decay modes with
$\rho, K^*$ resonances as intermediate states. Our predicted branching
fractions of decay modes dominated by the color-favored tree diagram or the
color-favored penguin diagram are consistent with the perturbative QCD
approach's predictions as well as QCD factorization approach. While for those
nonperturbative contribution dominated decay modes, the branching ratios in
this work are in better agreement with current experimental data than the PQCD
predictions and the QCD factorization results due to their shortage of the
nonperturbative contributions or $1/m_b$ power corrections. Many of the decays
channels, especially for direct $\it{CP}$ asymmetries, are waiting for the
future experiments.

sections.46: Wang, Contributions for the kaon pair from ρ​(770)𝜌770\rho(770), ω​(782)𝜔782\omega(782) and their excited states in the B→K​K¯​h→𝐵𝐾¯𝐾ℎB\to K\bar{K}h decays,  Phys

authors: Si-Hong Zhou, Xin-Xia Hai, Run-Hui Li and Cai-Dian Lu

qualitative: Three-body charmless B-meson decays are analyzed as quasi-two-body decays where a vector resonance (e.g., ρ, K*, ω, φ) is produced in the intermediate state and subsequently decays into two pseudoscalars, leading to nontrivial kinematics and phase space distributions ideal for studying CP asymmetries and factorization in QCD.

The factorization-assisted topological-amplitude (FAT) approach is employed to systematically analyze these decays, incorporating nonperturbative and nonfactorizable QCD effects through topological diagrams classified under SU(3) symmetry, which improves precision over traditional factorization methods.
The Breit-Wigner formalism is used to describe vector resonance contributions, with strong couplings accounting for the subsequent two-body decays of the resonances into pseudoscalar mesons.

The FAT approach successfully addresses long-standing puzzles in two-body B decays—such as the B→ππ branching ratio and CP asymmetry discrepancies—by including enhanced nonfactorizable QCD contributions, motivating its extension to three-body decays.

Nonperturbative contributions and 1/m_b power corrections are crucial for accurate predictions, especially in decay modes dominated by weak annihilation or chirally enhanced effects, where standard perturbative QCD and QCD factorization approaches fall short.

The analysis focuses on the edges of the Dalitz plot, where the invariant mass of two final-state pseudoscalars is small, indicating the presence of intermediate vector resonances, and where factorization theorems similar to two-body decays are expected to hold.
Virtual effects of resonances such as ρ, K*, ω, and φ are studied in quasi-two-body decays like B→πK(ρ,ω→KK), revealing their significant impact on branching fractions and CP asymmetries.
Many predicted direct CP asymmetries and branching ratios remain to be tested experimentally, particularly with the upgraded LHCb and Belle II experiments.
quantitative: Branching fractions for quasi-two-body charmless B decays B(s)→P₁V→P₁P₂P₃ are calculated using the FAT approach, with results showing better agreement with experimental data than PQCD and QCD factorization predictions for nonperturbative-dominated modes.
The predicted branching fractions for color-favored tree or penguin-dominated decay modes are consistent with both perturbative QCD (PQCD) and QCD factorization predictions.
The FAT approach includes nonfactorizable QCD contributions that are larger than in other approaches, resolving the B→ππ branching ratio puzzle and the B→πK CP asymmetry puzzle.

The vector resonance effects are modeled via the Breit-Wigner propagator: $ \mathcal{D}(m^2) = \frac{1}{m^2 - M_V^2 + i M_V \Gamma_V} $, where $ M_V $ and $ \Gamma_V $ are the mass and width of the vector meson (e.g., ρ, K*, ω, φ).
Strong coupling constants (e.g., $ g_{VPP} $) are used to describe the decay $ V \to P_1 P_2 $, with values derived from experimental data or theoretical models.
Direct CP asymmetries are predicted for decay modes involving ρ and K* resonances, with magnitudes expected to be measurable in future experiments.
The study includes all ground-state vector resonances: ρ(770), K*(892), ω(782), and φ(1020), with their contributions evaluated in the quasi-two-body framework.

recall_papers.title:
Enhanced Next-to-Leading-Order Corrections to Weak Annihilation
  $B$-Meson Decays
recall_papers.abstract:
We accomplish the analytical computation of the pure weak annihilation
non-leptonic $B$-meson decay amplitudes at leading power in the heavy quark
expansion. The novel observation regarding such fundamental hadronic quantities
is that adding the missing hard-collinear contribution on top of the hard gluon
exchange effect eliminates rapidity divergences entering the convolution
integrals of factorization formulae. Subsequently we identify the perturbative
enhancement mechanism due to the penguin contractions of the current-current
operators from the weak effective Hamiltonian, which yields the significant
impacts on the CP violating observables.
authors: Cai-Dian L\"u, Yue-Long Shen, Chao Wang, Yu-Ming Wang

qualitative: The weak annihilation contributions in exclusive charmless B-meson decays are crucial for understanding CP violation and strong interaction dynamics, but their theoretical treatment has been hindered by rapidity divergences in convolution integrals within QCD factorization.

The inclusion of hard-collinear gluon exchange (with invariant mass of order √(mbΛQCD)) provides an analytical regularization of rapidity divergences, resolving a long-standing issue in factorization-based calculations of weak annihilation amplitudes.

Penguin contractions of current-current operators in the weak effective Hamiltonian lead to a perturbative enhancement mechanism that significantly impacts CP-violating observables, particularly through the generation of sizable strong phases.

Previous calculations neglected the hard-collinear contribution, leading to inconsistent regularization and unreliable predictions for annihilation-dominated decays; this work rectifies that by incorporating the missing hard-collinear topology.

The improved formalism enables systematic computation of next-to-leading-order (NLO) corrections to weak annihilation amplitudes, which is essential for precise predictions of direct CP asymmetries and other strong-phase-sensitive observables.

The validity of the QCD factorization framework for B→PP, B→PV, and B→VV decays is strengthened by this analytical regularization, especially in the presence of finite bottom-quark mass and end-point singularities.

The topological amplitude analysis reveals that NLO Feynman diagrams enhanced by large Wilson coefficients and CKM factors are key to generating significant strong phases, which are critical for CP violation studies.
quantitative: The hard-collinear gluon exchange contribution has an invariant mass scale of 𝒪(√(mbΛQCD)), where mb is the bottom quark mass and ΛQCD ≈ 300 MeV.

The perturbative enhancement arises from penguin contractions of current-current operators in the weak effective Hamiltonian, which are enhanced by large Wilson coefficients and CKM factors (e.g., |Vtd/Vts| ≈ 0.2), leading to non-negligible contributions to decay amplitudes.
The paper focuses on pure annihilation channels: B¯s→ππ and B¯d→ϕϕ, which are dominated by weak annihilation topologies and sensitive to strong phases.
The analysis is performed at next-to-leading order (NLO) in αs, with the inclusion of NLO Feynman diagrams that are enhanced by large Wilson coefficients and CKM factors.

The formalism relies on leading-twist light-cone distribution amplitudes for the final-state mesons and the B-meson, with acceptable models used to evaluate the convolution integrals (e.g., Gaussian or asymptotic forms).
The regularization of rapidity divergences is achieved analytically through the hard-collinear contribution, eliminating the need for ad hoc parametrizations of divergent integrals.

recall_papers.title:
Estimating QCD-factorization amplitudes through $SU(3)$ symmetry in
  $B\rightarrow P P$ decays
recall_papers.abstract:
We estimate the potential size of the weak annihilation amplitudes in QCD
factorization as allowed by experimental data. To achieve this goal, we
establish a connection between the amplitudes in the QCD factorization and the
so-called topological and $SU(3)$-invariant descriptions. Our approach is based
purely on the analysis of the tensor structure of the decay amplitudes. By
focusing on the processes $B\rightarrow P P$ and considering data from CP
asymmetries and branching fractions, we perform a global fit to the
$SU(3)$-irreducible quantities assuming minimal theoretical bias. Subsequently
we translate the outcome to the QCD factorization decomposition, and find that
the most constrained weak annihilation amplitudes are below $4\%$. However, in
view of the large uncertainties in several of the experimental input
parameters, values up to $30\%$ are allowed in certain cases.
authors: Tobias Huber and Gilberto Tetlalmatzi-Xolocotzi

recall_papers.title: Hadronic three-body D decays mediated by scalar resonances
recall_papers.abstract:
We study the quasi-two-body $D\to SP$ decays and the three-body $D$ decays
proceeding through intermediate scalar resonances, where $S$ and $P$ denote
scalar and pseudoscalar mesons, respectively. Our main results are: (i) Certain
external and internal $W$-emission diagrams with the emitted meson being a
scalar meson are na{\"i}vely expected to vanish, but they actually receive
contributions from vertex and hard spectator-scattering corrections beyond the
factorization approximation. (ii) For light scalars with masses below or close
to 1~GeV, it is more sensible to study three-body decays directly and compare
with experiment as the two-body branching fractions are either unavailable or
subject to large finite-width effects of the scalar meson. (iii) We consider
the two-quark (scheme I) and four-quark (scheme II) descriptions of the light
scalar mesons, and find the latter generally in better agreement with
experiment. This is in line with recent BESIII measurements of semileptonic
charm decays that prefer the tetraquark description of light scalars produced
in charmed meson decays. (iv) The topological amplitude approach fails here as
the $D\to SP$ decay branching fractions cannot be reliably inferred from the
measurements of three-body decays, mainly because the decay rates cannot be
factorized into the topological amplitude squared and the phase space factor.
(v) The predicted rates for $D^0\to f_0 P, a_0 P$ are generally smaller than
experimental data by one order of magnitude, presumably implying the
significance of $W$-exchange amplitudes. (vi) The $W$-annihilation amplitude is
found to be very sizable in the $SP$ sector with $|A/T|_{SP}\sim 1/2$, contrary
to its suppression in the $PP$ sector with $|A/T|_{PP}\sim 0.18$.

sections.16: Barnes, “Two Photon Decays Support the (K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾K\bar{K}) Molecule Picture of the S∗​(975)superscript𝑆975S^{*}(975) and δ​(980)𝛿980\delta(980),” Phys

authors: Hai-Yang Cheng, Cheng-Wei Chiang, Zhi-Qing Zhang

qualitative: Certain external and internal W-emission diagrams with a scalar meson as the emitted particle are naively expected to vanish but receive non-zero contributions from vertex and hard spectator-scattering corrections beyond the factorization approximation.

For light scalar mesons with masses below or close to 1 GeV, direct study of three-body decays is more appropriate than two-body decays due to large finite-width effects and the unavailability of reliable two-body branching fractions.

The four-quark (tetraquark) description of light scalar mesons (scheme II) provides better agreement with experimental data compared to the two-quark (q¯q) description (scheme I), consistent with recent BESIII measurements of semileptonic charm decays.

The topological amplitude approach fails in the SP sector because decay rates cannot be reliably factorized into topological amplitude squared and phase space factor, making it impossible to infer D→SP branching fractions from three-body decay measurements.
The predicted branching fractions for D⁰→f₀P and D⁰→a₀P are generally smaller than experimental data by about one order of magnitude, suggesting a significant contribution from W-exchange amplitudes.
The W-annihilation amplitude in the SP sector is sizable, with |A/T|_{SP} ≈ 1/2, in contrast to its suppression in the PP sector where |A/T|_{PP} ≈ 0.18.
quantitative: Predicted D⁰→f₀P and D⁰→a₀P branching fractions are smaller than experimental data by one order of magnitude
Light scalar mesons below 1 GeV include σ/f₀(500), f₀(980), a₀(980), and κ/K₀*(700)
Scalar mesons above 1 GeV include a₀(1450), K₀*(1430), f₀(1370), f₀(1500), and f₀(1710)
Finite-width effects are particularly important for decays involving σ/f₀(500) and κ/K₀*(700) due to their broad widths relative to their masses

recall_papers.title: Tetraquark nature of the $a_0(980)$ meson in hadronic $D$ decays
recall_papers.abstract:
The internal structure of the light scalar meson $a_0(980)$ is explored in
the three-body $D$ decays of $D\to a_0(980)P\to P_1P_2P$ through the
intermediate state $a_0(980)$, where $P$ denotes a pseudoscalar meson. The
quasi-two-body $D\to a_0(980)^+P$ decays are governed by the external
$W$-emission diagram in which $a_0(980)^+$ is emitted. The predicted branching
fractions in the $q\bar q$ model of $a_0(980)$ are too small by one to two
orders of magnitude compared to experiment as the amplitude is suppressed by
the smallness of the $a_0(980)^+$ decay constant, while those for $D^+\to
a_0(980)^0 P$ and $D^0\to a_0(980)^{-}P$ are usually too large. These
discrepancies can be resolved provided that $a_0(980)$ is a tetraquark state.
In this case, there exist two additional $T$-like topological amplitudes,
denoted by $\overline{T}$ and $\tilde T$ which readily account for the
discrepancies. An important implication of the tetraquark model is that the
$D_s^+\to a_0(980)^+\pi^0+a_0(980)^0\pi^+$ decay is not a purely
$W$-annihilation process as in the diquark model of $a_0(980)$; it receives
dominant contributions from $\overline{T}$ newly noticed in this work.
Therefore, measurements of $(D,D_s^+)\to a_0(980)P$ decays lend strong support
to the tetraquark picture of $a_0(980)$.
authors: Hai-Yang Cheng, Cheng-Wei Chiang, Fanrong Xu

qualitative: The internal structure of the $a_0(980)$ meson is investigated in the context of three-body $D$ decays, particularly $D \to a_0(980)P \to P_1P_2P$, where $a_0(980)$ acts as an intermediate state.

In the $q\bar{q}$ quark model, the $a_0(980)$ meson has a small decay constant, leading to a suppression of the $W$-emission amplitude in $D \to a_0(980)^+P$ decays, resulting in predicted branching fractions that are too small by one to two orders of magnitude compared to experimental observations.
For $D^+ \to a_0(980)^0 P$ and $D^0 \to a_0(980)^- P$ decays, the $q\bar{q}$ model predicts branching fractions that are too large, indicating a significant discrepancy with experiment.

These discrepancies are resolved if the $a_0(980)$ is interpreted as a tetraquark state, which introduces two additional topological amplitudes, $\overline{T}$ and $\tilde{T}$, not present in the $q\bar{q}$ model.
The $\overline{T}$ and $\tilde{T}$ amplitudes are $T$-like and arise due to the tetraquark structure, enabling a more consistent description of the observed decay rates.

The decay $D_s^+ \to a_0(980)^+\pi^0 + a_0(980)^0\pi^+$ is not purely a $W$-annihilation process as assumed in the diquark model; instead, it is dominated by the newly identified $\overline{T}$ amplitude in the tetraquark picture.
Measurements of $(D, D_s^+) \to a_0(980)P$ decays provide strong empirical support for the tetraquark nature of the $a_0(980)$ meson.
quantitative: Predicted branching fractions in the $q\bar{q}$ model are too small by one to two orders of magnitude compared to experiment for $D \to a_0(980)^+P$ decays.
Branching fractions for $D^+ \to a_0(980)^0 P$ and $D^0 \to a_0(980)^- P$ in the $q\bar{q}$ model are too large compared to experimental data.
The presence of two additional topological amplitudes $\overline{T}$ and $\tilde{T}$ in the tetraquark model accounts for the observed discrepancies in branching fractions.
The decay $D_s^+ \to a_0(980)^+\pi^0 + a_0(980)^0\pi^+$ receives dominant contributions from the $\overline{T}$ amplitude, not from $W$-annihilation as in the diquark model.

recall_papers.title:
Searching a systematics for nonfactorizable contribution to B-and B0
  mesons
recall_papers.abstract:
Two-body weak decays / and are examined under isospin analysis to study
nonfactorizable contributions. After extracting the strong phases and obtaining
the factorizable contributions from spectator-quark diagrams for Nc=3, we
determine nonfactorizable isospin amplitudes from the experimental data for
these modes. Our results support the universality of ratio of nonfactorizable
isospin reduced amplitudes for these decays within experimental errors. In
order to show that this systematics is not coincidental, we also plot our
results w. r. t. this ratio.
authors: Maninder Kaur, Supreet Pal Singh and R. C. Verma

recall_papers.title:
Analysis of Hadronic Weak Decays of Charmed Baryons in the Topological
  Diagrammatic Approach
recall_papers.abstract:
We perform a global fit to the experimental data of two-body charmed baryon
decays based on the topological diagrammatic approach (TDA) and take into
account the phase shifts between $S$- and $P$-wave amplitudes as inspired by
the recent BESIII measurement of the decay asymmetry in the decay
$\Lambda_c^+\to \Xi^0K^+$. The TDA has the advantage that it is more intuitive,
graphic and easier to implement model calculations. The measured branching
fractions and decay asymmetries are well accommodated in the TDA except for a
few modes, in particular, the predicted ${\cal B}(\Xi_c^0\to
\Xi^-\pi^+)=(2.83\pm0.10)\%$ is larger than its current value. The equivalence
of the TDA and the irreducible SU(3) approach (IRA) is established. We show
that the number of the minimum set of tensor invariants in the IRA and the
topological amplitudes in the TDA is the same and present their relations. The
predicted magnitudes of $S$- and $P$-wave amplitudes and their phase shifts are
presented for measured and yet-to-be-measured modes in both the TDA and IRA
which can be tested in the near future. Besides the decay $\Lambda_c^+\to \Xi^0
K^+$, there exist several modes which proceed only through $W$-exchange. In
particular, the observed channel $\Xi_c^0\to \Sigma^+ K^-$ should have phase
shifts similar to that in $\Lambda_c^+\to \Xi^0 K^+$ and its decay asymmetry is
predicted to be $-0.21\pm0.17$ which can be used to test our theoretical
framework. In contrast, the TDA leads to a large $\alpha$ of order $-0.93$ for
the decay $\Xi_c^+\to \Xi^0\pi^+$ even after the phase-shift effect is
incorporated in the fit.

sections.4: Kohara, “Quark diagram analysis of charmed baryon decays,” Phys
sections.15: Kohara, “Quark diagram analysis of charmed baryon decays,” Phys
sections.17: Kohara, “Comments on ’Analysis of two-body decays of charmed baryons using the quark diagram scheme’,” [arXiv:hep-ph/9701287 [hep-ph]].

authors: Huiling Zhong, Fanrong Xu, Hai-Yang Cheng

qualitative: The topological diagrammatic approach (TDA) provides an intuitive, graphical, and implementable framework for analyzing two-body charmed baryon weak decays, offering advantages over traditional model-independent approaches.

The TDA incorporates phase shifts between S- and P-wave amplitudes, inspired by recent BESIII measurements of decay asymmetry in $\Lambda_c^+ \to \Xi^0 K^+$, which improves the description of experimental data.

The TDA and the irreducible SU(3) approach (IRA) are equivalent in their underlying structure, with the same minimum number of tensor invariants in IRA matching the number of topological amplitudes in TDA, and explicit relations between them are established.
Several decay modes proceed exclusively through W-exchange diagrams, making them sensitive probes of non-factorizable contributions and offering unique tests of theoretical frameworks.

The decay $\Xi_c^0 \to \Sigma^+ K^-$ is predicted to have a decay asymmetry similar to that of $\Lambda_c^+ \to \Xi^0 K^+$, with a predicted asymmetry of $-0.21 \pm 0.17$, providing a key test of the theoretical model.
The TDA predicts a large decay asymmetry $\alpha \approx -0.93$ for $\Xi_c^+ \to \Xi^0 \pi^+$, even after including phase-shift effects, indicating a potential tension with future measurements.

The TDA successfully describes most measured branching fractions and decay asymmetries, except for $\Xi_c^0 \to \Xi^- \pi^+$, where the predicted branching fraction $(2.83 \pm 0.10)\%$ exceeds the current experimental value.
quantitative: Predicted branching fraction: $\mathcal{B}(\Xi_c^0 \to \Xi^- \pi^+) = (2.83 \pm 0.10)\%$
Predicted decay asymmetry: $\alpha(\Xi_c^0 \to \Sigma^+ K^-) = -0.21 \pm 0.17$
Predicted decay asymmetry: $\alpha(\Xi_c^+ \to \Xi^0 \pi^+) \approx -0.93$ (after phase-shift inclusion)
Phase shift effects between S- and P-wave amplitudes are incorporated based on BESIII measurement in $\Lambda_c^+ \to \Xi^0 K^+$
The number of minimum tensor invariants in IRA equals the number of topological amplitudes in TDA, establishing their equivalence

recall_papers.title:
The factorization-assisted topological-amplitude approach and its
  applications
recall_papers.abstract:
Heavy meson decays provide an important platform for studies of both QCD and
electroweak dynamics, which may contain some portals to understanding of
nonperturbative QCD and physics beyond the Standard Model. The
factorization-assisted topological-amplitude approach was proposed to study
two-body non-leptonic $D$ meson decays, where a promising QCD inspired approach
from first principles is still missing. It was also applied to $B$ meson decays
whose subleading power contributions are difficult to calculate. By factorizing
topological amplitudes into short distance Wilson coefficients and long
distance hadronic matrix elements either to be calculated or to be
parameterized, it provides an effective framework to extract information of
nonperturbative dynamics involved. With important flavor SU(3) breaking effects
taken into account, the data of the decay branching ratios (and also CP
asymmetries in $B$ decays) can be fitted well. The extracted amplitudes were
further applied to make predictions for other observables, such as CP
asymmetries in $D$ decays, mixing parameters in the $D^0-\bar{D}^0$ system, and
so on. By this review, we will describe the formulation of the
factorization-assisted topological-amplitude approach and summarize its
applications in $D$ and $B$ meson decays and highlight some of its
achievements.
authors: Qin Qin, Chao Wang, Di Wang, Si-Hong Zhou

recall_papers.title:
Non-factorisable Contributions of Strong-Penguin Operators in $\Lambda_b
  \to \Lambda \ell^+\ell^-$ Decays
recall_papers.abstract:
We investigate for the first time a certain class of non-factorisable
contributions of the four-quark operators ${\cal O}_{3-6}$ in the weak
effective Hamiltonian to the $\Lambda_b \to \Lambda \ell^+\ell^-$ decay
amplitude. We focus on the case where a virtual photon is radiated from one of
the light constituents of the $\Lambda_b$ baryon, in the kinematic situation of
large hadronic recoil with an energetic $\Lambda$ baryon in the final state.
The effect on the suitably defined ``non-local form factors'' is calculated
using the light-cone sum rule approach for a correlator with an interpolating
current for the light $\Lambda$ baryon. We find that this approach requires the
introduction of new soft functions that generalise the standard light-cone
distribution amplitudes (LCDAs) for the heavy $\Lambda_b$ baryon. We give a
heuristic discussion of their properties and a model that relates them to the
standard LCDAs. Within this framework, we provide numerical results for the
size of the non-local form factors considered.
authors: Thorsten Feldmann, Nico Gubernari

recall_papers.title:
CP violation in quasi-two-body $D\to VP$ decays and three-body $D$
  decays mediated by vector resonances
recall_papers.abstract:
We re-examine direct CP violation in the quasi-two-body $D\to V\!P$ decays
and study CP asymmetries in three-body $D$ decays proceeding through
intermediate vector resonances within the framework of the topological
amplitude approach for tree amplitudes and the QCD factorization approach for
penguin amplitudes. Using the same mechanism of incorporating long-distance
penguin-exchange amplitude induced from final-state rescattering that nicely
accounts for the CP asymmetry difference between $D^0\to K^+K^-$ and
$\pi^+\pi^-$ modes, we find that CP asymmetry can also occur at the per mille
level in many of the $D\to V\!P$ channels or otherwise be negligibly small. We
point out six golden modes which have sufficiently large branching fractions
and direct CP violation of order $10^{-3}$. In particular, the direct CP
asymmetry difference $\Delta a_{CP}^{V\!P}$ between $D^0\to K^+K^{*-}$ and
$\pi^+\rho^-$ is predicted to be $(-1.61\pm0.33)\times 10^{-3}$, very similar
to the counterpart in the $P\!P$ sector. Besides, we find a positive and large
CP asymmetry of order $10^{-3}$ in $D^0\to K_S K^{*0}$, an example of the
asymmetry induced at the tree level. We take into account the flavor-singlet
QCD-penguin contributions to calculate their effects on the modes such as
$D\to\pi\phi$ and $D\to \eta \phi$. We compare our results with the other
approach in detail. For three-body $D$ decays, we perform the Dalitz plot
analysis of those decays mediated by several vector resonances with the same
mass but different electric charges. Local CP asymmetry varies in magnitude and
sign from region to region and can reach the percent level in certain local
regions of phase space due to interference.
authors: Hai-Yang Cheng, Cheng-Wei Chiang

recall_papers.title: Topological Diagrams and Hadronic Weak Decays of Charmed Baryons
recall_papers.abstract:
Inspired by the recent BESIII measurement of the decay asymmetry and the
phase shift between $S$- and $P$-wave amplitudes in the decay $\Lambda_c^+\to
\Xi^0K^+$, we perform a global fit to the experimental data of charmed baryon
decays based on the topological diagrammatic approach (TDA) which has the
advantage that it is more intuitive and easier to implement model calculations.
The measured branching fractions and decay asymmetries are well accommodated in
TDA except for three modes, in particular, the predicted ${\cal B}(\Xi_c^0\to
\Xi^-\pi^+)=(2.83\pm0.10)\%$ is larger than its current value. The predicted
magnitudes of $S$- and $P$-wave amplitudes and their phase shifts are presented
for measured and yet-measured modes which can be tested in forthcoming
experiments.
authors: Huiling Zhong, Fanrong Xu, Hai-Yang Cheng

recall_papers.title: Two-body Baryonic $B_{u,d,s}$ and $B_c$ to Charmless Final State Decays
recall_papers.abstract:
We study the rates and direct CP violations of two-body baryonic $\bar
B_{u,d,s}\to {{\rm\bf B}\overline{\rm\bf B}}'$ and $B^-_c\to{{\rm\bf
B}\overline{\rm\bf B}}'$ decays, where the final state baryons include
low-lying octet and decuplet baryons. We incorporate topological amplitude
formalism and the factorization approach. Asymptotic relations at large $m_b$
are used to simplify decay amplitudes. Using the most up-to-date data on $\bar
B^0\to p\bar p$ and $B^-\to\Lambda\bar p$ decay rates as inputs, rates and
direct CP violations of $\bar B_{u,d,s}\to {{\rm\bf B}\overline{\rm\bf B}}'$
decays are revised and predicted. It is interesting that the results on rates
satisfy all existing experimental bounds and some are close to the bounds.
Factorization diagrams contribute to penguin-exchange, exchange, annihilation
and penguin-annihilation amplitudes. Although the resulting penguin-exchange
factorization amplitudes are sizable, the rest suffer from severe chiral
suppression and are sensitive to non-factorizable contributions. The $\bar
B_s\to p\bar p$ decay rate predicted in factorization calculation is very rare,
but it can be enhanced by including non-factorizable contributions. The case
where the rate is enhanced to saturate the present experimental bound is
discussed. As annihilation modes $B^-_c\to{{\rm\bf B}\overline{\rm\bf B}}'$
decays from factorization calculation are found to be very rare, but they can
be enhanced by including non-factorizable contributions as well. Small direct
CP violations of pure penguin modes in $\Delta S=-1$ $\bar B_{u,d,s}\to{{\rm\bf
B}\overline{\rm\bf B}}'$ decays are robust predictions of the SM, while
vanishing direct CP violations of exchange modes in $\bar B_{u,d,s}\to{{\rm\bf
B}\overline{\rm\bf B}}'$ decays and in all $B^-_c\to{{\rm\bf B}\overline{\rm\bf
B}}'$ decay modes are null tests of the SM.
authors: Chun-Khiang Chua

recall_papers.title: Towards a Global Analysis of the $b\to c\bar{u} q$ Puzzle
recall_papers.abstract: We study the nonleptonic decays $\bar{B}_s^0 \to D_s^{(*)+} \pi^-$ and $\bar{B}^0 \to D^{(*)+} K^-$ within the Weak Effective Theory (WET) up to mass-dimension six. We revisit the calculation of the hadronic matrix elements within QCD Factorization including the full set of WET operators. We recalculate the two-particle contributions to the hard-scattering kernels at next-to-leading order in $\alpha_s$, confirming recent results in the literature. We also calculate the three-particle contributions at leading order in $\alpha_s$, clarifying the procedure, refining the SM results in the literature, and providing for the first time the complete set of contributions within the WET. We use these results to perform a global phenomenological study of the effective couplings, putting bounds on the size of the WET Wilson coefficients in four distinct fit models. The fits include constraints from the nonleptonic $B$-meson decay width, which we calculate at the leading order for the full set of WET operators for the first time. This study is the first one to account for simultaneous variation of up to six effective couplings. We identify two distinct modes in all fit models and discuss how future measurements can be used to distinguish between them.
authors: Stefan Meiser, Danny van Dyk, Javier Virto

sorted_papers.title:
Updated analysis of $D\to PP, V\!P$ and $VV$ decays: Implications for
  $K_S^0-K_L^0$ asymmetries and $D^0$-$\overline {D}^0$ mixing
sorted_papers.abstract:
An updated analysis of the two-body $D\to PP, V\!P$ and $VV$ decays within
the framework of the topological diagram approach is performed. A global fit to
the Cabibbo-favored (CF) modes in the $V\!P$ sector gives many solutions with
similarly small local minima in $\chi^2$. The solution degeneracy is lifted
once we use them to predict for the singly Cabibbo-suppressed (SCS) modes.
Topological amplitudes are extracted for the $\eta-\eta'$ mixing angles
$\phi=40.4^\circ$ and $43.5^\circ$. The $K_S^0-K_L^0$ asymmetries in $D\to
K_{S,L}^0M$ decays denoted by $R(D,M)$ are studied. While the predicted
$R(D^0,P)$ for $P=\pi^0, \eta$ and $\eta'$ agree with experiment, the
calculated $R(D^+,\pi^+)$, $R(D_s^+, K^+)$, $R(D^0,\omega)$ and $R(D^0,\phi)$
deviate from the data. We conjecture that the relative phase between the
topological amplitudes $(C+A)$ and $(T+C)$ should be slightly smaller than
$90^\circ$ in order to explain the first two discrepancies and that additional
singlet contributions due to the SU(3)-singlet nature of $\omega$ and $\phi$
are needed to account for the last two. For doubly Cabibbo-suppressed (DCS)
$D\to V\!P$ decays, their topological amplitudes (double-primed) cannot be all
the same as the corresponding ones in the CF modes. The assumption of
$E_{V,P}''=E_{V,P}$ for the $W$-exchange amplitude leads to some
inconsistencies with the experiment. Through the measured relative phases
between CF and DCS channels, the relations of $E_{V,P}''$ with $E_{V,P}$ are
determined. Long-distance contributions to the $D^0$-$\overline {D}^0$ mixing
parameter $y$ are evaluated in the exclusive approach. In particular, we focus
on $D\to PP$ and $V\!P$ decays where $y$ can be reliably estimated. We conclude
that $y_{_{P\!P}}\sim (0.110\pm 0.011)\%$ and the lower bound on $y_{_{V\!P}}$
is $(0.220\pm 0.071)\%$.

sections.18: Ambrosino et al., “A global fit to determine the pseudoscalar mixing angle and the gluonium content of the η′superscript𝜂′\eta^{\prime} meson,” JHEP 0907, 105 (2009) [arXiv:0906.3819 [hep-ph]].

论文完全满足查询所有约束。研究聚焦于弱衰变因子化近似下的拓扑振幅分析，明确使用顶点图方法分析D介子衰变，涵盖PP、VP、VV模式，与查询核心一致。论文提取拓扑振幅、分析相位关系、讨论长程贡献，均契合“拓扑振幅分析”与“弱衰变因子化”要求。未涉及其他领域或方法，无偏离。

authors: Hai-Yang Cheng, Cheng-Wei Chiang

content_sections.I Introduction: Contrary to the bottom sector where the physics of two-body nonleptonic B𝐵B decays can be formulated in a QCD-inspired approach, a theoretical description of the underlying mechanism for exclusive hadronic D𝐷D decays based on QCD is still absent today. This has to do with the mass of the charm quark, of order 1.5 GeV. It is not heavy enough to allow for a sensible heavy quark mass expansion and not light enough for an application of chiral perturbation theory. Nevertheless, a model-independent analysis of charm decays based on the topological diagram approach (TDA) is achievable. In this approach, the topological diagrams are classified according to the topologies of weak interactions with strong interaction effects at all orders implicitly taken into account. Analyses of D→P​P→𝐷𝑃𝑃Dto P!P and V​P𝑉𝑃V!P decays have been performed in Refs. citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6 within the framework of TDA, where P𝑃P and V𝑉V denote respectively pseudoscalar and vector mesons. In this work, we shall perform an updated analysis for the following reasons: (i) In previous analyses, we have set Γ​(K¯0)=2​Γ​(KS0)Γsuperscript¯𝐾02Γsuperscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0Gamma(overlineK0)=2Gamma(KS0) for decay modes involving a neutral KS0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0KS0. But this relation can be invalidated by the interference between Cabibbo-favored (CF) and doubly Cabibbo-suppressed (DCS) amplitudes. (ii) Branching fraction measurements of several CF and singly Cabibbo-suppressed (SCS) D→V​P→𝐷𝑉𝑃Dto V!P modes have been significantly improved in recent years. These new data provide valuable information and modify the sizes and phases of the relevant topological amplitudes. (iii) DCS decays were not carefully studied in the previous analyses, particularly due to the lack of sufficiently precise data in the V​P𝑉𝑃V!P channels. We shall examine whether the topological amplitudes in the DCS sector are the same as those in the CF decays. We will calculate the KS0−KL0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0KS0-KL0 asymmetries in D→KS,L0​M→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆𝐿0𝑀Dto KS,L0M decays with M=P,V𝑀𝑃𝑉M=P,V and compare them with the experiment. This will provide information on the DCS topological amplitudes. (iv) Thanks to BESIII, many new data on Ds+superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠Ds+ and D+superscript𝐷D+ decays to V​V𝑉𝑉V!V became available in the past few years. It turns out that several D→V​V→𝐷𝑉𝑉Dto V!V modes are dominated by the D𝐷D-wave amplitude and some dominated by the P𝑃P-wave, contrary to the naïve expectation of S𝑆S-wave dominance. Moreover, the decay D0→ω​ϕ→superscript𝐷0𝜔italic-ϕD0toomegaphi has been observed by BESIII to be transversely polarized with negligible longitudinal polarizationcitebib.bib7. In the end, there are many puzzles in the V​V𝑉𝑉V!V sector that need to be resolved. (v) One of our goals is to evaluate the D0superscript𝐷0D0-D¯0superscript¯𝐷0overlineD0 mixing parameter which we are going to elaborate on below. The D0superscript𝐷0D0-D¯0superscript¯𝐷0overlineD0 mixing occurs because the mass eigenstates D1,2subscript𝐷12D1,2 are not the same as the flavor eigenstates D0superscript𝐷0D0 and D¯0superscript¯𝐷0overlineD0. D𝐷D mixing is conventionally described by the two parameters x≡Δ​m/Γ𝑥Δ𝑚ΓxequivDelta m/Gamma and y≡Δ​Γ/2​Γ𝑦ΔΓ2ΓyequivDeltaGamma/2Gamma, where Δ​m=m1−m2Δ𝑚subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2Delta m=m1-m2 defined to be positive and Δ​Γ=Γ1−Γ2ΔΓsubscriptΓ1subscriptΓ2DeltaGamma=Gamma1-Gamma2. Evidence for the D0superscript𝐷0D0-D¯0superscript¯𝐷0overlineD0 mixing has been established and the current world averages for the CP allowed case arecitebib.bib8: x=(0.409−0.049+0.048)%,y=(0.615−0.055+0.056)%. formulae-sequence𝑥percentsubscriptsuperscript0.4090.0480.049𝑦percentsubscriptsuperscript0.6150.0560.055displaystyle x=(0.409+0.048-0.049)%,qquad y=(0.615+0.056-0.055)%. The absence of mixing, namely x=y=0𝑥𝑦0x=y=0, is excluded at 11.5​σ11.5𝜎11.5sigma. Very recently, LHCb has made a model-independent measurement of charm mixing parameters in B→D0(→KS0π+π−)μ−ν¯μXBto D0(to KS0pi+pi-)mu-barnumuX decays and obtained the resultscitebib.bib9 x=(0.401±0.049)%,y=(0.55±0.13)%,formulae-sequence𝑥percentplus-or-minus0.4010.049𝑦percentplus-or-minus0.550.13displaystyle x=(0.401pm 0.049)%,qquad y=(0.55pm 0.13)%, which are consistent with current averages given in Eq. . Early calculations of short-distance contributions to the D𝐷D mixing parameters already indicated that both x𝑥x and y𝑦y were very small, of order 10−6superscript10610-6, due to the Glashow-Iliopoulos-Maiani suppressioncitebib.bib10, bib.bib11. This implies that the observed D0superscript𝐷0D0-D¯0superscript¯𝐷0overlineD0 mixing is dominated by long-distance processes. Indeed, it is well-known that charm physics is governed by non-perturbative effects. In the literature, the D0superscript𝐷0D0-D¯0superscript¯𝐷0overlineD0 mixing is usually studied in three different approaches: inclusive, exclusive, and dispersive. In the inclusive approach, the mixing parameters are systematically investigated based on heavy quark expansion (HQE) dictated by the parameter 1/mc1subscript𝑚𝑐1/mccitebib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16 (for a review, see Ref. citebib.bib17). Since the lifetime of Ds+superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠Ds+ and the ratio τ​(D+)/τ​(D0)𝜏superscript𝐷𝜏superscript𝐷0tau(D+)/tau(D0) have been found to be in good agreement with experiment within the framework of HQEcitebib.bib18, it is natural to expect that this approach might also be viable for the mixing parameters. However, it turns out that the suppression of short-distance contributions cannot be alleviated in HQE. Contrary to the HQE approach at the quark level, long-distance contributions from the intermediate hadronic states are summed over in the exclusive approachcitebib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib21, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26. As pointed out in Ref. citebib.bib21, the mixing parameters x𝑥x and y𝑦y vanish in the flavor SU limit. In general, there are large cancellations of CF and DCS decays with the contributions from SCS decays. The cancellation will be perfect in the limit of SU symmetry, and the D0superscript𝐷0D0-D¯0superscript¯𝐷0overlineD0 mixing would occur only at the second order in SU breaking. For example, contributions from SCS decays D0→π+​π−,K+​K−→superscript𝐷0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋superscript𝐾superscript𝐾D0topi+pi-,K+K- are canceled by contributions from the CF D0→K−​π+→superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾superscript𝜋D0to K-pi+ decay and the DCS D0→K+​π−→superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾superscript𝜋D0to K+pi- decay. Since the intermediate states in this case are related by U-spin symmetry, it has been shown in Ref. citebib.bib25 that the D0superscript𝐷0D0-D¯0superscript¯𝐷0overlineD0 mixing in the Standard Model occurs only at the second order in U-spin breaking. A dispersive relation between x𝑥x and y𝑦y has been derived in Ref. citebib.bib27 in the heavy quark limit Δ​m=−12​π​𝒫​∫2​mπ∞𝑑E​[Δ​Γ​(E)E−mD+𝒪​(ΛQCDE)]. For a given model of Δ​Γ​(E)ΔΓ𝐸DeltaGamma(E) or y​(E)𝑦𝐸y(E), it is conceivable to have x𝑥x comparable to y𝑦y in magnitude. Writing ∫0Λ𝑑s′​y​(s′)s−s′=π​x​(s)−∫Λ∞𝑑s′​y​(s′)s−s′subscriptsuperscriptΛ0differential-dsuperscript𝑠′𝑦superscript𝑠′𝑠superscript𝑠′𝜋𝑥𝑠subscriptsuperscriptΛdifferential-dsuperscript𝑠′𝑦superscript𝑠′𝑠superscript𝑠′displaystyleintLambda0dsprimey(sprime)over s-sprime=pi x(s)-intinftyLambdadsprimey(sprime)over s-sprime and choosing the scale ΛΛLambda large enough to justify the perturbative calculation of y𝑦y on the right-hand side, and below the b𝑏b quark threshold to avoid the b𝑏b quark contribution to the left-hand side, the authors of Ref. citebib.bib28 showed that the study of the D𝐷D meson mixing was converted into an inverse problem: the mixing parameters at low masses are solved as source distributions, which produce the potential observed at high masses. The analysis is further improved in Ref. citebib.bib29 in which SU breaking is introduced through physical thresholds of different D𝐷D meson decay channels. In this work, we shall focus on the exclusive scenario for the D0superscript𝐷0D0-D¯0superscript¯𝐷0overlineD0 mixing parameters. Since the two-body D0→P​P→superscript𝐷0𝑃𝑃D0to PP decays and quasi-two-body decays such as D0→V​P,V​V,S​P,S​V,A​P,A​V→superscript𝐷0𝑉𝑃𝑉𝑉𝑆𝑃𝑆𝑉𝐴𝑃𝐴𝑉D0to V!P,V!V,SP,SV,AP,AV, T​P,T​V𝑇𝑃𝑇𝑉TP,TV account for about 3/4 of the total hadronic rates, where S𝑆S, A𝐴A and T𝑇T denote respectively the scalar, axial-vector and tensor mesons, it is arguable that these two-body and quasi-two-body channels dominate and provide a good estimate of the mixing parameters. Data on two-body D→P​P,V​P→𝐷𝑃𝑃𝑉𝑃Dto PP,V!P decays have been accumulated in the past few years with substantially improved precisioncitebib.bib30. For example, the measurements of all D0→P​P→superscript𝐷0𝑃𝑃D0to PP channels are available except for three of the DCS modes. Hence, in principle, one can estimate yP​Psubscript𝑦𝑃𝑃yPP directly from the data. The DCS decays are related to the CF ones through the relations such as ℬ​(D0→K0​η(′))=tan4⁡θC​ℬ​(D0→K¯0​η(′))cal B(D0to K0eta(prime))=tan4thetaCcal B(D0tooverlineK0eta(prime)), where θCsubscript𝜃𝐶thetaC is the Cabibbo angle. However, we have applied the TDA in Ref. citebib.bib24 to estimate the D𝐷D mixing parameters for the following reasons: (i) to predict the branching fractions of yet-to-be-measured SCS and DCS modes in the D→V​P→𝐷𝑉𝑃Dto V!P decays, (ii) to understand SU breaking effects in the SCS and DCS modes, (iii) to see explicitly the vanishing mixing parameters in the SU limit, and (iv) to reduce the uncertainties in the estimate of the parameter y𝑦y. A similar study based on the so-called factorization-assisted topological-amplitude (FAT) approach has been carried out in Ref. citebib.bib26. The layout of the present paper is as follows. The analysis of D→P​P→𝐷𝑃𝑃Dto P!P decays within the framework of TDA is presented in Sec. for ϕ=40.4∘italic-ϕsuperscript40.4phi=40.4circ and 43.5∘superscript43.543.5circ with ϕitalic-ϕphi being the η−η′𝜂superscript𝜂′eta-etaprime mixing angle. As for D→V​P→𝐷𝑉𝑃Dto V!P decays, its analysis is much more complicated. We shall discuss the CF, SCS and DCS decays separately and consider the KS0−KL0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0KS0-KL0 asymmetries in D→KS,L0​V→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆𝐿0𝑉Dto KS,L0V channels in Sec. . Sec. is devoted to the discussions of D→V​V→𝐷𝑉𝑉Dto V!V decays. We evaluate the D0superscript𝐷0D0-D¯0superscript¯𝐷0overlineD0 mixing parameter y𝑦y in Sec. with a focus on the contributions from the P​P𝑃𝑃P!P and V​P𝑉𝑃V!P sectors. Sec. comes to our conclusions.

The fitted χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 value almost vanishes with the fit quality of 99.6%percent99.699.6% for ϕ=40.4∘italic-ϕsuperscript40.4phi=40.4circ and is 1.46 per degree of freedom with the fit quality of 22.7% for ϕ=43.5∘italic-ϕsuperscript43.5phi=43.5circ. Previous fits obtained in 2010citebib.bib1 and 2019citebib.bib5 are also listed in Table for comparison. We see that the errors in T𝑇T, C𝐶C, E𝐸E, and A𝐴A are substantially reduced, especially for the annihilation amplitude A𝐴A, thanks to the improved data precision from the Particle Data Group (PDG)citebib.bib30. We have noticed beforecitebib.bib5 that since we fit only the observed branching fractions, the results will be the same if all the strong phases are subject to a simultaneous sign flip, resulting in a two-fold ambiguity. Throughout this paper, we only present one of them. Presumably, such a degeneracy in strong phases can be resolved by measurements of sufficiently many CPasymmetries. For example, a measurement of direct CPasymmetry in D0→KS0​KS0→superscript𝐷0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0D0to KS0KS0 will allow us to resolve the discrete phase ambiguitycitebib.bib5. We see in Table that the topological amplitudes respect the hierarchical pattern |T|>|C|>|E|>|A|𝑇𝐶𝐸𝐴|T|>|C|>|E|>|A|. The phase between C𝐶C and T𝑇T is 150∘superscript150150circ, not far from the expectation of 180∘superscript180180circ from naïve factorization. The W𝑊W-exchange amplitude E𝐸E is sizable with a large phase of order 120∘superscript120120circ. This implies the importance of 1/mc1subscript𝑚𝑐1/mc power corrections as the short-distance contributions to E𝐸E are helicity suppressed. Notice that the W𝑊W-annihilation amplitude is smaller than the W𝑊W-exchange amplitude. Under naïve factorization, all the predicted topological amplitudes except T𝑇T are too small compared to the values extracted from the data, implying that topological amplitudes C𝐶C, E𝐸E and A𝐴A are dominated by long-distance, nonfactorizable effects.
content_sections.II D→P​P→𝐷𝑃𝑃D\\to P\\!P decays II.0.2 Si: We follow the conventional practice to denote the primed amplitudes for SCS modes and double-primed amplitudes for DCS decays. In the flavor SU limit, primed and unprimed amplitudes should be the same. It is known that there exists significant SU breaking in some of the SCS modes from the symmetry limit. For example, the rate of D0→K+​K−→superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾superscript𝐾D0to K+K- is larger than that of D0→π+​π−→superscript𝐷0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋D0topi+pi- by a factor of 2.82.82.8citebib.bib30, while the magnitudes of their decay amplitudes should be the same in the SU limit. The observation of the D0→K0​K¯0→superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾0superscript¯𝐾0D0to K0overlineK0 decay indicates that SU symmetry must also be broken in the topological amplitude E𝐸E. Indeed, as explained in detail in Ref. citebib.bib5, the large rate disparity between K+​K−superscript𝐾superscript𝐾K+K- and π+​π−superscript𝜋superscript𝜋pi+pi- cannot rely solely on the nominal SU breaking in the tree or W𝑊W-exchange amplitude. However, our prediction of R​(D+,π+)𝑅superscript𝐷superscript𝜋R(D+,pi+) is opposite to experiment in sign and the calculated R​(Ds+,K+)𝑅superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾R(Ds+,K+) is too small compared to the data, though they are consistent if errors are taken into account. This can be traced back to the relative phase between (C+A)𝐶𝐴(C+A) and (T+C)𝑇𝐶(T+C) which is 94.5∘superscript94.594.5circ. We find that if the phase difference is decreased slightly by 10∘superscript1010circ, that is, (C′′+A′′)→(C+A)​ei​10∘→superscript𝐶′′superscript𝐴′′𝐶𝐴superscript𝑒𝑖superscript10(Cprimeprime+Aprimeprime)to(C+A)ei10circ in D+→KS,L​π+→superscript𝐷subscript𝐾𝑆𝐿superscript𝜋D+to KS,Lpi+ and (T′′+C′′)→(T+C)​e−i​10∘→superscript𝑇′′superscript𝐶′′𝑇𝐶superscript𝑒𝑖superscript10(Tprimeprime+Cprimeprime)to(T+C)e-i10circ in Ds+→KS,L​K+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝐾𝑆𝐿superscript𝐾Ds+to KS,LK+, then we will be able to accommodate both R​(D+,π+)𝑅superscript𝐷superscript𝜋R(D+,pi+) and R​(Ds+,K+)𝑅superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾R(Ds+,K+). In the so-called factorization-assisted topological (FAT) approachcitebib.bib42, R​(D+,π+)𝑅superscript𝐷superscript𝜋R(D+,pi+) and R​(Ds+,K+)𝑅superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾R(Ds+,K+) are predicted to be 0.025±0.008plus-or-minus0.0250.0080.025pm 0.008 and 0.012±0.006plus-or-minus0.0120.0060.012pm 0.006, respectivelycitebib.bib43. While the former agrees with experiment, the latter is wrong in sign.
content_sections.III D→V​P→𝐷𝑉𝑃D\\to V\\!P decays III.0.1: For D→V​P→𝐷𝑉𝑃Dto V!P decays, there exist two different sets of topological diagrams since the spectator quark of the charmed meson may end up in the pseudoscalar or vector meson. A subscript of P𝑃P or V𝑉V is attached to the flavor amplitudes and the associated strong phases to denote respectively whether the spectator quark in the charmed meson ends up in the pseudoscalar or vector meson in the final state. As mentioned in passing, for the CF decay modes involving a neutral kaon KS0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0KS0 or KL0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0KL0, it was customary to use the relation Γ​(K¯0)=2​Γ​(KS0)Γsuperscript¯𝐾02Γsuperscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0Gamma(overlineK0)=2Gamma(KS0), but this relation can be invalidated by the interference between CF and DCS amplitudes. Just as the P​P𝑃𝑃P!P decays, we also prefer to apply the relation ℬ​(D→K¯0​V)≅ℬ​(D→KS0​V)+ℬ​(D→KL0​V)ℬ→𝐷superscript¯𝐾0𝑉ℬ→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0𝑉ℬ→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0𝑉cal B(DtooverlineK0V)congcal B(Dto KS0V)+cal B(Dto KL0V) in the V​P𝑉𝑃V!P sector. Because the additional SU-breaking factor in phase space has been taken care of, we prefer to use Eq. . Comparing Tables and with the previous five best solutions (S1’)-(S5’) obtained in Table II ofcitebib.bib6, it is evident that (i) the magnitudes of |CV|subscript𝐶𝑉|CV| and |AV−AP|subscript𝐴𝑉subscript𝐴𝑃|AV-AP| are decreased, whereas |EV|subscript𝐸𝑉|EV| is increased, and (ii) the uncertainties in the magnitudes and phases of AP,Vsubscript𝐴𝑃𝑉AP,V are significantly improved. Since the annihilation amplitude APsubscript𝐴𝑃AP is very suppressed compared to CVsubscript𝐶𝑉CV, this implies that the magnitude of CV​(F​4)subscript𝐶𝑉𝐹4CV(F4), 1.38±0.03plus-or-minus1.380.031.38pm 0.03, (see Table) is smaller than |CV​(S​3′)|=1.69±0.04subscript𝐶𝑉𝑆superscript3′plus-or-minus1.690.04|CV(S3prime)|=1.69pm 0.04citebib.bib6. Since A​(D0→K¯0​ρ0)∝(CV−EV)proportional-to𝐴→superscript𝐷0superscript¯𝐾0superscript𝜌0subscript𝐶𝑉subscript𝐸𝑉A(D0tooverlineK0rho0)propto(CV-EV), a decrease in |CV|subscript𝐶𝑉|CV| implies an increase in |EV|subscript𝐸𝑉|EV|. A new measurement of Ds+→π+​ρ0→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜋superscript𝜌0Ds+topi+rho0 indicates that its branching fraction is significantly improved from (1.2±0.6)×10−4plus-or-minus1.20.6superscript104(1.2pm 0.6)times 10-4citebib.bib30 to (1.12±0.13)×10−4plus-or-minus1.120.13superscript104(1.12pm 0.13)times 10-4citebib.bib47 with a much better precision. This in turn implies a smaller |AV−AP|subscript𝐴𝑉subscript𝐴𝑃|AV-AP| and more precise values of AVsubscript𝐴𝑉AV and APsubscript𝐴𝑃AP. content_sections.III D→V​P→𝐷𝑉𝑃D\\to V\\!P decays III.0.2: Although the five solutions generally fit the CF modes well (see Table), there is one exception, namely, Ds+→ρ+​η′→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜌superscript𝜂′Ds+torho+etaprime, whose prediction is smaller than the experimental result. As explained in Ref. citebib.bib4, this mode has a decay amplitude respecting a sum rule: A​(Ds+→π+​ω)=cos⁡ϕ​A​(Ds+→ρ+​η)+sin⁡ϕ​A​(Ds+→ρ+​η′). 𝐴→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜋𝜔italic-ϕ𝐴→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜌𝜂italic-ϕ𝐴→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜌superscript𝜂′displaystyle A(Ds+topi+omega)=cosphi,A(Ds+torho+eta)+sinphi,A(Ds+torho+etaprime). The current data of ℬ​(Ds+→π+​ω)ℬ→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜋𝜔cal B(Ds+topi+omega) and ℬ​(Ds+→ρ+​η)ℬ→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜌𝜂cal B(Ds+torho+eta) give the bounds 1.6%ℬ​(D+→π+​ω)=0.28±0.06,ℬ​(D0→π0​ρ0)=3.86±0.23>ℬ​(D0→η​ω)=1.98±0.18,≫ℬ​(D0→π0​ω)=0.117±0.035. displaystylebeginsplitcal B(D+topi+rho0)=0.83pm 0.14>cal B(D+topi+omega)=0.28pm 0.06,, cal B(D0topi0rho0)=3.86pm 0.23>cal B(D0toetaomega)=1.98pm 0.18,, ggcal B(D0topi0omega)=0.117pm 0.035,. endsplit Since AVsubscript𝐴𝑉AV and APsubscript𝐴𝑃AP are comparable in magnitude and roughly parallel to each other, it is tempting to argue from Eq. that D+→π+​ω→superscript𝐷superscript𝜋𝜔D+topi+omega should have a rate larger than D+→π+​ρ0→superscript𝐷superscript𝜋superscript𝜌0D+topi+rho0, which is opposite to the experimental finding. Since CPsubscript𝐶𝑃CP is comparable to TVsubscript𝑇𝑉TV in magnitude, there is a large cancellation between TVsubscript𝑇𝑉TV and the real part of CPsubscript𝐶𝑃CP. In general, the experimental constraint from Eq. can be satisfied provided that the imaginary part of CPsubscript𝐶𝑃CP has a sign opposite to the imaginary part of AVsubscript𝐴𝑉AV or APsubscript𝐴𝑃AP. We find explicitly that the only allowed solutions are (F3), (F4), (F3’) and (F5’). Branching fractions of D0→V​V→superscript𝐷0𝑉𝑉D0to VV in partial waves calculated in the factorization approach are presented in Table. Since the W𝑊W-exchange contributions are neglected in naïve factorization, no estimate is made for the branching fractions of K∗0​K¯∗0superscript𝐾absent0superscript¯𝐾absent0K*0overlineK*0, K¯∗0​ϕsuperscript¯𝐾absent0italic-ϕoverlineK*0phi, K∗0​ϕsuperscript𝐾absent0italic-ϕK*0phi and ρ0​ωsuperscript𝜌0𝜔rho0omega. For the effective Wilson coefficients, we have used a1=0.90subscript𝑎10.90a1=0.90 and a2=−0.64subscript𝑎20.64a2=-0.64 . Comparing Table with the measured partial-wave rates given in Table shows that the predictions based on factorization deviate from the experimental measurements. This indicates the necessity of taking into account the non-factorizable W𝑊W-exchange contributions which might account for the D𝐷D-wave dominance observed in D0→K∗−​ρ+,K¯∗0​ρ0,ρ+​ρ−→superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾absentsuperscript𝜌superscript¯𝐾absent0superscript𝜌0superscript𝜌superscript𝜌D0to K*-rho+,overlineK*0rho0,rho+rho- and ρ0​ρ0superscript𝜌0superscript𝜌0rho0rho0 channels, an issue to be investigated in the near future. The predicted longitudinal polarization fLsubscript𝑓𝐿fL ranges from 0.31 to 0.49.
content_sections.V D0superscript𝐷0D^{0}-D¯0superscript¯𝐷0: The two-body decays D0→P​P→superscript𝐷0𝑃𝑃D0to PP and quasi-two-body decays D0→V​P,V​V,S​P,S​V,A​P,A​V→superscript𝐷0𝑉𝑃𝑉𝑉𝑆𝑃𝑆𝑉𝐴𝑃𝐴𝑉D0to V!P,V!V,SP,SV,AP,AV, T​P,T​V𝑇𝑃𝑇𝑉TP,TV account for about 3/4 of the total hadronic rates. Many of the 3-body final states arise from S​P𝑆𝑃SP, V​P𝑉𝑃V!P, and T​P𝑇𝑃T!P decays, the 4-body states from V​V𝑉𝑉V!V and A​P𝐴𝑃AP decays, and the 5-body states from A​V𝐴𝑉AV decays. The nonresonant 3-body and 4-body decays are at most 10% of the multi-body decay rates. Hence, it is arguable that these two-body and quasi-two-body channels dominate and can provide a good estimate of the mixing parameters. As mentioned in the Introduction, use of the TDA is made to reduce the uncertainties with the measured channels and estimate those modes yet to be observed. In particular, we focus on D→P​P→𝐷𝑃𝑃Dto PP and V​P𝑉𝑃V!P decays and present updated topological amplitudes. As shown in Ref. citebib.bib5, we find from the measured branching fractions that |TV+EP|π+​ρ−|TV+EP|K+​K∗−=1.08,|TP+EV|π−​ρ+|TV+EP|K−​K∗+=0.91. formulae-sequencesubscriptsubscript𝑇𝑉subscript𝐸𝑃superscript𝜋superscript𝜌subscriptsubscript𝑇𝑉subscript𝐸𝑃superscript𝐾superscript𝐾absent1.08subscriptsubscript𝑇𝑃subscript𝐸𝑉superscript𝜋superscript𝜌subscriptsubscript𝑇𝑉subscript𝐸𝑃superscript𝐾superscript𝐾absent0.91displaystyle|TV+EP|pi+rho-over|TV+EP|K+K*-=1.08,,qquadquad|TP+EV|pi-rho+over|TV+EP|K-K*+=0.91,. This implies that SU breaking in the amplitudes of TV+EPsubscript𝑇𝑉subscript𝐸𝑃TV+EP and TP+EVsubscript𝑇𝑃subscript𝐸𝑉TP+EV is small, contrary to the P​P𝑃𝑃P!P case. This means that SU violation in the decay amplitudes plays a less significant role in D→V​P→𝐷𝑉𝑃Dto V!P decays. Therefore, an estimate of yV​Psubscript𝑦𝑉𝑃yV!P solely based on SU symmetry breaking in the phase space leads to a correct sign of y𝑦y.

The V​V𝑉𝑉VV states with different partial waves contribute with different CP parties. From Table, it is easily seen that the contribution yK∗,ρ=ℬ​(ρ+​ρ−)ℓ+ℬ​(K∗+​K∗−)ℓ−2​cos⁡δK∗−​ρ+​ℬ​(K∗−​ρ+)ℓ​ℬ​(K∗+​ρ−)ℓsubscript𝑦superscript𝐾𝜌ℬsubscriptsuperscript𝜌superscript𝜌ℓℬsubscriptsuperscript𝐾absentsuperscript𝐾absentℓ2subscript𝛿superscript𝐾absentsuperscript𝜌ℬsubscriptsuperscript𝐾absentsuperscript𝜌ℓℬsubscriptsuperscript𝐾absentsuperscript𝜌ℓdisplaystyle yK*,rho=cal B(rho+rho-)ell+cal B(K*+K*-)ell-2cosdeltaK*-rho+sqrtcal B(K*-rho+)ellcal B(K*+rho-)ell vanishes in the SU limit. This U𝑈U-spin relation cannot be tested by the current data as only the D0→K∗−​ρ+→superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾absentsuperscript𝜌D0to K*-rho+ and D0→ρ+​ρ−→superscript𝐷0superscript𝜌superscript𝜌D0torho+rho- decays have been measured. Notice that although D0→ϕ​ϕ→superscript𝐷0italic-ϕitalic-ϕD0tophiphi is prohibited by phase space, it is needed in order to show a vanishing yV​V,ℓsubscript𝑦𝑉𝑉ℓyVV,ell in the SU limit. An estimate using the naïve factorization results from Table yields yV​V,S=−0.167%,yV​V,P=1.61×10−4,yV​V,D=4.71×10−5. formulae-sequencesubscript𝑦𝑉𝑉𝑆percent0.167formulae-sequencesubscript𝑦𝑉𝑉𝑃1.61superscript104subscript𝑦𝑉𝑉𝐷4.71superscript105displaystyle yV!V,S=-0.167%,qquad yV!V,P=1.61times 10-4,qquad yV!V,D=4.71times 10-5. Since the predicted rates of D0→V​V→superscript𝐷0𝑉𝑉D0to VV in partial waves based on naïve factorization do not resemble the data given in Table, particularly for the D𝐷D-wave dominance in the D0→K∗−​ρ+→superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾absentsuperscript𝜌D0to K*-rho+, K¯∗0​ρ0superscript¯𝐾absent0superscript𝜌0overlineK*0rho0 , ρ+​ρ−superscript𝜌superscript𝜌rho+rho- and ρ0​ρ0superscript𝜌0superscript𝜌0rho0rho0 decays as suggested by the current data, it is premature to have a reliable estimate of yV​V,ℓsubscript𝑦𝑉𝑉ℓyV!V,ell. Indeed, if we use the data in Table as the input and employ the predictions based on naïve factorization for those modes yet to be measured, we get yV​V,S=0.0271%,yV​V,P=−0.0958%,yV​V,D=0.361%. formulae-sequencesubscript𝑦𝑉𝑉𝑆percent0.0271formulae-sequencesubscript𝑦𝑉𝑉𝑃percent0.0958subscript𝑦𝑉𝑉𝐷percent0.361displaystyle yV!V,S=0.0271%,qquad yV!V,P=-0.0958%,qquad yV!V,D=0.361%. The results obtained in Ref. citebib.bib21 in which only SU violation in the phase space is considered are given by S-wave:yV​V,8=−0.39%,yV​V,27=−0.30%,P-wave:yV​V,8=−0.48%,yV​V,27=−0.70%,D-wave:yV​V,8=2.5%,yV​V,27=2.8%. displaystylebeginsplitSmbox-wave:qquad yV!V,8=-0.39%,qquad yV!V,27=-0.30%, Pmbox-wave:qquad yV!V,8=-0.48%,qquad yV!V,27=-0.70%, Dmbox-wave:qquad yV!V,8=2.5%,qquadquad yV!V,27=2.8%. endsplit Because of the momentum dependence of the D𝐷D-wave is proportional to pc3superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑐3pc3, the D𝐷D-wave phase space is most sensitive to the SU breaking in pcsubscript𝑝𝑐pc.

There are hadronic D𝐷D decays into an even-parity meson M𝑀M and a pseudoscalar meson or a vector meson, where M𝑀M represents a scalar meson S𝑆S, an axial-vector meson A𝐴A, or a tensor meson T𝑇T. They have been studied in the literaturecitebib.bib71, bib.bib72, bib.bib73, but the data are not adequate to allow for the extraction of topological amplitudes, especially for the W𝑊W-exchange amplitudes. These will have to be left to a future investigation.
content_sections.VI Conclusions: In this work, we have presented an updated analysis of the two-body decays D→P​P→𝐷𝑃𝑃Dto PP, V​P𝑉𝑃V!P and V​V𝑉𝑉VV decays within the framework of the topological diagram approach. For the CF decay modes involving a neutral kaon, KS0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0KS0 or KL0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0KL0, the relation Γ​(K¯0)=2​Γ​(KS0)Γsuperscript¯𝐾02Γsuperscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0Gamma(overlineK0)=2Gamma(KS0) can be invalidated by the interference between the CF and DCS amplitudes. Since the topological amplitudes in DCS modes are not necessarily the same as those in CF ones beyond flavor SU symmetry, we prefer to use the good approximate relation ℬ​(D→K¯0​M)≅ℬ​(D→KS0​M)+ℬ​(D→KL0​M)ℬ→𝐷superscript¯𝐾0𝑀ℬ→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0𝑀ℬ→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0𝑀cal B(DtooverlineK0M)congcal B(Dto KS0M)+cal B(Dto KL0M) for M=P𝑀𝑃M=P and V𝑉V. In the P​P𝑃𝑃PP sector, the tree topological amplitudes T𝑇T, C𝐶C, E𝐸E and A𝐴A are extracted from CF D→P​P→𝐷𝑃𝑃Dto PP decays for the η−η′𝜂superscript𝜂′eta-etaprime mixing angle ϕ=40.4∘italic-ϕsuperscript40.4phi=40.4circ and 43.5∘superscript43.543.5circ, respectively. The fitted χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 values almost vanish with the quality close to unity for ϕ=40.4∘italic-ϕsuperscript40.4phi=40.4circ. Assuming that the double-primed amplitudes in the DCS sector are the same as the unprimed ones, the calculated KS0−KL0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0KS0-KL0 asymmetries R​(D0,P)𝑅superscript𝐷0𝑃R(D0,P) for D0→KS,L0​P→superscript𝐷0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆𝐿0𝑃D0to KS,L0P decays with P=π0𝑃superscript𝜋0P=pi0, η𝜂eta and η′superscript𝜂′etaprime agree with the experiment, meaning that D0→KS0​P→superscript𝐷0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0𝑃D0to KS0P has a rate larger than that of D0→KL0​P→superscript𝐷0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0𝑃D0to KL0P. However, our predicted R​(D+,π+)𝑅superscript𝐷superscript𝜋R(D+,pi+) is opposite to the experiment in sign and the calculated R​(Ds+,K+)𝑅superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾R(Ds+,K+) is too small compared to the data. This is ascribed to the fact that the relative phase between (C+A)𝐶𝐴(C+A) and (T+C)𝑇𝐶(T+C) is slightly larger than 90∘superscript9090circ, rendering the interference between D+→K¯0​π+→superscript𝐷superscript¯𝐾0superscript𝜋D+tooverlineK0pi+ and D+→K0​π+→superscript𝐷superscript𝐾0superscript𝜋D+to K0pi+ destructive in D+→KS0​π+→superscript𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0superscript𝜋D+to KS0pi+ and constructive in D+→KL0​π+→superscript𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0superscript𝜋D+to KL0pi+. This is opposite to the pattern observed experimentally. We find that if the phase difference is decreased slightly by 10∘superscript1010circ, we are able to accommodate both R​(D+,π+)𝑅superscript𝐷superscript𝜋R(D+,pi+) and R​(Ds+,K+)𝑅superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾R(Ds+,K+). In the V​P𝑉𝑃V!P sector, if the double-primed topological amplitudes in DCS decays are taken to be the same as the unprimed ones, we will be led to some predictions not in accordance with the experiment. That is why we prefer to apply the relation ℬ​(D→K¯0​V)≅ℬ​(D→KS0​V)+ℬ​(D→KL0​V)ℬ→𝐷superscript¯𝐾0𝑉ℬ→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0𝑉ℬ→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0𝑉cal B(DtooverlineK0V)congcal B(Dto KS0V)+cal B(Dto KL0V). Unfortunately, we have the data of ℬ​(D→KL0​V)ℬ→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0𝑉cal B(Dto KL0V) for V=ω𝑉𝜔V=omega and ϕitalic-ϕphi, but not for V=ρ0𝑉superscript𝜌0V=rho0, ρ+superscript𝜌rho+ and K∗+superscript𝐾absentK*+. Since it is most likely that EV,P′′≠EV,Psuperscriptsubscript𝐸𝑉𝑃′′subscript𝐸𝑉𝑃EV,Pprimeprimeneq EV,P, we fit the topological amplitudes to D0→K¯0​(ω,ϕ)→superscript𝐷0superscript¯𝐾0𝜔italic-ϕD0tooverlineK0(omega,phi), D+→KS0​ρ+→superscript𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0superscript𝜌D+to KS0rho+ and Ds+→KS0​K∗+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0superscript𝐾absentDs+to KS0K*+, but not D0→KS0​ρ0→superscript𝐷0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0superscript𝜌0D0to KS0rho0. A global fit to the CF modes in the V​P𝑉𝑃V!P sector gives many solutions with similarly small local minima in χ2superscript𝜒2chi2: (F1)-(F5) for ϕ=40.4∘italic-ϕsuperscript40.4phi=40.4circ and (F1’)-(F5’) for ϕ=43.5∘italic-ϕsuperscript43.5phi=43.5circ, when we restrict ourselves to χmin2<10subscriptsuperscript𝜒2min10chi2rm min<10. The solution degeneracy is lifted once we use them to predict for the SCS modes. In the end, we find that only Solutions (F4) and (F1’) can accommodate all SCS modes except that the predicted ℬ​(D+→π+​ω)ℬ→superscript𝐷superscript𝜋𝜔cal B(D+topi+omega) in (F1’) is slightly larger than the data. These two solutions have a common feature that CVsubscript𝐶𝑉CV and CPsubscript𝐶𝑃CP are close in phase in order to simultaneously explain the small ℬ​(D0→π0​ω)ℬ→superscript𝐷0superscript𝜋0𝜔cal B(D0topi0omega) and large ℬ​(D0→π0​ρ0)ℬ→superscript𝐷0superscript𝜋0superscript𝜌0cal B(D0topi0rho0). The annihilation amplitudes AVsubscript𝐴𝑉AV and APsubscript𝐴𝑃AP are more precisely determined than before because of a significantly improved new measurement of Ds+→π+​ρ0→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜋superscript𝜌0Ds+topi+rho0; they are comparable in size and similar in phase. For DCS D→V​P→𝐷𝑉𝑃Dto V!P decays, the assumption of EV,P′′=EV,Psuperscriptsubscript𝐸𝑉𝑃′′subscript𝐸𝑉𝑃EV,Pprimeprime=EV,P leads to some inconsistencies with the experiment; for example, the predicted strong phases δK∗+​π−subscript𝛿superscript𝐾absentsuperscript𝜋deltaK*+pi- and δK∗0​π0subscript𝛿superscript𝐾absent0superscript𝜋0deltaK*0pi0 are too large compared to their experimental values. The relations of EV,P′′superscriptsubscript𝐸𝑉𝑃′′EV,Pprimeprime with EV,Psubscript𝐸𝑉𝑃EV,P are given in Eqs. and , respectively. The KS0−KL0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0KS0-KL0 asymmetries in the D→KS,L0​V→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆𝐿0𝑉Dto KS,L0V decays are shown in Table. The calculated R​(D0,ω)𝑅superscript𝐷0𝜔R(D0,omega) and R​(D0,ϕ)𝑅superscript𝐷0italic-ϕR(D0,phi) do not agree with the experiment. We conjecture that additional singlet contributions due to SU-singlet nature of ω𝜔omega and ϕitalic-ϕphi should account for the discrepancy. Thanks to BESIII, many new data on Ds+superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠Ds+ and D+superscript𝐷D+ to V​V𝑉𝑉V!V decays became available in recent years. In the meantime, several new puzzles have also emerged. For example, D0→K∗−​ρ+→superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾absentsuperscript𝜌D0to K*-rho+, K¯∗0​ρ0superscript¯𝐾absent0superscript𝜌0overlineK*0rho0, ρ+​ρ−superscript𝜌superscript𝜌rho+rho-, and ρ0​ρ0superscript𝜌0superscript𝜌0rho0rho0 seem to be dominated by the D𝐷D-wave, while Ds+→K∗0​ρ+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾absent0superscript𝜌Ds+to K*0rho+ and K∗−​ρ0superscript𝐾absentsuperscript𝜌0K*-rho0 dominated by the P𝑃P-wave, contrary to the naïve expectation of S𝑆S-wave dominance. In spite of the progresses in the field, the data are still not adequate to allow for a meaningful extraction of helicity or partial-wave amplitudes, especially for the W𝑊W-exchange and W𝑊W-annihilation ones. Yet another goal of this analysis is to evaluate the D0superscript𝐷0D0-D¯0superscript¯𝐷0overlineD0 mixing parameter y𝑦y using the exclusive approach through the 2-body decays of the D0superscript𝐷0D0 meson. The mixing parameter is usually small owing to large cancellation between the SCS terms and the interference of CF and DCS terms. As the topological amplitude analysis is available for D→P​P→𝐷𝑃𝑃Dto PP and V​P𝑉𝑃V!P decays, we are able to estimate yP​Psubscript𝑦𝑃𝑃yP!P and yV​Psubscript𝑦𝑉𝑃yV!P more reliably. We conclude that yP​P∼(0.110±0.011)%similar-tosubscript𝑦𝑃𝑃percentplus-or-minus0.1100.011yP!Psim(0.110pm 0.011)% and the lower bound on yV​Psubscript𝑦𝑉𝑃yV!P is (0.220±0.071)%percentplus-or-minus0.2200.071(0.220pm 0.071)%. It is thus conceivable that at least half of the D0superscript𝐷0D0-D¯0superscript¯𝐷0overlineD0 mixing parameter y𝑦y is accounted for by the P​P𝑃𝑃PP and V​P𝑉𝑃V!P modes. The main uncertainties arise from the DCS channels yet to be measured and the phase factors cos⁡δnsubscript𝛿𝑛cosdeltan’s.

sorted_papers.title:
Analysis of three-body decays $B \to D (V \to ) PP $ under the
  factorization-assisted topological-amplitude approach
sorted_papers.abstract:
Motived by the accumulated experimental results on three-body charmed $B$
decays with resonance contributions in Babar, LHCb and Belle (II), we
systematically analyze $B_{(s)} \to D_{(s)} (V \to) P_1 P_2 $ decays with $V$
representing a vector resonance ($\rho, K^*, \omega$ or $\phi$) and $P_{1,2}$
as a light pseudoscalar meson (pion or kaon). The intermediate subprocesses
$B_{(s)} \to D_{(s)} V$ are calculated with the factorization-assisted
topological-amplitude (FAT) approach and the intermediate resonant states $V$
described by the relativistic Breit-Wigner distribution successively decay to
$P_1 P_2$ via strong interaction. Taking all lowest resonance states ($\rho,
K^*, \omega, \phi$) into account, we calculate the branching fractions of these
decay modes as well as the Breit-Wigner-tail effects for $B_{(s)} \to D_{(s)}
(\rho ,\omega \to) KK$. Our results agree with the data by Babar, LHCb and
Belle (II). Among the predictions that are still not observed, there are some
branching ratios of order $10^{-6}-10^{-4}$ which are hopeful to be measured by
LHCb and Belle II. Our approach and the perturbative QCD approach (PQCD) adopt
the compatible theme to deal with the resonance contributions. What's more, our
data for the intermediate two-body charmed $B$-meson decays in FAT approach are
more precise. As a result, our results for branching fractions have smaller
uncertainties, especially for color-suppressed emission diagram dominated
modes.
