qualitative: The factorization-assisted topological-amplitude (FAT) approach is employed to systematically analyze three-body $B_{(s)} \to D_{(s)} (V \to) P_1 P_2$ decays, where $V$ denotes a vector resonance ($\rho, K^*, \omega, \phi$) and $P_{1,2}$ are light pseudoscalar mesons (pions or kaons).
The intermediate subprocess $B_{(s)} \to D_{(s)} V$ is calculated using the FAT approach, which incorporates topological amplitude decomposition and factorization assumptions.
The resonant vector states $V$ are described by relativistic Breit-Wigner distributions, and their subsequent decays to $P_1 P_2$ are governed by strong interactions.
The analysis includes all lowest-lying vector resonances ($\rho, K^*, \omega, \phi$) and accounts for Breit-Wigner tail effects, particularly in $B_{(s)} \to D_{(s)} (\rho, \omega \to) KK$ decays.
The FAT approach provides more precise results for intermediate two-body charmed $B$-meson decays compared to other methods, leading to smaller uncertainties in branching fraction predictions.
The FAT approach and the perturbative QCD (PQCD) approach share a compatible framework for handling resonance contributions in three-body decays.
The predictions for branching fractions in unobserved modes are in the range $10^{-6} - 10^{-4}$, which are within reach of future measurements by LHCb and Belle II.
Color-suppressed emission diagram-dominated modes benefit significantly from the reduced uncertainties in the FAT approach due to improved precision in the underlying two-body decay amplitudes.
quantitative: Branching fractions for $B_{(s)} \to D_{(s)} (V \to) P_1 P_2$ decays are calculated with the FAT approach, showing agreement with experimental data from Babar, LHCb, and Belle (II).
Predicted branching ratios for unobserved modes are in the range $\mathcal{B} \sim 10^{-6} - 10^{-4}$.
The relativistic Breit-Wigner distribution is used to model resonant states: $\rho, K^*, \omega, \phi$, with their decay widths and masses taken from experimental inputs.
The FAT approach yields smaller uncertainties in branching fractions, especially for color-suppressed emission diagram-dominated modes, due to more precise input amplitudes.
The Breit-Wigner-tail effects are explicitly included in the analysis of $B_{(s)} \to D_{(s)} (\rho, \omega \to) KK$ decays, contributing to the shape of the invariant mass distributions.

content_sections.I Introduction: Three-body nonleptonic B𝐵B meson decays not only are important for the study of common topics of nonleptonic B𝐵B meson decays, such as testing the standard model (SM), the studying the mechanism of C​P𝐶𝑃CP violation and the emergence of quantum chromodynamics, but also provide opportunities for the analysis on the hadron spectroscopy. Specifically, three-body B𝐵B meson decays have nontrivial kinematics and phase space distributions, which are usually described in terms of three two-body invariant mass squared combinations and two of them constitute two axes to form a Dalitz plot. In the edges of the Dalitz plot, the invariant mass squared combinations of two final-state particles will generally peak as resonances, which indicates that intermediate resonances in three-body B𝐵B meson decays show up, and we are able to study the properties of these resonances through three-body B𝐵B meson decays. The Dalitz plot technique has proven to be a powerful tool to analyze the hadron spectroscopy and is widely adopted by experiments. The informations on various resonance substructures including the mass, spin-parity quantum numbers, etc. have been collected by Babar, Belle (II) and LHCb citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8. Simultaneously, usually under the framework of isobar model citebib.bib9, these collaborations have also measured fit fractions of each resonance and nonresonance components. In the isobar model, the total decay amplitude can be expressed as a coherent sum of amplitudes of different resonant and nonresonant intermediate processes, where the relativistic Breit-Wigner (RBW) model usually describe resonant dynamics and exponential distribution for nonresonant terms. It is a very good approximation to adopt the RBW function for narrow width resonances which can be well separated from any other resonant or nonresonant components in the same partial wave, so that the three-body decays with narrow intermediate states, such as ρ,K∗,ω,ϕ𝜌superscript𝐾𝜔italic-ϕrho,K*,omega,phi, have been precisely measured by experiments citebib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib10, bib.bib4, bib.bib11, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib12. On the theoretical side, analysis on the nonresonant contributions of three-body B𝐵B meson decays are in an early stage of development. Approaches or models such as the heavy meson chiral perturbation theory citebib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15 and a model combing the heavy quark effective theory and chiral Lagrangian citebib.bib16 have been applied for calculating the nonresonant fraction of three-body charmless B𝐵B meson decays, such as B→K​K​K,B→K​π​πformulae-sequence→𝐵𝐾𝐾𝐾→𝐵𝐾𝜋𝜋Bto KKK,,Bto Kpipi which are dominated by nonresonant contribution citebib.bib17. More theoretical interest is concentrated on the resonant component of three-body B𝐵B decays, where two of the three final particles are produced from a resonance and recoil against the third meson called a “bachelor” meson. This type of three-body decay is also called quasi-two-body decay. Because of the large energy release in B𝐵B meson decays, the two meson pair moves fast antiparrallelly to the bachelor meson in the B𝐵B meson rest frame. Therefore, the interactions between the meson pair and the bachelor particle are power suppressed naturally which is similar to the statement of “color transparency” in two-body B𝐵B meson decays. Then approaches based on the factorization hypothesis have been proposed for calculating the quasi-two-body decays, such as the QCD Factorization (QCDF) citebib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib18, the PQCD approachcitebib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35 and factorization-assisted topological-amplitude (FAT) approach citebib.bib36, bib.bib37. In this work, we focus on three-body charmed B𝐵B decays B(s)→D(s)​P1​P2→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2B(s)to D(s)P1P2, where P1,2subscript𝑃12P1,2 represents a pion or kaon. Different from charmless decays, intermediate resonances in B(s)→D(s)​P1​P2→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2B(s)to D(s)P1P2 decays are expected to appear in the m2​(D​P1)superscript𝑚2𝐷subscript𝑃1m2(DP1) and m2​(P1​P2)superscript𝑚2subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2m2(P1P2) combinations, thus more resonances, such as charmed states D∗superscript𝐷D* and light vector or scalar resonances, can be researched simultaneously in one Dalitz plot. In addition, they provide opportunities for studies of CP violations. In particular, the Dalitz plot analysis of B0→D​K+​π−→superscript𝐵0𝐷superscript𝐾superscript𝜋B0to DK+pi- can be used as a channel to measure the unitarity triangle angle γ𝛾gamma citebib.bib38, bib.bib39 and B0→D¯0​π+​π−→superscript𝐵0superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋B0tobarD0pi+pi- is sensitive to the β𝛽beta angle citebib.bib40, bib.bib41. Therefore, much attention has been already paid to B(s)→D(s)​P1​P2→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2B(s)to D(s)P1P2 decays in experiments and theoretical calculations. LHCb Collaborations have investigated structures of ground and excited states of D∗superscript𝐷D*, K∗superscript𝐾K* with their corresponding fit fractions through B(s)→D(s)​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠𝐾𝜋B(s)to D(s)Kpi citebib.bib10, bib.bib4, and D∗superscript𝐷D*, ρ𝜌rho in B(s)→D(s)​π​π→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠𝜋𝜋B(s)to D(s)pipi by Babar and Belle citebib.bib1, bib.bib3. Recently, the virtual contribution from ρ𝜌rho in B(s)→D(s)​K​K→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠𝐾𝐾B(s)to D(s)KK has been measured by Belle II citebib.bib8. Motived by the experimental progress on B(s)→D(s)​P1​P2→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2B(s)to D(s)P1P2 decays, theoretical calculation on the branching fractions of various types of charmed quasi-two-body decays, B(s)→D(s)​π​π→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠𝜋𝜋B(s)to D(s)pipi, B(s)→D(s)​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠𝐾𝜋B(s)to D(s)Kpi and B(s)→D(s)​K​K→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠𝐾𝐾B(s)to D(s)KK with intermediate resonances D∗superscript𝐷D*, ρ,K∗,ϕ𝜌superscript𝐾italic-ϕrho,K*,phi have been completed in a series of works with the PQCD approach citebib.bib42, bib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib45, bib.bib46, bib.bib47. In FAT approach, we have done a systematic research on B(s)→D(s)​P1​P2→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2B(s)to D(s)P1P2 with ground charmed mesons D∗superscript𝐷D* as the intermediate states and P1,P2subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2P1,P2 representing π𝜋pi or K𝐾K citebib.bib36. The results of B→D∗​P2→D​P1​P2→𝐵superscript𝐷subscript𝑃2→𝐷subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2Bto D*P2to DP1P2 in FAT approach are in better agreement with experimental data and more precise than the PQCD approach’s predictions. FAT approach is firstly proposed to resolve the problem about nonfactorizable contributions in two-body D𝐷D and B𝐵B meson decays citebib.bib48, bib.bib49, bib.bib50, bib.bib51, bib.bib52, bib.bib53, bib.bib36, bib.bib54 and then has been successfully generalized to quasi-two-body B𝐵B meson decays citebib.bib36, bib.bib37. It is based on the framework of conventional topological diagram approach, which is used to classify the decay amplitudes by different electroweak Feynman diagrams, but keeping S​U​𝑆𝑈3SU breaking effects. Only a few unknown nonfactorization parameters need to be fitted globally with all experimental data. Therefore, FAT approach is able to provide the most precise decay amplitudes of (intermediate) two-body B𝐵B meson decays especially with charmed meson final states. Then in a quasi-two-body B𝐵B meson decay the intermediate resonances successively decay to final meson pairs via strong interaction, which are described in terms of the usual RBW formalism as what’s done in experiments. Actually, in the PQCD approach the light cone distribution amplitude (LCDA) of a meson pair originating from a P-wave resonance can be expressed as time-like form factors and then is also parameterized by the RBW distribution. So their framework of dealing with the resonances is compatible with that in FAT. More details about this can be found in citebib.bib47. Therefore, the main difference between the FAT and the PQCD approach in quasi-two-body decays is how to calculate the intermediate two-body weak decays. It is well known that large nonperturbative contributions and power corrections expanded in mc/mbsubscript𝑚𝑐subscript𝑚𝑏mc/mb of color suppressed and W-exchange diagrams in charmed B𝐵B decays have not been able to be calculated in PQCD approachcitebib.bib55, which results in large uncertainties of PQCD approach’s predictions for B→D​P1​P2→𝐵𝐷subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2Bto DP1P2 with resonances D∗superscript𝐷D*, ρ,K∗,ϕ𝜌superscript𝐾italic-ϕrho,K*,phi. In this paper we will apply FAT approach to study the B(s)→D(s)​P1​P2→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2B(s)to D(s)P1P2 decay with the ground state light vector intermediate resonances ρ,K∗,ϕ,ω𝜌superscript𝐾italic-ϕ𝜔rho,K*,phi,omega, which are generally the largest components and their fit fractions have been well measured separately from any other vector resonances in LHCb and Belle II citebib.bib1, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8. This paper is organized as follows. In Sec. , the theoretical framework is introduced. The numerical results and discussions about B(s)→D(s)(ρ→)ππB(s)to D(s)(rhoto)pipi, B(s)→D(s)(K∗→)KπB(s)to D(s)(K*to)Kpi, B(s)→D(s)(ϕ→)KK¯B(s)to D(s)(phito)KbarK and B(s)→D(s)(ρ,ω→)KK¯B(s)to D(s)(rho,omegato)KbarK are collected in Sec. . Finally, a summary is given in Section .
content_sections.II FACTORIZATION OF AMPLITUDES FOR TOPOL: The charmed quasi-two-body decay B(s)→D(s)(V→)P1P2B(s)to D(s)(Vto)P1P2 happens through two subprocesses, where the D(s)subscript𝐷𝑠D(s) meson represents D(s)subscript𝐷𝑠D(s) or its antiparticle D¯(s)subscript¯𝐷𝑠barD(s). B(s)subscript𝐵𝑠B(s) meson decays to an intermediate resonant state D(s)​Vsubscript𝐷𝑠𝑉D(s)V firstly, and subsequently the unstable resonance decays to a pair of light psudoscaler, V→P1​P2→𝑉subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2Vto P1P2. The first subprocess at quark level is induced by weak transitions b→c​q​u¯​(q=d,s)→𝑏𝑐𝑞¯𝑢𝑞𝑑𝑠brightarrow,c,q,baru,,(q=d,s) and b→u​q​c¯​(q=d,s)→𝑏𝑢𝑞¯𝑐𝑞𝑑𝑠brightarrow,u,q,barc,,(q=d,s) for D(s)subscript𝐷𝑠D(s) and D¯(s)subscript¯𝐷𝑠barD(s) final states, respectively. The secondary one proceeds directly by strong interaction. According to the topological structures of b→c​q​u¯→𝑏𝑐𝑞¯𝑢brightarrow,c,q,baru, the diagrams contributing to B¯(s)→D(s)(V→)P1P2barB(s)to D(s)(Vto)P1P2 can be classified into three types as listed in Fig. , a) color-favored emission diagram T𝑇T, b) color-suppressed emission diagram C𝐶C, and c) W𝑊W-exchange diagram E𝐸E. content_sections.III Numerical results and discussion: The input parameters are classified into (a) electroweak coefficients: Cabibbo-Kobayashi-Maskawa (CKM) matrix elements and Wilson coefficients; (b) nonperturbative QCD parameters: decay constants, transition form factors and nonfactorizable parameters χC​(E)superscript𝜒𝐶𝐸chiC(E), ϕC​(E)superscriptitalic-ϕ𝐶𝐸phiC(E); (c) Hadronic parameters: mVsubscript𝑚𝑉mV, Γ0subscriptΓ0Gamma0, and gV​P1​P2subscript𝑔𝑉subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2gVP1P2 involved in strong interaction decays of vector mesons, which have been listed in tab. given in previous section. The Wolfenstein parametrization of the CKM matrix is utilized with the Wolfenstein parameters ascitebib.bib61 λ=0.22650±0.00048,A=0.790−0.012+0.017,ρ¯=0.141−0.017+0.016,η¯=0.357±0.01. formulae-sequence𝜆plus-or-minus0.226500.00048formulae-sequence𝐴subscriptsuperscript0.7900.0170.012formulae-sequence¯𝜌subscriptsuperscript0.1410.0160.017¯𝜂plus-or-minus0.3570.01lambda=0.22650pm 0.00048,A=0.790+0.017-0.012,barrho=0.141+0.016-0.017,bareta=0.357pm 0.01. The decay constants of pseudoscalar mesons and light vector mesons, and transition form factors of B𝐵B meson decays F1​(Q2)subscript𝐹1superscript𝑄2F1(Q2) and A0​(Q2)subscript𝐴0superscript𝑄2A0(Q2) at recoil momentum square Q2=0superscript𝑄20Q2=0 are listed in Tables and , respectively. The decay constants of π,K,D𝜋𝐾𝐷pi,K,D and B𝐵B are from the PDG by global fit with experimental data citebib.bib61. The remaining input nonperturbative QCD parameters, the decay constants of Dssubscript𝐷𝑠Ds and Bssubscript𝐵𝑠Bs, and all form factors are obtained from various theoretical results, such as light-cone sum rules citebib.bib63, bib.bib64, bib.bib65. We will utilize the same theoretical values as in the previous work by two of us (S. -H. Z. and C. -D. L. ) with other colleaguescitebib.bib50, with 5% uncertainty kept for decay constants and 10% uncertainty for form factors. Also as done in citebib.bib50, the dipole model of form factors is adopted with dipole parameters α1,2subscript𝛼12alpha1,2 listed in Tab. . The effective Wilson coefficients a1subscript𝑎1a1 is 1.0361.0361.036 calculated at scale μ=mb/2𝜇subscript𝑚𝑏2mu=mb/2. The nonfactorizable parameters χC​(E)superscript𝜒𝐶𝐸chiC(E) and ϕC​(E)superscriptitalic-ϕ𝐶𝐸phiC(E) fitted with experimental data citebib.bib50 are χC=0.48±0.01,ϕC=(56.6−3.8+3.2)∘,χE=0.024−0.001+0.002,ϕE=(123.9−2.2+3.3)∘. superscript𝜒𝐶formulae-sequenceabsentplus-or-minus0.480.01superscriptitalic-ϕ𝐶superscriptsuperscriptsubscript56.63.83.2superscript𝜒𝐸formulae-sequenceabsentsuperscriptsubscript0.0240.0010.002superscriptitalic-ϕ𝐸superscriptsuperscriptsubscript123.92.23.3displaystylebeginaligned chiC=0.48pm 0.01,phiC=left(56.6-3.8+3.2right)circ, chiE=0.024-0.001+0.002,phiE=left(123.9-2.2+3.3right)circ. endaligned With all the inputs, we integrate the the differential width in Eq. over the kinematics region to obtain the branching fractions of B¯→D(V→)P1P2barBto D(Vto)P1P2 and B¯→D¯(V→)P1P2barBtobarD(Vto)P1P2. Specifically, the numerical results for B¯(s)→D(ρ→)ππbarB(s)to D(rhoto)pipi, B¯(s)→D(K∗→)KπbarB(s)to D(K*to)Kpi, B¯(s)→D(ϕ→)KKbarB(s)to D(phito)KK and B¯(s)→D(ρ,ω→)KKbarB(s)to D(rho,omegato)KK, together with their corresponding doubly CKM suppressed decays B¯→D¯(V→)P1P2barBtobarD(Vto)P1P2, are collected in Tables , , and , respectively. In our results denoted by ℬFATsubscriptℬFATBrmFAT, the uncertainties are in sequence from the fitted parameters, form factors, decay constants for B¯→D(V→)P1P2barBto D(Vto)P1P2, and an additional error from Vu​bsubscript𝑉𝑢𝑏Vub for B¯→D¯(V→)P1P2barBtobarD(Vto)P1P2 decays induced by b→u→𝑏𝑢bto u transitions. One can see that the dominating errors are from the uncertainties of form factors, which can be improved by more precise calculations. Besides the CKM matrix elements shown in these tables we also list the intermediate resonance decays as well as the topological contributions T,C,E𝑇𝐶𝐸T,C,E and A𝐴A for convenience of analyzing hierarchies of branching fractions in the following. Experimental data in the third column and the results in PQCD approach in last column are also list for comparison.
content_sections.III Numerical results and discussion III: The decay modes are classified by CKM matrix elements involved, Cabibbo favored Vc​b​Vu​d∗subscript𝑉𝑐𝑏subscriptsuperscript𝑉𝑢𝑑VcbV*ud, Cabibbo suppressed Vc​b​Vu​s∗subscript𝑉𝑐𝑏subscriptsuperscript𝑉𝑢𝑠VcbV*us, and doubly Cabibbo suppressed Vu​b​Vu​d∗subscript𝑉𝑢𝑏subscriptsuperscript𝑉𝑢𝑑VubV*ud and Vu​b​Vu​s∗subscript𝑉𝑢𝑏subscriptsuperscript𝑉𝑢𝑠VubV*us, shown in the second column of Tables , and . The hierarchies of branching fractions can be seen clearly from this classification. The Cabibbo favored decay modes are about two orders larger than the doubly Cabibbo suppressed ones of the same type in the same table. As a result, these Cabibbo favored decay modes are able to be measured firstly by experiments, such as the first four modes of B¯(s)→D(ρ→)ππbarB(s)to D(rhoto)pipi citebib.bib61 in table and B¯s0→D0(K∗0→)K+π−barBs0to D0(K*0to)K+pi- citebib.bib4 in table . Our results and the experimental data are consistent within errors. Besides CKM matrix elements, the hierarchy of branching fraction is also dependent on contributions from different topological diagrams. Similar to the dynamics of two-body hadronic B𝐵B decays, the color favored emission diagram (T𝑇T) is absolutely dominating in the quasi-two-body decays. For instance, the topology T𝑇T dominated decay modes, B−→D0(K∗−→)K−π0B-to D0(K*-to)K-pi0, B¯0→D+(K∗−→)K−π0barB0to D+(K*-to)K-pi0 and B¯s0→Ds+(K∗−→)K−π0barBs0to Ds+(K*-to)K-pi0 happening through Cabibbo suppressed Vc​b​Vu​s∗subscript𝑉𝑐𝑏subscriptsuperscript𝑉𝑢𝑠VcbV*us, are the same order as the Cabibbo favored but only C𝐶C contributed decay, B¯s0→D0(K∗0→)K+π−barBs0to D0(K*0to)K+pi-. Besides the mode B¯s0→D0(K∗0→)K+π−barBs0to D0(K*0to)K+pi- with large branching ration has been measured by LHCb experiment through Dalitz plot analysis and isobar model citebib.bib4, another mode B¯0→D0(K¯∗0→)K−π+barB0to D0(barK*0to)K-pi+ with one order smaller branching ration is also measured by LHCb citebib.bib6. The remaining three modes with comparable branching ratio as B¯s0→D0(K∗0→)K+π−barBs0to D0(K*0to)K+pi- are also measurable in LHCb and Belle II. Our results of other decays, especially those with branching ratios in the range 10−6−10−4superscript106superscript10410-6-10-4 in Tables , and are expected to be observed in future experiments.

Since most quasi-two-body decays B(s)→D(s)(V→)P1P2B(s)to D(s)(Vto)P1P2 have not been measured by experiments until now, we list the results calculated in PQCD approach citebib.bib42, bib.bib43, bib.bib45, bib.bib46, bib.bib47 in the last column of the tables for comparison. As we have stated in Sec. , LCDA of P-wave P1​P2subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2P1P2 meson pair from resonance is described by RBW model in PQCD, which is the same theme adopted in the FAT approach for V→P1​P2→𝑉subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2Vto P1P2. The two approaches are effectively compatible for intermediate resonance strong decays, the main difference between them is the calculation of the weak decays of B𝐵B to D𝐷D meson and a vector resonance. As known, T𝑇T diagram is proved to be factorizable at all orders of αssubscript𝛼𝑠alphas for these decays, thus the perturbative calculation is reliable. Our results of the T𝑇T diagram dominating decay modes in Tables and are in good agreement with PQCD’s predictions. The magnitude of topologies C𝐶C is larger than E𝐸E, |C|>|E|𝐶𝐸left|Cright|>left|Eright|, in the FAT approach as shown in eq. , while C𝐶C is approximately equal to E𝐸E, |C|∼|E|similar-to𝐶𝐸left|Cright|simleft|Eright|, in the PQCD approach citebib.bib66, because it is sensitive to the power corrections and high order contributions which are hard to be calculated in PQCD approach. Therefore, it is easy to find in Tables and that the results of the FAT approach for the decays dominated only by C𝐶C, larger than those in the PQCD approach, are in better agreement with the current experimental data. However, our results of decay modes with only power suppressed E𝐸E contribution are a little smaller than those in PQCD, which need to be tested by the future experiments. At last, we emphasize that the branching ratios of decays in the FAT approach are more precise than those in the PQCD in Tables , and . The reason is that the topological amplitudes in the FAT including the nonfactorizable QCD contributions were extracted through a global fit with experimental data of these decays, while large uncertainties arise from non-perturbative parameters and QCD power and radiative corrections in the PQCD.

Contrary to the quasi-two-body decays through ρ→π​π→𝜌𝜋𝜋rhotopipi, K∗→K​π→superscript𝐾𝐾𝜋K*to Kpi and ϕ→K​K→italic-ϕ𝐾𝐾phito KK proceeding by the pole mass dynamics, i. e. , the pole mass is larger than the invariant mass threshold of two final states, the other modes with strong decays by ρ,ω→K​K→𝜌𝜔𝐾𝐾rho,omegato KK can only happen by ρ,ω𝜌𝜔rho,omega off-shell effect. It is also called the Breit-Wigner tail (BWT) effect, which has also appeared in charmed quasi-two-body decay with off-shell D∗superscript𝐷D* resonance citebib.bib36, bib.bib44 and charmless one through ρ,ω𝜌𝜔rho,omega resonances citebib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib25, bib.bib37. We denote branching ratios of this kind of decays by ℬvsuperscriptℬ𝑣Bv and their numerical results are listed on Table , together with the PQCD’s predictions for B→D(ρ→)KKBto D(rhoto)KK in last column. Apparently, the branching ratios of ℬvsuperscriptℬ𝑣Bv modes are approximately two orders smaller than those of ℬℬcal B modes in Table , that is, the BWT effect in B→D(ρ→)KKBto D(rhoto)KK is only about 1%percent11% of the on-shell resonance contribution, B→D(ρ→)ππBto D(rhoto)pipi. In Tables and , one can see that all the intermediate states of ρ,ω𝜌𝜔rho,omega, and ϕitalic-ϕphi can decay into K​K𝐾𝐾KK via virtual effects (for ρ,ω𝜌𝜔rho,omega) or pole mass dynamics (for ϕitalic-ϕphi). However, different from neutral states ρ0,ω,ϕsuperscript𝜌0𝜔italic-ϕrho0,omega,phi, the charged ρ±superscript𝜌plus-or-minusrhopm is the unique resonance contributing to the charged meson pair K±​K0superscript𝐾plus-or-minussuperscript𝐾0KpmK0 in the low mass region of K±​K0superscript𝐾plus-or-minussuperscript𝐾0KpmK0 system, which have been measured recently by Belle II collaboration citebib.bib8 based on a study of the small m​(K−​KS0)𝑚superscript𝐾superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0m(K-KS0) invariant mass for B−→D0​K−​KS0→superscript𝐵superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0B-to D0K-KS0 and B¯0→D+​K−​KS0→superscript¯𝐵0superscript𝐷superscript𝐾superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0barB0to D+K-KS0. With ρ−superscript𝜌rho--like resonances and non-resonance contribution, the branching ratios are ℬ​(B−→D0​K−​KS0)=(1.89±0.16±0.10)×10−4ℬ→superscript𝐵superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0plus-or-minus1.890.160.10superscript104B(B-to D0K-KS0)=(1.89pm 0.16pm 0.10)times 10-4 and ℬ​(B¯0→D+​K−​KS0)=(0.85±0.11±0.05)×10−4ℬ→superscript¯𝐵0superscript𝐷superscript𝐾superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0plus-or-minus0.850.110.05superscript104B(barB0to D+K-KS0)=(0.85pm 0.11pm 0.05)times 10-4, respectively. Our result for only ground state ρ​𝜌770rho is ℬ(B−→D0(ρ−→)K−K0)=(7.01−0.14−1.16−0.60+0.13+1.26+0.63)×10−5B(B-to D0(rho-to)K-K0)=(7.01+0.13+1.26+0.63-0.14-1.16-0.60)times 10-5 and ℬ(B−→D0(ρ−→)K−K0)=(4.64−0.02−0.90−0.47+0.03+1.00+0.49)×10−5B(B-to D0(rho-to)K-K0)=(4.64+0.03+1.00+0.49-0.02-0.90-0.47)times 10-5 in Table , which can reach a proportion of about 20%percent2020% of above measured all ρ−superscript𝜌rho- resonant and non-resonant components (considering half of branching ratios of K0superscript𝐾0K0 or K¯0superscript¯𝐾0barK0 to become KSsubscript𝐾𝑆KS). The similar mode B¯s0→Ds+(ρ−→)K−K0barBs0to Ds+(rho-to)K-K0 with comparable branching ratio (5.63−0−1.07−0.60+0+1.18+0.60)×10−5subscriptsuperscript5.6301.180.6001.070.60superscript105(5.63+0+1.18+0.60-0-1.07-0.60)times 10-5 is suggested to be measured in LHCb and Belle II. The study of invariant mass of neutral K+​K−superscript𝐾superscript𝐾K+K- system for B¯→D​K+​K−→¯𝐵𝐷superscript𝐾superscript𝐾barBto DK+K- in experiments is relatively complex, as it involves various resonances ρ0,ωsuperscript𝜌0𝜔rho0,omega and ϕitalic-ϕphi as well as non-resonances. Especially, in the low-mass region of m​(K+​K−)𝑚superscript𝐾superscript𝐾m(K+K-), the BWT effects from neutral resonance ρ0superscript𝜌0rho0 and ω𝜔omega in decay modes such as B¯0→D0(ρ0→)K+K−barB0to D0(rho0to)K+K- and B¯0→D0(ω→)K+K−barB0to D0(omegato)K+K-, B¯s0→D0(ρ0→)K+K−barBs0to D0(rho0to)K+K- and B¯s0→D0(ω→)K+K−barBs0to D0(omegato)K+K-, are pretty much the same in Table , even though the decay widths of ρ𝜌rho and ω𝜔omega meson are very different, shown in Table . As we have mentioned in citebib.bib36, bib.bib37, the BWT effects in these decays are not very sensitive to the widths of resonances. It can be attribute to the behavior of the Breit-Wigner propagator in eq. describing off-shell resonance, where the invariant mass square s𝑠s is far away from the on-shell mass of resonance, e. g. the real part, ∣s−mρ,ω∣delimited-∣∣𝑠subscript𝑚𝜌𝜔mid s-mrho,omegamid, of denominators of Breit-Wigner formula is much larger than the imaginary part i​mρ,ω​Γρ,ω𝑖subscript𝑚𝜌𝜔subscriptΓ𝜌𝜔i,mrho,omega,Gammarho,omega. Finally, the comparison of BWT effect in B→D(ρ→)KKBto D(rhoto)KK between the FAT and PQCD approaches is very similar with that of the on-shell resonance contributions in B→D(ρ→)ππBto D(rhoto)pipi. They are in agreement for T𝑇T diagram dominated modes, but different from those dominated by C𝐶C and E𝐸E diagrams. It indicates again that no matter for on-shell resonance or for off-shell one, the mechanisms or models applied by the two approaches are effectively consistent.
content_sections.IV Conclusion: Motived by the measurements of three-body charmed B𝐵B meson decays with resonance contributions, especially ground state resonance contributions, from Babar, LHCb and Belle (II), we systematically analyze the corresponding quasi-two-body decays B(s)→D(s)(V→)P1P2B(s)to D(s)(Vto)P1P2 through intermediate ground states ρ,K∗,ω𝜌superscript𝐾𝜔rho,K*,omega and ϕitalic-ϕphi. They proceed by b→c→𝑏𝑐brightarrow,c, or b→u→𝑏𝑢brightarrow,u, transitions to a D(s)​Vsubscript𝐷𝑠𝑉D(s)V intermediate state with V𝑉V as a resonant state which decays consequently into final states P1,P2subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2P1,P2 via strong interaction. We utilize the decay amplitudes extracted from the two-body charmed B𝐵B decays in the FAT approach for the first subprocess B(s)→D(s)​V→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠𝑉B(s)to D(s)V and RBW function for the narrow widths resonances V𝑉V as usually done in experiments and the PQCD approach. We categorize B(s)→D(s)(V→)P1P2B(s)to D(s)(Vto)P1P2 into four groups according to different vector resonance, B(s)→D(s)(ρ→)ππB(s)to D(s)(rhoto)pipi, B(s)→D(s)(K∗→)KπB(s)to D(s)(K*to)Kpi, B(s)→D(s)(ϕ→)KKB(s)to D(s)(phito)KK and B(s)→D(s)(ρ,ω→)KKB(s)to D(s)(rho,omegato)KK, where the former three kinds of modes decay by pole dynamics, and the last one by BWT effect. We calculate the branching ratios of all the four kinds of decay modes in the FAT method. Our results are consistent with the data by Babar, LHCb and Belle (II). Our predictions of order 10−6−10−4superscript106superscript10410-6-10-4 without any experimental data are hopeful to be observed in the future experiments. The FAT approach and the PQCD approach have effectively compatible mechanism of resonant state strong decays. Meanwhile, their treatments on the weak decays of B𝐵B to a D𝐷D meson and a vector resonance are different. Since the calculation of the first subprocess is done by a global fit with experimental data in the FAT approach, our results for the color suppressed diagram dominating modes are larger than those in the PQCD approach whose information on nonperturbative contribution and mc/mbsubscript𝑚𝑐subscript𝑚𝑏mc/mb power corrections are not included so far. In addition, our results have significantly less theoretical uncertainties due to accurate nonfactorizable parameters extracted from experimental data. The fourth type of modes happen through the tail effects of ρ𝜌rho and ω𝜔omega resonance to K​K𝐾𝐾KK. It’s found that the BWT effect of ρ𝜌rho resonance approximately is about two orders smaller than ρ𝜌rho on-shell resonance contribution, which induce that they are usually ignored in experimental analysis. However charged ρ±superscript𝜌plus-or-minusrhopm-like resonance of low mass region of K±​K0superscript𝐾plus-or-minussuperscript𝐾0KpmK0 system have been started to be studied in Belle II recently. The comparable modes, such as B¯s0→Ds+(ρ−→)K−K0barBs0to Ds+(rho-to)K-K0, also have the potential to be measurabled in LHCb and Belle II.

sorted_papers.title:
Analysis of three-body charmed B meson decays under the
  factorization-assisted topological-amplitude approach
sorted_papers.abstract:
We analyze the quasi-two-body charmed $B$ decays $B^{+,0}_{(s)} \to D_{(s)}^*
P_2 \to D_{(s)} P_1 P_2$ with $P_{1,2}$ as a pion or kaon. The intermediate
processes $B_{(s)} \to D_{(s)}^* P_2 $ are calculated with the
factorization-assisted topological-amplitude approach and the resonant effects
are calculated with the Breit-Wigner formalism. Taking all p-wave resonance
states $ \bar D_{(s)}^*$ into consideration, we present the related branching
fractions, calculate the Breit-Wigner-Tail effects, and investigate the flavor
$SU(3)$ breaking effects. Most of our branching fractions are consistent with
the perturbative QCD approach's predictions as well as the current experimental
data. With more precision calculation of the intermediate two body charmed B
meson decays, our quasi-two-body B decays calculation has significantly less
theoretical uncertainty than the perturbative QCD approach. Many of those
channels without any experimental data will be confronted with the future more
accurate experiment measurements. Our results of the Breit-Wigner-tail effects
also agree with the experimental very well. In $B^0$ decays this effect can
reach approximately to $5\%$. It is also found that the Breit-Wigner-tail
effects are not sensitive to the widths of their corresponding resonances. The
flavor $SU(3)$ symmetry breaking effect is also investigated.

sections.35: Aubert et al.(BABAR Collaboration), “Measurement of branching fractions and resonance contributions for B0→D¯0​K+​π−→superscript𝐵0superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝐾superscript𝜋B^{0}\to\bar{D}^{0}K^{+}\pi^{-} and search for B0→D0​K+​π−→superscript𝐵0superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾superscript𝜋B^{0}\to D^{0}K^{+}\pi^{-} decays,” Phys
sections.37: Abe et al.(BELLE Collaboration), “Observation of Cabibbo suppressed B→D(∗)​K−→𝐵superscript𝐷superscript𝐾B\to D^{(*)}K^{-} decays at BELLE,” Phys

qualitative: The factorization-assisted topological-amplitude (FAT) approach is used to analyze quasi-two-body charmed B meson decays, providing more precise decay amplitudes for intermediate two-body decays B(s) → D(s)*P2 compared to other approaches.
The quasi-two-body framework treats three-body decays B(s) → D(s)*P2 → D(s)P1P2 as sequential two-body processes, where the intermediate D(s)* resonance decays into D(s)P1.

Resonant contributions are modeled using the Breit-Wigner formalism, while non-resonant effects are accounted for through the Breit-Wigner-tail (BWT) effect, which captures off-shell contributions when the invariant mass of D(s)P1 is below the resonance pole mass.

The FAT approach significantly reduces theoretical uncertainty in three-body decay calculations by leveraging precise amplitudes from two-body decays, especially for non-factorizable QCD contributions.
Flavor SU(3) symmetry breaking effects are investigated, and the Breit-Wigner-tail effects are found to be insensitive to the widths of the corresponding resonances.
The BWT effect is non-negligible, reaching up to approximately 5% in B⁰ decays, and is in good agreement with experimental observations.

Theoretical uncertainties in the perturbative QCD (PQCD) approach are dominated by uncertainties in the two-body B → D*P decays, which the FAT approach mitigates through a global fit to experimental data.
quantitative: Breit-Wigner-tail (BWT) effects reach approximately 5% in B⁰ decays.
Off-shell contributions from D* resonances are about 5% in three-body B → Dππ(K) decays.
Contributions from Ds* resonances are found to be tiny, less than 1%.
Theoretical uncertainty in the quasi-two-body decay calculation is significantly reduced compared to the perturbative QCD approach due to improved precision in intermediate two-body decay amplitudes.
The Breit-Wigner-tail effects are not sensitive to the widths of the corresponding resonances.

content_sections.I Introduction: B𝐵B meson non-leptonic decays are very important for the study of many frontier topics, such as the mechanism of C​P𝐶𝑃CP violation and the emergence of quantum chromodynamics. The three-body hadronic decays of B𝐵B mesons, with many kinds of intermediate states parameterized by the sequential two body decays, provide opportunities for the study of these common topics and of the hadron spectroscopy. So far, plenty of data on the three-body hadronic B𝐵B meson decays have been collected by BABAR, Belle and LHCb experiments using Dalitz plot technique, which induced a lot of theoretical studies. On the theoretical side, contrary to two-body B𝐵B decays, which have been analyzed extensively in the past two decades within the frameworks of the factorization approach, QCD factorization approach citebib.bib1, perturbative QCD (PQCD) citebib.bib2, soft collinear effective theory citebib.bib3 and some model independent approaches such as the factorization-assisted topological-amplitude (FAT) approach citebib.bib4, three-body B𝐵B decays are more complicated. On one hand the three-body decays receive both resonant and nonresonant contributions, on the other hand factorization of these decays has not been completely proved. Some theoretical models aimed to calculate the non-resonant effects of some three-body B𝐵B decays are in development such as the heavy meson chiral perturbation theory citebib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, a model combing the heavy quark effective theory and chiral Lagrangian citebib.bib8, and the perturbative QCD approach by introducing di-meson distribution amplitudes citebib.bib9. The three-body B𝐵B meson decays are usually dominated by intermediate resonances. They proceed formally as quasi-two-body decays with an intermediate sate of a vector or scalar resonance and a “bachelor” meson. The quasi-two-body framework is employed by the PQCD approach citebib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16 and some phenomenological analysis based on factorization citebib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7. Some three-body B𝐵B decays are also analyzed by the QCD factorization approach citebib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib17 and implemented within the U𝑈U-spin, isospin and flavour SU symmetries (see also Refscitebib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25). In this work, we concentrate on three-body charmed B𝐵B decays B(s)→D(s)​P1​P2→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2B(s)to D(s)P1P2, where P1,2subscript𝑃12P1,2 is a pion or kaon, via quasi-two-body decays B(s)→D(s)∗​P2→D(s)​P1​P2→subscript𝐵𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃2→subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2B(s)to D(s)*P2to D(s)P1P2 with P1subscript𝑃1P1 stemming from the decay of the resonance and P2subscript𝑃2P2 representing a “bachelor” light meson. The Belle, BABAR and LHCb Collaborations have achieved brilliant progress in identifying the excited charmd states and also found their off-shell effects are indispensable citebib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32. The off-shell effect, also called the Breit-Wigner-tail (BWT) effect, is the contribution when the pole mass of D(s)∗superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠D(s)* is smaller than the invariant mass of D(s)​P1subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1D(s)P1 or, in the other case, the contribution with the on-shell effect excluded by a cut on the invariant mass of D(s)​P1subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1D(s)P1. Recently, motivated by the experimental measurements on the off-shell effects of D∗superscript𝐷D* meson in B→D​π​π​(K)→𝐵𝐷𝜋𝜋𝐾Bto Dpipi(K) decays, theoretical attention is paid to these virtual effectscitebib.bib33, bib.bib14. A new systematic research on the three-body charmed B𝐵B meson decays including D(s)∗superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠D(s)* resonant states have been done subsequently in the PQCD approachcitebib.bib16. About 5%percent55% off-shell contributions of D∗superscript𝐷D* resonances and tiny contributions (<1%absentpercent1<1%) from Ds∗superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠Ds* resonances are found. The theoretical uncertainty of the two-step process of B(s)→D(s)∗​P2→D(s)​P1​P2→subscript𝐵𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃2→subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2B(s)to D(s)*P2to D(s)P1P2 is dominated by the uncertainty from two-body non-leptonic B𝐵B decays B(s)→D(s)∗​P→subscript𝐵𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠𝑃B(s)to D(s)*P. Large non-factorizable contribution has been found in the factorization approachcitebib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36 and the PQCD approachcitebib.bib37, bib.bib38. Since the charm quark mass scale is also involved in these decays in addition to the b𝑏b quark mass scale, the power corrections mc/mbsubscript𝑚𝑐subscript𝑚𝑏mc/mb of these decays are very difficult to calculate. In a previous workcitebib.bib39, two of us (S-H Z, C-D L), with other colleagues, utilize the framework of conventional topological diagram approach to group the decay amplitudes by different electroweak Feynman diagramscitebib.bib40. A global fit with all experimental data of these decays to extract the topological amplitudes including the nonfactorizable QCD contributions as well as the S​U​𝑆𝑈3SU breaking effects is performed. This so-called FAT approach citebib.bib39, bib.bib4, bib.bib41, bib.bib42, bib.bib43, bib.bib44 gives the most precise decay amplitudes of the two body B𝐵B meson decays with charmed meson final states. For example, the amplitudes of color-suppresses topological diagram (C𝐶C) of Fig. (b), dominated by the nonfactorizable QCD effect is larger in the FAT approach than that in other approaches. In this paper we apply the FAT approach to quasi-two-body B𝐵B meson decays, which will significantly reduce the theoretical uncertainty. The Breit-Wigner formalism is used to describe the resonance and a strong coupling accounts for the subsequent decay D(s)∗→D(s)​P1→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1D(s)*to D(s)P1 . This paper is organized as follows. In Section , the theoretical framework is introduced. The numerical results and discussions are collected in Section . Section is a summary.
content_sections.II FACTORIZATION OF AMPLITUDES FOR TOPOL: Under the framework of quasi-two-body decay, the B(s)→D(s)​P1​P2→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2B(s)to D(s)P1P2 decay is divided into two stages. B(s)subscript𝐵𝑠B(s) meson decays to D(s)∗​P2superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃2D(s)*P2 firstly and D(s)∗superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠D(s)* decays to D(s)​P1subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1D(s)P1 subsequently. The first decay B(s)→D(s)∗​P2→subscript𝐵𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃2B(s)to D(s)*P2 is a weak decay induced by b→c​q​u¯​(q=d,s)→𝑏𝑐𝑞¯𝑢𝑞𝑑𝑠brightarrow,c,q,baru,,(q=d,s) at quark level and secondary decay D(s)∗→D(s)​P1→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1D(s)*to D(s)P1 proceeds via strong interaction. In Fig. we list the topological diagrams of these decays under the framework of quasi-two-body decay, including the color-favored tree emission diagram T𝑇T (a), color-suppressed tree emission diagram C𝐶C (b) and W𝑊W-exchange diagram E𝐸E (c), which are specified by topological structures of the weak interaction. We will not consider processes induced by b→u​q​u¯​(q=d,s)→𝑏𝑢𝑞¯𝑢𝑞𝑑𝑠brightarrow,u,q,baru,,(q=d,s) transitions as they are Cabibbo-Kobayashi-Maskawa (CKM)-suppressed and there are no enough experimental data to do global fit for their nonfactorizable contributions as done in the b→c→𝑏𝑐bto c transition case. The two body charmless B𝐵B decays have been proved factorization at high precision citebib.bib3. Large non-factorizable contribution has been found in the two-body B𝐵B decays with charmed meson final states citebib.bib36, bib.bib38, which we have illustrated in the fourth paragraph of Sec. . content_sections.III Numerical results and discussion III: The hierarchies of branching fractions can be seen clearly in the last columns of Tables and . Firstly, let us pay attention to those decays in Table whose intermediate states can be changed to each other by swapping the “bachelor” light mesons pion with kaon, e. g. , B0→D∗−​π+→D¯0​π−​π+→superscript𝐵0superscript𝐷∗absentsuperscript𝜋→superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋B0to Dast-pi+tobarD0pi-pi+ and B0→D∗−​K+→D¯0​π−​K+→superscript𝐵0superscript𝐷∗absentsuperscript𝐾→superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝜋superscript𝐾B0to Dast-K+tobarD0pi-K+. One can find that the branching ratios of the modes with a pion “bachelor” meson are one power larger than their corresponding ones with a kaon “bachelor” meson. The reason is that the pion “bachelor” modes are the Cabibbo favored (Vu​dsubscript𝑉𝑢𝑑Vud) processes, while the kaon “bachelor” modes are Cabibbo suppressed (Vu​ssubscript𝑉𝑢𝑠Vus) ones. The branching ratios of the Cabibbo favored decays Bs0→Ds∗−​π+→D¯0​K−​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠0superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠∗absentsuperscript𝜋→superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝐾superscript𝜋Bs0to Dsast-pi+tobarD0K-pi+ and Bs0→D¯∗0​K¯0→D¯0​π0​K¯0→superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠0superscript¯𝐷absent0superscript¯𝐾0→superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝜋0superscript¯𝐾0Bs0tobarD*0barK0tobarD0pi0barK0 shown in Table are larger than those of the remaining Cabibbo suppressed decays. Similar to the dynamics in two body hadronic B𝐵B decays, the color favored tree (T𝑇T) topological diagram is absolutely dominating. Branching fractions of decays with T𝑇T diagram are larger than those with only C𝐶C or E𝐸E diagrams. Our results for these branching fractions with T𝑇T diagram are in good agreement with the PQCD predictionscitebib.bib16. We have |C|>|E|𝐶𝐸left|Cright|>left|Eright| in the FAT approach citebib.bib39 while |C|∼|E|similar-to𝐶𝐸left|Cright|simleft|Eright| in the PQCD approach citebib.bib58. The |C|𝐶left|Cright| contributes larger in the FAT approach than in the PQCD approach. Therefore, our branching fractions of B0→D¯∗0​K0→D¯0​π0​K0→superscript𝐵0superscript¯𝐷∗absent0superscript𝐾0→superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝜋0superscript𝐾0B0tobarDast 0K0tobarD0pi0K0 and Bs0→D¯∗0​K¯0→D¯0​π0​K¯0→superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠0superscript¯𝐷absent0superscript¯𝐾0→superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝜋0superscript¯𝐾0Bs0tobarD*0barK0tobarD0pi0barK0 decays with only C𝐶C diagram are about two times larger than that in the PQCD approach. For those decays with exactly the same intermediate state D(s)∗​P2subscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃2D*(s)P2, their hierarchies of branching ratios are completely caused by the strong decays of D(s)∗→D(s)​P1→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1D(s)*to D(s)P1. There are two hierarchy sources. One is the BWT effect, the other is the strong couplings. Some of the decays, labeled by ℬℬB in the tables, can proceed by the pole mass dynamics, i. e. , the pole mass is larger than the invariant mass threshold of two final states. The others labeled by ℬvsubscriptℬ𝑣Bv can only happen by the BWT effect. The branching ratios of ℬℬcal B mode decays are apparently one to two orders larger than those of ℬvsubscriptℬ𝑣Bv mode decays. The difference due to strong couplings are also obvious. For instance, the isospin relation leads to gD∗−​D¯0​π−=−2​gD∗−​D−​π0subscript𝑔superscript𝐷absentsuperscript¯𝐷0superscript𝜋2subscript𝑔superscript𝐷absentsuperscript𝐷superscript𝜋0gD*-barD0pi-=-sqrt2,gD*-D-pi0. As a result, the branching ratio of B(s)0→D¯0​π−​π+​(K+)→superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠0superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋superscript𝐾B(s)0tobarD0pi-pi+(K+) is approximatively two times larger than that of B(s)0→D−​π0​π+​(K+)→superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠0superscript𝐷superscript𝜋0superscript𝜋superscript𝐾B(s)0to D-pi0pi+(K+). The strong decays with a pair of s​s¯𝑠¯𝑠sbars quarks from the sea proceed only by the BWT effect because of the heavy strange quark mass. Consequently, the corresponding branching fractions are smaller than the others.

We take the decays B+→D¯∗0​π+→D¯0​π0​(D−​π+,Ds−​K+)​π+→superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐷∗absent0superscript𝜋→superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝜋0superscript𝐷superscript𝜋superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾superscript𝜋B+tobarDast 0pi+;tobarD0pi0,(D-pi+,,Ds-K+),pi+ as an example to show the dependence of branching fractions on the invariant mass of D¯0​π0superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝜋0barD0pi0 pair (or D−​π+superscript𝐷superscript𝜋D-pi+, Ds−​K+superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾Ds-K+), which is depicted in the left diagram of Fig. . To demonstrate the flavor SU symmetry breaking effect, curves of their corresponding decays with the “bachelor” pion replaced with kaon are plotted in the right diagram of Fig. . The decay with a pole mass dynamical strong decay D¯∗0→D¯0​π0→superscript¯𝐷∗absent0superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝜋0barDast 0tobarD0pi0 and those with the BWT effect strong decays D¯∗0→D−​π+→superscript¯𝐷∗absent0superscript𝐷superscript𝜋barDast 0to D-pi+ and D¯∗0→Ds−​K+→superscript¯𝐷∗absent0superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾barDast 0to Ds-K+ are represented by solid (red) lines, dash (blue) lines and dot-dashed (green) lines, respectively. Data of the decays with D¯∗0→Ds−​K+→superscript¯𝐷∗absent0superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾barDast 0to Ds-K+,, which are one order magnitude smaller than those with D¯∗0→D−​π+→superscript¯𝐷∗absent0superscript𝐷superscript𝜋barDast 0to D-pi+,, are multiplied by ten in oder to be shown clearly on the same figure. One may notice that the two conspicuous flagpoles located at the mass of D¯∗0superscript¯𝐷∗absent0barDast 0 in Fig. . It is the distinguishing characteristic of pole dynamics. Our curves of B+→D¯∗0​π+→D¯0​π0​(D−​π+,Ds−​K+)​π+→superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐷∗absent0superscript𝜋→superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝜋0superscript𝐷superscript𝜋superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾superscript𝜋B+tobarDast 0pi+;tobarD0pi0,(D-pi+,,Ds-K+),pi+ (the red solid lines) have higher peaks than that from the ones in the PQCD approach citebib.bib16, which indicates the pole mass dynamics plays a more important role in our results. Only a few charmed three-body B decays are measured by the experiments. We collected these channels in Table as well as the results of the PQCD approach. Our results are consistent with the PQCD ones except the decay Bs0→Ds∗−​π+→D¯0​K−​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠0superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠∗absentsuperscript𝜋→superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝐾superscript𝜋Bs0to Dsast-pi+tobarD0K-pi+. However, the experimental data of this decay has a very large uncertainty. It may be a good choice to wait for more accurate experimental measurement. Our calculation of the intermediate decays B→D(s)∗​P→𝐵superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠𝑃Bto D(s)*P is more precise than the PQCD approach, especially for the color suppressed (C) or exchange decay amplitudes (E) as this two non-factorizable amplitudes, C𝐶C and E𝐸E, are fitted from precise experiment data in FAT not calculated perturbatively. Therefore our theoretical uncertainty is much smaller than the previous PQCD approach. We also study the BWT effects in these decays. Although the Bssubscript𝐵𝑠Bs decay in Table has only BWT contribution, the four decays of B+,0superscript𝐵0B+,0 are dominated by pole dynamics. In order to make an effective comparison, the BWT effects of B+,0superscript𝐵0B+,0 decays are calculated with a cut of s≥2.1​GeV𝑠2.1GeVsqrtsgeq 2.1GeV, which is also adopted by experiments and the PQCD approach. Comparing the data in Tables and , one can see that the BWT effect contributes about 5%percent55% in B0superscript𝐵0B0 meson decays. For example, the centre value of the branching ratio of B0→D∗−​π+→D¯0​π−​π+→superscript𝐵0superscript𝐷∗absentsuperscript𝜋→superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋B0to Dast-pi+tobarD0pi-pi+ in Tables by a cut of s≥2.1​GeV𝑠2.1GeVsqrtsgeq 2.1GeV in FAT is 10.26×10−510.26superscript10510.26times 10-5 and the one in the whole physical region of the D¯0​π−superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝜋barD0pi- invariant mass is 1.77×10−31.77superscript1031.77times 10-3, which lead to the percentage of BWT effect is 10.26×10−5/1.77×10−3∼5%similar-to10.26superscript1051.77superscript103percent510.26times 10-5/1.77times 10-3sim 5%. Comparing the BWT effect between B+→D¯∗0​π+→D−​π+​π+→superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐷∗absent0superscript𝜋→superscript𝐷superscript𝜋superscript𝜋B+tobarDast 0pi+;to D-pi+,pi+ and B+→D¯∗0​π+→Ds−​K+​π+→superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐷∗absent0superscript𝜋→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾superscript𝜋B+tobarDast 0pi+;to Ds-K+,pi+, we find that the peaks of the green (dot-dashed) lines locate farther from the pole mass of D¯∗0superscript¯𝐷∗absent0barDast 0 than the peaks of the blue (dashed) lines in Fig. . The reason is that Ds−​K+superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾Ds-K+ has much larger threshold mass than D−​π+superscript𝐷superscript𝜋D-pi+. It can also be seen that the green (dot-dashed) line is smoother than the blue (dashed) line, which is very steep in the vicinity of the pole mass. Our calculation shows that the BWT effect in B+→D¯∗0​π+→D−​π+​π+→superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐷∗absent0superscript𝜋→superscript𝐷superscript𝜋superscript𝜋B+tobarDast 0pi+;to D-pi+,pi+ is about 4%percent44%. To check the dependence of the BWT effect on the widths of the intermediate resonance, we can compare those decays with different intermediate states. For instance, we find that contribution of the BWT effect is (1.64−0.32+0.36)×10−5subscriptsuperscript1.640.360.32superscript105(1.64+0.36-0.32)times 10-5 for B0→D∗−​π+→Ds−​K0​π+→superscript𝐵0superscript𝐷∗absentsuperscript𝜋→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾0superscript𝜋B0to Dast-pi+,to Ds-K0pi+, (3.21−0.50+0.54)×10−5subscriptsuperscript3.210.540.50superscript105(3.21+0.54-0.50)times 10-5 for B+→D¯∗0​π+→Ds−​K+​π+→superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐷∗absent0superscript𝜋→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾superscript𝜋B+tobarDast 0pi+to Ds-K+pi+, and (4.21−0.81+0.89)×10−5subscriptsuperscript4.210.890.81superscript105(4.21+0.89-0.81)times 10-5 for Bs0→Ds∗−​π+→D¯0​K−​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠0superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠∗absentsuperscript𝜋→superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝐾superscript𝜋Bs0to Dsast-pi+tobarD0K-pi+. Their BWT effects are at the same order even though the widths of the resonances vary widely with ΓD∗+=83.4±1.8​keVsubscriptΓsuperscript𝐷absentplus-or-minus83.41.8keVGammaD*+=83.4pm 1.8keV, ΓD∗0=55.3±1.4​keVsubscriptΓsuperscript𝐷absent0plus-or-minus55.31.4keVGammaD*0=55.3pm 1.4keV and ΓDs∗−≃1similar-to-or-equalssubscriptΓsubscriptsuperscript𝐷∗absent𝑠1GammaDast-ssimeq 1 keV. It indicates that the BWT effects in these decays are not very sensitive to the widths of resonances, which is confirmed by the PQCD approach citebib.bib14, bib.bib16. This can be explained by the behaviour of the Breit-Wigner propagators in the kinematics regions of these decays, where the real parts of their denominators are much larger than the imaginary parts. Taking Ds∗−→D¯0​K−→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠∗absentsuperscript¯𝐷0superscript𝐾Dsast-tobarD0K- as an example, the kinematics region starts from s=2.359​GeV𝑠2.359GeVsqrts=2.359,GeV, while ΓDs∗−≃1similar-to-or-equalssubscriptΓsubscriptsuperscript𝐷∗absent𝑠1GammaDast-ssimeq 1keV. content_sections.IV Conclusion: The three-body charmed B𝐵B meson decays are dominated by intermediate resonances, i. e. , they proceed via quasi-two-body decays as B(s)→D(s)∗​P2→D(s)​P1​P2→subscript𝐵𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃2→subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2B(s)to D(s)*P2to D(s)P1P2. The first step of two-body decay is induced by flavor changing weak decays b→c​q​u¯(q=d,s)→𝑏𝑐𝑞¯𝑢𝑞𝑑𝑠brightarrow,c,q,baruquad(q=d,s) and the intermediate resonant state D(s)∗superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠D(s)* successionally decays into D(s)​P1subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1D(s)P1 via strong interaction. We utilize the decay amplitudes extracted from the two-body charmed B𝐵B decays to quasi-two-body decays with the intermediate resonance described by the Breit-Wigner propagator. Including all the P-wave resonant states D¯(s)∗superscriptsubscript¯𝐷𝑠barD(s)*, we systematically study these decays. The B(s)→D(s)∗​P2→D(s)​P1​P2→subscript𝐵𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃2→subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2B(s)to D(s)*P2to D(s)P1P2 decays can be divided into two groups: ℬℬB and ℬvsubscriptℬ𝑣Bv. ℬℬB represents the group of decays whose pole mass of the resonance D¯(s)∗superscriptsubscript¯𝐷𝑠barD(s)* is larger than the threshold mass of producing D¯(s)​P1subscript¯𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1barD(s)P1. This group of decays are dominated by the pole dynamics, and the main contributions to their branching fractions are from vicinity of the resonances’ pole masses. The others labeled by ℬvsubscriptℬ𝑣Bv can only happen by the BWT effect. The BWT effects in B0superscript𝐵0B0 decays are approximately 5%percent55%. We also found that the Breit-Wigner-tail effects are not sensitive to the widths of their corresponding resonances. We compare our results of the branching fractions with the PQCD approach’s predictions as well as the experimental data. The largest theoretical uncertainty in the calculation is from the intermediate two-body charmed B𝐵B meson decays. Since this calculation is done by a global fit to all experimental data, our results of three body B𝐵B decays have significantly less theoretical uncertainty than the PQCD calculation. We use the same cut on the kinematics region of those decays proceeding via pole dynamics as the experiments. Our results agree well with the experimental data. For those ones without any experimental data, we would like to confront them with the future more accurate experiment measurements. The flavor-S​U​𝑆𝑈3SU symmetry breaking effect in these decays is studied. Our results RD∗−=0.0831−0.0006+0.0008subscript𝑅superscript𝐷absentsubscriptsuperscript0.08310.00080.0006RD*-=0.0831+0.0008-0.0006 and RD∗0=0.0761±0.0001subscript𝑅superscript𝐷absent0plus-or-minus0.07610.0001RD*0=0.0761pm 0.0001 are perfectly consistent with their experimental values. Besides, the local S​U​𝑆𝑈3SU-breaking effects, which are defined as ratios of corresponding differential branching fractions, are investigated. Their magnitudes are found not sensitive to the invariant mass of the strong decay final states, which confirms the that the SU asymmetry in three-body decays is dominated by the two-body weak decays.

sorted_papers.title:
Analysis of three-body charmless $B$-meson decays under the
  factorization-assisted topological-amplitude approach
sorted_papers.abstract:
We analyze quasi-two-body charmless $B$ decays $B_{(s)} \to P_1 V \to P_1 P_2
P_3$ with $V$ representing a vector resonant, and $P_{1,2,3}$ as a light
pseudo-scalar meson, pion, kaon or $\eta^{(\prime)}$. The intermediate
processes $B_{(s)} \to P_1 V $ are calculated in the factorization-assisted
topological-amplitude approach and the vector resonant effects are described by
the Breit-Wigner propagator, which successively decay to $P_1 P_2$ via strong
interaction. Taking into account of all vector resonances in ground state,
$\rho, K^*, \omega, \phi$, we present the related branching fractions, and
calculate the virtual effects for $B_{(s)} \to \pi, K (\rho ,\omega \to) KK$.
We also predict direct $\it{CP}$ asymmetries of three body B decay modes with
$\rho, K^*$ resonances as intermediate states. Our predicted branching
fractions of decay modes dominated by the color-favored tree diagram or the
color-favored penguin diagram are consistent with the perturbative QCD
approach's predictions as well as QCD factorization approach. While for those
nonperturbative contribution dominated decay modes, the branching ratios in
this work are in better agreement with current experimental data than the PQCD
predictions and the QCD factorization results due to their shortage of the
nonperturbative contributions or $1/m_b$ power corrections. Many of the decays
channels, especially for direct $\it{CP}$ asymmetries, are waiting for the
future experiments.

qualitative: Three-body charmless B-meson decays are analyzed as quasi-two-body decays where a vector resonance (e.g., ρ, K*, ω, φ) is produced in the intermediate state and subsequently decays into two pseudoscalars, leading to nontrivial kinematics and phase space distributions ideal for studying CP asymmetries and factorization in QCD.

The factorization-assisted topological-amplitude (FAT) approach is employed to systematically analyze these decays, incorporating nonperturbative and nonfactorizable QCD effects through topological diagrams classified under SU(3) symmetry, which improves precision over traditional factorization methods.
The Breit-Wigner formalism is used to describe vector resonance contributions, with strong couplings accounting for the subsequent two-body decays of the resonances into pseudoscalar mesons.

The FAT approach successfully addresses long-standing puzzles in two-body B decays—such as the B→ππ branching ratio and CP asymmetry discrepancies—by including enhanced nonfactorizable QCD contributions, motivating its extension to three-body decays.

Nonperturbative contributions and 1/m_b power corrections are crucial for accurate predictions, especially in decay modes dominated by weak annihilation or chirally enhanced effects, where standard perturbative QCD and QCD factorization approaches fall short.

The analysis focuses on the edges of the Dalitz plot, where the invariant mass of two final-state pseudoscalars is small, indicating the presence of intermediate vector resonances, and where factorization theorems similar to two-body decays are expected to hold.
Virtual effects of resonances such as ρ, K*, ω, and φ are studied in quasi-two-body decays like B→πK(ρ,ω→KK), revealing their significant impact on branching fractions and CP asymmetries.
Many predicted direct CP asymmetries and branching ratios remain to be tested experimentally, particularly with the upgraded LHCb and Belle II experiments.
quantitative: Branching fractions for quasi-two-body charmless B decays B(s)→P₁V→P₁P₂P₃ are calculated using the FAT approach, with results showing better agreement with experimental data than PQCD and QCD factorization predictions for nonperturbative-dominated modes.
The predicted branching fractions for color-favored tree or penguin-dominated decay modes are consistent with both perturbative QCD (PQCD) and QCD factorization predictions.
The FAT approach includes nonfactorizable QCD contributions that are larger than in other approaches, resolving the B→ππ branching ratio puzzle and the B→πK CP asymmetry puzzle.

The vector resonance effects are modeled via the Breit-Wigner propagator: $ \mathcal{D}(m^2) = \frac{1}{m^2 - M_V^2 + i M_V \Gamma_V} $, where $ M_V $ and $ \Gamma_V $ are the mass and width of the vector meson (e.g., ρ, K*, ω, φ).
Strong coupling constants (e.g., $ g_{VPP} $) are used to describe the decay $ V \to P_1 P_2 $, with values derived from experimental data or theoretical models.
Direct CP asymmetries are predicted for decay modes involving ρ and K* resonances, with magnitudes expected to be measurable in future experiments.
The study includes all ground-state vector resonances: ρ(770), K*(892), ω(782), and φ(1020), with their contributions evaluated in the quasi-two-body framework.

content_sections.I Introduction: Three-body nonleptonic B𝐵B meson decays constitute a large portion of B𝐵B decay channels. Contrary to two-body B𝐵B meson decays, they have nontrivial kinematics and phase space distributions, which can provide more opportunities for the study of C​P𝐶𝑃CP asymmetries and factorization issues in QCD for multibody nonleptonic decays. Generally, one utilize the Dalitz plot technique to analyze the phase space of three-body B𝐵B meson decays, where the invariant mass of a pair of final-state particles peaks as a resonance. In the edges of the Dalitz plot, various resonances as the intermediate states in three-body B𝐵B meson decays will show up, so that the analysis on these decays enables one to study the properties of these resonances. Experimentally, a large amount of branching ratios and C​P𝐶𝑃CP asymmetry parameters of three-body charmless B𝐵B decays have already been measured by BABAR, Belle and LHCb collaborationscitebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, while more are expected from the upgrade of LHCb and Belle II experiments. On the theoretical side, three-body nonleptonic B𝐵B meson decays are more complicated than two-body cases as they involve three-body decay matrix elements. In a phenomenological factorization modelcitebib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, the three-body matrix elements of charmless B𝐵B decays were factorized naively, with the resulting local correlaters studied extensively based on heavy meson chiral perturbation theory for nonresonant contributions and the usual Breit-Wigner formalism for resonant effects. Besides these phenomenological descriptions of three-body charmless B𝐵B decays in the whole Dalitz plot, theoretical analysis in the context of QCD factorization mainly concentrated on the edges of the Dalitz distribution, since the contribution in the center regions of phase space is argued to be both 1/mb1subscript𝑚𝑏1/mb power and αssubscript𝛼𝑠alphas suppressedcitebib.bib9, bib.bib10. Since the vector (scalar) mesons usually decay dominantly to two pseudoscalar mesons with large decay width, the three-body B𝐵B decays are found to be dominantly happened by B𝐵B decaying to a vector (scalar) meson and a pseudoscalar meson, and the vector (scalar) meson subsequently decaying to two pseudoscalar mesons. In this case, two of the three final particles are collinear, generating a small invariant mass in the edges of the Dalitz distribution, and recoil against the third meson in three-body decays. Then one expects a similar factorization theorem as for two-body decays, with the difference that one of the mesons in the two-body case is substituted by a pair of collinear moving mesons with small invariant mass. A series of workscitebib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22 using perturbative QCD approach concentrated on this situation that the pair of collinear mesons are described collectively through intermediate resonances, that is, the three-body decays are processed as quasi-two-body decays. The 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry of B→π​π​π→𝐵𝜋𝜋𝜋Btopipipi decays has been studied within the QCD factorization framework at leading order in αssubscript𝛼𝑠alphascitebib.bib23, bib.bib24, using naive factorization for three-body matrix elements, which involve generalized form factor of B→π​π→𝐵𝜋𝜋Btopipi transition and dimeson light cone distribution amplitudes incitebib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28. The calculation of the nonleptonic three-body B𝐵B decays with heavy-to-heavy transitions, up to the precision of next-next-to-leading order in αssubscript𝛼𝑠alphas has also been done in QCD factorization citebib.bib29. In all these quasi-two-body calculations of the three-body B𝐵B decays, the theoretical calculations rely heavily on the precision of two-body theoretical calculation methods. In the case of two-body nonleptonic B𝐵B decays, power corrections in the heavy-quark expansion, such as weak annihilation effects and chirally enhanced power corrections, are difficult to estimate and remain the sources of uncertainty in QCD factorization approachcitebib.bib30. A complete next-to-leading order calculation of two-body B𝐵B decays in perturbative QCD factorization approach has not been finished citebib.bib31. The problem about precision predictions in the calculation of two-body nonperturbative matrix elements also exist in three-body case. As discussed in Refcitebib.bib8, the predicted branch ratios of quasi-two-body decays such as B→ϕ​(K+​K−)​K,K∗​(K−​π+)​π,ρ​(π+​π−)​K→𝐵italic-ϕsuperscript𝐾superscript𝐾𝐾superscript𝐾superscript𝐾superscript𝜋𝜋𝜌superscript𝜋superscript𝜋𝐾Btophi(K+K-)K,K*(K-pi+)pi,rho(pi+pi-)K are smaller than experimental data due to the absence of 1/mb1subscript𝑚𝑏1/mb power corrections. To include the nonperturbative and nonfactorization contribution in two-body B𝐵B decays, a model independent framework of topological diagram approach citebib.bib32 has been introduced. The equivalence of the topological decay amplitudes and the S​U​𝑆𝑈3SU irreducible amplitudes has been approved citebib.bib33, bib.bib34. In this approach, one classifies the decay amplitudes of two-body charmless B𝐵B decays into different electroweak topological Feynman diagrams under SU symmetry. The topological amplitudes including the nonfactorizable QCD contributions were extracted through a global fit from all experimental data of these decays. The precision of this topological diagram approach is limited to the size of SU breaking effect. In order to include the SU breaking effects, the so-called factorization-assisted topological-amplitude (FAT) approachcitebib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib41, bib.bib42 has been introduced. They can give the most precise decay amplitudes of the two-body charmless B𝐵B-meson decays. For instance, the amplitude of the color-suppressed topological diagram dominated by the nonfactorizable QCD effect, was larger in the FAT approach than that in other perturbative approaches so that the long-standing B→π​π→𝐵𝜋𝜋Btopipi branching ratio puzzle and B→π​K→𝐵𝜋𝐾Btopi K 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry puzzle were solved simultaneously. Encouraged by the success in two-body B𝐵B decays, we will systematically analyze three-body charmless B𝐵B decays using the FAT approach at the edges of the Dalitz distribution with only vector resonance contribution, i. e. , quasi-two-body decays with two of the pseudoscalar mesons decaying from the vector resonance. The vector resonant effects are described in terms of the usual Breit-Wigner formalism, and a strong coupling accounts for the subsequent vector meson two-body decay. We will study the branching ratios and 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP violations of these three-body B𝐵B decays, and discuss the virtual effects of intermediate resonance ρ,K∗,ω𝜌superscript𝐾𝜔rho,K*,omega , and ϕitalic-ϕphi on quasi-two-body decays. This paper is organized as follows. In Sec. , the theoretical framework is introduced. The numerical results and discussions are collected in Sec. . Section is a summary.
content_sections.II Factorization Amplitudes for Topologi: As a quasi-two-body process, the B(s)→P1​P2​P3→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2subscript𝑃3B(s)to P1P2P3 decay (with P𝑃P denoting a light pseudoscalar meson) is divided into two stages. First, B(s)subscript𝐵𝑠B(s) meson decays to P1​Vsubscript𝑃1𝑉P1V, and the intermediate vector resonance V𝑉V decays to P2​P3subscript𝑃2subscript𝑃3P2P3 subsequently. The first decay B(s)→P1​V→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1𝑉B(s)to P1V is a weak decay induced by b→u​u¯​d​(s)→𝑏𝑢¯𝑢𝑑𝑠brightarrow,u,,baru,d(s), at quark level in leading order (tree diagram) and b→d​(s)​q​q¯​(q=u,d,s)→𝑏𝑑𝑠𝑞¯𝑞𝑞𝑢𝑑𝑠brightarrow,d(s),,q,barq,,(q=u,d,s) in next-to-leading order (penguin loop diagram). The secondary decay V→P2​P3→𝑉subscript𝑃2subscript𝑃3Vto P2P3 proceeds via strong interaction. In Fig. we show the topological diagrams of B(s)→P1​P2​P3→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2subscript𝑃3B(s)to P1P2P3 under the framework of quasi-two-body decay at tree level, including (i) color-favored tree emission diagram T𝑇T, (ii) color-suppressed tree emission diagram C𝐶C, (iii) W𝑊W-exchange tree diagram E𝐸E, and (iv) W𝑊W-annihilation tree diagram A𝐴A, which are specified by topological structures of the weak interaction. Likewise, one loop penguin diagram can also be grouped into four categories: (i) QCD-penguin emission diagram P𝑃P, (ii) flavor-singlet QCD-penguin diagram PCsubscript𝑃𝐶PC or EW-penguin emission diagram PE​Wsubscript𝑃𝐸𝑊PEW, (iii) time-like QCD-penguin diagram PEsubscript𝑃𝐸PE, and (iv) space-like QCD-penguin annihilation diagram PAsubscript𝑃𝐴PA, which are shown in Fig. . In the two kinds of figures, we only show one case that the intermediate resonance (labeled by gray ovals) is produced as a recoiling particle in emission diagrams. The electroweak B𝐵B decays at tree level have been proven to be factorizable at high precision citebib.bib43. Therefore, we can calculate the decay amplitude of color-favored tree diagram T𝑇T in perturbative QCD order by order. Large nonfactorizable contributions, such as soft corrections to the color-suppressed tree amplitude and 1/mb1subscript𝑚𝑏1/mb power corrections from annihilation type diagram, are found to be non-negligible citebib.bib38. Similar to the FAT approach for two-body B𝐵B decayscitebib.bib38, we have to introduce unknown parameters for nonfactorizable contributions for color-suppressed tree diagram C𝐶C, and W𝑊W-exchange tree diagram E𝐸E (W𝑊W-annihilation tree diagramA𝐴A is negligible) to be fitted from experimental data. content_sections.III Numerical Results of Branching Ratio: In the numerical calculations, we need input parameters, such as mVsubscript𝑚𝑉mV, Γ0subscriptΓ0Gamma0, and gV​P1​P2subscript𝑔𝑉subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2gVP1P2 involved in strong interaction decay of vector mesons, which are listed in the previous section. For the CKM matrix elements needed in the weak transition calculation, we use Wolfenstein parametrization with parameters from global fitcitebib.bib50 as follows: λ=0.22650±0.00048,A=0.790−0.012+0.017,ρ¯=0.141−0.017+0.016,η¯=0.357±0.01. formulae-sequence𝜆plus-or-minus0.226500.00048formulae-sequence𝐴subscriptsuperscript0.7900.0170.012formulae-sequence¯𝜌subscriptsuperscript0.1410.0160.017¯𝜂plus-or-minus0.3570.01lambda=0.22650pm 0.00048,A=0.790+0.017-0.012,barrho=0.141+0.016-0.017,bareta=0.357pm 0.01. The decay constants of light pseudoscalar mesons and vector mesons, and transition form factors of B𝐵B-meson decays at recoil momentum square Q2=0superscript𝑄20Q2=0 are listed in Tables and , respectively. The decay constants of pseudoscalar mesons π,K𝜋𝐾pi,K, and B𝐵B are from Particle Data Groupcitebib.bib50. The decay constants of Bssubscript𝐵𝑠Bs and the vector mesons, and all form factors are not measured by experiments and only calculated theoretically, such as in constitute quark model and light cone quark model citebib.bib52, bib.bib53, bib.bib54, bib.bib55, covariant light front approach citebib.bib56, bib.bib57, bib.bib58, light-cone sum rules citebib.bib59, bib.bib60, bib.bib61, bib.bib62, bib.bib63, bib.bib64, bib.bib65, bib.bib66, bib.bib67, bib.bib68, bib.bib69, bib.bib70, bib.bib71, bib.bib72, bib.bib73, bib.bib74, bib.bib75, bib.bib76, bib.bib77, bib.bib78, bib.bib79, PQCD citebib.bib80, bib.bib81, bib.bib82, bib.bib83, bib.bib84, bib.bib85, bib.bib86, bib.bib87, bib.bib88, and lattice QCD citebib.bib89, bib.bib90, bib.bib91. We will use the same theoretical values as in the charmless two-body decayscitebib.bib38, with 5% uncertainty kept for decay constants and 10% for form factors. The dipole model parameters α1,α2subscript𝛼1subscript𝛼2alpha1,alpha2 applied to describe the Q2superscript𝑄2Q2 dependence of form factors are also listed in Tablecitebib.bib54, bib.bib81.
