Our numerical results for the branching ratios of B(s)subscript𝐵𝑠B(s) decays are collected in Tables -, for decays B(s)→P1​ρ→P1​π​π→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1𝜌→subscript𝑃1𝜋𝜋B(s)to P1rhoto P1pipi, B(s)→P1​K∗→P1​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1superscript𝐾→subscript𝑃1𝐾𝜋B(s)to P1K*to P1Kpi, B(s)→P1​ϕ→P1​K​K¯→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1italic-ϕ→subscript𝑃1𝐾¯𝐾B(s)to P1phito P1KbarK, and B(s)→P1​ρ,P1​ω→P1​K​K¯formulae-sequence→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1𝜌→subscript𝑃1𝜔subscript𝑃1𝐾¯𝐾B(s)to P1rho,P1omegato P1KbarK, respectively. In these tables, we also list the intermediate resonances decays as well as the topological contributions represented by the corresponding symbols T,C(′)𝑇superscript𝐶′T,C(prime) and so on. In our results, the first uncertainty is from the nonpertubative parameters in Eq. , and the other two uncertainties are estimated with 10%percent1010% variations of form factors, 5%percent55% variations of decay constants. One can see that the dominant source of uncertainties are from form factors.

Comparing the results shown in Table with the corresponding ones calculated in the previous work citebib.bib38, we find that the branching ratios of quasi-two-body decay B(s)→P1​ρ→P1​π​π→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1𝜌→subscript𝑃1𝜋𝜋B(s)to P1rhoto P1pipi are a little smaller than that of the direct two-body decays B(s)→P1​ρ→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1𝜌B(s)to P1rho. It can be attributed to the not narrow resonance width ΓρsubscriptΓ𝜌Gammarho. We cannot apply the narrow width approximation to factorize quasi-two-body decay formula together with ℬ​(ρ→π​π)∼100%similar-toℬ→𝜌𝜋𝜋percent100B(rhotopipi)sim 100% to obtain the precise results of two-body decays. From Table , it is easy to see that our results are well consistent with the experimental data shown in the last column of the Table. For the decay modes B→π(ρ→)ππBtopi(rhoto)pipi, B→η(′)(ρ→)ππBtoeta(prime)(rhoto)pipi with color favored T𝑇T diagram dominated contribution, and the penguin diagram P𝑃P dominated modes B(s)→K(ρ→)ππB(s)to K(rhoto)pipi, the perturbation calculation is reliable, since these kinds of diagrams are proved to be factorizable to all orders of αssubscript𝛼𝑠alphas. Our results are in good agreement with those calculated under the QCD factorization citebib.bib8 and that in PQCD approachcitebib.bib13 within errors. For the decay modes with dominated color suppressed diagrams C(′)superscript𝐶′C(prime) contributions, such as B¯0→π0(ρ0→)ππbarB0topi0(rho0to)pipi and B¯0→η(′)(ρ0→)ππbarB0toeta(prime)(rho0to)pipi, the theoretical results are sensitive to the power corrections and next-to-leading order contributions. With the magnitude and phase of this C(′)superscript𝐶′C(prime) diagram given in Eq. , our results of branching ratios are consistent with experimental data and results calculated under the QCD factorization approach citebib.bib8. On the contrary, the calculations of these decay modes are only done to the leading order in the perturbative QCD approach with one order magnitude smaller branching ratiocitebib.bib13.

In Table , we only list quasi-two-body decays B(s)→P1​K∗→P1​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1superscript𝐾→subscript𝑃1𝐾𝜋B(s)to P1K*to P1Kpi through strong decays K∗0→K+​π−→superscript𝐾absent0superscript𝐾superscript𝜋K*0to K+pi-, K¯∗0→K−​π+→superscript¯𝐾absent0superscript𝐾superscript𝜋barK*0to K-pi+, and K∗−→K−​π0→superscript𝐾absentsuperscript𝐾superscript𝜋0K*-to K-pi0 with a pair of u​u¯𝑢¯𝑢ubaru from the sea. The branching ratios for B(s)→P1​K∗→P1​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1superscript𝐾→subscript𝑃1𝐾𝜋B(s)to P1K*to P1Kpi via sea quark pair d​d¯𝑑¯𝑑dbard strong decay K∗0→K0​π0→superscript𝐾absent0superscript𝐾0superscript𝜋0K*0to K0pi0, K¯∗0→K¯0​π0→superscript¯𝐾absent0superscript¯𝐾0superscript𝜋0barK*0tobarK0pi0, and K∗−→K¯0​π−→superscript𝐾absentsuperscript¯𝐾0superscript𝜋K*-tobarK0pi- can be calculated under the narrow width approximation according to the isospin conservation relationship for strong interaction, ℬ​(K¯∗0→K−​π+)ℬ→superscript¯𝐾absent0superscript𝐾superscript𝜋displaystyleB(barK*0to K-pi+) =displaystyle= 2​ℬ​(K¯∗0→K¯0​π0),2ℬ→superscript¯𝐾absent0superscript¯𝐾0superscript𝜋0displaystyle 2,B(barK*0tobarK0pi0), ℬ​(K∗−→K¯0​π−)ℬ→superscript𝐾absentsuperscript¯𝐾0superscript𝜋displaystyleB(K*-tobarK0pi-) =displaystyle= 2​ℬ​(K∗−→K−​π0).2ℬ→superscript𝐾absentsuperscript𝐾superscript𝜋0displaystyle 2,B(K*-to K-pi0). The experimental data listed in the last column are measured by BABAR citebib.bib94, bib.bib95, bib.bib96, bib.bib44 and Bellecitebib.bib97, bib.bib98 experiments. As we have considered the power corrections from “chiral enhanced” term, penguin annihilation contribution and EW-penguin diagram for the penguin-dominated modes Bu,d→π​K∗→π​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑢𝑑𝜋superscript𝐾→𝜋𝐾𝜋Bu,dtopi K*topi Kpi, our results are well consistent with experimental data, and larger by a factor of 2∼4similar-to242sim 4 than QCD factorization approachcitebib.bib8. The decay channels Bu,d→K​K∗→K​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑢𝑑𝐾superscript𝐾→𝐾𝐾𝜋Bu,dto KK*to KKpi listed in Table are CKM suppressed (|Vt​d/Vt​s|subscript𝑉𝑡𝑑subscript𝑉𝑡𝑠|Vtd/Vts|) comparing with decay Bu,d→π​K∗→π​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑢𝑑𝜋superscript𝐾→𝜋𝐾𝜋Bu,dtopi K*topi Kpi, so that the branching ratios of the former are one order magnitude smaller than the latter. Since there are not experimental measurements of these decays and Bs→K​K∗→K​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑠𝐾superscript𝐾→𝐾𝐾𝜋Bsto KK*to KKpi, we show in the last column the results of QCD factorization approachcitebib.bib8, bib.bib93 for comparison. Similarly with the case happened in Bu,d→π​K∗→π​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑢𝑑𝜋superscript𝐾→𝜋𝐾𝜋Bu,dtopi K*topi Kpi decays, the results of Bu,d→K​K∗→K​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑢𝑑𝐾superscript𝐾→𝐾𝐾𝜋Bu,dto KK*to KKpi as well as penguin-dominated decays Bs→K​K∗→K​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑠𝐾superscript𝐾→𝐾𝐾𝜋Bsto KK*to KKpi calculated in FAT approach are larger by a factor of 2∼3similar-to232sim 3 than those in QCD factorization approachcitebib.bib8. We also give the numerical results of branching ratios of decay modes B(s)→η​K∗→η​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑠𝜂superscript𝐾→𝜂𝐾𝜋B(s)toeta K*toeta Kpi, shown in Table . These decays have not been measured by experiments from the three-body Dalitz plot analyses. However the corresponding two-body decays have been measured experimentally. Applying the narrow width approximation for the K∗superscript𝐾K* resonance, we have Γ​(B(s)→η​K∗→η​K​π)=Γ​(B→η​K∗)​ℬ​(K∗→K​π)Γ→subscript𝐵𝑠𝜂superscript𝐾→𝜂𝐾𝜋Γ→𝐵𝜂superscript𝐾ℬ→superscript𝐾𝐾𝜋Gammaleft(B(s)toeta K*toeta Kpiright)=Gamma(Brightarroweta K*)Bleft(K*to Kpiright). Utilizing the isospin symmetry relationship ℬ​(K∗0→K+​π−)=ℬ​(K∗−→K¯0​π−)=2​ℬ​(K∗−→K−​π0),ℬ→superscript𝐾absent0superscript𝐾superscript𝜋ℬ→superscript𝐾absentsuperscript¯𝐾0superscript𝜋2ℬ→superscript𝐾absentsuperscript𝐾superscript𝜋0displaystyleBleft(K*0rightarrow K+pi-right)=Bleft(K*-rightarrowbarK0pi-right)=2,Bleft(K*-rightarrow K-pi0right), we can obtain the branching ratios for two-body decays B→η​K∗→𝐵𝜂superscript𝐾Btoeta K*, ℬ​(B−→η​K∗−)ℬ→superscript𝐵𝜂superscript𝐾absentdisplaystyleBleft(B-toeta K*-right) =displaystyle= (16.05±1.11±2.22±0.57)×10−6,plus-or-minus16.051.112.220.57superscript106displaystyle(16.05pm 1.11pm 2.22pm 0.57)times 10-6, ℬ​(B−→η′​K∗−)ℬ→superscript𝐵superscript𝜂′superscript𝐾absentdisplaystyleBleft(B-toetaprimeK*-right) =displaystyle= (3.06±1.05±0.36±0.18)×10−6,plus-or-minus3.061.050.360.18superscript106displaystyle(3.06pm 1.05pm 0.36pm 0.18)times 10-6, ℬ​(B¯0→η​K¯∗0)ℬ→superscript¯𝐵0𝜂superscript¯𝐾absent0displaystyleBleft(barB0toetabarK*0right) =displaystyle= (16.95±1.13±2.30±0.62)×10−6,plus-or-minus16.951.132.300.62superscript106displaystyle(16.95pm 1.13pm 2.30pm 0.62)times 10-6, ℬ​(B¯0→η′​K¯∗0)ℬ→superscript¯𝐵0superscript𝜂′superscript¯𝐾absent0displaystyleBleft(barB0toetaprimebarK*0right) =displaystyle= (3.02±1.11±0.32±0.18)×10−6. plus-or-minus3.021.110.320.18superscript106displaystyle(3.02pm 1.11pm 0.32pm 0.18)times 10-6. These results agree well with the experimental measurements of these two-body decays and the ones given in FAT approachcitebib.bib38.

We show the branching fraction results of B(s)→P1(ϕ→)KK¯B(s)to P1(phito)KbarK decays in Table together with the results from PQCD approachcitebib.bib16 in the last column. For penguin diagram P𝑃P dominated decay modes, Bu,d→K(ϕ→)KK¯Bu,dto K(phito)KbarK, the results in FAT approach are consistent with the ones in PQCD approach. As discussed in the previous section, this kind of decays are dominated by reliable perturbation contributions, which agree well with the experimental data ℬ(B−→K−(ϕ→)K+K−)=(4.48±0.22−0.24+0.33)×10−6B(B-to K-(phito)K+K-)=(4.48pm 0.22+0.33-0.24)times 10-6 citebib.bib99 and ℬ(B−→K−(ϕ→)K+K−)=(4.72±0.45±0.35−0.22+0.39)×10−6B(B-to K-(phito)K+K-)=(4.72pm 0.45pm 0.35-0.22+0.39)times 10-6 citebib.bib100. The decay modes Bs→K(ϕ→)KK¯Bsto K(phito)KbarK are CKM suppressed (|Vt​d/Vt​s|subscript𝑉𝑡𝑑subscript𝑉𝑡𝑠|Vtd/Vts|) comparing with Bu,d→K(ϕ→)KK¯Bu,dto K(phito)KbarK decays, so that the branching ratios of the former are one order smaller than the latter. It is easy to see from Table that branching ratios of all the CKM suppressed decay modes from the FAT approach are larger that that from PQCD approach. It can be attribute to the missing power corrections and next-to-leading order corrections to the color suppressed penguin diagram PC′superscriptsubscript𝑃𝐶′PCprime in PQCD approach. Summing over the two quasi-two-body decay modes with same weak decay but different subsequent strong decay, i. e. B(s)→P1(ϕ→)K+K−B(s)to P1(phi,to)K+K- and B(s)→P1(ϕ→)K0K¯0B(s)to P1(phi,to)K0barK0, we should obtain results of the corresponding two-body decays B(s)→P1​ϕ→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1italic-ϕB(s)to P1phi, by applying the narrow width approximation. For instance, the summation of the results of ℬ(B−→π−(ϕ→)K+K−)B(B-topi-(phi,to)K+K-) and ℬ(B−→π−(ϕ→)K0K¯0)B(B-topi-(phi,to)K0barK0) in Table is in agreement with the two-body decay value ℬ​(B−→π−​ϕ)=(2.80±0.04±0.55±0.03)×10−7ℬ→superscript𝐵superscript𝜋italic-ϕplus-or-minus2.800.040.550.03superscript107B(B-topi-phi)=(2.80pm 0.04pm 0.55pm 0.03)times 10-7 incitebib.bib38 within the error bar.

The decay modes B(s)→π,K(ρ,ω→)KK¯B(s)topi,K(rho,omegato)KbarK represent a category of decays, whose pole masses of the resonances ρ,ω𝜌𝜔rho,omega smaller than the threshold mass of producing K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK pair. They can only happen with the virtual effect, which is also called the Breit-Wigner tail effect. In Table , we collect these virtual effects in FAT approach, together with the results in PQCD approach citebib.bib17 for comparison. Here we only show subprocesses ρ0,ω→K+​K−→superscript𝜌0𝜔superscript𝐾superscript𝐾rho0,omegato K+K- without ρ0,ω→K0​K¯0→superscript𝜌0𝜔superscript𝐾0superscript¯𝐾0rho0,omegato K0barK0 due to the tiny mass difference between K+​K−superscript𝐾superscript𝐾K+K- and K0​K¯0superscript𝐾0superscript¯𝐾0K0barK0. The virtual effects of B(s)→π,K(ρ→)KK¯B(s)topi,K(rhoto)KbarK are approximately 1∼2similar-to121sim 2 order smaller than the dominated contribution of B(s)→π,K(ρ→)ππB(s)topi,K(rhoto)pipi in Table. Unlike the on-shell resonance contributions that mostly give the similar contributions between the FAT and PQCD approaches, the virtual contributions give quite different results between the two approaches. Since the color suppressed tree diagram C(′)superscript𝐶′C(prime) is calculated in the PQCD approach only to the leading order, its size is significantly smaller that that fitted from experimental data in the FAT approachcitebib.bib38. As a result, the off-shell effects in C(′)superscript𝐶′C(prime) dominated decay modes, B¯0→π0(ρ0,ω→)K+K−barB0topi0;(rho0,omega,to)K+K-, B¯s0→K0(ρ0,ω→)K+K−barBs0to K0;(rho0,omega;to)K+K- using FAT approach are nearly one order magnitude larger than that of PQCD approach. For another example, the hierarchy between the magnitude of virtual contributions for quasi-two-body decays B−→π0(ρ−→)K−K0B-topi0(rho-to)K-K0 and B−→π−(ρ0→)K+K−B-topi-(rho0to)K+K- is opposite in the PQCD and the FAT approaches, which need further experimental measurements to check. Comparing the results between Tables and , one can see that the off-shell effects in B→π,K(ρ→)KK¯Btopi,K(rhoto)KbarK are small, that is only about 1% of the on-resonance contribution. However from Table , one can see that the off-shell effect from the ground state ρ​0𝜌superscript7700rho0 in B−→π−(ρ0→)K+K−B-topi-(rho0to)K+K- decay in FAT approach is at the same order as the branching ratio ℬ(B−→π−(ρ0→)K+K−)displaystyleB(B-topi-(rho0to)K+K-) =displaystyle= (9.46−1.65−1.14−0.69−0.38−1.82+1.79+1.16+0.72+0.49+1.82)×10−8,superscriptsubscript9.461.651.140.690.381.821.791.160.720.491.82superscript108displaystyle(9.46-1.65-1.14-0.69-0.38-1.82+1.79+1.16+0.72+0.49+1.82)times 10-8, calculated by PQCD approachcitebib.bib17. The latter branching fraction of B−→π−(ρ0→)K+K−B-topi-(rho0to)K+K- was measured by LHCbcitebib.bib101, Bellecitebib.bib102, and BABARcitebib.bib103. Therefore the virtual contribution of B−→π−(ρ0→)K+K−B-topi-(rho0to)K+K- should also be considered in experimental analysis. Comparing the virtual effect contributions of ρ𝜌rho with ω𝜔omega resonance in Table , for instance, B−→π−(ρ0→)K+K−B-topi-(rho0to)K+K- and B−→π−(ω→)K+K−B-topi-(omegato)K+K-, we find that the virtual effects are at the same order magnitude, even though the decay widths of ρ𝜌rho and ω𝜔omega meson are very different, shown in Table. It means that the virtual contributions of these decays are not very sensitive to the full decay width of ρ𝜌rho and ω𝜔omega meson, which is also confirmed by the PQCD approachcitebib.bib17. This can be explained by the behavior of the Breit-Wigner propagators in Eq. in the kinematics regions of these decays, where the imaginary part i​mρ,ω​Γρ,ω​(s)isubscript𝑚𝜌𝜔subscriptΓ𝜌𝜔𝑠i,mrho,,omegaGammarho,,omega(s) becomes unimportant when the invariant mass square s𝑠s is larger than 1​G​e​V21GesuperscriptV21GeV2.
content_sections.IV 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃\\it{CP} Asymmetry: The study of three-body B𝐵B decays attracts a lot of interests because of its large 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry. In this work, we will concentrate on direct 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP violation and will not discuss mixing-induced 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP violation. We also do not consider integrated 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry of the whole phase space of three-body B𝐵B decays, but we only consider the direct 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry of quasi-two-body decay with one resonant as the intermediate state. Consequently this 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry will arise from the interference between tree and penguin amplitudes of the two-body resonances but not from the interference between three-body nonresonant and resonant contribution or between different resonant states. It is well known that direct 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetries are induced by the interference between difference of strong phases and different CKM phases of different contributions. The strong phase is also the major source of direct 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP violation uncertainty as it is mostly from nonperturbative QCD dynamics. As illustrated in Ref. citebib.bib38 for the two-body B𝐵B decays, the strong phases extracted from experimental data are sufficient to induce correct direct 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry, while QCD factorization approaches and soft-collinear effect theory usually make wrong predictions or no prediction for the direct 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetries due to absence of nonperturbative strong phases, such as the well-known K​π𝐾𝜋Kpi puzzle. The problem in two-body decays will also exist in three-body decays. For example, the signs of 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry of B−→π−​K+​K−→superscript𝐵superscript𝜋superscript𝐾superscript𝐾B-rightarrowpi-K+K- and B−→K−​π+​π−→superscript𝐵superscript𝐾superscript𝜋superscript𝜋B-rightarrow K-pi+pi- decay were conflict with experimental data under the QCD factorization approach citebib.bib8. The authors need to consider final-state rescattering effect by introducing an unknown strong phase δ𝛿delta to account for the sign flip of 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry. Therefore possible 1/mb1subscript𝑚𝑏1/mb power corrections, such as final-state interactions, or other nonperturbative contributions are necessary to correctly describe 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry. For the quasi-two-body decays under the framework of FAT, the strong phases result in from the nonperturbative contribution parameters in Eq. and the Breit-Wigner formalism for resonance in Eq. . These strong phases have already contained all perturbative and nonperturbative QCD effects, for instance, final-state interactions, color suppressed contribution, penguin annihilation contribution, and so on. They explain well the measured branching ratios, and thus are expected to give the right prediction of 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry parameters in three-body B𝐵B decays. We show the 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetries of B(s)→P1​ρ→P1​π​π→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1𝜌→subscript𝑃1𝜋𝜋B(s)to P1rhoto P1pipi and B(s)→P1​K∗→P1​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1superscript𝐾→subscript𝑃1𝐾𝜋B(s)to P1K*to P1Kpi in Tables and , respectively. As the theoretical uncertainty from hadronic parameters (form factors and decay constants ) mostly cancel, the major theoretical uncertainties for 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry parameters are from the nonperturbative contribution parameters in Eq. and weak phases. Here we only list the uncertainty arise from the strong phase in Eq. due to small uncertainties of CKM matrix elements. We also list the experimental data in the last column of the two tables. Actually only three decay modes with the intermediate process K−​ρ0superscript𝐾superscript𝜌0K-rho0, π+​K∗−superscript𝜋superscript𝐾absentpi+K*-, and η​K¯∗0𝜂superscript¯𝐾absent0etabarK*0 are well measured in experiments with more than 3​σ3𝜎3sigma signal significance. Most 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetries listed here are predictions to be tested by the future experiments. For example, the 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry of B−→π+​ρ0→π+​π+​π−→superscript𝐵superscript𝜋superscript𝜌0→superscript𝜋superscript𝜋superscript𝜋B-topi+rho0topi+pi+pi- is predicted quite large in this work, which is consistent with the theoretical predictions using QCDFcitebib.bib30 and the PQCD approachcitebib.bib13 but not yet measured by the experiments. We did not list the 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetries of B→P1​ϕ→P1​K​K¯→𝐵subscript𝑃1italic-ϕ→subscript𝑃1𝐾¯𝐾Bto P1phito P1KbarK decays in our table, since most of them are from pure penguin diagram contributions, PCsubscript𝑃𝐶PC, PAsubscript𝑃𝐴PA, whose 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry is expected to be zero with only one single CKM phase, at leading order approximation. We also do not list the 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetries of B(s)→P1​ρ​(ω)→P1​K​K¯→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1𝜌𝜔→subscript𝑃1𝐾¯𝐾B(s)to P1rho(omega)to P1KbarK decays contributed by the virtual effects. Most of the quasi-two-body decays are extracted from the Dalitz-plot analysis of three-body ones in experimental analysis. For example, B−→K−​ρ0→superscript𝐵superscript𝐾superscript𝜌0B-to K-rho0 and B−→π−​K¯∗0→superscript𝐵superscript𝜋superscript¯𝐾absent0B-topi-barK*0 decays are extracted from B−→K−​π+​π−→superscript𝐵superscript𝐾superscript𝜋superscript𝜋B-to K-pi+pi- decays. Comparing the 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetries of quasi-two-body decay by integrating out the invariant mass square (s𝑠s) distribution of 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry over the kinematics region in the Dalitz plot with the corresponding ones of two-body decay with s𝑠s fixed as mV2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑉2mV2 as in Ref citebib.bib38, we find that the size of 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetries of B(s)→P1​ρ→P1​π​π→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1𝜌→subscript𝑃1𝜋𝜋B(s)to P1rhoto P1pipi in Table are slightly smaller than that of the B(s)→P1​ρ→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1𝜌B(s)to P1rho. The 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry of B(s)→P1​K∗→P1​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1superscript𝐾→subscript𝑃1𝐾𝜋B(s)to P1K*to P1Kpi in Table are slightly larger than that of B(s)→P1​K∗→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1superscript𝐾B(s)to P1K* decay. These very small changes of 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry between two-body and quasi-two-body decays arise from the finite decay width of vector resonances.
content_sections.V Conclusion: We systematically analyze the three-body charmless B𝐵B meson decays through the intermediate resonance, i. e. , they proceed via quasi-two-body decays as B(s)→P1​V→P1​P2​P3→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1𝑉→subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2subscript𝑃3B(s)to P1Vto P1P2P3, with the vector resonant state V𝑉V including all ground states ρ,K∗,ω,ϕ𝜌superscript𝐾𝜔italic-ϕrho,K*,omega,phi. The first step of two-body B𝐵B decays to P1​Vsubscript𝑃1𝑉P1V intermediate states is induced by flavor changing weak decays b→u​u¯​d​(s)→𝑏𝑢¯𝑢𝑑𝑠brightarrow,u,,baru,d(s), at leading order and b→d​(s)​q​q¯​(q=u,d,s)→𝑏𝑑𝑠𝑞¯𝑞𝑞𝑢𝑑𝑠brightarrow,d(s),,q,barq,,(q=u,d,s) at one loop level. The second step is that the intermediate vector resonant state V𝑉V, described by the Breit-Wigner propagator, decays into two light pseudoscalar mesons via strong interaction. In order to include all possible perturbative and nonperturbative QCD corrections, the two-body weak B𝐵B decays are described by the FAT approach with the decay amplitudes extracted from experimental data. We compare results of the branching fractions from FAT approach with the PQCD approach’s predictions and the ones of QCD factorization, as well as the experimental data. For a color suppressed tree diagram and color suppressed or annihilation penguin diagram dominated decay modes, the branching ratios in FAT approach are larger than that of the PQCD approach and the QCD factorization approach. The reason is that the decay magnitudes and phases extracted from experimental data in FAT approach are larger than that in the other two approaches due to the shortage of nonperturbative contribution and 1/mb1subscript𝑚𝑏1/mb power corrections. We have also considered the virtual effects from ρ𝜌rho and ω𝜔omega resonance tail, which are usually ignored by the experimental analysis. These virtual effects from ground state ρ​0𝜌superscript7700rho0 are about 1∼2similar-to121sim 2 order magnitude smaller than the dominated contribution of B(s)→π,K(ρ→)ππB(s)topi,K(rhoto)pipi decays. However, these virtual effects contributions are at the same order as the higher resonance contributions, such as ρ​𝜌1450rho. We also find that the virtual contributions of these decays are not very sensitive to the decay widths of ρ𝜌rho and ω𝜔omega. The branching ratios of B(s)→P1(ρ→)ππB(s)to P1(rhoto)pipi, B(s)→P1(K∗→)KπB(s)to P1(K*to)Kpi are consistent with experimental data, and the others are predictions waiting for future experiments to test. For 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry, theoretical predictions of three-body decays are mostly based on quasi-two-body decays via intermediate resonance, where the strong phases extracted from experimental data in FAT approach are sufficient to induce correct direct 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetries. In this work, we utilize the same strong phases of two-body decays together with the phases from Breit-Wigner formalism to predict the direct 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetries of quasi-two-body decays, B(s)→P1(ρ→)ππB(s)to P1(rhoto)pipi and B(s)→P1(K∗→)KπB(s)to P1(K*to)Kpi. Our results agree well with the existing experimental measurements. But many of the 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry predictions are waiting for future experiments.

sorted_papers.title:
Enhanced Next-to-Leading-Order Corrections to Weak Annihilation
  $B$-Meson Decays
sorted_papers.abstract:
We accomplish the analytical computation of the pure weak annihilation
non-leptonic $B$-meson decay amplitudes at leading power in the heavy quark
expansion. The novel observation regarding such fundamental hadronic quantities
is that adding the missing hard-collinear contribution on top of the hard gluon
exchange effect eliminates rapidity divergences entering the convolution
integrals of factorization formulae. Subsequently we identify the perturbative
enhancement mechanism due to the penguin contractions of the current-current
operators from the weak effective Hamiltonian, which yields the significant
impacts on the CP violating observables.

qualitative: The weak annihilation contributions in exclusive charmless B-meson decays are crucial for understanding CP violation and strong interaction dynamics, but their theoretical treatment has been hindered by rapidity divergences in convolution integrals within QCD factorization.

The inclusion of hard-collinear gluon exchange (with invariant mass of order √(mbΛQCD)) provides an analytical regularization of rapidity divergences, resolving a long-standing issue in factorization-based calculations of weak annihilation amplitudes.

Penguin contractions of current-current operators in the weak effective Hamiltonian lead to a perturbative enhancement mechanism that significantly impacts CP-violating observables, particularly through the generation of sizable strong phases.

Previous calculations neglected the hard-collinear contribution, leading to inconsistent regularization and unreliable predictions for annihilation-dominated decays; this work rectifies that by incorporating the missing hard-collinear topology.

The improved formalism enables systematic computation of next-to-leading-order (NLO) corrections to weak annihilation amplitudes, which is essential for precise predictions of direct CP asymmetries and other strong-phase-sensitive observables.

The validity of the QCD factorization framework for B→PP, B→PV, and B→VV decays is strengthened by this analytical regularization, especially in the presence of finite bottom-quark mass and end-point singularities.

The topological amplitude analysis reveals that NLO Feynman diagrams enhanced by large Wilson coefficients and CKM factors are key to generating significant strong phases, which are critical for CP violation studies.
quantitative: The hard-collinear gluon exchange contribution has an invariant mass scale of 𝒪(√(mbΛQCD)), where mb is the bottom quark mass and ΛQCD ≈ 300 MeV.

The perturbative enhancement arises from penguin contractions of current-current operators in the weak effective Hamiltonian, which are enhanced by large Wilson coefficients and CKM factors (e.g., |Vtd/Vts| ≈ 0.2), leading to non-negligible contributions to decay amplitudes.
The paper focuses on pure annihilation channels: B¯s→ππ and B¯d→ϕϕ, which are dominated by weak annihilation topologies and sensitive to strong phases.
The analysis is performed at next-to-leading order (NLO) in αs, with the inclusion of NLO Feynman diagrams that are enhanced by large Wilson coefficients and CKM factors.

The formalism relies on leading-twist light-cone distribution amplitudes for the final-state mesons and the B-meson, with acceptable models used to evaluate the convolution integrals (e.g., Gaussian or asymptotic forms).
The regularization of rapidity divergences is achieved analytically through the hard-collinear contribution, eliminating the need for ad hoc parametrizations of divergent integrals.

authors: Cai-Dian L\"u, Yue-Long Shen, Chao Wang, Yu-Ming Wang

content_sections.I Introduction: It is generally accepted that the exclusive two-body charmless bottom-meson decays are of fundamental importance for advancing our understanding towards diverse facets of the strong interaction dynamics governing the flavour-changing heavy-quark decay processes and for exploring the peculiar implications of the Cabibbo-Kobayashi-Maskawa (CKM) mechanism for CP violation in electroweak interactions. The primary challenge of predicting such hadronic B𝐵B-meson decay observables consists in constructing a field-theoretic framework to disentangle the calculable perturbative QCD fluctuations from the non-perturbative soft and collinear physics systematically. In this respect, the QCD factorization formalism based upon expansions in the small parameter ΛQCD/mbsubscriptΛQCDsubscript𝑚𝑏Lambdarm QCD/mb citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6 has proven to provide the precise prescriptions for evaluating the appeared non-leptonic matrix elements of the effective weak Hamiltonian operators. However, the long-standing obstacle to improve the factorization calculations of the two-body B𝐵B-meson decay amplitudes arises from the disturbing end-point divergences in the convolution integrals describing the power-suppressed but phenomenologically pronounced weak annihilation corrections. In particular, the inadequacy of achieving the factorization-compatible regularization of the annihilation amplitudes apparently does not allow to provide quantitative predictions for the annihilation-dominated decay processes. Additionally, the abiding lack of higher-order perturbative corrections to the weak annihilation topologies prevents us from obtaining reliable estimates of the strong-phase sensitive quantities (for instance, the direct CP asymmetry) due to a variety of potential enhancements, which do not manifest themselves at leading-order (LO) in the strong coupling. The process-independent parametrizations for logarithmically and linearly divergent integrals in the basic building blocks for the exclusive hadronic B¯q→P​P→subscript¯𝐵𝑞𝑃𝑃barBqto PP, B¯q→P​V→subscript¯𝐵𝑞𝑃𝑉barBqto PV and B¯q→V​V→subscript¯𝐵𝑞𝑉𝑉barBqto VV (with q=d,s𝑞𝑑𝑠q=d,,s) decay amplitudes citebib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10 (see also citebib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13 and references therein) introduce further assumption for the current QCD factorization predictions, especially with the finite bottom-quark mass in practice, which has been examined exploratorily by employing the data-driven strategy citebib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19. The striking importance of the weak annihilation mechanism in investigating the non-leptonic B𝐵B-meson decay amplitudes also motivated the interesting dynamical anatomy citebib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22 on the basis of distinct QCD techniques (see citebib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31 for an alternative treatment taking into account the non-vanishing transverse momenta of the active partons). As a consequence, accomplishing the systematic computation of the weak annihilation topologies with the non-trivial strong phases will be of the top priority for developing the conceptual framework of the charmless two-body B¯q→M1​M2→subscript¯𝐵𝑞subscript𝑀1subscript𝑀2barBqto M1M2 decays and for better confronting the anticipated precision measurements of a large number of CP violating observables at the Belle II experiment citebib.bib32. In order to probe factorization properties of the weak annihilation contributions immaculately, we report on a novel observation of the hard-collinear gluon exchange (the short-distance configuration with the invariant mass of 𝒪(mb​ΛQCD))cal O(sqrtmb,Lambdarm QCD)) in regularizing the unwanted rapidity divergences analytically, which has been unfortunately neglected in all previous calculations of the two-body hadronic B𝐵B-meson decays, by concentrating on the pure annihilation channels such as B¯s→π​π→subscript¯𝐵𝑠𝜋𝜋barBstopipi and B¯d→ϕ​ϕ→subscript¯𝐵𝑑italic-ϕitalic-ϕbarBdtophiphi. The significance of this essential insight for consolidating the theory foundation of QCD factorization in B𝐵B-meson decaying into two light mesons will be further strengthened by inspecting a sample set of the next-to-leading order (NLO) Feynman diagrams, which are enhanced by the large Wilson coefficients and/or the multiplication CKM factors numerically and are promising to yield the sizeable strong phases. General implications of the improved formalism on the accessible decay observables will be then discussed with the acceptable models for the leading-twist light-cone distribution amplitudes of both the bottom meson and the final-state hadrons.

The yielding contribution due to the gluon emission from the bottom quark (i. e. , the LO diagram (b)) has been demonstrated to be calculable self-consistently at twist-two order citebib.bib39, bib.bib2. content_sections.IV Phenomenological implications: We are now equipped to explore the phenomenological significance of the factorized expressions for the hadronic quantities 𝒯p,superscript𝒯𝑝0cal Tp,, and 𝒯p,superscript𝒯𝑝1cal Tp,, dictating the exclusive B¯q→M1​M2→subscript¯𝐵𝑞subscript𝑀1subscript𝑀2barBqto M1M2 decay amplitude . To this end, we will employ the three-parameter ansätz of the leading-twist bottom-meson distribution amplitude as proposed in citebib.bib55 (see citebib.bib59, bib.bib60, bib.bib61, bib.bib62, bib.bib63, bib.bib64, bib.bib65, bib.bib66, bib.bib67, bib.bib68 for additional discussions on the higher-twist distribution amplitudes) ϕB+​(ω,μ0)=Γ​(β)Γ​(α)​U​(β−α,3−α,ωω0)​ωω02​exp​(−ωω0)superscriptsubscriptitalic-ϕ𝐵𝜔subscript𝜇0Γ𝛽Γ𝛼𝑈𝛽𝛼3𝛼𝜔subscript𝜔0𝜔superscriptsubscript𝜔02exp𝜔subscript𝜔0phiB+(omega,,mu0)=Gamma(beta)overGamma(alpha),Uleft(beta-alpha,3-alpha,omegaoveromega0right)omegaoveromega02rm expleft(-omegaoveromega0right) at the reference scale μ0=1​GeVsubscript𝜇01GeVmu0=1,rm GeV, where U​(a,b,z)𝑈𝑎𝑏𝑧U(a,b,z) represents the confluent hypergeometric function of the second kind and the allowed intervals of the shape parameters ω0subscript𝜔0omega0, α𝛼alpha and β𝛽beta are determined by reproducing the numerical values of the inverse-logarithmic moments citebib.bib48, bib.bib56 λBd=350±150​MeVsubscript𝜆subscript𝐵𝑑plus-or-minus350150MeVlambdaBd=350pm 150,rm MeV, λBs=400±150​MeVsubscript𝜆subscript𝐵𝑠plus-or-minus400150MeVlambdaBs=400pm 150,rm MeV, σBd,s=0.0±0.7superscriptsubscript𝜎subscript𝐵𝑑𝑠1plus-or-minus0.00.7widehatsigmaBd,s=0.0pm 0.7, σBd,s=0.0±6.0superscriptsubscript𝜎subscript𝐵𝑑𝑠2plus-or-minus0.06.0widehatsigmaBd,s=0.0pm 6.0. We proceed to adopt the lattice predictions for the pseudoscalar-meson decay constants (fBdsubscript𝑓subscript𝐵𝑑fBd, fBssubscript𝑓subscript𝐵𝑠fBs, fπsubscript𝑓𝜋fpi, fKsubscript𝑓𝐾fK) as summarized in citebib.bib69 and take the improved extractions of the decay constants of the longitudinally polarized vector mesons (fρsubscript𝑓𝜌frho, fωsubscript𝑓𝜔fomega, fϕsubscript𝑓italic-ϕfphi) from citebib.bib70. Furthermore, we will truncate the Gegenbauer expansions of the twist-two light-meson distribution amplitudes at the next-to-next-to-leading conformal spin accuracy and take advantage of the non-perturbative determinations of the two lowest moments a1,2​(μ0)subscript𝑎12subscript𝜇0a1,2(mu0) from citebib.bib71 for the energetic pseudoscalar mesons and from citebib.bib70 for the light vector mesons. The default values and uncertainties for the remaining SM parameters follow Table 1 of citebib.bib56. In order to develop a transparent understanding of the numerical feature for the identified enhancement mechanism, we present in Table the obtained results of 𝒯p,superscript𝒯𝑝0cal Tp,, and 𝒯p,superscript𝒯𝑝1cal Tp,, for the weak annihilation decay B¯s→π+​π−→subscript¯𝐵𝑠superscript𝜋superscript𝜋barBstopi+pi- with three distinct values of the renormalization scale ν=mb/2,mb, 2​mb𝜈subscript𝑚𝑏2subscript𝑚𝑏2subscript𝑚𝑏nu=mb/2,,mb,,2,mb, while employing the central values of the remaining theory inputs. Interestingly, the dominant NLO contribution from the charm-loop diagrams displayed in Figure (c) and (d) (plus the two diagrams due to the crossing symmetry) will result in the significant increase of the real part of 𝒯c,superscript𝒯𝑐0cal Tc,, in magnitude and simultaneously generates the considerable cancellation of its imaginary part, keeping in mind the CKM hierarchy Vu​b​Vu​s∗/Vc​b​Vc​s∗=0.021​e−1.20​isubscript𝑉𝑢𝑏superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑢𝑠∗subscript𝑉𝑐𝑏superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑐𝑠∗0.021superscript𝑒1.20𝑖VubVusast/VcbVcsast=0.021,e-1.20,i. In consequence, the perturbative rescattering effect under discussion provides an important source of the strong phases of the weak-annihilation non-leptonic B¯qsubscript¯𝐵𝑞barBq-meson decay amplitudes, which can then be probed feasibly by investigating the CP violating observables. It is straightforward to derive the time-dependent CP asymmetries for the neutral B¯qsubscript¯𝐵𝑞barBq-meson decaying into CP eigenstates 𝒜CP​(t)subscript𝒜CP𝑡displaystylecal Arm CP(t) =displaystyle= Γ​(B¯q→M1​M2)−Γ​(Bq→M1​M2)Γ​(B¯q→M1​M2)+Γ​(Bq→M1​M2)Γ→subscript¯𝐵𝑞subscript𝑀1subscript𝑀2Γ→subscript𝐵𝑞subscript𝑀1subscript𝑀2Γ→subscript¯𝐵𝑞subscript𝑀1subscript𝑀2Γ→subscript𝐵𝑞subscript𝑀1subscript𝑀2displaystylefracGamma(barBqto M1M2)-Gamma(Bqto M1M2)Gamma(barBqto M1M2)+Gamma(Bqto M1M2) where the general definitions of 𝒜CPdirsuperscriptsubscript𝒜CPdircal Arm CPrm dir, 𝒜CPmixsuperscriptsubscript𝒜CPmixcal Arm CPrm mix and 𝒜Δ​Γsubscript𝒜ΔΓcal ADeltaGamma can be found, for instance, in citebib.bib72, bib.bib73, bib.bib74, satisfying the exact algebra relation |𝒜CPdir|2+|𝒜CPmix|2+|𝒜Δ​Γ|2=1superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝒜CPdir2superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝒜CPmix2superscriptsubscript𝒜ΔΓ21|cal Arm CPrm dir|2+|cal Arm CPrm mix|2+|cal ADeltaGamma|2=1. The resulting predictions for the two independent CP asymmetries displayed in Table evidently reveal the striking numerical impacts of the enhanced NLO QCD correction to the weak annihilation amplitudes described in this Letter, without which the direct CP violation for the entire pure annihilation channels would vanish due to the absence of at least two separate terms with different strong phases involved in 𝒜¯​(B¯q→M1​M2)¯𝒜→subscript¯𝐵𝑞subscript𝑀1subscript𝑀2barcal A(barBqto M1M2). By contrast, the time-dependent CP asymmetries of B¯s→ρL​ωL→subscript¯𝐵𝑠subscript𝜌𝐿subscript𝜔𝐿barBstorhoL,omegaL turn out to be insensitive to this particular perturbative contribution in question, on account of the isopsin non-conservation of the QCD scattering amplitude ⟨ρL​(p)​ωL​(q)|g∗​(pg,α)​g∗​(pg,β)⟩inner-productsubscript𝜌𝐿𝑝subscript𝜔𝐿𝑞superscript𝑔∗subscript𝑝𝑔𝛼superscript𝑔∗subscript𝑝𝑔𝛽rhoL(p),omegaL(q)|gast(pg,alpha),gast(tildepg,beta). We further mention in passing that the newly computed NLO quantities 𝒯p,superscript𝒯𝑝1cal Tp,, generate the subdominant corrections to the CP-averaged branching fractions of the pure annihilation decay channels (numerically at the level of 𝒪​(10%)𝒪percent10cal O(10,%)).
content_sections.V Conclusions: To summarize, we have endeavored to achieve the analytical regularization of end-point divergences in the factorization formulae of the peculiar weak annihilation non-leptonic bottom-meson decay amplitudes, due to the gluon radiation off the initial-state partons, by adding the missing hard-collinear contribution on top of the hard gluon exchange effect, both of which led to the leading power contributions in the heavy quark expansion. Applying the improved factorization formalism, we then identified the novel perturbative mechanism generated by the penguin contractions of the current-current operators, yielding the significant numerical impacts on the strong phases of the pure annihilation amplitudes. Our results are of importance for enhancing the predictive power of the perturbative factorization approach in addressing the entire spectrum of CP violating observables from exclusive heavy hadron decays, accessible at the LHCb and Belle II experiments. sorted_papers.title:
Estimating QCD-factorization amplitudes through $SU(3)$ symmetry in
  $B\rightarrow P P$ decays
sorted_papers.abstract:
We estimate the potential size of the weak annihilation amplitudes in QCD
factorization as allowed by experimental data. To achieve this goal, we
establish a connection between the amplitudes in the QCD factorization and the
so-called topological and $SU(3)$-invariant descriptions. Our approach is based
purely on the analysis of the tensor structure of the decay amplitudes. By
focusing on the processes $B\rightarrow P P$ and considering data from CP
asymmetries and branching fractions, we perform a global fit to the
$SU(3)$-irreducible quantities assuming minimal theoretical bias. Subsequently
we translate the outcome to the QCD factorization decomposition, and find that
the most constrained weak annihilation amplitudes are below $4\%$. However, in
view of the large uncertainties in several of the experimental input
parameters, values up to $30\%$ are allowed in certain cases.

sorted_papers.title: Hadronic three-body D decays mediated by scalar resonances
sorted_papers.abstract:
We study the quasi-two-body $D\to SP$ decays and the three-body $D$ decays
proceeding through intermediate scalar resonances, where $S$ and $P$ denote
scalar and pseudoscalar mesons, respectively. Our main results are: (i) Certain
external and internal $W$-emission diagrams with the emitted meson being a
scalar meson are na{\"i}vely expected to vanish, but they actually receive
contributions from vertex and hard spectator-scattering corrections beyond the
factorization approximation. (ii) For light scalars with masses below or close
to 1~GeV, it is more sensible to study three-body decays directly and compare
with experiment as the two-body branching fractions are either unavailable or
subject to large finite-width effects of the scalar meson. (iii) We consider
the two-quark (scheme I) and four-quark (scheme II) descriptions of the light
scalar mesons, and find the latter generally in better agreement with
experiment. This is in line with recent BESIII measurements of semileptonic
charm decays that prefer the tetraquark description of light scalars produced
in charmed meson decays. (iv) The topological amplitude approach fails here as
the $D\to SP$ decay branching fractions cannot be reliably inferred from the
measurements of three-body decays, mainly because the decay rates cannot be
factorized into the topological amplitude squared and the phase space factor.
(v) The predicted rates for $D^0\to f_0 P, a_0 P$ are generally smaller than
experimental data by one order of magnitude, presumably implying the
significance of $W$-exchange amplitudes. (vi) The $W$-annihilation amplitude is
found to be very sizable in the $SP$ sector with $|A/T|_{SP}\sim 1/2$, contrary
to its suppression in the $PP$ sector with $|A/T|_{PP}\sim 0.18$.

sections.16: Barnes, “Two Photon Decays Support the (K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾K\bar{K}) Molecule Picture of the S∗​(975)superscript𝑆975S^{*}(975) and δ​(980)𝛿980\delta(980),” Phys

qualitative: Certain external and internal W-emission diagrams with a scalar meson as the emitted particle are naively expected to vanish but receive non-zero contributions from vertex and hard spectator-scattering corrections beyond the factorization approximation.

For light scalar mesons with masses below or close to 1 GeV, direct study of three-body decays is more appropriate than two-body decays due to large finite-width effects and the unavailability of reliable two-body branching fractions.

The four-quark (tetraquark) description of light scalar mesons (scheme II) provides better agreement with experimental data compared to the two-quark (q¯q) description (scheme I), consistent with recent BESIII measurements of semileptonic charm decays.

The topological amplitude approach fails in the SP sector because decay rates cannot be reliably factorized into topological amplitude squared and phase space factor, making it impossible to infer D→SP branching fractions from three-body decay measurements.
The predicted branching fractions for D⁰→f₀P and D⁰→a₀P are generally smaller than experimental data by about one order of magnitude, suggesting a significant contribution from W-exchange amplitudes.
The W-annihilation amplitude in the SP sector is sizable, with |A/T|_{SP} ≈ 1/2, in contrast to its suppression in the PP sector where |A/T|_{PP} ≈ 0.18.
quantitative: Predicted D⁰→f₀P and D⁰→a₀P branching fractions are smaller than experimental data by one order of magnitude
Light scalar mesons below 1 GeV include σ/f₀(500), f₀(980), a₀(980), and κ/K₀*(700)
Scalar mesons above 1 GeV include a₀(1450), K₀*(1430), f₀(1370), f₀(1500), and f₀(1710)
Finite-width effects are particularly important for decays involving σ/f₀(500) and κ/K₀*(700) due to their broad widths relative to their masses

authors: Hai-Yang Cheng, Cheng-Wei Chiang, Zhi-Qing Zhang

content_sections.I Introduction: In recent years many measurements of hadronic three-body and four-body decays of charmed mesons have been performed with Dalitz-plot amplitude analyses. Amplitudes describing D𝐷D meson decays into multibody final states are dominated by quasi-two-body processes, such as D→P​P,V​P,S​P,A​P→𝐷𝑃𝑃𝑉𝑃𝑆𝑃𝐴𝑃Dto PP,VP,SP,AP and T​P𝑇𝑃TP, where P,V,S,A𝑃𝑉𝑆𝐴P,V,S,A and T𝑇T denote pseudoscalar, vector, scalar, axial-vector and tensor mesons, respectively. Among various S𝑆S-, P𝑃P- and D𝐷D-wave intermediate resonances, the identification of the scalar mesons is rather difficult due to their broad widths and flat angular distributions. Scalar mesons with masses lower than 2 GeV can be classified into two nonets: one nonet with masses below or close to 1 GeV, including σ/f0​𝜎subscript𝑓0500sigma/f0, f0​subscript𝑓0980f0, a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 and κ/K0∗​𝜅superscriptsubscript𝐾0700kappa/K0*; and the other nonet with masses above 1 GeV, including a0​subscript𝑎01450a0, K0∗​subscriptsuperscript𝐾01430K*0, f0​subscript𝑓01370f0, f0​subscript𝑓01500f0 and f0​subscript𝑓01710f0. Since the last three are all isosinglet scalars and only two of them can be accommodated in the quark model, implying a dominant scalar glueball content in one of the three isosinglets. In this work, we shall study the quasi-two-body D→S​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃Dto SP decays and the three-body D𝐷D decays proceeding through intermediate scalar resonances. In Tables and we collect all the measured branching fractions of D→S​P→P1​P2​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃→subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2𝑃Dto SPto P1P2P decays available in the Particle Data Group (PDG) citebib.bib1. It is clear that f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and the f0subscript𝑓0f0 family such as f0​subscript𝑓01370f0, f0​subscript𝑓01500f0 and f0​subscript𝑓01710f0 are observed in the three-body decays of D+,D0superscript𝐷superscript𝐷0D+,D0 and Ds+superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠Ds+, while a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 is seen exclusively in three-body D0superscript𝐷0D0 decays (except for Ds+→a0+,0​π0,+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑎00superscript𝜋0Ds+to a0+,0pi0,+). Contrary to f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 which are relatively easy to identify experimentally, the establishment of σ𝜎sigma and κ𝜅kappa is very difficult and controversial because their widths are so broad that their shapes are not clearly resonant. Nevertheless, their signals in three-body D𝐷D decays have been identified in D+,0→σ​π+,0→π+​π−​π+,0→superscript𝐷0𝜎superscript𝜋0→superscript𝜋superscript𝜋superscript𝜋0D+,0tosigmapi+,0topi+pi-pi+,0, D+→κ¯0​π+→KS​π0​π+→superscript𝐷superscript¯𝜅0superscript𝜋→subscript𝐾𝑆superscript𝜋0superscript𝜋D+tobarkappa0pi+to KSpi0pi+ and D+→κ¯0​K+→π+​K−​K+→superscript𝐷superscript¯𝜅0superscript𝐾→superscript𝜋superscript𝐾superscript𝐾D+tobarkappa0K+topi+K-K+, respectively. Because of threshold and coupled-channel effects for f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 and the very broad widths for σ𝜎sigma and κ𝜅kappa, it is no longer pertinent to use the conventional Breit-Wigner parametrization to describe their line shapes. The D→S​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃Dto SP decays and related three-body D𝐷D decays have been studied previously in Refs. citebib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib5, bib.bib4, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib11, bib.bib10, bib.bib12, bib.bib13. In the D→S​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃Dto SP decays, the flavor diagram of each topology has two possibilities: one with the spectator quark in the charmed meson going to the pseudoscalar meson in the final state, and the other with the spectator quark ending up in the scalar meson. We thus need two copies of each topological diagram to describe the decay processes. Many of these decays have been observed in recent years through dedicated experiments and powerful Dalitz plot analysis of multi-body decays. We will investigate whether an extraction of the sizes and relative strong phases of these amplitudes is possible. One purpose of studying these decays is to check our understanding in the structures and properties of light even-parity scalar mesons. Another goal is to learn the final-state interaction pattern in view of the rich resonance spectrum around the D𝐷D meson mass range. Not only does this work update our previous study citebib.bib9, we also study the finite-width effect in the three-body decays mediated by the scalar mesons. Such an effect is observed to be particularly important for decays involving σ/f0​𝜎subscript𝑓0500sigma/f0 and κ/K0∗​𝜅superscriptsubscript𝐾0700kappa/K0* in the intermediate state because of their broad widths compared to their masses, respectively. Therefore, one should be careful in the use of the narrow width approximation (NWA) to extract the D→S​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃Dto SP two-body decays from the three-body decay rates. This paper is organized as follows. In Section, we review the current experimental status about how various D→S​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃Dto SP decay branching fractions are extracted using the NWA from three-body decay rates. In Section, we discuss the two-quark q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq and tetraquark pictures of the scalar nonet near or below 1GeV along with the associated conundrums. The decay constants and form factors required for subsequent numerical calculations are given in this section, too. Section sets up the notation and formalism of flavor amplitude analysis, for both quark-antiquark and tetraquark pictures. In Section, we take the factorization approach as an alternative toward analyzing these decays. We also introduce line shapes for the scalar resonances when describing various three-body decays. Section gives the results obtained based upon the approaches in the previous two sections for a comparison. Section is devoted to the study of finite-width effect and how the NWA should be modified. We summarize our findings in Section.
content_sections.II Experimental status: It is known that three- and four-body decays of heavy mesons provide a rich laboratory for studying the intermediate-state resonances. The Dalitz plot analysis of three-body or four-body decays of charmed mesons is a very useful technique for this purpose. We are interested in D→S​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃Dto SP decays followed by S→P1​P2→𝑆subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2Sto P1P2. The results of various experiments are summarized in Tables and . To extract the branching fraction for a D→S​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃Dto SP decay, it is the usual practice to use the NWA: Γ​(D→S​P→P1​P2​P)=Γ​(D→S​P)NWA​ℬ​(S→P1​P2). content_sections.III Physical properties of scalar mesons: It is known that the underlying structure of scalar mesons is not well established theoretically (see, e. g. , Refs. citebib.bib19, bib.bib20 for a review). Scalar mesons with masses lower than 2GeV can be classified into two nonets: one nonet with masses below or close to 1GeV, including the isoscalars f0​subscript𝑓0500f0 (or σ𝜎sigma), f0​subscript𝑓0980f0, the isodoublet K0∗​superscriptsubscript𝐾0700K0* (or κ𝜅kappa) and the isovector a0​subscript𝑎0980a0; and the other nonet with masses above 1GeV, including f0​subscript𝑓01370f0, a0​subscript𝑎01450a0, K0∗​subscriptsuperscript𝐾01430K*0 and f0​/f0​subscript𝑓01500subscript𝑓01710f0/f0. If the scalar meson states below or near 1GeV are identified as the conventional low-lying 0+superscript00+ q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq nonet, then the nonet states above 1GeV could be excited q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq states. In the naïve quark model, the flavor wave functions of the light scalars read σ=12​(u​u¯+d​d¯),f0=s​s¯,formulae-sequence𝜎12𝑢¯𝑢𝑑¯𝑑subscript𝑓0𝑠¯𝑠displaystylesigma=1oversqrt2(ubaru+dbard),qquadqquadf0=sbars, a00=12​(u​u¯−d​d¯),a0+=u​d¯,a0−=d​u¯,formulae-sequencesuperscriptsubscript𝑎0012𝑢¯𝑢𝑑¯𝑑formulae-sequencesuperscriptsubscript𝑎0𝑢¯𝑑superscriptsubscript𝑎0𝑑¯𝑢displaystyle a00=1oversqrt2(ubaru-dbard),qquadqquad a0+=ubard,qquad a0-=dbaru, κ+=u​s¯,κ0=d​s¯,κ¯0=s​d¯,κ−=s​u¯,formulae-sequencesuperscript𝜅𝑢¯𝑠formulae-sequencesuperscript𝜅0𝑑¯𝑠formulae-sequencesuperscript¯𝜅0𝑠¯𝑑superscript𝜅𝑠¯𝑢displaystylekappa+=ubars,qquadkappa0=dbars,qquadbarkappa0=sbard,qquadkappa-=sbaru, where an ideal mixing for f0subscript𝑓0f0 and σ𝜎sigma is assumed as f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 is the heaviest one and σ𝜎sigma the lightest one in the light scalar nonet. However, as summarized in Ref. citebib.bib9, this simple picture encounters several serious problems: 1. It is impossible to understand the mass degeneracy between f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0, which is the so-called “inverted spectrum problem. ” 2. The P𝑃P-wave 0+superscript00+ meson has one unit of orbital angular momentum which costs an energy around 500MeV. Hence, it should have a mass lying above rather than below 1GeV. 3. It is difficult to explain why σ𝜎sigma and κ𝜅kappa are much broader than f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 in width. 4. The γ​γ𝛾𝛾gammagamma widths of a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 and f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 are much smaller than naïvely expected for a q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq statecitebib.bib21. 5. The radiative decay ϕ→a0​​γ→italic-ϕsubscript𝑎0980𝛾phito a0gamma, which cannot proceed if a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 is a pure q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq state, can be nicely described by the four-quark nature of a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 citebib.bib22, bib.bib23 or the kaon loop mechanismcitebib.bib24. Likewise, the observation of the radiative decay ϕ→f0​​γ→π​π​γ→italic-ϕsubscript𝑓0980𝛾→𝜋𝜋𝛾phito f0gammatopipigamma is also accounted for by the four-quark state of f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 citebib.bib23. It turns out that these difficulties can be readily resolved in the tetraquark scenario where the four-quark flavor wave functions of light scalar mesons are symbolically given by citebib.bib25 σ=u​u¯​d​d¯,f0=12​(u​u¯+d​d¯)​s​s¯,formulae-sequence𝜎𝑢¯𝑢𝑑¯𝑑subscript𝑓012𝑢¯𝑢𝑑¯𝑑𝑠¯𝑠displaystylesigma=ubarudbard,qquadqquadqquadf0=frac1sqrt2(ubaru+dbard)sbars, a00=12​(u​u¯−d​d¯)​s​s¯,a0+=u​d¯​s​s¯,a0−=d​u¯​s​s¯,formulae-sequencesuperscriptsubscript𝑎0012𝑢¯𝑢𝑑¯𝑑𝑠¯𝑠formulae-sequencesuperscriptsubscript𝑎0𝑢¯𝑑𝑠¯𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑎0𝑑¯𝑢𝑠¯𝑠displaystyle a00=frac1sqrt2(ubaru-dbard)sbars,qquad a0+=ubardsbars,qquad a0-=dbarusbars, κ+=u​s¯​d​d¯,κ0=d​s¯​u​u¯,κ¯0=s​d¯​u​u¯,κ−=s​u¯​d​d¯. formulae-sequencesuperscript𝜅𝑢¯𝑠𝑑¯𝑑formulae-sequencesuperscript𝜅0𝑑¯𝑠𝑢¯𝑢formulae-sequencesuperscript¯𝜅0𝑠¯𝑑𝑢¯𝑢superscript𝜅𝑠¯𝑢𝑑¯𝑑displaystylekappa+=ubarsdbard,qquadkappa0=dbarsubaru,qquadbarkappa0=sbardubaru,qquadkappa-=sbarudbard. The four quarks q2​q¯2superscript𝑞2superscript¯𝑞2q2barq2 can form an S𝑆S-wave (rather than P𝑃P-wave) 0+superscript00+ meson without introducing one unit of orbital angular momentum. This four-quark description explains naturally the inverted mass spectrum of the light nonet, 1However, it has been claimed recently in Ref. citebib.bib26 that the inverse mass hierarchy can be realized in the q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq picture through a U​𝑈1U axial anomaly including explicit S​U​F𝑆𝑈subscript3𝐹SUF breaking. The anomaly term contributes to a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 with the strange quark mass and to κ/K0∗​𝜅superscriptsubscript𝐾0700kappa/K0* with the up or down quark mass due to its flavor singlet nature. The current mass of the strange quark makes the a0subscript𝑎0a0 meson heavier than the κ𝜅kappa meson. especially the mass degeneracy between f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0, and accounts for the broad widths of σ𝜎sigma and κ𝜅kappa while f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 are narrow because of the suppressed phase space for their decays to the kaon pairs. Lattice calculations have confirmed that a0​subscript𝑎01450a0 and K0∗​superscriptsubscript𝐾01430K0* are q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq mesons, and suggested that σ𝜎sigma, κ𝜅kappa and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 are tetraquark mesonia citebib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib31, bib.bib29, bib.bib30. The inverted spectrum problem can also be alleviated in the scenario where the light scalars are dynamically generated from the meson-meson interaction, with the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 coupling strongly to the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KoverlineK channel with isospin 0 and 1, respectively. Indeed, the whole light scalar nonet appears naturally from properly unitarized chiral amplitudes for pseudoscalar-pseudoscalar scatteringscitebib.bib32, bib.bib33. Consequently, both f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 are good candidates of K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KoverlineK molecular statescitebib.bib34, while σ𝜎sigma and κ𝜅kappa can be considered as the bound states of π​π𝜋𝜋pipi and K​π𝐾𝜋Kpi, respectively. In the naïve two-quark model with ideal mixing for f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and σ​𝜎500sigma, f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 is purely an s​s¯𝑠¯𝑠sbars state, while σ​𝜎500sigma is an n​n¯𝑛¯𝑛nbarn state with n​n¯≡(u¯​u+d¯​d)/2𝑛¯𝑛¯𝑢𝑢¯𝑑𝑑2nbarnequiv(baruu+bardd)/sqrt2. However, there also exists some experimental evidence indicating that f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 is not a purely s​s¯𝑠¯𝑠sbars state. Ref. citebib.bib51). content_sections.IV Diagrammatic amplitudes: A least model-dependent analysis of heavy meson decays can be carried out in the so-called topological diagram approach. In this diagrammatic scenario, all two-body nonleptonic weak decays of heavy mesons can be expressed in terms of six distinct quark diagrams citebib.bib53, bib.bib54, bib.bib52: T𝑇T, the external W𝑊W-emission tree diagram; C𝐶C, the internal W𝑊W-emission; E𝐸E, the W𝑊W-exchange; A𝐴A, the W𝑊W-annihilation; H𝐻H, the horizontal W𝑊W-loop; and V𝑉V, the vertical W𝑊W-loop. The one-gluon exchange approximation of the H𝐻H graph is the so-called “penguin diagram. ” These diagrams are classified according to the topologies of weak interactions with all strong interaction effects encoded. The topological amplitudes for D→S​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃Dto SP decays have been discussed in citebib.bib6, bib.bib9. Just as D→V​P→𝐷𝑉𝑃Dto V!P decays, one generally has two sets of distinct diagrams for each topology. For example, there are two external W𝑊W-emission and two internal W𝑊W-emission diagrams, depending on whether the emitted particle is an even-party meson or an odd-parity one. Following the convention in citebib.bib6, bib.bib9, we shall denote the primed amplitudes T′superscript𝑇′Tprime and C′superscript𝐶′Cprime for the case when the emitted meson is a scalar one. For the W𝑊W-exchange and W𝑊W-annihilation diagrams with the final state q1​q¯2subscript𝑞1subscript¯𝑞2q1barq2, the primed amplitude denotes that the even-parity meson contains the quark q1subscript𝑞1q1. Since K0∗subscriptsuperscript𝐾0K*0, a0​subscript𝑎01450a0 and the light scalars σ,κ,f0​,a0​𝜎𝜅subscript𝑓0980subscript𝑎0980sigma,kappa,f0,a0 fall into two different SU flavor nonets, in principle one cannot apply SU symmetry to relate the topological amplitudes in D+→f0​​π+→superscript𝐷subscript𝑓0980superscript𝜋D+to f0pi+ to, for example, those in D+→K¯0∗0​π+→superscript𝐷subscriptsuperscript¯𝐾absent00superscript𝜋D+tooverlineK*00pi+. In Ref. citebib.bib9 we have presented the topological amplitude decomposition in D→S​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃Dto SP decays in two different schemes. In scheme I, light scalar mesons σ,κ,a0​𝜎𝜅subscript𝑎0980sigma,kappa,a0 and f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 are described by the ground-state q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq states, while K0∗subscriptsuperscript𝐾0K*0 and a0​subscript𝑎01450a0 as excited q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq states. In scheme II, light scalars are tetraquark states, while K0∗subscriptsuperscript𝐾0K*0 and a0​subscript𝑎01450a0 are ground-state q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq. The topological amplitudes for D→S​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃Dto SP decays are listed in Table. The expressions of topological amplitudes are the same in both schemes I and II except for the channels involving f0subscript𝑓0f0 and σ𝜎sigma. For example, A​(D+→f0​π+)𝐴→superscript𝐷subscript𝑓0superscript𝜋displaystyle A(D+to f0pi+) =displaystyle= 12​Vc​d∗​Vu​d​(T+C′+A+A′)​sin⁡θ+Vc​s∗​Vu​s​C′​cos⁡θSchemeI,12​Vc​d∗​Vu​d​(T+C′+A+A′)+2​Vc​s∗​Vu​s​C′SchemeII,cases12superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑐𝑑subscript𝑉𝑢𝑑𝑇superscript𝐶′𝐴superscript𝐴′𝜃superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑐𝑠subscript𝑉𝑢𝑠superscript𝐶′𝜃SchemeI12superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑐𝑑subscript𝑉𝑢𝑑𝑇superscript𝐶′𝐴superscript𝐴′2superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑐𝑠subscript𝑉𝑢𝑠superscript𝐶′SchemeIIdisplaystyleleftbeginarraycl1oversqrt2Vcd*Vud(T+Cprime+A+Aprime)sintheta+Vcs*VusCprimecosthetaquadmboxSchemeI , 1oversqrt2Vcd*Vud(T+Cprime+A+Aprime)+sqrt2Vcs*VusCprimequadmboxSchemeII ,endarrayright. A​(D+→σ​π+)𝐴→superscript𝐷𝜎superscript𝜋displaystyle A(D+tosigmapi+) =displaystyle= 12​Vc​d∗​Vu​d​(T+C′+A+A′)​cos⁡θ−Vc​s∗​Vu​s​C′​sin⁡θSchemeI,Vc​d∗​Vu​d​(T+C′+A+A′)SchemeII. cases12superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑐𝑑subscript𝑉𝑢𝑑𝑇superscript𝐶′𝐴superscript𝐴′𝜃superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑐𝑠subscript𝑉𝑢𝑠superscript𝐶′𝜃SchemeIsuperscriptsubscript𝑉𝑐𝑑subscript𝑉𝑢𝑑𝑇superscript𝐶′𝐴superscript𝐴′SchemeIIdisplaystyleleftbeginarraycl1oversqrt2Vcd*Vud(T+Cprime+A+Aprime)costheta-Vcs*VusCprimesinthetaquadmboxSchemeI , Vcd*Vud(T+Cprime+A+Aprime)quadmboxSchemeII . endarrayright. In our numerical estimates, we will take θ=30∘𝜃superscript30theta=30circ, saturating the measured upper bound mentioned earlier. In Table the upper part involves only light scalar mesons (f0subscript𝑓0f0, a0subscript𝑎0a0, σ𝜎sigma, and κ𝜅kappa), whereas the lower part involves the a0​subscript𝑎01450a0 and K0∗​superscriptsubscript𝐾01430K0* mesons in the heavier nonet representation. This division is made because the amplitudes of the same topology in these two groups have no a priori relations. In each group we have 15 unknown parameters for the 8 topological amplitudes T,C,E,A𝑇𝐶𝐸𝐴T,C,E,A and T′,C′,E′,A′superscript𝑇′superscript𝐶′superscript𝐸′superscript𝐴′Tprime,Cprime,Eprime,Aprime. For neutral scalar mesons σ,f0𝜎subscript𝑓0sigma,f0 and a00superscriptsubscript𝑎00a00, we cannot set T′=C′=0superscript𝑇′superscript𝐶′0Tprime=Cprime=0 even though their vector decay constants vanish. As will be discussed in Sec. V. A, T′superscript𝑇′Tprime and C′superscript𝐶′Cprime do receive nonfactorizable contributions through vertex and spectator-scattering corrections citebib.bib55, bib.bib56. Nevertheless, it is naïvely expected that, for example, |T′|≪|T|much-less-thansuperscript𝑇′𝑇|Tprime|ll|T| and |C′|≪|C|much-less-thansuperscript𝐶′𝐶|Cprime|ll|C| for charged a0subscript𝑎0a0. However, as we shall see in Sec. V. C, a realistic calculation yields |C′|>|C|superscript𝐶′𝐶|Cprime|>|C| instead. At any rate, we have more theory parameters than observables (6 in the upper part and 5 in the lower part of the table), barring a fit. Since the branching fractions of f0→π​π→subscript𝑓0𝜋𝜋f0topipi and (f0,a0)→K​K¯→subscript𝑓0subscript𝑎0𝐾¯𝐾(f0,a0)to KoverlineK are unknown, many of the two-body decays in Table cannot be extracted from the data of three-body decays. Nevertheless, the strong couplings such as gf0→π​π,gf0→K​K¯,ga0→K​K¯subscript𝑔→subscript𝑓0𝜋𝜋subscript𝑔→subscript𝑓0𝐾¯𝐾subscript𝑔→subscript𝑎0𝐾¯𝐾gf0topipi,gf0to KbarK,ga0to KbarK and ga0→η​πsubscript𝑔→subscript𝑎0𝜂𝜋ga0toetapi have been inferred from a fit to the data. There are 17 available D→S​P→P1​P2​P2→𝐷𝑆𝑃→subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2subscript𝑃2Dto SPto P1P2P2 modes, but there are only 14 data related to D→S​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃Dto SP and we have 15 parameters to fit. Moreover, since we need to introduce appropriate energy-dependent line shapes for the scalar mesons, it is not conceivable to extract the topological amplitudes from three-body decays as the decay rate cannot be factorized into the topological amplitude squared and the phase space factor. We will come back to this point later. It is interesting to notice that the current data already imply the importance of W𝑊W-exchange and W𝑊W-annihilation amplitudes. Consider the decays: D0→a0+​π−→K+​K¯0​π−→superscript𝐷0superscriptsubscript𝑎0superscript𝜋→superscript𝐾superscript¯𝐾0superscript𝜋D0to a0+pi-to K+overlineK0pi- and D0→a0−​π+→K−​K0​π+→superscript𝐷0superscriptsubscript𝑎0superscript𝜋→superscript𝐾superscript𝐾0superscript𝜋D0to a0-pi+to K-K0pi+ with the two-body decay amplitudes proportional to (T′+E)superscript𝑇′𝐸(Tprime+E) and (T+E′)𝑇superscript𝐸′(T+Eprime), respectively (see Table). If the W𝑊W-exchange contributions are negligible, the former mode governed by the amplitude T′superscript𝑇′Tprime is expected to have a rate smaller than the latter (cf. Table ). Experimentally, it is the other way around. This is an indication that E𝐸E and E′superscript𝐸′Eprime play some role.
content_sections.V Factorization Approach: The diagrammatic approach has been applied quite successfully to hadronic decays of charmed mesons into P​P𝑃𝑃PP and V​P𝑉𝑃V!P final states citebib.bib58, bib.bib59, bib.bib60, bib.bib57, bib.bib61, bib.bib62, bib.bib63, bib.bib64, bib.bib65, bib.bib66. When generalized to the decay modes involving a scalar meson in the final state, it appears that the current data are still insufficient for us to fully extract the information of all amplitudes. Therefore, we take the naïve factorization formalism as a complementary approach to estimate the rates of these decay modes. In this framework, the W𝑊W-exchange and -annihilation type of contributions will be neglected.

content_sections.V Factorization Approach V.4 Flatté line: To describe three-body decays we need to introduce a line shape of the scalar resonance. Normally we use the relativistic Breit-Wigner line shape to describe the scalar resonance contributions to three-body decays D→S​P→P1​P2​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃→subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2𝑃Dto SPto P1P2P: TBW​(s)=1s−mR2+i​mR​ΓR​(s),superscript𝑇BW𝑠1𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑅2𝑖subscript𝑚𝑅subscriptΓ𝑅𝑠displaystyle Trm BW(s)=1over s-mR2+imRGammaR(s), with ΓR​(s)=ΓR0​(qq0)​mRs,subscriptΓ𝑅𝑠superscriptsubscriptΓ𝑅0𝑞subscript𝑞0subscript𝑚𝑅𝑠displaystyleGammaR(s)=GammaR0left(qover q0right)mRoversqrts, where q=|p→1|=|p→2|𝑞subscript→𝑝1subscript→𝑝2q=|vecp1|=|vecp2| is the c. m. momentum in the rest frame of R𝑅R, q0subscript𝑞0q0 the value of q𝑞q when s𝑠s is equal to mR2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑅2mR2. However, this parametrization is not suitable to describe the decay of f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 or a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 into K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KoverlineK as m​(K+)+m​(K−)=987.4𝑚superscript𝐾𝑚superscript𝐾987.4m(K+)+m(K-)=987.4 MeV and m​(K0)+m​(K¯0)=995.2𝑚superscript𝐾0𝑚superscript¯𝐾0995.2m(K0)+m(barK0)=995.2 MeV are near threshold. In other words, one has to take the threshold effect into account. content_sections.V Factorization Approach V.5 Line shape: As stressed in Ref. citebib.bib74, the scalar resonance σ/f0​𝜎subscript𝑓0500sigma/f0 is very broad and cannot be described by the usual Breit-Wigner line shape. Its partial wave amplitude does not resemble a Breit-Wigner shape with a clear peak and a simultaneous steep rise in the phase. The mass and width of the σ𝜎sigma resonance are identified from the associated pole position sσsubscript𝑠𝜎sqrtssigma of the partial wave amplitude in the second Riemann sheet as sσ=mσ−i​Γσ/2subscript𝑠𝜎subscript𝑚𝜎𝑖subscriptΓ𝜎2sqrtssigma=msigma-iGammasigma/2citebib.bib74. content_sections.VI Results and Discussion: In Tables and we have calculated two-body D→S​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃Dto SP and three-body D→S​P→P1​P2​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃→subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2𝑃Dto SPto P1P2P decays, respectively, in schemes I and II using the factorization approach with W𝑊W-exchange and W𝑊W-annihilation being neglected. We see from Table that the decay modes D+→a0+​K¯0,κ¯​π+→superscript𝐷superscriptsubscript𝑎0superscript¯𝐾0¯𝜅superscript𝜋D+to a0+overlineK0,barkappapi+ and K¯0∗​π+superscriptsubscript¯𝐾0superscript𝜋overlineK0*pi+ are free of W𝑊W-annihilation contributions and they are ideal for testing the validity of the factorization approach. From Table it is evident that the calculated rates of D+→κ¯​π+→KS​π0​π+→superscript𝐷¯𝜅superscript𝜋→subscript𝐾𝑆superscript𝜋0superscript𝜋D+tobarkappapi+to KSpi0pi+ and D+→K¯0∗0​π+→(K​π)0​π+→superscript𝐷subscriptsuperscript¯𝐾absent00superscript𝜋→superscript𝐾𝜋0superscript𝜋D+tooverlineK*00pi+to(Kpi)0pi+ in scheme II are in agreement with experiment. These modes are governed by the topologies T+C′𝑇superscript𝐶′T+Cprime which interfere constructively. This is in contrast to the Cabibbo-favored (CF) D+→K¯0​π+→superscript𝐷superscript¯𝐾0superscript𝜋D+tooverlineK0pi+ decay in the P​P𝑃𝑃P!P sector where T𝑇T and C𝐶C contribute destructively. For (D+,D0,Ds+)→f0​P;f0→P1​P2formulae-sequence→superscript𝐷subscript𝐷0superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑓0𝑃→subscript𝑓0subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2(D+,D0,Ds+)to f0P;f0to P1P2, predictions in scheme II are improved over that in scheme I and the discrepancies presumably arise from the W𝑊W-exchange or W𝑊W-annihilation amplitude. This implies that the tetraquark picture for light scalars works better than the quark-antiquark scenario. Upon an inspection of Table , the reader may wonder (i) why the branching fractions for D→(f0,σ)​P→𝐷subscript𝑓0𝜎𝑃Dto(f0,sigma)P decays in schemeII are always larger than that in schemeI except for D0→f0​π0→superscript𝐷0subscript𝑓0superscript𝜋0D0to f0pi0, and (ii) why the predicted branching fractions of D+→σ​π+→superscript𝐷𝜎superscript𝜋D+tosigmapi+ and D+→κ¯0​π+→superscript𝐷superscript¯𝜅0superscript𝜋D+tobarkappa0pi+ are larger than experimental data, while the corresponding three-body decays agree with the measurements. For (i), we see from TableIV and also Eq. that the W𝑊W-emission decay amplitude involving σ𝜎sigma is suppressed by a factor of cos⁡θ/2𝜃2costheta/sqrt2 in schemeI relative to that in schemeII, while it is suppressed by a factor of sin⁡θ𝜃sintheta for the W𝑊W-emission decay amplitude involving f0​subscript𝑓0980f0. As a consequence our choice of θ=30∘𝜃superscript30theta=30circ, the branching fractions for D→(f0,σ)​P→𝐷subscript𝑓0𝜎𝑃Dto(f0,sigma)P in schemeII are always larger than schemeI except for D0→f0​π0→superscript𝐷0subscript𝑓0superscript𝜋0D0to f0pi0. For (ii), it has something to do with the finite-width effects of σ𝜎sigma and κ𝜅kappa as they are both very broad. We shall see in Sec. that the extraction of ℬ​(D→S​P)ℬ→𝐷𝑆𝑃cal B(Dto SP) from the data is affected by the broad widths of both σ𝜎sigma and κ𝜅kappa.
content_sections.VI Results and Discussion VI.1 W𝑊W-annih: In the factorization calculations presented in Tables and , we have neglected both W𝑊W-exchange and W𝑊W-annihilation amplitudes. The Ds+→a0+​π0+a00​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑎0superscript𝜋0superscriptsubscript𝑎00superscript𝜋Ds+to a0+pi0+a00pi+ mode recently observed by BESIII citebib.bib17 proceeds only through the W𝑊W-annihilation amplitudes. However, its branching fraction at a percent level is much larger than the other two W𝑊W-annihilation channels Ds+→ω​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠𝜔superscript𝜋Ds+toomegapi+ and ρ0​π+superscript𝜌0superscript𝜋rho0pi+ whose branching fractions are (1.92±0.30)×10−3plus-or-minus1.920.30superscript103(1.92pm 0.30)times 10-3 and (1.9±1.2)×10−4plus-or-minus1.91.2superscript104(1.9pm 1.2)times 10-4, respectively citebib.bib1. This implies that |A​(S​P)|>|A​(V​P)|𝐴𝑆𝑃𝐴𝑉𝑃|A(SP)|>|A(V!P)|. In other words, the W𝑊W-annihilation amplitude plays a more significant role in the S​P𝑆𝑃SP sector than in the V​P𝑉𝑃V!P one. Consider the decay amplitude of Ds+→a00​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑎00superscript𝜋Ds+to a00pi+ and the W𝑊W-annihilation contribution to Ds+→f0​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑓0superscript𝜋Ds+to f0pi+ (in scheme II) 𝒜​(Ds+→a00​π+)=12​Vc​s∗​Vu​d​(−A+A′),𝒜​(Ds+→f0​π+)ann=12​Vc​s∗​Vu​d​(A+A′). formulae-sequence𝒜→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑎00superscript𝜋12superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑐𝑠subscript𝑉𝑢𝑑𝐴superscript𝐴′𝒜subscript→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑓0superscript𝜋ann12superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑐𝑠subscript𝑉𝑢𝑑𝐴superscript𝐴′displaystylecal A(Ds+to a00pi+)=1oversqrt2Vcs*Vud(-A+Aprime),qquadcal A(Ds+to f0pi+)rm ann=1oversqrt2Vcs*Vud(A+Aprime). Following the G𝐺G-parity argument given in Ref. citebib.bib57, it is obvious that the direct W𝑊W-annihilation process through c​s¯→W→u​d¯→𝑐¯𝑠𝑊→𝑢¯𝑑cbarsto Wto ubard is allowed in Ds+→f0​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑓0superscript𝜋Ds+to f0pi+ decay but not in Ds+→a00​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑎00superscript𝜋Ds+to a00pi+ decay as G​(u​d¯)=−𝐺𝑢¯𝑑G(ubard)=-, G​(a0​π)=+𝐺subscript𝑎0𝜋G(a0pi)=+ and G​(f0​π)=−𝐺subscript𝑓0𝜋G(f0pi)=-. This means that short-distance W𝑊W-annihilation contributions respect the relation A′=Asuperscript𝐴′𝐴Aprime=A, contrary to the naïve expectation. Hence, one needs large long-distance W𝑊W-annihilation which yields A′=−Asuperscript𝐴′𝐴Aprime=-A. Since Ds+→ρ+​η→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜌𝜂Ds+torho+eta has the largest branching fraction of (8.9±0.8)%percentplus-or-minus8.90.8(8.9pm 0.8)% among the CF Ds+→V​P→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠𝑉𝑃Ds+to VP decays citebib.bib1 , it is conceivable that long-distance contribution from the weak decays Ds+→ρ+​η→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜌𝜂Ds+torho+eta followed by the resonantlike final-state rescattering of ρ+​η→a00​π+→superscript𝜌𝜂superscriptsubscript𝑎00superscript𝜋rho+etato a00pi+ (see Fig. ), which has the same topology as W𝑊W-annihilation, may explain the large W𝑊W-annihilation rate. 6The hadronic weak decays Ds+→ρ+​η′,K¯∗0​K+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜌superscript𝜂′superscript¯𝐾absent0superscript𝐾Ds+torho+etaprime,overlineK*0K+ and K¯0​K∗+superscript¯𝐾0superscript𝐾absentoverlineK0K*+ followed by final-state rescattering will also contribute to Ds+→a00​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑎00superscript𝜋Ds+to a00pi+. It is customary to evaluate the final-state rescattering contribution, Fig. , at the hadron level manifested in Fig. . One of the diagrams, namely, the triangle graph in Fig. (b) has been evaluated recently in citebib.bib78, bib.bib79. It yields a major contribution to Ds+→a00​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑎00superscript𝜋Ds+to a00pi+ owing to the large coupling constants for ρ+→π+​π0→superscript𝜌superscript𝜋superscript𝜋0rho+topi+pi0 and a00→π0​η→superscriptsubscript𝑎00superscript𝜋0𝜂a00topi0eta. The graph in Fig. (a) shows the resonant final-state interactions manifested by the nearby resonance π​𝜋1800pi whose strong decay to a0​πsubscript𝑎0𝜋a0pi has been seen experimentally citebib.bib1. However, we are not able to have a quantitative statement owing to the lack of information on its partial width. Assuming A′≈−Asuperscript𝐴′𝐴Aprimeapprox-A, the annihilation amplitude extracted from the data of Ds+→a0+​π0+a00​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑎0superscript𝜋0superscriptsubscript𝑎00superscript𝜋Ds+to a0+pi0+a00pi+ is (in units of 10−6superscript10610-6 GeV), |A|=0.91±0.12.
