content_sections.I Introduction: Contrary to the bottom sector where the physics of two-body nonleptonic B𝐵B decays can be formulated in a QCD-inspired approach, a theoretical description of the underlying mechanism for exclusive hadronic D𝐷D decays based on QCD is still absent today. This has to do with the mass of the charm quark, of order 1.5 GeV. It is not heavy enough to allow for a sensible heavy quark mass expansion and not light enough for an application of chiral perturbation theory. Nevertheless, a model-independent analysis of charm decays based on the topological diagram approach (TDA) is achievable. In this approach, the topological diagrams are classified according to the topologies of weak interactions with strong interaction effects at all orders implicitly taken into account. Analyses of D→P​P→𝐷𝑃𝑃Dto P!P and V​P𝑉𝑃V!P decays have been performed in Refs. citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6 within the framework of TDA, where P𝑃P and V𝑉V denote respectively pseudoscalar and vector mesons. In this work, we shall perform an updated analysis for the following reasons: (i) In previous analyses, we have set Γ​(K¯0)=2​Γ​(KS0)Γsuperscript¯𝐾02Γsuperscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0Gamma(overlineK0)=2Gamma(KS0) for decay modes involving a neutral KS0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0KS0. But this relation can be invalidated by the interference between Cabibbo-favored (CF) and doubly Cabibbo-suppressed (DCS) amplitudes. (ii) Branching fraction measurements of several CF and singly Cabibbo-suppressed (SCS) D→V​P→𝐷𝑉𝑃Dto V!P modes have been significantly improved in recent years. These new data provide valuable information and modify the sizes and phases of the relevant topological amplitudes. (iii) DCS decays were not carefully studied in the previous analyses, particularly due to the lack of sufficiently precise data in the V​P𝑉𝑃V!P channels. We shall examine whether the topological amplitudes in the DCS sector are the same as those in the CF decays. We will calculate the KS0−KL0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0KS0-KL0 asymmetries in D→KS,L0​M→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆𝐿0𝑀Dto KS,L0M decays with M=P,V𝑀𝑃𝑉M=P,V and compare them with the experiment. This will provide information on the DCS topological amplitudes. (iv) Thanks to BESIII, many new data on Ds+superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠Ds+ and D+superscript𝐷D+ decays to V​V𝑉𝑉V!V became available in the past few years. It turns out that several D→V​V→𝐷𝑉𝑉Dto V!V modes are dominated by the D𝐷D-wave amplitude and some dominated by the P𝑃P-wave, contrary to the naïve expectation of S𝑆S-wave dominance. Moreover, the decay D0→ω​ϕ→superscript𝐷0𝜔italic-ϕD0toomegaphi has been observed by BESIII to be transversely polarized with negligible longitudinal polarizationcitebib.bib7. In the end, there are many puzzles in the V​V𝑉𝑉V!V sector that need to be resolved. (v) One of our goals is to evaluate the D0superscript𝐷0D0-D¯0superscript¯𝐷0overlineD0 mixing parameter which we are going to elaborate on below. The D0superscript𝐷0D0-D¯0superscript¯𝐷0overlineD0 mixing occurs because the mass eigenstates D1,2subscript𝐷12D1,2 are not the same as the flavor eigenstates D0superscript𝐷0D0 and D¯0superscript¯𝐷0overlineD0. D𝐷D mixing is conventionally described by the two parameters x≡Δ​m/Γ𝑥Δ𝑚ΓxequivDelta m/Gamma and y≡Δ​Γ/2​Γ𝑦ΔΓ2ΓyequivDeltaGamma/2Gamma, where Δ​m=m1−m2Δ𝑚subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2Delta m=m1-m2 defined to be positive and Δ​Γ=Γ1−Γ2ΔΓsubscriptΓ1subscriptΓ2DeltaGamma=Gamma1-Gamma2. Evidence for the D0superscript𝐷0D0-D¯0superscript¯𝐷0overlineD0 mixing has been established and the current world averages for the CP allowed case arecitebib.bib8: x=(0.409−0.049+0.048)%,y=(0.615−0.055+0.056)%. formulae-sequence𝑥percentsubscriptsuperscript0.4090.0480.049𝑦percentsubscriptsuperscript0.6150.0560.055displaystyle x=(0.409+0.048-0.049)%,qquad y=(0.615+0.056-0.055)%. The absence of mixing, namely x=y=0𝑥𝑦0x=y=0, is excluded at 11.5​σ11.5𝜎11.5sigma. Very recently, LHCb has made a model-independent measurement of charm mixing parameters in B→D0(→KS0π+π−)μ−ν¯μXBto D0(to KS0pi+pi-)mu-barnumuX decays and obtained the resultscitebib.bib9 x=(0.401±0.049)%,y=(0.55±0.13)%,formulae-sequence𝑥percentplus-or-minus0.4010.049𝑦percentplus-or-minus0.550.13displaystyle x=(0.401pm 0.049)%,qquad y=(0.55pm 0.13)%, which are consistent with current averages given in Eq. . Early calculations of short-distance contributions to the D𝐷D mixing parameters already indicated that both x𝑥x and y𝑦y were very small, of order 10−6superscript10610-6, due to the Glashow-Iliopoulos-Maiani suppressioncitebib.bib10, bib.bib11. This implies that the observed D0superscript𝐷0D0-D¯0superscript¯𝐷0overlineD0 mixing is dominated by long-distance processes. Indeed, it is well-known that charm physics is governed by non-perturbative effects. In the literature, the D0superscript𝐷0D0-D¯0superscript¯𝐷0overlineD0 mixing is usually studied in three different approaches: inclusive, exclusive, and dispersive. In the inclusive approach, the mixing parameters are systematically investigated based on heavy quark expansion (HQE) dictated by the parameter 1/mc1subscript𝑚𝑐1/mccitebib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16 (for a review, see Ref. citebib.bib17). Since the lifetime of Ds+superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠Ds+ and the ratio τ​(D+)/τ​(D0)𝜏superscript𝐷𝜏superscript𝐷0tau(D+)/tau(D0) have been found to be in good agreement with experiment within the framework of HQEcitebib.bib18, it is natural to expect that this approach might also be viable for the mixing parameters. However, it turns out that the suppression of short-distance contributions cannot be alleviated in HQE. Contrary to the HQE approach at the quark level, long-distance contributions from the intermediate hadronic states are summed over in the exclusive approachcitebib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib21, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26. As pointed out in Ref. citebib.bib21, the mixing parameters x𝑥x and y𝑦y vanish in the flavor SU limit. In general, there are large cancellations of CF and DCS decays with the contributions from SCS decays. The cancellation will be perfect in the limit of SU symmetry, and the D0superscript𝐷0D0-D¯0superscript¯𝐷0overlineD0 mixing would occur only at the second order in SU breaking. For example, contributions from SCS decays D0→π+​π−,K+​K−→superscript𝐷0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋superscript𝐾superscript𝐾D0topi+pi-,K+K- are canceled by contributions from the CF D0→K−​π+→superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾superscript𝜋D0to K-pi+ decay and the DCS D0→K+​π−→superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾superscript𝜋D0to K+pi- decay. Since the intermediate states in this case are related by U-spin symmetry, it has been shown in Ref. citebib.bib25 that the D0superscript𝐷0D0-D¯0superscript¯𝐷0overlineD0 mixing in the Standard Model occurs only at the second order in U-spin breaking. A dispersive relation between x𝑥x and y𝑦y has been derived in Ref. citebib.bib27 in the heavy quark limit Δ​m=−12​π​𝒫​∫2​mπ∞𝑑E​[Δ​Γ​(E)E−mD+𝒪​(ΛQCDE)]. Δ𝑚12𝜋𝒫superscriptsubscript2subscript𝑚𝜋differential-d𝐸delimited-ΔΓ𝐸𝐸subscript𝑚𝐷𝒪subscriptΛQCD𝐸displaystyleDelta m=-1over 2pical Pint2mpiinftydEleft[DeltaGamma(E)over E-mD+cal Oleft(Lambdarm QCDover Eright)right]. For a given model of Δ​Γ​(E)ΔΓ𝐸DeltaGamma(E) or y​(E)𝑦𝐸y(E), it is conceivable to have x𝑥x comparable to y𝑦y in magnitude. Writing ∫0Λ𝑑s′​y​(s′)s−s′=π​x​(s)−∫Λ∞𝑑s′​y​(s′)s−s′subscriptsuperscriptΛ0differential-dsuperscript𝑠′𝑦superscript𝑠′𝑠superscript𝑠′𝜋𝑥𝑠subscriptsuperscriptΛdifferential-dsuperscript𝑠′𝑦superscript𝑠′𝑠superscript𝑠′displaystyleintLambda0dsprimey(sprime)over s-sprime=pi x(s)-intinftyLambdadsprimey(sprime)over s-sprime and choosing the scale ΛΛLambda large enough to justify the perturbative calculation of y𝑦y on the right-hand side, and below the b𝑏b quark threshold to avoid the b𝑏b quark contribution to the left-hand side, the authors of Ref. citebib.bib28 showed that the study of the D𝐷D meson mixing was converted into an inverse problem: the mixing parameters at low masses are solved as source distributions, which produce the potential observed at high masses. The analysis is further improved in Ref. citebib.bib29 in which SU breaking is introduced through physical thresholds of different D𝐷D meson decay channels. In this work, we shall focus on the exclusive scenario for the D0superscript𝐷0D0-D¯0superscript¯𝐷0overlineD0 mixing parameters. Since the two-body D0→P​P→superscript𝐷0𝑃𝑃D0to PP decays and quasi-two-body decays such as D0→V​P,V​V,S​P,S​V,A​P,A​V→superscript𝐷0𝑉𝑃𝑉𝑉𝑆𝑃𝑆𝑉𝐴𝑃𝐴𝑉D0to V!P,V!V,SP,SV,AP,AV, T​P,T​V𝑇𝑃𝑇𝑉TP,TV account for about 3/4 of the total hadronic rates, where S𝑆S, A𝐴A and T𝑇T denote respectively the scalar, axial-vector and tensor mesons, it is arguable that these two-body and quasi-two-body channels dominate and provide a good estimate of the mixing parameters. Data on two-body D→P​P,V​P→𝐷𝑃𝑃𝑉𝑃Dto PP,V!P decays have been accumulated in the past few years with substantially improved precisioncitebib.bib30. For example, the measurements of all D0→P​P→superscript𝐷0𝑃𝑃D0to PP channels are available except for three of the DCS modes. Hence, in principle, one can estimate yP​Psubscript𝑦𝑃𝑃yPP directly from the data. The DCS decays are related to the CF ones through the relations such as ℬ​(D0→K0​η(′))=tan4⁡θC​ℬ​(D0→K¯0​η(′))cal B(D0to K0eta(prime))=tan4thetaCcal B(D0tooverlineK0eta(prime)), where θCsubscript𝜃𝐶thetaC is the Cabibbo angle. However, we have applied the TDA in Ref. citebib.bib24 to estimate the D𝐷D mixing parameters for the following reasons: (i) to predict the branching fractions of yet-to-be-measured SCS and DCS modes in the D→V​P→𝐷𝑉𝑃Dto V!P decays, (ii) to understand SU breaking effects in the SCS and DCS modes, (iii) to see explicitly the vanishing mixing parameters in the SU limit, and (iv) to reduce the uncertainties in the estimate of the parameter y𝑦y. A similar study based on the so-called factorization-assisted topological-amplitude (FAT) approach has been carried out in Ref. citebib.bib26. The layout of the present paper is as follows. The analysis of D→P​P→𝐷𝑃𝑃Dto P!P decays within the framework of TDA is presented in Sec. for ϕ=40.4∘italic-ϕsuperscript40.4phi=40.4circ and 43.5∘superscript43.543.5circ with ϕitalic-ϕphi being the η−η′𝜂superscript𝜂′eta-etaprime mixing angle. As for D→V​P→𝐷𝑉𝑃Dto V!P decays, its analysis is much more complicated. We shall discuss the CF, SCS and DCS decays separately and consider the KS0−KL0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0KS0-KL0 asymmetries in D→KS,L0​V→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆𝐿0𝑉Dto KS,L0V channels in Sec. . Sec. is devoted to the discussions of D→V​V→𝐷𝑉𝑉Dto V!V decays. We evaluate the D0superscript𝐷0D0-D¯0superscript¯𝐷0overlineD0 mixing parameter y𝑦y in Sec. with a focus on the contributions from the P​P𝑃𝑃P!P and V​P𝑉𝑃V!P sectors. Sec. comes to our conclusions.
content_sections.II D→P​P→𝐷𝑃𝑃D\\to P\\!P decays II.0.1 Ca: It was established some time ago that a least model-dependent analysis of heavy meson decays could be carried out in the TDAcitebib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib33. For the present purposes, it suffices to consider tree amplitudes: color-allowed tree amplitude T𝑇T; color-suppressed tree amplitude C𝐶C; W𝑊W-exchange amplitude E𝐸E and W𝑊W-annihilation amplitude A𝐴A. The topological amplitudes for CF D→P​P→𝐷𝑃𝑃Dto P!P decayscitebib.bib30 are shown in Table where ϕitalic-ϕphi is the η−η′𝜂superscript𝜂′eta-etaprime mixing angle defined in the flavor basis (ηη′)=(cos⁡ϕ−sin⁡ϕsin⁡ϕcos⁡ϕ)​(ηqηs),matrix𝜂superscript𝜂′matrixitalic-ϕitalic-ϕitalic-ϕitalic-ϕmatrixsubscript𝜂𝑞subscript𝜂𝑠displaystylebeginpmatrixetacretaprimeendpmatrix=beginpmatrixcosphi-sinphicrsinphicosphicrendpmatrixbeginpmatrixetaqcretasendpmatrix, with ηq=12​(u​u¯+d​d¯)subscript𝜂𝑞12𝑢¯𝑢𝑑¯𝑑etaq=1oversqrt2(ubaru+dbard) and ηs=s​s¯subscript𝜂𝑠𝑠¯𝑠etas=sbars. For the η−η′𝜂superscript𝜂′eta-etaprime mixing angle, an early study gave ϕ=(39.3±1.0)∘italic-ϕsuperscriptplus-or-minus39.31.0phi=(39.3pm 1.0)circcitebib.bib34. This mixing angle has been measured by KLOE to be ϕ=(40.4±0.6)∘italic-ϕsuperscriptplus-or-minus40.40.6phi=(40.4pm 0.6)circcitebib.bib35. We have previously followed the LHCb measurement of ϕ=(43.5−1.3+1.4)∘italic-ϕsuperscriptsubscriptsuperscript43.51.41.3phi=(43.5+1.4-1.3)circcitebib.bib36 to fix ϕitalic-ϕphi to be 43.5∘superscript43.543.5circ. Recent precision measurements of Ds+→η(′)​e+​νeDs+toeta(prime)e+nue and Ds+→η(′)​μ+​νμDs+toeta(prime)mu+numu by BESIII yield ϕ=(40.0±2.0±0.6)∘italic-ϕsuperscriptplus-or-minus40.02.00.6phi=(40.0pm 2.0pm 0.6)circcitebib.bib37 and ϕ=(40.2±2.1±0.7)∘italic-ϕsuperscriptplus-or-minus40.22.10.7phi=(40.2pm 2.1pm 0.7)circcitebib.bib38, respectively. In this work, we will study the cases for ϕ=40.4∘italic-ϕsuperscript40.4phi=40.4circ and 43.5∘superscript43.543.5circ. Though these two choices differ by only a few degrees, they do produce observable differences in fitting the data. We shall see that the former mixing angle is preferred by the D→P​P→𝐷𝑃𝑃Dto P!P data, whereas the latter is slightly favored by the D→V​P→𝐷𝑉𝑃Dto V!P data. The topological amplitudes for the Cabibbo-favored D→P​P→𝐷𝑃𝑃Dto P!P decayscitebib.bib30 are shown in Table. For the CF decay modes involving a neutral kaon KS0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0KS0 or KL0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0KL0, it was customary to use the relation Γ​(K¯0)=2​Γ​(KS0)Γsuperscript¯𝐾02Γsuperscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0Gamma(overlineK0)=2Gamma(KS0). However, this relation can be invalidated by the interference between CF and DCS amplitudes. Using the phase convention that KS0=12​(K0−K¯0)superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆012superscript𝐾0superscript¯𝐾0KS0=1oversqrt2(K0-overlineK0) and KL0=12​(K0+K¯0)superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿012superscript𝐾0superscript¯𝐾0KL0=1oversqrt2(K0+overlineK0) in the absence of C​P𝐶𝑃C!P violation, we have A​(D→KS0​M)=−12​[A​(D→K¯0​M)−A​(D→K0​M)],A​(D→KL0​M)=12​[A​(D→K¯0​M)+A​(D→K0​M)],formulae-sequence𝐴→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0𝑀12delimited-𝐴→𝐷superscript¯𝐾0𝑀𝐴→𝐷superscript𝐾0𝑀𝐴→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0𝑀12delimited-𝐴→𝐷superscript¯𝐾0𝑀𝐴→𝐷superscript𝐾0𝑀displaystylebeginsplitA(Dto KS0M)=-1oversqrt2[A(DtooverlineK0M)-A(Dto K0M)], A(Dto KL0M)=1oversqrt2[A(DtooverlineK0M)+A(Dto K0M)],endsplit where M=P𝑀𝑃M=P or V𝑉V. Consequently, ℬ​(D→KS0​M)+ℬ​(D→KL0​M)=ℬ​(D→K¯0​M)+ℬ​(D→K0​M)ℬ→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0𝑀ℬ→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0𝑀ℬ→𝐷superscript¯𝐾0𝑀ℬ→𝐷superscript𝐾0𝑀cal B(Dto KS0M)+cal B(Dto KL0M)=cal B(DtooverlineK0M)+cal B(Dto K0M). Hence, we shall use ℬ​(D→K¯0​M)≅ℬ​(D→KS0​M)+ℬ​(D→KL0​M)ℬ→𝐷superscript¯𝐾0𝑀ℬ→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0𝑀ℬ→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0𝑀cal B(DtooverlineK0M)congcal B(Dto KS0M)+cal B(Dto KL0M) which is valid to a good approximation. For example, using the measured branching fractions of D0→KS,L0​π0→superscript𝐷0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆𝐿0superscript𝜋0D0to KS,L0pi0 given in Table below, we shall get ℬ​(D0→K¯0​π0)=(2.311±0.036)%ℬ→superscript𝐷0superscript¯𝐾0superscript𝜋0percentplus-or-minus2.3110.036cal B(D0tooverlineK0pi0)=(2.311pm 0.036)%. Of course, one can also perform a fit to the data of D→KS0​P→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0𝑃Dto KS0P and/or D→KL0​P→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0𝑃Dto KL0P instead of D→K¯0​P→𝐷superscript¯𝐾0𝑃DtooverlineK0P, assuming that the DCS (double-primed) amplitudes are the same as the CF (unprimed) ones. We shall see later that it is more convenient to consider the CF D0→K¯0​P→superscript𝐷0superscript¯𝐾0𝑃D0tooverlineK0P and DCS D0→K0​P→superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾0𝑃D0to K0P decays in order to compute the D0superscript𝐷0D0-D¯0superscript¯𝐷0overlineD0 mixing parameter y𝑦y. It is clear from Table that we have 8 data points for 7 unknown parameters. Hence, the topological amplitudes T𝑇T, C𝐶C, E𝐸E and A𝐴A can be extracted from the CF D→P​P→𝐷𝑃𝑃Dto P!P decays through a χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 fit, as shown in Table for ϕ=40.4∘italic-ϕsuperscript40.4phi=40.4circ and 43.5∘superscript43.543.5circ, respectively. The fitted χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 value almost vanishes with the fit quality of 99.6%percent99.699.6% for ϕ=40.4∘italic-ϕsuperscript40.4phi=40.4circ and is 1.46 per degree of freedom with the fit quality of 22.7% for ϕ=43.5∘italic-ϕsuperscript43.5phi=43.5circ. Previous fits obtained in 2010citebib.bib1 and 2019citebib.bib5 are also listed in Table for comparison. We see that the errors in T𝑇T, C𝐶C, E𝐸E, and A𝐴A are substantially reduced, especially for the annihilation amplitude A𝐴A, thanks to the improved data precision from the Particle Data Group (PDG)citebib.bib30. We have noticed beforecitebib.bib5 that since we fit only the observed branching fractions, the results will be the same if all the strong phases are subject to a simultaneous sign flip, resulting in a two-fold ambiguity. Throughout this paper, we only present one of them. Presumably, such a degeneracy in strong phases can be resolved by measurements of sufficiently many CPasymmetries. For example, a measurement of direct CPasymmetry in D0→KS0​KS0→superscript𝐷0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0D0to KS0KS0 will allow us to resolve the discrete phase ambiguitycitebib.bib5. We see in Table that the topological amplitudes respect the hierarchical pattern |T|>|C|>|E|>|A|𝑇𝐶𝐸𝐴|T|>|C|>|E|>|A|. The phase between C𝐶C and T𝑇T is 150∘superscript150150circ, not far from the expectation of 180∘superscript180180circ from naïve factorization. The W𝑊W-exchange amplitude E𝐸E is sizable with a large phase of order 120∘superscript120120circ. This implies the importance of 1/mc1subscript𝑚𝑐1/mc power corrections as the short-distance contributions to E𝐸E are helicity suppressed. Notice that the W𝑊W-annihilation amplitude is smaller than the W𝑊W-exchange amplitude. Under naïve factorization, all the predicted topological amplitudes except T𝑇T are too small compared to the values extracted from the data, implying that topological amplitudes C𝐶C, E𝐸E and A𝐴A are dominated by long-distance, nonfactorizable effects.
content_sections.II D→P​P→𝐷𝑃𝑃D\\to P\\!P decays II.0.2 Si: We follow the conventional practice to denote the primed amplitudes for SCS modes and double-primed amplitudes for DCS decays. In the flavor SU limit, primed and unprimed amplitudes should be the same. It is known that there exists significant SU breaking in some of the SCS modes from the symmetry limit. For example, the rate of D0→K+​K−→superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾superscript𝐾D0to K+K- is larger than that of D0→π+​π−→superscript𝐷0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋D0topi+pi- by a factor of 2.82.82.8citebib.bib30, while the magnitudes of their decay amplitudes should be the same in the SU limit. The observation of the D0→K0​K¯0→superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾0superscript¯𝐾0D0to K0overlineK0 decay indicates that SU symmetry must also be broken in the topological amplitude E𝐸E. Indeed, as explained in detail in Ref. citebib.bib5, the large rate disparity between K+​K−superscript𝐾superscript𝐾K+K- and π+​π−superscript𝜋superscript𝜋pi+pi- cannot rely solely on the nominal SU breaking in the tree or W𝑊W-exchange amplitude. SU-breaking effects in the topological amplitudes T′superscript𝑇′Tprime and C′superscript𝐶′Cprime can be estimated in the factorization approach as the topological unprimed amplitudes extracted from the CF D→K¯​π→𝐷¯𝐾𝜋DtooverlineKpi decays have the expressions T=GF2​a1​(K​π)​fπ​(mD2−mK2)​F0D​K​(mπ2),C=GF2​a2​(K​π)​fK​(mD2−mπ2)​F0D​π​(mK2). formulae-sequence𝑇subscript𝐺𝐹2subscript𝑎1𝐾𝜋subscript𝑓𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑚𝐷2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝐾2superscriptsubscript𝐹0𝐷𝐾superscriptsubscript𝑚𝜋2𝐶subscript𝐺𝐹2subscript𝑎2𝐾𝜋subscript𝑓𝐾superscriptsubscript𝑚𝐷2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝜋2superscriptsubscript𝐹0𝐷𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑚𝐾2displaystylebeginsplitT=GFoversqrt2a1(Kpi),fpi(mD2-mK2)F0DK(mpi2), C=GFoversqrt2a2(Kpi),fK(mD2-mpi2)F0Dpi(mK2). endsplit SU breaking effects in the T′superscript𝑇′Tprime and C′superscript𝐶′Cprime amplitudes of SCS modes are then addressed by comparing them with the factorizable amplitudes given by Eq. citebib.bib3. For example, we found |TK​K/T|=1.269subscript𝑇𝐾𝐾𝑇1.269|TK!K/T|=1.269 and |Tπ​π/T|=0.964subscript𝑇𝜋𝜋𝑇0.964|Tpipi/T|=0.964 in Ref. citebib.bib5. We can fix the SU breaking effects in the W𝑊W-exchange amplitudes from the following four D0superscript𝐷0D0 decay modes, K+​K−superscript𝐾superscript𝐾K+K-, π+​π−superscript𝜋superscript𝜋pi+pi-, π0​π0superscript𝜋0superscript𝜋0pi0pi0 and K0​K¯0superscript𝐾0superscript¯𝐾0K0overlineK0: A​(D0→π+​π−)=λd​(0.96​T+Ed),A​(D0→π0​π0)=12​λd​(−0.78​C+Ed),A​(D0→K+​K−)=λs​(1.27​T+Es),A​(D0→K0​K¯0)=λd​Ed+λs​Es,formulae-sequence𝐴→superscript𝐷0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋subscript𝜆𝑑0.96𝑇subscript𝐸𝑑formulae-sequence𝐴→superscript𝐷0superscript𝜋0superscript𝜋012subscript𝜆𝑑0.78𝐶subscript𝐸𝑑formulae-sequence𝐴→superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾superscript𝐾subscript𝜆𝑠1.27𝑇subscript𝐸𝑠𝐴→superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾0superscript¯𝐾0subscript𝜆𝑑subscript𝐸𝑑subscript𝜆𝑠subscript𝐸𝑠displaystylebeginsplitA(D0topi+pi-)=lambdad(0.96T+Ed),quadA(D0topi0pi0)=1oversqrt2lambdad(-0.78C+Ed), A(D0to K+K-)=lambdas(1.27T+Es),quadA(D0to K0overlineK0)=lambdadEd+lambdasEs,endsplit where λq≡Vc​q∗​Vu​qsubscript𝜆𝑞subscriptsuperscript𝑉𝑐𝑞subscript𝑉𝑢𝑞lambdaqequiv V*cqVuq with Vq​q′subscript𝑉𝑞superscript𝑞′Vqqprime denoting the q​q′𝑞superscript𝑞′qqprime element of the Cabibbo-Kobayashi-Maskawa matrix, and Eqsubscript𝐸𝑞Eq refers to the W𝑊W-exchange amplitude associated with c​u¯→q​q¯→𝑐¯𝑢𝑞¯𝑞cbaruto qbarq (q=d,s𝑞𝑑𝑠q=d,s). A fit to the data (see Table) yields two possible solutionscitebib.bib5 I:Ed=1.244​ei​13.7∘​E,Es=0.823​e−i​17.9∘​E;II:Ed=1.244​ei​13.7∘​E,Es=1.548​e−i​12.3∘​E,displaystylebeginsplitrm I:Ed=1.244,ei13.7circE,qquad Es=0.823,e-i17.9circE; rm II:Ed=1.244,ei13.7circE,qquad Es=1.548,e-i12.3circE,endsplit for ϕ=40.4∘italic-ϕsuperscript40.4phi=40.4circ and I:Ed=1.325​ei​12.9∘​E,Es=0.795​e−i​17.7∘​E;II:Ed=1.325​ei​12.9∘​E,Es=1.665​e−i​13.5∘​E,displaystylebeginsplitrm I:Ed=1.325,ei12.9circE,qquad Es=0.795,e-i17.7circE; rm II:Ed=1.325,ei12.9circE,qquad Es=1.665,e-i13.5circE,endsplit for ϕ=43.5∘italic-ϕsuperscript43.5phi=43.5circ. In the P​P𝑃𝑃P!P sector, we thus need SU breaking in the W𝑊W-exchange diagrams in order to induce the observed D0→KS0​KS0→superscript𝐷0subscriptsuperscript𝐾0𝑆subscriptsuperscript𝐾0𝑆D0to K0SK0S decay and explain the large rate difference between the D0→K+​K−→superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾superscript𝐾D0to K+K- and D0→π+​π−→superscript𝐷0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋D0topi+pi- decays. Since the W𝑊W-exchange and W𝑊W-annihilation amplitudes are mainly governed by long-distance physics, their SU breaking effects are obtained by fitting to the data, see Eq. . Topological amplitudes for the SCS D→P​P→𝐷𝑃𝑃Dto PP decays including perturbative SU breaking effects in the T𝑇T and C𝐶C amplitudes and nonperturbative SU breaking in the E𝐸E amplitude are summarized in TableI of Ref. citebib.bib5. 1In order to discuss CPviolation in charmed meson decays, we have included QCD penguin, penguin exchange and penguin annihilation in TableI of Ref. citebib.bib5 which can be neglected in the present study. The measured and fitted branching fractions are shown in Table for the CF and SCS D→P​P→𝐷𝑃𝑃Dto PP decays and in Table for the DCS decays.
content_sections.II D→P​P→𝐷𝑃𝑃D\\to P\\!P decays II.0.3 KS: Assuming that the double-primed amplitudes in the DCS sector are the same as that in the CF one, the calculated D→KS,L0​P→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆𝐿0𝑃Dto KS,L0P decays and their asymmetries defined by R​(D,P)≡Γ​(D→KS0​P)−Γ​(D→KL0​P)Γ​(D→KS0​P)+Γ​(D→KL0​P)𝑅𝐷𝑃Γ→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0𝑃Γ→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0𝑃Γ→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0𝑃Γ→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0𝑃displaystyle R(D,P)equivGamma(Dto KS0P)-Gamma(Dto KL0P)overGamma(Dto KS0P)+Gamma(Dto KL0P) are summarized in Table. It is expected that D0→K¯0​(π0,η,η′)→superscript𝐷0superscript¯𝐾0superscript𝜋0𝜂superscript𝜂′D0tooverlineK0(pi0,eta,etaprime) and D0→K0​(π0,η,η′)→superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾0superscript𝜋0𝜂superscript𝜂′D0to K0(pi0,eta,etaprime) contribute constructively to D0→KS0​(π0,η,η′)→superscript𝐷0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0superscript𝜋0𝜂superscript𝜂′D0to KS0(pi0,eta,etaprime) and destructively to D0→KL0​(π0,η,η′)→superscript𝐷0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0superscript𝜋0𝜂superscript𝜂′D0to KL0(pi0,eta,etaprime) and hence, R​(D0,(π0,η,η′))=2​tan2⁡θC=0.107. 𝑅superscript𝐷0superscript𝜋0𝜂superscript𝜂′2superscript2subscript𝜃𝐶0.107displaystyle R(D0,(pi0,eta,etaprime))=2tan2thetaC=0.107,. This prediction is in agreement with CLEO for R​(D0,π0)𝑅superscript𝐷0superscript𝜋0R(D0,pi0)citebib.bib39 and with BESIII for R​(D0,η(′))R(D0,eta(prime))citebib.bib40. However, our prediction of R​(D+,π+)𝑅superscript𝐷superscript𝜋R(D+,pi+) is opposite to experiment in sign and the calculated R​(Ds+,K+)𝑅superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾R(Ds+,K+) is too small compared to the data, though they are consistent if errors are taken into account. This can be traced back to the relative phase between (C+A)𝐶𝐴(C+A) and (T+C)𝑇𝐶(T+C) which is 94.5∘superscript94.594.5circ. Consequently, D+→K¯0​π+→superscript𝐷superscript¯𝐾0superscript𝜋D+tooverlineK0pi+ and D+→K0​π+→superscript𝐷superscript𝐾0superscript𝜋D+to K0pi+ will contribute destructively to D+→KS0​π+→superscript𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0superscript𝜋D+to KS0pi+ and constructively to D+→KL0​π+→superscript𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0superscript𝜋D+to KL0pi+. This is opposite to the pattern observed experimentally. We find that if the phase difference is decreased slightly by 10∘superscript1010circ, that is, (C′′+A′′)→(C+A)​ei​10∘→superscript𝐶′′superscript𝐴′′𝐶𝐴superscript𝑒𝑖superscript10(Cprimeprime+Aprimeprime)to(C+A)ei10circ in D+→KS,L​π+→superscript𝐷subscript𝐾𝑆𝐿superscript𝜋D+to KS,Lpi+ and (T′′+C′′)→(T+C)​e−i​10∘→superscript𝑇′′superscript𝐶′′𝑇𝐶superscript𝑒𝑖superscript10(Tprimeprime+Cprimeprime)to(T+C)e-i10circ in Ds+→KS,L​K+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝐾𝑆𝐿superscript𝐾Ds+to KS,LK+, then we will be able to accommodate both R​(D+,π+)𝑅superscript𝐷superscript𝜋R(D+,pi+) and R​(Ds+,K+)𝑅superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾R(Ds+,K+). In the so-called factorization-assisted topological (FAT) approachcitebib.bib42, R​(D+,π+)𝑅superscript𝐷superscript𝜋R(D+,pi+) and R​(Ds+,K+)𝑅superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾R(Ds+,K+) are predicted to be 0.025±0.008plus-or-minus0.0250.0080.025pm 0.008 and 0.012±0.006plus-or-minus0.0120.0060.012pm 0.006, respectivelycitebib.bib43. While the former agrees with experiment, the latter is wrong in sign.
content_sections.III D→V​P→𝐷𝑉𝑃D\\to V\\!P decays III.0.1: For D→V​P→𝐷𝑉𝑃Dto V!P decays, there exist two different sets of topological diagrams since the spectator quark of the charmed meson may end up in the pseudoscalar or vector meson. A subscript of P𝑃P or V𝑉V is attached to the flavor amplitudes and the associated strong phases to denote respectively whether the spectator quark in the charmed meson ends up in the pseudoscalar or vector meson in the final state. As mentioned in passing, for the CF decay modes involving a neutral kaon KS0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0KS0 or KL0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0KL0, it was customary to use the relation Γ​(K¯0)=2​Γ​(KS0)Γsuperscript¯𝐾02Γsuperscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0Gamma(overlineK0)=2Gamma(KS0), but this relation can be invalidated by the interference between CF and DCS amplitudes. How do we understand the puzzles with the rates and polarizations for D0→ω​ϕ→superscript𝐷0𝜔italic-ϕD0toomegaphi and ρ0​ϕsuperscript𝜌0italic-ϕrho0phi? It is conceivable that D0→ρ0​ϕ→superscript𝐷0superscript𝜌0italic-ϕD0torho0phi receives flavor-singlet contribution Sϕsubscript𝑆italic-ϕSphi, while the contributions Sϕsubscript𝑆italic-ϕSphi and Sωsubscript𝑆𝜔Somega are essentially canceled out in D0→ω​ϕ→superscript𝐷0𝜔italic-ϕD0toomegaphi. Branching fractions of D0→V​V→superscript𝐷0𝑉𝑉D0to VV in partial waves calculated in the factorization approach are presented in Table. Since the W𝑊W-exchange contributions are neglected in naïve factorization, no estimate is made for the branching fractions of K∗0​K¯∗0superscript𝐾absent0superscript¯𝐾absent0K*0overlineK*0, K¯∗0​ϕsuperscript¯𝐾absent0italic-ϕoverlineK*0phi, K∗0​ϕsuperscript𝐾absent0italic-ϕK*0phi and ρ0​ωsuperscript𝜌0𝜔rho0omega. For the effective Wilson coefficients, we have used a1=0.90subscript𝑎10.90a1=0.90 and a2=−0.64subscript𝑎20.64a2=-0.64 . Comparing Table with the measured partial-wave rates given in Table shows that the predictions based on factorization deviate from the experimental measurements. This indicates the necessity of taking into account the non-factorizable W𝑊W-exchange contributions which might account for the D𝐷D-wave dominance observed in D0→K∗−​ρ+,K¯∗0​ρ0,ρ+​ρ−→superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾absentsuperscript𝜌superscript¯𝐾absent0superscript𝜌0superscript𝜌superscript𝜌D0to K*-rho+,overlineK*0rho0,rho+rho- and ρ0​ρ0superscript𝜌0superscript𝜌0rho0rho0 channels, an issue to be investigated in the near future. The predicted longitudinal polarization fLsubscript𝑓𝐿fL ranges from 0.31 to 0.49.
content_sections.V D0superscript𝐷0D^{0}-D¯0superscript¯𝐷0: The two-body decays D0→P​P→superscript𝐷0𝑃𝑃D0to PP and quasi-two-body decays D0→V​P,V​V,S​P,S​V,A​P,A​V→superscript𝐷0𝑉𝑃𝑉𝑉𝑆𝑃𝑆𝑉𝐴𝑃𝐴𝑉D0to V!P,V!V,SP,SV,AP,AV, T​P,T​V𝑇𝑃𝑇𝑉TP,TV account for about 3/4 of the total hadronic rates. Many of the 3-body final states arise from S​P𝑆𝑃SP, V​P𝑉𝑃V!P, and T​P𝑇𝑃T!P decays, the 4-body states from V​V𝑉𝑉V!V and A​P𝐴𝑃AP decays, and the 5-body states from A​V𝐴𝑉AV decays. The nonresonant 3-body and 4-body decays are at most 10% of the multi-body decay rates. Hence, it is arguable that these two-body and quasi-two-body channels dominate and can provide a good estimate of the mixing parameters. As mentioned in the Introduction, use of the TDA is made to reduce the uncertainties with the measured channels and estimate those modes yet to be observed. In particular, we focus on D→P​P→𝐷𝑃𝑃Dto PP and V​P𝑉𝑃V!P decays and present updated topological amplitudes. From Table, it is easily seen that the contribution yK∗,ρ=ℬ​(ρ+​ρ−)ℓ+ℬ​(K∗+​K∗−)ℓ−2​cos⁡δK∗−​ρ+​ℬ​(K∗−​ρ+)ℓ​ℬ​(K∗+​ρ−)ℓsubscript𝑦superscript𝐾𝜌ℬsubscriptsuperscript𝜌superscript𝜌ℓℬsubscriptsuperscript𝐾absentsuperscript𝐾absentℓ2subscript𝛿superscript𝐾absentsuperscript𝜌ℬsubscriptsuperscript𝐾absentsuperscript𝜌ℓℬsubscriptsuperscript𝐾absentsuperscript𝜌ℓdisplaystyle yK*,rho=cal B(rho+rho-)ell+cal B(K*+K*-)ell-2cosdeltaK*-rho+sqrtcal B(K*-rho+)ellcal B(K*+rho-)ell vanishes in the SU limit. This U𝑈U-spin relation cannot be tested by the current data as only the D0→K∗−​ρ+→superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾absentsuperscript𝜌D0to K*-rho+ and D0→ρ+​ρ−→superscript𝐷0superscript𝜌superscript𝜌D0torho+rho- decays have been measured. Notice that although D0→ϕ​ϕ→superscript𝐷0italic-ϕitalic-ϕD0tophiphi is prohibited by phase space, it is needed in order to show a vanishing yV​V,ℓsubscript𝑦𝑉𝑉ℓyVV,ell in the SU limit. An estimate using the naïve factorization results from Table yields yV​V,S=−0.167%,yV​V,P=1.61×10−4,yV​V,D=4.71×10−5. formulae-sequencesubscript𝑦𝑉𝑉𝑆percent0.167formulae-sequencesubscript𝑦𝑉𝑉𝑃1.61superscript104subscript𝑦𝑉𝑉𝐷4.71superscript105displaystyle yV!V,S=-0.167%,qquad yV!V,P=1.61times 10-4,qquad yV!V,D=4.71times 10-5. Since the predicted rates of D0→V​V→superscript𝐷0𝑉𝑉D0to VV in partial waves based on naïve factorization do not resemble the data given in Table, particularly for the D𝐷D-wave dominance in the D0→K∗−​ρ+→superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾absentsuperscript𝜌D0to K*-rho+, K¯∗0​ρ0superscript¯𝐾absent0superscript𝜌0overlineK*0rho0 , ρ+​ρ−superscript𝜌superscript𝜌rho+rho- and ρ0​ρ0superscript𝜌0superscript𝜌0rho0rho0 decays as suggested by the current data, it is premature to have a reliable estimate of yV​V,ℓsubscript𝑦𝑉𝑉ℓyV!V,ell. Indeed, if we use the data in Table as the input and employ the predictions based on naïve factorization for those modes yet to be measured, we get yV​V,S=0.0271%,yV​V,P=−0.0958%,yV​V,D=0.361%. formulae-sequencesubscript𝑦𝑉𝑉𝑆percent0.0271formulae-sequencesubscript𝑦𝑉𝑉𝑃percent0.0958subscript𝑦𝑉𝑉𝐷percent0.361displaystyle yV!V,S=0.0271%,qquad yV!V,P=-0.0958%,qquad yV!V,D=0.361%. The results obtained in Ref. citebib.bib21 in which only SU violation in the phase space is considered are given by S-wave:yV​V,8=−0.39%,yV​V,27=−0.30%,P-wave:yV​V,8=−0.48%,yV​V,27=−0.70%,D-wave:yV​V,8=2.5%,yV​V,27=2.8%. displaystylebeginsplitSmbox-wave:qquad yV!V,8=-0.39%,qquad yV!V,27=-0.30%, Pmbox-wave:qquad yV!V,8=-0.48%,qquad yV!V,27=-0.70%, Dmbox-wave:qquad yV!V,8=2.5%,qquadquad yV!V,27=2.8%. endsplit Because of the momentum dependence of the D𝐷D-wave is proportional to pc3superscriptsubscript𝑝𝑐3pc3, the D𝐷D-wave phase space is most sensitive to the SU breaking in pcsubscript𝑝𝑐pc.

There are hadronic D𝐷D decays into an even-parity meson M𝑀M and a pseudoscalar meson or a vector meson, where M𝑀M represents a scalar meson S𝑆S, an axial-vector meson A𝐴A, or a tensor meson T𝑇T. They have been studied in the literaturecitebib.bib71, bib.bib72, bib.bib73, but the data are not adequate to allow for the extraction of topological amplitudes, especially for the W𝑊W-exchange amplitudes. These will have to be left to a future investigation.
content_sections.VI Conclusions: In this work, we have presented an updated analysis of the two-body decays D→P​P→𝐷𝑃𝑃Dto PP, V​P𝑉𝑃V!P and V​V𝑉𝑉VV decays within the framework of the topological diagram approach. For the CF decay modes involving a neutral kaon, KS0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0KS0 or KL0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0KL0, the relation Γ​(K¯0)=2​Γ​(KS0)Γsuperscript¯𝐾02Γsuperscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0Gamma(overlineK0)=2Gamma(KS0) can be invalidated by the interference between the CF and DCS amplitudes. Since the topological amplitudes in DCS modes are not necessarily the same as those in CF ones beyond flavor SU symmetry, we prefer to use the good approximate relation ℬ​(D→K¯0​M)≅ℬ​(D→KS0​M)+ℬ​(D→KL0​M)ℬ→𝐷superscript¯𝐾0𝑀ℬ→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0𝑀ℬ→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0𝑀cal B(DtooverlineK0M)congcal B(Dto KS0M)+cal B(Dto KL0M) for M=P𝑀𝑃M=P and V𝑉V. In the P​P𝑃𝑃PP sector, the tree topological amplitudes T𝑇T, C𝐶C, E𝐸E and A𝐴A are extracted from CF D→P​P→𝐷𝑃𝑃Dto PP decays for the η−η′𝜂superscript𝜂′eta-etaprime mixing angle ϕ=40.4∘italic-ϕsuperscript40.4phi=40.4circ and 43.5∘superscript43.543.5circ, respectively. The fitted χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 values almost vanish with the quality close to unity for ϕ=40.4∘italic-ϕsuperscript40.4phi=40.4circ. Assuming that the double-primed amplitudes in the DCS sector are the same as the unprimed ones, the calculated KS0−KL0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0KS0-KL0 asymmetries R​(D0,P)𝑅superscript𝐷0𝑃R(D0,P) for D0→KS,L0​P→superscript𝐷0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆𝐿0𝑃D0to KS,L0P decays with P=π0𝑃superscript𝜋0P=pi0, η𝜂eta and η′superscript𝜂′etaprime agree with the experiment, meaning that D0→KS0​P→superscript𝐷0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0𝑃D0to KS0P has a rate larger than that of D0→KL0​P→superscript𝐷0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0𝑃D0to KL0P. However, our predicted R​(D+,π+)𝑅superscript𝐷superscript𝜋R(D+,pi+) is opposite to the experiment in sign and the calculated R​(Ds+,K+)𝑅superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾R(Ds+,K+) is too small compared to the data. This is ascribed to the fact that the relative phase between (C+A)𝐶𝐴(C+A) and (T+C)𝑇𝐶(T+C) is slightly larger than 90∘superscript9090circ, rendering the interference between D+→K¯0​π+→superscript𝐷superscript¯𝐾0superscript𝜋D+tooverlineK0pi+ and D+→K0​π+→superscript𝐷superscript𝐾0superscript𝜋D+to K0pi+ destructive in D+→KS0​π+→superscript𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0superscript𝜋D+to KS0pi+ and constructive in D+→KL0​π+→superscript𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0superscript𝜋D+to KL0pi+. This is opposite to the pattern observed experimentally. We find that if the phase difference is decreased slightly by 10∘superscript1010circ, we are able to accommodate both R​(D+,π+)𝑅superscript𝐷superscript𝜋R(D+,pi+) and R​(Ds+,K+)𝑅superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾R(Ds+,K+). In the V​P𝑉𝑃V!P sector, if the double-primed topological amplitudes in DCS decays are taken to be the same as the unprimed ones, we will be led to some predictions not in accordance with the experiment. That is why we prefer to apply the relation ℬ​(D→K¯0​V)≅ℬ​(D→KS0​V)+ℬ​(D→KL0​V)ℬ→𝐷superscript¯𝐾0𝑉ℬ→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0𝑉ℬ→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0𝑉cal B(DtooverlineK0V)congcal B(Dto KS0V)+cal B(Dto KL0V). Unfortunately, we have the data of ℬ​(D→KL0​V)ℬ→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0𝑉cal B(Dto KL0V) for V=ω𝑉𝜔V=omega and ϕitalic-ϕphi, but not for V=ρ0𝑉superscript𝜌0V=rho0, ρ+superscript𝜌rho+ and K∗+superscript𝐾absentK*+. Since it is most likely that EV,P′′≠EV,Psuperscriptsubscript𝐸𝑉𝑃′′subscript𝐸𝑉𝑃EV,Pprimeprimeneq EV,P, we fit the topological amplitudes to D0→K¯0​(ω,ϕ)→superscript𝐷0superscript¯𝐾0𝜔italic-ϕD0tooverlineK0(omega,phi), D+→KS0​ρ+→superscript𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0superscript𝜌D+to KS0rho+ and Ds+→KS0​K∗+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0superscript𝐾absentDs+to KS0K*+, but not D0→KS0​ρ0→superscript𝐷0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0superscript𝜌0D0to KS0rho0. A global fit to the CF modes in the V​P𝑉𝑃V!P sector gives many solutions with similarly small local minima in χ2superscript𝜒2chi2: (F1)-(F5) for ϕ=40.4∘italic-ϕsuperscript40.4phi=40.4circ and (F1’)-(F5’) for ϕ=43.5∘italic-ϕsuperscript43.5phi=43.5circ, when we restrict ourselves to χmin2<10subscriptsuperscript𝜒2min10chi2rm min<10. The solution degeneracy is lifted once we use them to predict for the SCS modes. In the end, we find that only Solutions (F4) and (F1’) can accommodate all SCS modes except that the predicted ℬ​(D+→π+​ω)ℬ→superscript𝐷superscript𝜋𝜔cal B(D+topi+omega) in (F1’) is slightly larger than the data. These two solutions have a common feature that CVsubscript𝐶𝑉CV and CPsubscript𝐶𝑃CP are close in phase in order to simultaneously explain the small ℬ​(D0→π0​ω)ℬ→superscript𝐷0superscript𝜋0𝜔cal B(D0topi0omega) and large ℬ​(D0→π0​ρ0)ℬ→superscript𝐷0superscript𝜋0superscript𝜌0cal B(D0topi0rho0). The annihilation amplitudes AVsubscript𝐴𝑉AV and APsubscript𝐴𝑃AP are more precisely determined than before because of a significantly improved new measurement of Ds+→π+​ρ0→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜋superscript𝜌0Ds+topi+rho0; they are comparable in size and similar in phase. For DCS D→V​P→𝐷𝑉𝑃Dto V!P decays, the assumption of EV,P′′=EV,Psuperscriptsubscript𝐸𝑉𝑃′′subscript𝐸𝑉𝑃EV,Pprimeprime=EV,P leads to some inconsistencies with the experiment; for example, the predicted strong phases δK∗+​π−subscript𝛿superscript𝐾absentsuperscript𝜋deltaK*+pi- and δK∗0​π0subscript𝛿superscript𝐾absent0superscript𝜋0deltaK*0pi0 are too large compared to their experimental values. The relations of EV,P′′superscriptsubscript𝐸𝑉𝑃′′EV,Pprimeprime with EV,Psubscript𝐸𝑉𝑃EV,P are given in Eqs. and , respectively. The KS0−KL0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0KS0-KL0 asymmetries in the D→KS,L0​V→𝐷superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆𝐿0𝑉Dto KS,L0V decays are shown in Table. The calculated R​(D0,ω)𝑅superscript𝐷0𝜔R(D0,omega) and R​(D0,ϕ)𝑅superscript𝐷0italic-ϕR(D0,phi) do not agree with the experiment. We conjecture that additional singlet contributions due to SU-singlet nature of ω𝜔omega and ϕitalic-ϕphi should account for the discrepancy. Thanks to BESIII, many new data on Ds+superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠Ds+ and D+superscript𝐷D+ to V​V𝑉𝑉V!V decays became available in recent years. In the meantime, several new puzzles have also emerged. For example, D0→K∗−​ρ+→superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾absentsuperscript𝜌D0to K*-rho+, K¯∗0​ρ0superscript¯𝐾absent0superscript𝜌0overlineK*0rho0, ρ+​ρ−superscript𝜌superscript𝜌rho+rho-, and ρ0​ρ0superscript𝜌0superscript𝜌0rho0rho0 seem to be dominated by the D𝐷D-wave, while Ds+→K∗0​ρ+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾absent0superscript𝜌Ds+to K*0rho+ and K∗−​ρ0superscript𝐾absentsuperscript𝜌0K*-rho0 dominated by the P𝑃P-wave, contrary to the naïve expectation of S𝑆S-wave dominance. In spite of the progresses in the field, the data are still not adequate to allow for a meaningful extraction of helicity or partial-wave amplitudes, especially for the W𝑊W-exchange and W𝑊W-annihilation ones. Yet another goal of this analysis is to evaluate the D0superscript𝐷0D0-D¯0superscript¯𝐷0overlineD0 mixing parameter y𝑦y using the exclusive approach through the 2-body decays of the D0superscript𝐷0D0 meson. The mixing parameter is usually small owing to large cancellation between the SCS terms and the interference of CF and DCS terms. As the topological amplitude analysis is available for D→P​P→𝐷𝑃𝑃Dto PP and V​P𝑉𝑃V!P decays, we are able to estimate yP​Psubscript𝑦𝑃𝑃yP!P and yV​Psubscript𝑦𝑉𝑃yV!P more reliably. We conclude that yP​P∼(0.110±0.011)%similar-tosubscript𝑦𝑃𝑃percentplus-or-minus0.1100.011yP!Psim(0.110pm 0.011)% and the lower bound on yV​Psubscript𝑦𝑉𝑃yV!P is (0.220±0.071)%percentplus-or-minus0.2200.071(0.220pm 0.071)%. It is thus conceivable that at least half of the D0superscript𝐷0D0-D¯0superscript¯𝐷0overlineD0 mixing parameter y𝑦y is accounted for by the P​P𝑃𝑃PP and V​P𝑉𝑃V!P modes. The main uncertainties arise from the DCS channels yet to be measured and the phase factors cos⁡δnsubscript𝛿𝑛cosdeltan’s.

all_papers.title:
Analysis of three-body decays $B \to D (V \to ) PP $ under the
  factorization-assisted topological-amplitude approach
all_papers.abstract:
Motived by the accumulated experimental results on three-body charmed $B$
decays with resonance contributions in Babar, LHCb and Belle (II), we
systematically analyze $B_{(s)} \to D_{(s)} (V \to) P_1 P_2 $ decays with $V$
representing a vector resonance ($\rho, K^*, \omega$ or $\phi$) and $P_{1,2}$
as a light pseudoscalar meson (pion or kaon). The intermediate subprocesses
$B_{(s)} \to D_{(s)} V$ are calculated with the factorization-assisted
topological-amplitude (FAT) approach and the intermediate resonant states $V$
described by the relativistic Breit-Wigner distribution successively decay to
$P_1 P_2$ via strong interaction. Taking all lowest resonance states ($\rho,
K^*, \omega, \phi$) into account, we calculate the branching fractions of these
decay modes as well as the Breit-Wigner-tail effects for $B_{(s)} \to D_{(s)}
(\rho ,\omega \to) KK$. Our results agree with the data by Babar, LHCb and
Belle (II). Among the predictions that are still not observed, there are some
branching ratios of order $10^{-6}-10^{-4}$ which are hopeful to be measured by
LHCb and Belle II. Our approach and the perturbative QCD approach (PQCD) adopt
the compatible theme to deal with the resonance contributions. What's more, our
data for the intermediate two-body charmed $B$-meson decays in FAT approach are
more precise. As a result, our results for branching fractions have smaller
uncertainties, especially for color-suppressed emission diagram dominated
modes.

qualitative: The factorization-assisted topological-amplitude (FAT) approach is employed to systematically analyze three-body $B_{(s)} \to D_{(s)} (V \to) P_1 P_2$ decays, where $V$ denotes a vector resonance ($\rho, K^*, \omega, \phi$) and $P_{1,2}$ are light pseudoscalar mesons (pions or kaons).
The intermediate subprocess $B_{(s)} \to D_{(s)} V$ is calculated using the FAT approach, which incorporates topological amplitude decomposition and factorization assumptions.
The resonant vector states $V$ are described by relativistic Breit-Wigner distributions, and their subsequent decays to $P_1 P_2$ are governed by strong interactions.
The analysis includes all lowest-lying vector resonances ($\rho, K^*, \omega, \phi$) and accounts for Breit-Wigner tail effects, particularly in $B_{(s)} \to D_{(s)} (\rho, \omega \to) KK$ decays.
The FAT approach provides more precise results for intermediate two-body charmed $B$-meson decays compared to other methods, leading to smaller uncertainties in branching fraction predictions.
The FAT approach and the perturbative QCD (PQCD) approach share a compatible framework for handling resonance contributions in three-body decays.
The predictions for branching fractions in unobserved modes are in the range $10^{-6} - 10^{-4}$, which are within reach of future measurements by LHCb and Belle II.
Color-suppressed emission diagram-dominated modes benefit significantly from the reduced uncertainties in the FAT approach due to improved precision in the underlying two-body decay amplitudes.
quantitative: Branching fractions for $B_{(s)} \to D_{(s)} (V \to) P_1 P_2$ decays are calculated with the FAT approach, showing agreement with experimental data from Babar, LHCb, and Belle (II).
Predicted branching ratios for unobserved modes are in the range $\mathcal{B} \sim 10^{-6} - 10^{-4}$.
The relativistic Breit-Wigner distribution is used to model resonant states: $\rho, K^*, \omega, \phi$, with their decay widths and masses taken from experimental inputs.
The FAT approach yields smaller uncertainties in branching fractions, especially for color-suppressed emission diagram-dominated modes, due to more precise input amplitudes.
The Breit-Wigner-tail effects are explicitly included in the analysis of $B_{(s)} \to D_{(s)} (\rho, \omega \to) KK$ decays, contributing to the shape of the invariant mass distributions.

content_sections.I Introduction: Three-body nonleptonic B𝐵B meson decays not only are important for the study of common topics of nonleptonic B𝐵B meson decays, such as testing the standard model (SM), the studying the mechanism of C​P𝐶𝑃CP violation and the emergence of quantum chromodynamics, but also provide opportunities for the analysis on the hadron spectroscopy. Specifically, three-body B𝐵B meson decays have nontrivial kinematics and phase space distributions, which are usually described in terms of three two-body invariant mass squared combinations and two of them constitute two axes to form a Dalitz plot. In the edges of the Dalitz plot, the invariant mass squared combinations of two final-state particles will generally peak as resonances, which indicates that intermediate resonances in three-body B𝐵B meson decays show up, and we are able to study the properties of these resonances through three-body B𝐵B meson decays. The Dalitz plot technique has proven to be a powerful tool to analyze the hadron spectroscopy and is widely adopted by experiments. The informations on various resonance substructures including the mass, spin-parity quantum numbers, etc. have been collected by Babar, Belle (II) and LHCb citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8. Simultaneously, usually under the framework of isobar model citebib.bib9, these collaborations have also measured fit fractions of each resonance and nonresonance components. In the isobar model, the total decay amplitude can be expressed as a coherent sum of amplitudes of different resonant and nonresonant intermediate processes, where the relativistic Breit-Wigner (RBW) model usually describe resonant dynamics and exponential distribution for nonresonant terms. It is a very good approximation to adopt the RBW function for narrow width resonances which can be well separated from any other resonant or nonresonant components in the same partial wave, so that the three-body decays with narrow intermediate states, such as ρ,K∗,ω,ϕ𝜌superscript𝐾𝜔italic-ϕrho,K*,omega,phi, have been precisely measured by experiments citebib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib10, bib.bib4, bib.bib11, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib12. On the theoretical side, analysis on the nonresonant contributions of three-body B𝐵B meson decays are in an early stage of development. Approaches or models such as the heavy meson chiral perturbation theory citebib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15 and a model combing the heavy quark effective theory and chiral Lagrangian citebib.bib16 have been applied for calculating the nonresonant fraction of three-body charmless B𝐵B meson decays, such as B→K​K​K,B→K​π​πformulae-sequence→𝐵𝐾𝐾𝐾→𝐵𝐾𝜋𝜋Bto KKK,,Bto Kpipi which are dominated by nonresonant contribution citebib.bib17. More theoretical interest is concentrated on the resonant component of three-body B𝐵B decays, where two of the three final particles are produced from a resonance and recoil against the third meson called a “bachelor” meson. This type of three-body decay is also called quasi-two-body decay. Because of the large energy release in B𝐵B meson decays, the two meson pair moves fast antiparrallelly to the bachelor meson in the B𝐵B meson rest frame. Therefore, the interactions between the meson pair and the bachelor particle are power suppressed naturally which is similar to the statement of “color transparency” in two-body B𝐵B meson decays. Then approaches based on the factorization hypothesis have been proposed for calculating the quasi-two-body decays, such as the QCD Factorization (QCDF) citebib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib18, the PQCD approachcitebib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35 and factorization-assisted topological-amplitude (FAT) approach citebib.bib36, bib.bib37. In this work, we focus on three-body charmed B𝐵B decays B(s)→D(s)​P1​P2→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2B(s)to D(s)P1P2, where P1,2subscript𝑃12P1,2 represents a pion or kaon. Different from charmless decays, intermediate resonances in B(s)→D(s)​P1​P2→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2B(s)to D(s)P1P2 decays are expected to appear in the m2​(D​P1)superscript𝑚2𝐷subscript𝑃1m2(DP1) and m2​(P1​P2)superscript𝑚2subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2m2(P1P2) combinations, thus more resonances, such as charmed states D∗superscript𝐷D* and light vector or scalar resonances, can be researched simultaneously in one Dalitz plot. In addition, they provide opportunities for studies of CP violations. In particular, the Dalitz plot analysis of B0→D​K+​π−→superscript𝐵0𝐷superscript𝐾superscript𝜋B0to DK+pi- can be used as a channel to measure the unitarity triangle angle γ𝛾gamma citebib.bib38, bib.bib39 and B0→D¯0​π+​π−→superscript𝐵0superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋B0tobarD0pi+pi- is sensitive to the β𝛽beta angle citebib.bib40, bib.bib41. Therefore, much attention has been already paid to B(s)→D(s)​P1​P2→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2B(s)to D(s)P1P2 decays in experiments and theoretical calculations. LHCb Collaborations have investigated structures of ground and excited states of D∗superscript𝐷D*, K∗superscript𝐾K* with their corresponding fit fractions through B(s)→D(s)​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠𝐾𝜋B(s)to D(s)Kpi citebib.bib10, bib.bib4, and D∗superscript𝐷D*, ρ𝜌rho in B(s)→D(s)​π​π→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠𝜋𝜋B(s)to D(s)pipi by Babar and Belle citebib.bib1, bib.bib3. Recently, the virtual contribution from ρ𝜌rho in B(s)→D(s)​K​K→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠𝐾𝐾B(s)to D(s)KK has been measured by Belle II citebib.bib8. Motived by the experimental progress on B(s)→D(s)​P1​P2→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2B(s)to D(s)P1P2 decays, theoretical calculation on the branching fractions of various types of charmed quasi-two-body decays, B(s)→D(s)​π​π→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠𝜋𝜋B(s)to D(s)pipi, B(s)→D(s)​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠𝐾𝜋B(s)to D(s)Kpi and B(s)→D(s)​K​K→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠𝐾𝐾B(s)to D(s)KK with intermediate resonances D∗superscript𝐷D*, ρ,K∗,ϕ𝜌superscript𝐾italic-ϕrho,K*,phi have been completed in a series of works with the PQCD approach citebib.bib42, bib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib45, bib.bib46, bib.bib47. In FAT approach, we have done a systematic research on B(s)→D(s)​P1​P2→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2B(s)to D(s)P1P2 with ground charmed mesons D∗superscript𝐷D* as the intermediate states and P1,P2subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2P1,P2 representing π𝜋pi or K𝐾K citebib.bib36. The results of B→D∗​P2→D​P1​P2→𝐵superscript𝐷subscript𝑃2→𝐷subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2Bto D*P2to DP1P2 in FAT approach are in better agreement with experimental data and more precise than the PQCD approach’s predictions. FAT approach is firstly proposed to resolve the problem about nonfactorizable contributions in two-body D𝐷D and B𝐵B meson decays citebib.bib48, bib.bib49, bib.bib50, bib.bib51, bib.bib52, bib.bib53, bib.bib36, bib.bib54 and then has been successfully generalized to quasi-two-body B𝐵B meson decays citebib.bib36, bib.bib37. It is based on the framework of conventional topological diagram approach, which is used to classify the decay amplitudes by different electroweak Feynman diagrams, but keeping S​U​𝑆𝑈3SU breaking effects. Only a few unknown nonfactorization parameters need to be fitted globally with all experimental data. Therefore, FAT approach is able to provide the most precise decay amplitudes of (intermediate) two-body B𝐵B meson decays especially with charmed meson final states. Then in a quasi-two-body B𝐵B meson decay the intermediate resonances successively decay to final meson pairs via strong interaction, which are described in terms of the usual RBW formalism as what’s done in experiments. Actually, in the PQCD approach the light cone distribution amplitude (LCDA) of a meson pair originating from a P-wave resonance can be expressed as time-like form factors and then is also parameterized by the RBW distribution. So their framework of dealing with the resonances is compatible with that in FAT. More details about this can be found in citebib.bib47. Therefore, the main difference between the FAT and the PQCD approach in quasi-two-body decays is how to calculate the intermediate two-body weak decays. It is well known that large nonperturbative contributions and power corrections expanded in mc/mbsubscript𝑚𝑐subscript𝑚𝑏mc/mb of color suppressed and W-exchange diagrams in charmed B𝐵B decays have not been able to be calculated in PQCD approachcitebib.bib55, which results in large uncertainties of PQCD approach’s predictions for B→D​P1​P2→𝐵𝐷subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2Bto DP1P2 with resonances D∗superscript𝐷D*, ρ,K∗,ϕ𝜌superscript𝐾italic-ϕrho,K*,phi. In this paper we will apply FAT approach to study the B(s)→D(s)​P1​P2→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2B(s)to D(s)P1P2 decay with the ground state light vector intermediate resonances ρ,K∗,ϕ,ω𝜌superscript𝐾italic-ϕ𝜔rho,K*,phi,omega, which are generally the largest components and their fit fractions have been well measured separately from any other vector resonances in LHCb and Belle II citebib.bib1, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8. This paper is organized as follows. In Sec. , the theoretical framework is introduced. The numerical results and discussions about B(s)→D(s)(ρ→)ππB(s)to D(s)(rhoto)pipi, B(s)→D(s)(K∗→)KπB(s)to D(s)(K*to)Kpi, B(s)→D(s)(ϕ→)KK¯B(s)to D(s)(phito)KbarK and B(s)→D(s)(ρ,ω→)KK¯B(s)to D(s)(rho,omegato)KbarK are collected in Sec. . Finally, a summary is given in Section .
content_sections.II FACTORIZATION OF AMPLITUDES FOR TOPOL: The charmed quasi-two-body decay B(s)→D(s)(V→)P1P2B(s)to D(s)(Vto)P1P2 happens through two subprocesses, where the D(s)subscript𝐷𝑠D(s) meson represents D(s)subscript𝐷𝑠D(s) or its antiparticle D¯(s)subscript¯𝐷𝑠barD(s). B(s)subscript𝐵𝑠B(s) meson decays to an intermediate resonant state D(s)​Vsubscript𝐷𝑠𝑉D(s)V firstly, and subsequently the unstable resonance decays to a pair of light psudoscaler, V→P1​P2→𝑉subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2Vto P1P2. The first subprocess at quark level is induced by weak transitions b→c​q​u¯​(q=d,s)→𝑏𝑐𝑞¯𝑢𝑞𝑑𝑠brightarrow,c,q,baru,,(q=d,s) and b→u​q​c¯​(q=d,s)→𝑏𝑢𝑞¯𝑐𝑞𝑑𝑠brightarrow,u,q,barc,,(q=d,s) for D(s)subscript𝐷𝑠D(s) and D¯(s)subscript¯𝐷𝑠barD(s) final states, respectively. The secondary one proceeds directly by strong interaction. According to the topological structures of b→c​q​u¯→𝑏𝑐𝑞¯𝑢brightarrow,c,q,baru, the diagrams contributing to B¯(s)→D(s)(V→)P1P2barB(s)to D(s)(Vto)P1P2 can be classified into three types as listed in Fig. , a) color-favored emission diagram T𝑇T, b) color-suppressed emission diagram C𝐶C, and c) W𝑊W-exchange diagram E𝐸E. Similarly, besides T,C,E𝑇𝐶𝐸T,,C,,E diagrams, the topologies of B¯(s)→D¯(s)(V→)P1P2barB(s)tobarD(s)(Vto)P1P2 induced by b→u​q​c¯→𝑏𝑢𝑞¯𝑐brightarrow,u,q,barc transitions include an additional W-annihilation diagram A𝐴A as shown in Fig. . The amplitudes of the first subprocess B¯(s)→D(s)​V→subscript¯𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠𝑉barB(s)to D(s)V can be refered to the ones of two-body charmed B𝐵B decays in FAT approach citebib.bib50. Factorization has been proven in T𝑇T topology at high precision. However, large nonfactorizable contributions have been found in C𝐶C and E𝐸E. As done in citebib.bib50, we parameterize matrix elements of the nonfactorizable diagrams C𝐶C and E𝐸E in FAT approach and a well proven factorization formula for T𝑇T, which can be expressed as follows, TD​V=2​GF​Vc​b​Vu​q∗​a1​(μ)​fV​mV​F1B→D​(mV2)​(εV∗⋅pB),CD​V=2​GF​Vc​b​Vu​q∗​fD(s)​mV​A0B→V​(mD2)​(εV∗⋅pB)​χC​ei​ϕC,ED​V=2​GF​Vc​b​Vu​q∗​mV​fB​fD(s)​fVfD​fπ​(εV∗⋅pB)​χE​ei​ϕE. superscript𝑇𝐷𝑉absent2subscript𝐺𝐹subscript𝑉𝑐𝑏superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑢𝑞subscript𝑎1𝜇subscript𝑓𝑉subscript𝑚𝑉superscriptsubscript𝐹1→𝐵𝐷superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑉2⋅superscriptsubscript𝜀𝑉subscript𝑝𝐵superscript𝐶𝐷𝑉absent2subscript𝐺𝐹subscript𝑉𝑐𝑏superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑢𝑞subscript𝑓subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑚𝑉superscriptsubscript𝐴0→𝐵𝑉superscriptsubscript𝑚𝐷2⋅superscriptsubscript𝜀𝑉subscript𝑝𝐵superscript𝜒𝐶superscript𝑒𝑖superscriptitalic-ϕ𝐶superscript𝐸𝐷𝑉absent2subscript𝐺𝐹subscript𝑉𝑐𝑏superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑢𝑞subscript𝑚𝑉subscript𝑓𝐵subscript𝑓subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑓𝑉subscript𝑓𝐷subscript𝑓𝜋⋅superscriptsubscript𝜀𝑉subscript𝑝𝐵superscript𝜒𝐸superscript𝑒𝑖superscriptitalic-ϕ𝐸displaystylebeginaligned TDV=sqrt2,GF,VcbVuq*,a1(mu),fV,mV,F1Brightarrow Dleft(mV2right)left(varepsilonV*cdot pBright), CDV=sqrt2,GF,VcbVuq*,fD(s),mV,A0Brightarrow Vleft(mD2right)left(varepsilonV*cdot pBright),chiC,eiphiC, EDV=sqrt2,GF,VcbVuq*,mV,fB,fracfD(s)fVfDfpi,left(varepsilonV*cdot pBright),chiEeiphiE,. endaligned So far there is not enough experimental data to do a global fit for B¯(s)→D¯(s)​V→subscript¯𝐵𝑠subscript¯𝐷𝑠𝑉barB(s)tobarD(s)V decays to extract the unknown nonfactorizable parameters in the C,E𝐶𝐸C,E amplitudes. content_sections.III Numerical results and discussion: The input parameters are classified into (a) electroweak coefficients: Cabibbo-Kobayashi-Maskawa (CKM) matrix elements and Wilson coefficients; (b) nonperturbative QCD parameters: decay constants, transition form factors and nonfactorizable parameters χC​(E)superscript𝜒𝐶𝐸chiC(E), ϕC​(E)superscriptitalic-ϕ𝐶𝐸phiC(E); (c) Hadronic parameters: mVsubscript𝑚𝑉mV, Γ0subscriptΓ0Gamma0, and gV​P1​P2subscript𝑔𝑉subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2gVP1P2 involved in strong interaction decays of vector mesons, which have been listed in tab. given in previous section. The Wolfenstein parametrization of the CKM matrix is utilized with the Wolfenstein parameters ascitebib.bib61 λ=0.22650±0.00048,A=0.790−0.012+0.017,ρ¯=0.141−0.017+0.016,η¯=0.357±0.01. formulae-sequence𝜆plus-or-minus0.226500.00048formulae-sequence𝐴subscriptsuperscript0.7900.0170.012formulae-sequence¯𝜌subscriptsuperscript0.1410.0160.017¯𝜂plus-or-minus0.3570.01lambda=0.22650pm 0.00048,A=0.790+0.017-0.012,barrho=0.141+0.016-0.017,bareta=0.357pm 0.01. The decay constants of pseudoscalar mesons and light vector mesons, and transition form factors of B𝐵B meson decays F1​(Q2)subscript𝐹1superscript𝑄2F1(Q2) and A0​(Q2)subscript𝐴0superscript𝑄2A0(Q2) at recoil momentum square Q2=0superscript𝑄20Q2=0 are listed in Tables and , respectively. The decay constants of π,K,D𝜋𝐾𝐷pi,K,D and B𝐵B are from the PDG by global fit with experimental data citebib.bib61. The remaining input nonperturbative QCD parameters, the decay constants of Dssubscript𝐷𝑠Ds and Bssubscript𝐵𝑠Bs, and all form factors are obtained from various theoretical results, such as light-cone sum rules citebib.bib63, bib.bib64, bib.bib65. We will utilize the same theoretical values as in the previous work by two of us (S. -H. Z. and C. -D. L. ) with other colleaguescitebib.bib50, with 5% uncertainty kept for decay constants and 10% uncertainty for form factors. Also as done in citebib.bib50, the dipole model of form factors is adopted with dipole parameters α1,2subscript𝛼12alpha1,2 listed in Tab. . The effective Wilson coefficients a1subscript𝑎1a1 is 1.0361.0361.036 calculated at scale μ=mb/2𝜇subscript𝑚𝑏2mu=mb/2. The nonfactorizable parameters χC​(E)superscript𝜒𝐶𝐸chiC(E) and ϕC​(E)superscriptitalic-ϕ𝐶𝐸phiC(E) fitted with experimental data citebib.bib50 are χC=0.48±0.01,ϕC=(56.6−3.8+3.2)∘,χE=0.024−0.001+0.002,ϕE=(123.9−2.2+3.3)∘. superscript𝜒𝐶formulae-sequenceabsentplus-or-minus0.480.01superscriptitalic-ϕ𝐶superscriptsuperscriptsubscript56.63.83.2superscript𝜒𝐸formulae-sequenceabsentsuperscriptsubscript0.0240.0010.002superscriptitalic-ϕ𝐸superscriptsuperscriptsubscript123.92.23.3displaystylebeginaligned chiC=0.48pm 0.01,phiC=left(56.6-3.8+3.2right)circ, chiE=0.024-0.001+0.002,phiE=left(123.9-2.2+3.3right)circ. endaligned With all the inputs, we integrate the the differential width in Eq. over the kinematics region to obtain the branching fractions of B¯→D(V→)P1P2barBto D(Vto)P1P2 and B¯→D¯(V→)P1P2barBtobarD(Vto)P1P2. Specifically, the numerical results for B¯(s)→D(ρ→)ππbarB(s)to D(rhoto)pipi, B¯(s)→D(K∗→)KπbarB(s)to D(K*to)Kpi, B¯(s)→D(ϕ→)KKbarB(s)to D(phito)KK and B¯(s)→D(ρ,ω→)KKbarB(s)to D(rho,omegato)KK, together with their corresponding doubly CKM suppressed decays B¯→D¯(V→)P1P2barBtobarD(Vto)P1P2, are collected in Tables , , and , respectively. In our results denoted by ℬFATsubscriptℬFATBrmFAT, the uncertainties are in sequence from the fitted parameters, form factors, decay constants for B¯→D(V→)P1P2barBto D(Vto)P1P2, and an additional error from Vu​bsubscript𝑉𝑢𝑏Vub for B¯→D¯(V→)P1P2barBtobarD(Vto)P1P2 decays induced by b→u→𝑏𝑢bto u transitions. One can see that the dominating errors are from the uncertainties of form factors, which can be improved by more precise calculations. Besides the CKM matrix elements shown in these tables we also list the intermediate resonance decays as well as the topological contributions T,C,E𝑇𝐶𝐸T,C,E and A𝐴A for convenience of analyzing hierarchies of branching fractions in the following. Experimental data in the third column and the results in PQCD approach in last column are also list for comparison.
content_sections.III Numerical results and discussion III: The decay modes are classified by CKM matrix elements involved, Cabibbo favored Vc​b​Vu​d∗subscript𝑉𝑐𝑏subscriptsuperscript𝑉𝑢𝑑VcbV*ud, Cabibbo suppressed Vc​b​Vu​s∗subscript𝑉𝑐𝑏subscriptsuperscript𝑉𝑢𝑠VcbV*us, and doubly Cabibbo suppressed Vu​b​Vu​d∗subscript𝑉𝑢𝑏subscriptsuperscript𝑉𝑢𝑑VubV*ud and Vu​b​Vu​s∗subscript𝑉𝑢𝑏subscriptsuperscript𝑉𝑢𝑠VubV*us, shown in the second column of Tables , and . The hierarchies of branching fractions can be seen clearly from this classification. The Cabibbo favored decay modes are about two orders larger than the doubly Cabibbo suppressed ones of the same type in the same table. As a result, these Cabibbo favored decay modes are able to be measured firstly by experiments, such as the first four modes of B¯(s)→D(ρ→)ππbarB(s)to D(rhoto)pipi citebib.bib61 in table and B¯s0→D0(K∗0→)K+π−barBs0to D0(K*0to)K+pi- citebib.bib4 in table . Our results and the experimental data are consistent within errors. Besides CKM matrix elements, the hierarchy of branching fraction is also dependent on contributions from different topological diagrams. Similar to the dynamics of two-body hadronic B𝐵B decays, the color favored emission diagram (T𝑇T) is absolutely dominating in the quasi-two-body decays. For instance, the topology T𝑇T dominated decay modes, B−→D0(K∗−→)K−π0B-to D0(K*-to)K-pi0, B¯0→D+(K∗−→)K−π0barB0to D+(K*-to)K-pi0 and B¯s0→Ds+(K∗−→)K−π0barBs0to Ds+(K*-to)K-pi0 happening through Cabibbo suppressed Vc​b​Vu​s∗subscript𝑉𝑐𝑏subscriptsuperscript𝑉𝑢𝑠VcbV*us, are the same order as the Cabibbo favored but only C𝐶C contributed decay, B¯s0→D0(K∗0→)K+π−barBs0to D0(K*0to)K+pi-. Besides the mode B¯s0→D0(K∗0→)K+π−barBs0to D0(K*0to)K+pi- with large branching ration has been measured by LHCb experiment through Dalitz plot analysis and isobar model citebib.bib4, another mode B¯0→D0(K¯∗0→)K−π+barB0to D0(barK*0to)K-pi+ with one order smaller branching ration is also measured by LHCb citebib.bib6. The remaining three modes with comparable branching ratio as B¯s0→D0(K∗0→)K+π−barBs0to D0(K*0to)K+pi- are also measurable in LHCb and Belle II. Our results of other decays, especially those with branching ratios in the range 10−6−10−4superscript106superscript10410-6-10-4 in Tables , and are expected to be observed in future experiments.

Since most quasi-two-body decays B(s)→D(s)(V→)P1P2B(s)to D(s)(Vto)P1P2 have not been measured by experiments until now, we list the results calculated in PQCD approach citebib.bib42, bib.bib43, bib.bib45, bib.bib46, bib.bib47 in the last column of the tables for comparison. As we have stated in Sec. , LCDA of P-wave P1​P2subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2P1P2 meson pair from resonance is described by RBW model in PQCD, which is the same theme adopted in the FAT approach for V→P1​P2→𝑉subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2Vto P1P2. The two approaches are effectively compatible for intermediate resonance strong decays, the main difference between them is the calculation of the weak decays of B𝐵B to D𝐷D meson and a vector resonance. As known, T𝑇T diagram is proved to be factorizable at all orders of αssubscript𝛼𝑠alphas for these decays, thus the perturbative calculation is reliable. Our results of the T𝑇T diagram dominating decay modes in Tables and are in good agreement with PQCD’s predictions. The magnitude of topologies C𝐶C is larger than E𝐸E, |C|>|E|𝐶𝐸left|Cright|>left|Eright|, in the FAT approach as shown in eq. , while C𝐶C is approximately equal to E𝐸E, |C|∼|E|similar-to𝐶𝐸left|Cright|simleft|Eright|, in the PQCD approach citebib.bib66, because it is sensitive to the power corrections and high order contributions which are hard to be calculated in PQCD approach. Therefore, it is easy to find in Tables and that the results of the FAT approach for the decays dominated only by C𝐶C, larger than those in the PQCD approach, are in better agreement with the current experimental data. However, our results of decay modes with only power suppressed E𝐸E contribution are a little smaller than those in PQCD, which need to be tested by the future experiments. At last, we emphasize that the branching ratios of decays in the FAT approach are more precise than those in the PQCD in Tables , and . The reason is that the topological amplitudes in the FAT including the nonfactorizable QCD contributions were extracted through a global fit with experimental data of these decays, while large uncertainties arise from non-perturbative parameters and QCD power and radiative corrections in the PQCD.

Contrary to the quasi-two-body decays through ρ→π​π→𝜌𝜋𝜋rhotopipi, K∗→K​π→superscript𝐾𝐾𝜋K*to Kpi and ϕ→K​K→italic-ϕ𝐾𝐾phito KK proceeding by the pole mass dynamics, i. e. , the pole mass is larger than the invariant mass threshold of two final states, the other modes with strong decays by ρ,ω→K​K→𝜌𝜔𝐾𝐾rho,omegato KK can only happen by ρ,ω𝜌𝜔rho,omega off-shell effect. It is also called the Breit-Wigner tail (BWT) effect, which has also appeared in charmed quasi-two-body decay with off-shell D∗superscript𝐷D* resonance citebib.bib36, bib.bib44 and charmless one through ρ,ω𝜌𝜔rho,omega resonances citebib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib25, bib.bib37. We denote branching ratios of this kind of decays by ℬvsuperscriptℬ𝑣Bv and their numerical results are listed on Table , together with the PQCD’s predictions for B→D(ρ→)KKBto D(rhoto)KK in last column. Apparently, the branching ratios of ℬvsuperscriptℬ𝑣Bv modes are approximately two orders smaller than those of ℬℬcal B modes in Table , that is, the BWT effect in B→D(ρ→)KKBto D(rhoto)KK is only about 1%percent11% of the on-shell resonance contribution, B→D(ρ→)ππBto D(rhoto)pipi. In Tables and , one can see that all the intermediate states of ρ,ω𝜌𝜔rho,omega, and ϕitalic-ϕphi can decay into K​K𝐾𝐾KK via virtual effects (for ρ,ω𝜌𝜔rho,omega) or pole mass dynamics (for ϕitalic-ϕphi). However, different from neutral states ρ0,ω,ϕsuperscript𝜌0𝜔italic-ϕrho0,omega,phi, the charged ρ±superscript𝜌plus-or-minusrhopm is the unique resonance contributing to the charged meson pair K±​K0superscript𝐾plus-or-minussuperscript𝐾0KpmK0 in the low mass region of K±​K0superscript𝐾plus-or-minussuperscript𝐾0KpmK0 system, which have been measured recently by Belle II collaboration citebib.bib8 based on a study of the small m​(K−​KS0)𝑚superscript𝐾superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0m(K-KS0) invariant mass for B−→D0​K−​KS0→superscript𝐵superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0B-to D0K-KS0 and B¯0→D+​K−​KS0→superscript¯𝐵0superscript𝐷superscript𝐾superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0barB0to D+K-KS0. With ρ−superscript𝜌rho--like resonances and non-resonance contribution, the branching ratios are ℬ​(B−→D0​K−​KS0)=(1.89±0.16±0.10)×10−4ℬ→superscript𝐵superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0plus-or-minus1.890.160.10superscript104B(B-to D0K-KS0)=(1.89pm 0.16pm 0.10)times 10-4 and ℬ​(B¯0→D+​K−​KS0)=(0.85±0.11±0.05)×10−4ℬ→superscript¯𝐵0superscript𝐷superscript𝐾superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0plus-or-minus0.850.110.05superscript104B(barB0to D+K-KS0)=(0.85pm 0.11pm 0.05)times 10-4, respectively. Our result for only ground state ρ​𝜌770rho is ℬ(B−→D0(ρ−→)K−K0)=(7.01−0.14−1.16−0.60+0.13+1.26+0.63)×10−5B(B-to D0(rho-to)K-K0)=(7.01+0.13+1.26+0.63-0.14-1.16-0.60)times 10-5 and ℬ(B−→D0(ρ−→)K−K0)=(4.64−0.02−0.90−0.47+0.03+1.00+0.49)×10−5B(B-to D0(rho-to)K-K0)=(4.64+0.03+1.00+0.49-0.02-0.90-0.47)times 10-5 in Table , which can reach a proportion of about 20%percent2020% of above measured all ρ−superscript𝜌rho- resonant and non-resonant components (considering half of branching ratios of K0superscript𝐾0K0 or K¯0superscript¯𝐾0barK0 to become KSsubscript𝐾𝑆KS). The similar mode B¯s0→Ds+(ρ−→)K−K0barBs0to Ds+(rho-to)K-K0 with comparable branching ratio (5.63−0−1.07−0.60+0+1.18+0.60)×10−5subscriptsuperscript5.6301.180.6001.070.60superscript105(5.63+0+1.18+0.60-0-1.07-0.60)times 10-5 is suggested to be measured in LHCb and Belle II. The study of invariant mass of neutral K+​K−superscript𝐾superscript𝐾K+K- system for B¯→D​K+​K−→¯𝐵𝐷superscript𝐾superscript𝐾barBto DK+K- in experiments is relatively complex, as it involves various resonances ρ0,ωsuperscript𝜌0𝜔rho0,omega and ϕitalic-ϕphi as well as non-resonances. Especially, in the low-mass region of m​(K+​K−)𝑚superscript𝐾superscript𝐾m(K+K-), the BWT effects from neutral resonance ρ0superscript𝜌0rho0 and ω𝜔omega in decay modes such as B¯0→D0(ρ0→)K+K−barB0to D0(rho0to)K+K- and B¯0→D0(ω→)K+K−barB0to D0(omegato)K+K-, B¯s0→D0(ρ0→)K+K−barBs0to D0(rho0to)K+K- and B¯s0→D0(ω→)K+K−barBs0to D0(omegato)K+K-, are pretty much the same in Table , even though the decay widths of ρ𝜌rho and ω𝜔omega meson are very different, shown in Table . As we have mentioned in citebib.bib36, bib.bib37, the BWT effects in these decays are not very sensitive to the widths of resonances. It can be attribute to the behavior of the Breit-Wigner propagator in eq. describing off-shell resonance, where the invariant mass square s𝑠s is far away from the on-shell mass of resonance, e. g. the real part, ∣s−mρ,ω∣delimited-∣∣𝑠subscript𝑚𝜌𝜔mid s-mrho,omegamid, of denominators of Breit-Wigner formula is much larger than the imaginary part i​mρ,ω​Γρ,ω𝑖subscript𝑚𝜌𝜔subscriptΓ𝜌𝜔i,mrho,omega,Gammarho,omega. Finally, the comparison of BWT effect in B→D(ρ→)KKBto D(rhoto)KK between the FAT and PQCD approaches is very similar with that of the on-shell resonance contributions in B→D(ρ→)ππBto D(rhoto)pipi. They are in agreement for T𝑇T diagram dominated modes, but different from those dominated by C𝐶C and E𝐸E diagrams. It indicates again that no matter for on-shell resonance or for off-shell one, the mechanisms or models applied by the two approaches are effectively consistent.
content_sections.IV Conclusion: Motived by the measurements of three-body charmed B𝐵B meson decays with resonance contributions, especially ground state resonance contributions, from Babar, LHCb and Belle (II), we systematically analyze the corresponding quasi-two-body decays B(s)→D(s)(V→)P1P2B(s)to D(s)(Vto)P1P2 through intermediate ground states ρ,K∗,ω𝜌superscript𝐾𝜔rho,K*,omega and ϕitalic-ϕphi. They proceed by b→c→𝑏𝑐brightarrow,c, or b→u→𝑏𝑢brightarrow,u, transitions to a D(s)​Vsubscript𝐷𝑠𝑉D(s)V intermediate state with V𝑉V as a resonant state which decays consequently into final states P1,P2subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2P1,P2 via strong interaction. We utilize the decay amplitudes extracted from the two-body charmed B𝐵B decays in the FAT approach for the first subprocess B(s)→D(s)​V→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠𝑉B(s)to D(s)V and RBW function for the narrow widths resonances V𝑉V as usually done in experiments and the PQCD approach. We categorize B(s)→D(s)(V→)P1P2B(s)to D(s)(Vto)P1P2 into four groups according to different vector resonance, B(s)→D(s)(ρ→)ππB(s)to D(s)(rhoto)pipi, B(s)→D(s)(K∗→)KπB(s)to D(s)(K*to)Kpi, B(s)→D(s)(ϕ→)KKB(s)to D(s)(phito)KK and B(s)→D(s)(ρ,ω→)KKB(s)to D(s)(rho,omegato)KK, where the former three kinds of modes decay by pole dynamics, and the last one by BWT effect. We calculate the branching ratios of all the four kinds of decay modes in the FAT method. Our results are consistent with the data by Babar, LHCb and Belle (II). Our predictions of order 10−6−10−4superscript106superscript10410-6-10-4 without any experimental data are hopeful to be observed in the future experiments. The FAT approach and the PQCD approach have effectively compatible mechanism of resonant state strong decays. Meanwhile, their treatments on the weak decays of B𝐵B to a D𝐷D meson and a vector resonance are different. Since the calculation of the first subprocess is done by a global fit with experimental data in the FAT approach, our results for the color suppressed diagram dominating modes are larger than those in the PQCD approach whose information on nonperturbative contribution and mc/mbsubscript𝑚𝑐subscript𝑚𝑏mc/mb power corrections are not included so far. In addition, our results have significantly less theoretical uncertainties due to accurate nonfactorizable parameters extracted from experimental data. The fourth type of modes happen through the tail effects of ρ𝜌rho and ω𝜔omega resonance to K​K𝐾𝐾KK. It’s found that the BWT effect of ρ𝜌rho resonance approximately is about two orders smaller than ρ𝜌rho on-shell resonance contribution, which induce that they are usually ignored in experimental analysis. However charged ρ±superscript𝜌plus-or-minusrhopm-like resonance of low mass region of K±​K0superscript𝐾plus-or-minussuperscript𝐾0KpmK0 system have been started to be studied in Belle II recently. The comparable modes, such as B¯s0→Ds+(ρ−→)K−K0barBs0to Ds+(rho-to)K-K0, also have the potential to be measurabled in LHCb and Belle II.

all_papers.title:
Analysis of three-body charmed B meson decays under the
  factorization-assisted topological-amplitude approach
all_papers.abstract:
We analyze the quasi-two-body charmed $B$ decays $B^{+,0}_{(s)} \to D_{(s)}^*
P_2 \to D_{(s)} P_1 P_2$ with $P_{1,2}$ as a pion or kaon. The intermediate
processes $B_{(s)} \to D_{(s)}^* P_2 $ are calculated with the
factorization-assisted topological-amplitude approach and the resonant effects
are calculated with the Breit-Wigner formalism. Taking all p-wave resonance
states $ \bar D_{(s)}^*$ into consideration, we present the related branching
fractions, calculate the Breit-Wigner-Tail effects, and investigate the flavor
$SU(3)$ breaking effects. Most of our branching fractions are consistent with
the perturbative QCD approach's predictions as well as the current experimental
data. With more precision calculation of the intermediate two body charmed B
meson decays, our quasi-two-body B decays calculation has significantly less
theoretical uncertainty than the perturbative QCD approach. Many of those
channels without any experimental data will be confronted with the future more
accurate experiment measurements. Our results of the Breit-Wigner-tail effects
also agree with the experimental very well. In $B^0$ decays this effect can
reach approximately to $5\%$. It is also found that the Breit-Wigner-tail
effects are not sensitive to the widths of their corresponding resonances. The
flavor $SU(3)$ symmetry breaking effect is also investigated.

sections.35: Aubert et al.(BABAR Collaboration), “Measurement of branching fractions and resonance contributions for B0→D¯0​K+​π−→superscript𝐵0superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝐾superscript𝜋B^{0}\to\bar{D}^{0}K^{+}\pi^{-} and search for B0→D0​K+​π−→superscript𝐵0superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾superscript𝜋B^{0}\to D^{0}K^{+}\pi^{-} decays,” Phys
sections.37: Abe et al.(BELLE Collaboration), “Observation of Cabibbo suppressed B→D(∗)​K−→𝐵superscript𝐷superscript𝐾B\to D^{(*)}K^{-} decays at BELLE,” Phys

qualitative: The factorization-assisted topological-amplitude (FAT) approach is used to analyze quasi-two-body charmed B meson decays, providing more precise decay amplitudes for intermediate two-body decays B(s) → D(s)*P2 compared to other approaches.
The quasi-two-body framework treats three-body decays B(s) → D(s)*P2 → D(s)P1P2 as sequential two-body processes, where the intermediate D(s)* resonance decays into D(s)P1.

Resonant contributions are modeled using the Breit-Wigner formalism, while non-resonant effects are accounted for through the Breit-Wigner-tail (BWT) effect, which captures off-shell contributions when the invariant mass of D(s)P1 is below the resonance pole mass.

The FAT approach significantly reduces theoretical uncertainty in three-body decay calculations by leveraging precise amplitudes from two-body decays, especially for non-factorizable QCD contributions.
Flavor SU(3) symmetry breaking effects are investigated, and the Breit-Wigner-tail effects are found to be insensitive to the widths of the corresponding resonances.
The BWT effect is non-negligible, reaching up to approximately 5% in B⁰ decays, and is in good agreement with experimental observations.

Theoretical uncertainties in the perturbative QCD (PQCD) approach are dominated by uncertainties in the two-body B → D*P decays, which the FAT approach mitigates through a global fit to experimental data.
quantitative: Breit-Wigner-tail (BWT) effects reach approximately 5% in B⁰ decays.
Off-shell contributions from D* resonances are about 5% in three-body B → Dππ(K) decays.
Contributions from Ds* resonances are found to be tiny, less than 1%.
Theoretical uncertainty in the quasi-two-body decay calculation is significantly reduced compared to the perturbative QCD approach due to improved precision in intermediate two-body decay amplitudes.
The Breit-Wigner-tail effects are not sensitive to the widths of the corresponding resonances.
