content_sections.I Introduction: B𝐵B meson non-leptonic decays are very important for the study of many frontier topics, such as the mechanism of C​P𝐶𝑃CP violation and the emergence of quantum chromodynamics. The three-body hadronic decays of B𝐵B mesons, with many kinds of intermediate states parameterized by the sequential two body decays, provide opportunities for the study of these common topics and of the hadron spectroscopy. So far, plenty of data on the three-body hadronic B𝐵B meson decays have been collected by BABAR, Belle and LHCb experiments using Dalitz plot technique, which induced a lot of theoretical studies. On the theoretical side, contrary to two-body B𝐵B decays, which have been analyzed extensively in the past two decades within the frameworks of the factorization approach, QCD factorization approach citebib.bib1, perturbative QCD (PQCD) citebib.bib2, soft collinear effective theory citebib.bib3 and some model independent approaches such as the factorization-assisted topological-amplitude (FAT) approach citebib.bib4, three-body B𝐵B decays are more complicated. On one hand the three-body decays receive both resonant and nonresonant contributions, on the other hand factorization of these decays has not been completely proved. Some theoretical models aimed to calculate the non-resonant effects of some three-body B𝐵B decays are in development such as the heavy meson chiral perturbation theory citebib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, a model combing the heavy quark effective theory and chiral Lagrangian citebib.bib8, and the perturbative QCD approach by introducing di-meson distribution amplitudes citebib.bib9. The three-body B𝐵B meson decays are usually dominated by intermediate resonances. They proceed formally as quasi-two-body decays with an intermediate sate of a vector or scalar resonance and a “bachelor” meson. The quasi-two-body framework is employed by the PQCD approach citebib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16 and some phenomenological analysis based on factorization citebib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7. Some three-body B𝐵B decays are also analyzed by the QCD factorization approach citebib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib17 and implemented within the U𝑈U-spin, isospin and flavour SU symmetries (see also Refscitebib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25). In this work, we concentrate on three-body charmed B𝐵B decays B(s)→D(s)​P1​P2→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2B(s)to D(s)P1P2, where P1,2subscript𝑃12P1,2 is a pion or kaon, via quasi-two-body decays B(s)→D(s)∗​P2→D(s)​P1​P2→subscript𝐵𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃2→subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2B(s)to D(s)*P2to D(s)P1P2 with P1subscript𝑃1P1 stemming from the decay of the resonance and P2subscript𝑃2P2 representing a “bachelor” light meson. The Belle, BABAR and LHCb Collaborations have achieved brilliant progress in identifying the excited charmd states and also found their off-shell effects are indispensable citebib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32. The off-shell effect, also called the Breit-Wigner-tail (BWT) effect, is the contribution when the pole mass of D(s)∗superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠D(s)* is smaller than the invariant mass of D(s)​P1subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1D(s)P1 or, in the other case, the contribution with the on-shell effect excluded by a cut on the invariant mass of D(s)​P1subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1D(s)P1. Recently, motivated by the experimental measurements on the off-shell effects of D∗superscript𝐷D* meson in B→D​π​π​(K)→𝐵𝐷𝜋𝜋𝐾Bto Dpipi(K) decays, theoretical attention is paid to these virtual effectscitebib.bib33, bib.bib14. A new systematic research on the three-body charmed B𝐵B meson decays including D(s)∗superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠D(s)* resonant states have been done subsequently in the PQCD approachcitebib.bib16. About 5%percent55% off-shell contributions of D∗superscript𝐷D* resonances and tiny contributions (<1%absentpercent1<1%) from Ds∗superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠Ds* resonances are found. The theoretical uncertainty of the two-step process of B(s)→D(s)∗​P2→D(s)​P1​P2→subscript𝐵𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃2→subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2B(s)to D(s)*P2to D(s)P1P2 is dominated by the uncertainty from two-body non-leptonic B𝐵B decays B(s)→D(s)∗​P→subscript𝐵𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠𝑃B(s)to D(s)*P. Large non-factorizable contribution has been found in the factorization approachcitebib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36 and the PQCD approachcitebib.bib37, bib.bib38. Since the charm quark mass scale is also involved in these decays in addition to the b𝑏b quark mass scale, the power corrections mc/mbsubscript𝑚𝑐subscript𝑚𝑏mc/mb of these decays are very difficult to calculate. In a previous workcitebib.bib39, two of us (S-H Z, C-D L), with other colleagues, utilize the framework of conventional topological diagram approach to group the decay amplitudes by different electroweak Feynman diagramscitebib.bib40. A global fit with all experimental data of these decays to extract the topological amplitudes including the nonfactorizable QCD contributions as well as the S​U​𝑆𝑈3SU breaking effects is performed. This so-called FAT approach citebib.bib39, bib.bib4, bib.bib41, bib.bib42, bib.bib43, bib.bib44 gives the most precise decay amplitudes of the two body B𝐵B meson decays with charmed meson final states. For example, the amplitudes of color-suppresses topological diagram (C𝐶C) of Fig. (b), dominated by the nonfactorizable QCD effect is larger in the FAT approach than that in other approaches. In this paper we apply the FAT approach to quasi-two-body B𝐵B meson decays, which will significantly reduce the theoretical uncertainty. The Breit-Wigner formalism is used to describe the resonance and a strong coupling accounts for the subsequent decay D(s)∗→D(s)​P1→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1D(s)*to D(s)P1 . This paper is organized as follows. In Section , the theoretical framework is introduced. The numerical results and discussions are collected in Section . Section is a summary.
content_sections.II FACTORIZATION OF AMPLITUDES FOR TOPOL: Under the framework of quasi-two-body decay, the B(s)→D(s)​P1​P2→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2B(s)to D(s)P1P2 decay is divided into two stages. B(s)subscript𝐵𝑠B(s) meson decays to D(s)∗​P2superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃2D(s)*P2 firstly and D(s)∗superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠D(s)* decays to D(s)​P1subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1D(s)P1 subsequently. The first decay B(s)→D(s)∗​P2→subscript𝐵𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃2B(s)to D(s)*P2 is a weak decay induced by b→c​q​u¯​(q=d,s)→𝑏𝑐𝑞¯𝑢𝑞𝑑𝑠brightarrow,c,q,baru,,(q=d,s) at quark level and secondary decay D(s)∗→D(s)​P1→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1D(s)*to D(s)P1 proceeds via strong interaction. In Fig. we list the topological diagrams of these decays under the framework of quasi-two-body decay, including the color-favored tree emission diagram T𝑇T (a), color-suppressed tree emission diagram C𝐶C (b) and W𝑊W-exchange diagram E𝐸E (c), which are specified by topological structures of the weak interaction. We will not consider processes induced by b→u​q​u¯​(q=d,s)→𝑏𝑢𝑞¯𝑢𝑞𝑑𝑠brightarrow,u,q,baru,,(q=d,s) transitions as they are Cabibbo-Kobayashi-Maskawa (CKM)-suppressed and there are no enough experimental data to do global fit for their nonfactorizable contributions as done in the b→c→𝑏𝑐bto c transition case. The two body charmless B𝐵B decays have been proved factorization at high precision citebib.bib3. Large non-factorizable contribution has been found in the two-body B𝐵B decays with charmed meson final states citebib.bib36, bib.bib38, which we have illustrated in the fourth paragraph of Sec. . content_sections.III Numerical results and discussion III: The hierarchies of branching fractions can be seen clearly in the last columns of Tables and . Firstly, let us pay attention to those decays in Table whose intermediate states can be changed to each other by swapping the “bachelor” light mesons pion with kaon, e. g. , B0→D∗−​π+→D¯0​π−​π+→superscript𝐵0superscript𝐷∗absentsuperscript𝜋→superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋B0to Dast-pi+tobarD0pi-pi+ and B0→D∗−​K+→D¯0​π−​K+→superscript𝐵0superscript𝐷∗absentsuperscript𝐾→superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝜋superscript𝐾B0to Dast-K+tobarD0pi-K+. One can find that the branching ratios of the modes with a pion “bachelor” meson are one power larger than their corresponding ones with a kaon “bachelor” meson. The reason is that the pion “bachelor” modes are the Cabibbo favored (Vu​dsubscript𝑉𝑢𝑑Vud) processes, while the kaon “bachelor” modes are Cabibbo suppressed (Vu​ssubscript𝑉𝑢𝑠Vus) ones. The branching ratios of the Cabibbo favored decays Bs0→Ds∗−​π+→D¯0​K−​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠0superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠∗absentsuperscript𝜋→superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝐾superscript𝜋Bs0to Dsast-pi+tobarD0K-pi+ and Bs0→D¯∗0​K¯0→D¯0​π0​K¯0→superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠0superscript¯𝐷absent0superscript¯𝐾0→superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝜋0superscript¯𝐾0Bs0tobarD*0barK0tobarD0pi0barK0 shown in Table are larger than those of the remaining Cabibbo suppressed decays. Similar to the dynamics in two body hadronic B𝐵B decays, the color favored tree (T𝑇T) topological diagram is absolutely dominating. Branching fractions of decays with T𝑇T diagram are larger than those with only C𝐶C or E𝐸E diagrams. Our results for these branching fractions with T𝑇T diagram are in good agreement with the PQCD predictionscitebib.bib16. We have |C|>|E|𝐶𝐸left|Cright|>left|Eright| in the FAT approach citebib.bib39 while |C|∼|E|similar-to𝐶𝐸left|Cright|simleft|Eright| in the PQCD approach citebib.bib58. The |C|𝐶left|Cright| contributes larger in the FAT approach than in the PQCD approach. Therefore, our branching fractions of B0→D¯∗0​K0→D¯0​π0​K0→superscript𝐵0superscript¯𝐷∗absent0superscript𝐾0→superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝜋0superscript𝐾0B0tobarDast 0K0tobarD0pi0K0 and Bs0→D¯∗0​K¯0→D¯0​π0​K¯0→superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠0superscript¯𝐷absent0superscript¯𝐾0→superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝜋0superscript¯𝐾0Bs0tobarD*0barK0tobarD0pi0barK0 decays with only C𝐶C diagram are about two times larger than that in the PQCD approach. For those decays with exactly the same intermediate state D(s)∗​P2subscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃2D*(s)P2, their hierarchies of branching ratios are completely caused by the strong decays of D(s)∗→D(s)​P1→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1D(s)*to D(s)P1. There are two hierarchy sources. One is the BWT effect, the other is the strong couplings. Some of the decays, labeled by ℬℬB in the tables, can proceed by the pole mass dynamics, i. e. , the pole mass is larger than the invariant mass threshold of two final states. The others labeled by ℬvsubscriptℬ𝑣Bv can only happen by the BWT effect. The branching ratios of ℬℬcal B mode decays are apparently one to two orders larger than those of ℬvsubscriptℬ𝑣Bv mode decays. The difference due to strong couplings are also obvious. For instance, the isospin relation leads to gD∗−​D¯0​π−=−2​gD∗−​D−​π0subscript𝑔superscript𝐷absentsuperscript¯𝐷0superscript𝜋2subscript𝑔superscript𝐷absentsuperscript𝐷superscript𝜋0gD*-barD0pi-=-sqrt2,gD*-D-pi0. As a result, the branching ratio of B(s)0→D¯0​π−​π+​(K+)→superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠0superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋superscript𝐾B(s)0tobarD0pi-pi+(K+) is approximatively two times larger than that of B(s)0→D−​π0​π+​(K+)→superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠0superscript𝐷superscript𝜋0superscript𝜋superscript𝐾B(s)0to D-pi0pi+(K+). The strong decays with a pair of s​s¯𝑠¯𝑠sbars quarks from the sea proceed only by the BWT effect because of the heavy strange quark mass. Consequently, the corresponding branching fractions are smaller than the others.

We take the decays B+→D¯∗0​π+→D¯0​π0​(D−​π+,Ds−​K+)​π+→superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐷∗absent0superscript𝜋→superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝜋0superscript𝐷superscript𝜋superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾superscript𝜋B+tobarDast 0pi+;tobarD0pi0,(D-pi+,,Ds-K+),pi+ as an example to show the dependence of branching fractions on the invariant mass of D¯0​π0superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝜋0barD0pi0 pair (or D−​π+superscript𝐷superscript𝜋D-pi+, Ds−​K+superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾Ds-K+), which is depicted in the left diagram of Fig. . To demonstrate the flavor SU symmetry breaking effect, curves of their corresponding decays with the “bachelor” pion replaced with kaon are plotted in the right diagram of Fig. . The decay with a pole mass dynamical strong decay D¯∗0→D¯0​π0→superscript¯𝐷∗absent0superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝜋0barDast 0tobarD0pi0 and those with the BWT effect strong decays D¯∗0→D−​π+→superscript¯𝐷∗absent0superscript𝐷superscript𝜋barDast 0to D-pi+ and D¯∗0→Ds−​K+→superscript¯𝐷∗absent0superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾barDast 0to Ds-K+ are represented by solid (red) lines, dash (blue) lines and dot-dashed (green) lines, respectively. Data of the decays with D¯∗0→Ds−​K+→superscript¯𝐷∗absent0superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾barDast 0to Ds-K+,, which are one order magnitude smaller than those with D¯∗0→D−​π+→superscript¯𝐷∗absent0superscript𝐷superscript𝜋barDast 0to D-pi+,, are multiplied by ten in oder to be shown clearly on the same figure. One may notice that the two conspicuous flagpoles located at the mass of D¯∗0superscript¯𝐷∗absent0barDast 0 in Fig. . It is the distinguishing characteristic of pole dynamics. Our curves of B+→D¯∗0​π+→D¯0​π0​(D−​π+,Ds−​K+)​π+→superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐷∗absent0superscript𝜋→superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝜋0superscript𝐷superscript𝜋superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾superscript𝜋B+tobarDast 0pi+;tobarD0pi0,(D-pi+,,Ds-K+),pi+ (the red solid lines) have higher peaks than that from the ones in the PQCD approach citebib.bib16, which indicates the pole mass dynamics plays a more important role in our results. Only a few charmed three-body B decays are measured by the experiments. We collected these channels in Table as well as the results of the PQCD approach. Our results are consistent with the PQCD ones except the decay Bs0→Ds∗−​π+→D¯0​K−​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠0superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠∗absentsuperscript𝜋→superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝐾superscript𝜋Bs0to Dsast-pi+tobarD0K-pi+. However, the experimental data of this decay has a very large uncertainty. It may be a good choice to wait for more accurate experimental measurement. Our calculation of the intermediate decays B→D(s)∗​P→𝐵superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠𝑃Bto D(s)*P is more precise than the PQCD approach, especially for the color suppressed (C) or exchange decay amplitudes (E) as this two non-factorizable amplitudes, C𝐶C and E𝐸E, are fitted from precise experiment data in FAT not calculated perturbatively. Therefore our theoretical uncertainty is much smaller than the previous PQCD approach. We also study the BWT effects in these decays. Although the Bssubscript𝐵𝑠Bs decay in Table has only BWT contribution, the four decays of B+,0superscript𝐵0B+,0 are dominated by pole dynamics. In order to make an effective comparison, the BWT effects of B+,0superscript𝐵0B+,0 decays are calculated with a cut of s≥2.1​GeV𝑠2.1GeVsqrtsgeq 2.1GeV, which is also adopted by experiments and the PQCD approach. Comparing the data in Tables and , one can see that the BWT effect contributes about 5%percent55% in B0superscript𝐵0B0 meson decays. For example, the centre value of the branching ratio of B0→D∗−​π+→D¯0​π−​π+→superscript𝐵0superscript𝐷∗absentsuperscript𝜋→superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋B0to Dast-pi+tobarD0pi-pi+ in Tables by a cut of s≥2.1​GeV𝑠2.1GeVsqrtsgeq 2.1GeV in FAT is 10.26×10−510.26superscript10510.26times 10-5 and the one in the whole physical region of the D¯0​π−superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝜋barD0pi- invariant mass is 1.77×10−31.77superscript1031.77times 10-3, which lead to the percentage of BWT effect is 10.26×10−5/1.77×10−3∼5%similar-to10.26superscript1051.77superscript103percent510.26times 10-5/1.77times 10-3sim 5%. Comparing the BWT effect between B+→D¯∗0​π+→D−​π+​π+→superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐷∗absent0superscript𝜋→superscript𝐷superscript𝜋superscript𝜋B+tobarDast 0pi+;to D-pi+,pi+ and B+→D¯∗0​π+→Ds−​K+​π+→superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐷∗absent0superscript𝜋→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾superscript𝜋B+tobarDast 0pi+;to Ds-K+,pi+, we find that the peaks of the green (dot-dashed) lines locate farther from the pole mass of D¯∗0superscript¯𝐷∗absent0barDast 0 than the peaks of the blue (dashed) lines in Fig. . The reason is that Ds−​K+superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾Ds-K+ has much larger threshold mass than D−​π+superscript𝐷superscript𝜋D-pi+. It can also be seen that the green (dot-dashed) line is smoother than the blue (dashed) line, which is very steep in the vicinity of the pole mass. Our calculation shows that the BWT effect in B+→D¯∗0​π+→D−​π+​π+→superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐷∗absent0superscript𝜋→superscript𝐷superscript𝜋superscript𝜋B+tobarDast 0pi+;to D-pi+,pi+ is about 4%percent44%. To check the dependence of the BWT effect on the widths of the intermediate resonance, we can compare those decays with different intermediate states. For instance, we find that contribution of the BWT effect is (1.64−0.32+0.36)×10−5subscriptsuperscript1.640.360.32superscript105(1.64+0.36-0.32)times 10-5 for B0→D∗−​π+→Ds−​K0​π+→superscript𝐵0superscript𝐷∗absentsuperscript𝜋→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾0superscript𝜋B0to Dast-pi+,to Ds-K0pi+, (3.21−0.50+0.54)×10−5subscriptsuperscript3.210.540.50superscript105(3.21+0.54-0.50)times 10-5 for B+→D¯∗0​π+→Ds−​K+​π+→superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐷∗absent0superscript𝜋→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝐾superscript𝜋B+tobarDast 0pi+to Ds-K+pi+, and (4.21−0.81+0.89)×10−5subscriptsuperscript4.210.890.81superscript105(4.21+0.89-0.81)times 10-5 for Bs0→Ds∗−​π+→D¯0​K−​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠0superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠∗absentsuperscript𝜋→superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝐾superscript𝜋Bs0to Dsast-pi+tobarD0K-pi+. Their BWT effects are at the same order even though the widths of the resonances vary widely with ΓD∗+=83.4±1.8​keVsubscriptΓsuperscript𝐷absentplus-or-minus83.41.8keVGammaD*+=83.4pm 1.8keV, ΓD∗0=55.3±1.4​keVsubscriptΓsuperscript𝐷absent0plus-or-minus55.31.4keVGammaD*0=55.3pm 1.4keV and ΓDs∗−≃1similar-to-or-equalssubscriptΓsubscriptsuperscript𝐷∗absent𝑠1GammaDast-ssimeq 1 keV. It indicates that the BWT effects in these decays are not very sensitive to the widths of resonances, which is confirmed by the PQCD approach citebib.bib14, bib.bib16. This can be explained by the behaviour of the Breit-Wigner propagators in the kinematics regions of these decays, where the real parts of their denominators are much larger than the imaginary parts. Taking Ds∗−→D¯0​K−→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠∗absentsuperscript¯𝐷0superscript𝐾Dsast-tobarD0K- as an example, the kinematics region starts from s=2.359​GeV𝑠2.359GeVsqrts=2.359,GeV, while ΓDs∗−≃1similar-to-or-equalssubscriptΓsubscriptsuperscript𝐷∗absent𝑠1GammaDast-ssimeq 1keV. content_sections.IV Conclusion: The three-body charmed B𝐵B meson decays are dominated by intermediate resonances, i. e. , they proceed via quasi-two-body decays as B(s)→D(s)∗​P2→D(s)​P1​P2→subscript𝐵𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃2→subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2B(s)to D(s)*P2to D(s)P1P2. The first step of two-body decay is induced by flavor changing weak decays b→c​q​u¯(q=d,s)→𝑏𝑐𝑞¯𝑢𝑞𝑑𝑠brightarrow,c,q,baruquad(q=d,s) and the intermediate resonant state D(s)∗superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠D(s)* successionally decays into D(s)​P1subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1D(s)P1 via strong interaction. We utilize the decay amplitudes extracted from the two-body charmed B𝐵B decays to quasi-two-body decays with the intermediate resonance described by the Breit-Wigner propagator. Including all the P-wave resonant states D¯(s)∗superscriptsubscript¯𝐷𝑠barD(s)*, we systematically study these decays. The B(s)→D(s)∗​P2→D(s)​P1​P2→subscript𝐵𝑠superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃2→subscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2B(s)to D(s)*P2to D(s)P1P2 decays can be divided into two groups: ℬℬB and ℬvsubscriptℬ𝑣Bv. ℬℬB represents the group of decays whose pole mass of the resonance D¯(s)∗superscriptsubscript¯𝐷𝑠barD(s)* is larger than the threshold mass of producing D¯(s)​P1subscript¯𝐷𝑠subscript𝑃1barD(s)P1. This group of decays are dominated by the pole dynamics, and the main contributions to their branching fractions are from vicinity of the resonances’ pole masses. The others labeled by ℬvsubscriptℬ𝑣Bv can only happen by the BWT effect. The BWT effects in B0superscript𝐵0B0 decays are approximately 5%percent55%. We also found that the Breit-Wigner-tail effects are not sensitive to the widths of their corresponding resonances. We compare our results of the branching fractions with the PQCD approach’s predictions as well as the experimental data. The largest theoretical uncertainty in the calculation is from the intermediate two-body charmed B𝐵B meson decays. Since this calculation is done by a global fit to all experimental data, our results of three body B𝐵B decays have significantly less theoretical uncertainty than the PQCD calculation. We use the same cut on the kinematics region of those decays proceeding via pole dynamics as the experiments. Our results agree well with the experimental data. For those ones without any experimental data, we would like to confront them with the future more accurate experiment measurements. The flavor-S​U​𝑆𝑈3SU symmetry breaking effect in these decays is studied. Our results RD∗−=0.0831−0.0006+0.0008subscript𝑅superscript𝐷absentsubscriptsuperscript0.08310.00080.0006RD*-=0.0831+0.0008-0.0006 and RD∗0=0.0761±0.0001subscript𝑅superscript𝐷absent0plus-or-minus0.07610.0001RD*0=0.0761pm 0.0001 are perfectly consistent with their experimental values. Besides, the local S​U​𝑆𝑈3SU-breaking effects, which are defined as ratios of corresponding differential branching fractions, are investigated. Their magnitudes are found not sensitive to the invariant mass of the strong decay final states, which confirms the that the SU asymmetry in three-body decays is dominated by the two-body weak decays.

all_papers.title:
Analysis of three-body charmless $B$-meson decays under the
  factorization-assisted topological-amplitude approach
all_papers.abstract:
We analyze quasi-two-body charmless $B$ decays $B_{(s)} \to P_1 V \to P_1 P_2
P_3$ with $V$ representing a vector resonant, and $P_{1,2,3}$ as a light
pseudo-scalar meson, pion, kaon or $\eta^{(\prime)}$. The intermediate
processes $B_{(s)} \to P_1 V $ are calculated in the factorization-assisted
topological-amplitude approach and the vector resonant effects are described by
the Breit-Wigner propagator, which successively decay to $P_1 P_2$ via strong
interaction. Taking into account of all vector resonances in ground state,
$\rho, K^*, \omega, \phi$, we present the related branching fractions, and
calculate the virtual effects for $B_{(s)} \to \pi, K (\rho ,\omega \to) KK$.
We also predict direct $\it{CP}$ asymmetries of three body B decay modes with
$\rho, K^*$ resonances as intermediate states. Our predicted branching
fractions of decay modes dominated by the color-favored tree diagram or the
color-favored penguin diagram are consistent with the perturbative QCD
approach's predictions as well as QCD factorization approach. While for those
nonperturbative contribution dominated decay modes, the branching ratios in
this work are in better agreement with current experimental data than the PQCD
predictions and the QCD factorization results due to their shortage of the
nonperturbative contributions or $1/m_b$ power corrections. Many of the decays
channels, especially for direct $\it{CP}$ asymmetries, are waiting for the
future experiments.

qualitative: Three-body charmless B-meson decays are analyzed as quasi-two-body decays where a vector resonance (e.g., ρ, K*, ω, φ) is produced in the intermediate state and subsequently decays into two pseudoscalars, leading to nontrivial kinematics and phase space distributions ideal for studying CP asymmetries and factorization in QCD.

The factorization-assisted topological-amplitude (FAT) approach is employed to systematically analyze these decays, incorporating nonperturbative and nonfactorizable QCD effects through topological diagrams classified under SU(3) symmetry, which improves precision over traditional factorization methods.
The Breit-Wigner formalism is used to describe vector resonance contributions, with strong couplings accounting for the subsequent two-body decays of the resonances into pseudoscalar mesons.

The FAT approach successfully addresses long-standing puzzles in two-body B decays—such as the B→ππ branching ratio and CP asymmetry discrepancies—by including enhanced nonfactorizable QCD contributions, motivating its extension to three-body decays.

Nonperturbative contributions and 1/m_b power corrections are crucial for accurate predictions, especially in decay modes dominated by weak annihilation or chirally enhanced effects, where standard perturbative QCD and QCD factorization approaches fall short.

The analysis focuses on the edges of the Dalitz plot, where the invariant mass of two final-state pseudoscalars is small, indicating the presence of intermediate vector resonances, and where factorization theorems similar to two-body decays are expected to hold.
Virtual effects of resonances such as ρ, K*, ω, and φ are studied in quasi-two-body decays like B→πK(ρ,ω→KK), revealing their significant impact on branching fractions and CP asymmetries.
Many predicted direct CP asymmetries and branching ratios remain to be tested experimentally, particularly with the upgraded LHCb and Belle II experiments.
quantitative: Branching fractions for quasi-two-body charmless B decays B(s)→P₁V→P₁P₂P₃ are calculated using the FAT approach, with results showing better agreement with experimental data than PQCD and QCD factorization predictions for nonperturbative-dominated modes.
The predicted branching fractions for color-favored tree or penguin-dominated decay modes are consistent with both perturbative QCD (PQCD) and QCD factorization predictions.
The FAT approach includes nonfactorizable QCD contributions that are larger than in other approaches, resolving the B→ππ branching ratio puzzle and the B→πK CP asymmetry puzzle.

The vector resonance effects are modeled via the Breit-Wigner propagator: $ \mathcal{D}(m^2) = \frac{1}{m^2 - M_V^2 + i M_V \Gamma_V} $, where $ M_V $ and $ \Gamma_V $ are the mass and width of the vector meson (e.g., ρ, K*, ω, φ).
Strong coupling constants (e.g., $ g_{VPP} $) are used to describe the decay $ V \to P_1 P_2 $, with values derived from experimental data or theoretical models.
Direct CP asymmetries are predicted for decay modes involving ρ and K* resonances, with magnitudes expected to be measurable in future experiments.
The study includes all ground-state vector resonances: ρ(770), K*(892), ω(782), and φ(1020), with their contributions evaluated in the quasi-two-body framework.

content_sections.I Introduction: Three-body nonleptonic B𝐵B meson decays constitute a large portion of B𝐵B decay channels. Contrary to two-body B𝐵B meson decays, they have nontrivial kinematics and phase space distributions, which can provide more opportunities for the study of C​P𝐶𝑃CP asymmetries and factorization issues in QCD for multibody nonleptonic decays. Generally, one utilize the Dalitz plot technique to analyze the phase space of three-body B𝐵B meson decays, where the invariant mass of a pair of final-state particles peaks as a resonance. In the edges of the Dalitz plot, various resonances as the intermediate states in three-body B𝐵B meson decays will show up, so that the analysis on these decays enables one to study the properties of these resonances. Experimentally, a large amount of branching ratios and C​P𝐶𝑃CP asymmetry parameters of three-body charmless B𝐵B decays have already been measured by BABAR, Belle and LHCb collaborationscitebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, while more are expected from the upgrade of LHCb and Belle II experiments. On the theoretical side, three-body nonleptonic B𝐵B meson decays are more complicated than two-body cases as they involve three-body decay matrix elements. In a phenomenological factorization modelcitebib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, the three-body matrix elements of charmless B𝐵B decays were factorized naively, with the resulting local correlaters studied extensively based on heavy meson chiral perturbation theory for nonresonant contributions and the usual Breit-Wigner formalism for resonant effects. Besides these phenomenological descriptions of three-body charmless B𝐵B decays in the whole Dalitz plot, theoretical analysis in the context of QCD factorization mainly concentrated on the edges of the Dalitz distribution, since the contribution in the center regions of phase space is argued to be both 1/mb1subscript𝑚𝑏1/mb power and αssubscript𝛼𝑠alphas suppressedcitebib.bib9, bib.bib10. Since the vector (scalar) mesons usually decay dominantly to two pseudoscalar mesons with large decay width, the three-body B𝐵B decays are found to be dominantly happened by B𝐵B decaying to a vector (scalar) meson and a pseudoscalar meson, and the vector (scalar) meson subsequently decaying to two pseudoscalar mesons. In this case, two of the three final particles are collinear, generating a small invariant mass in the edges of the Dalitz distribution, and recoil against the third meson in three-body decays. Then one expects a similar factorization theorem as for two-body decays, with the difference that one of the mesons in the two-body case is substituted by a pair of collinear moving mesons with small invariant mass. A series of workscitebib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22 using perturbative QCD approach concentrated on this situation that the pair of collinear mesons are described collectively through intermediate resonances, that is, the three-body decays are processed as quasi-two-body decays. The 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry of B→π​π​π→𝐵𝜋𝜋𝜋Btopipipi decays has been studied within the QCD factorization framework at leading order in αssubscript𝛼𝑠alphascitebib.bib23, bib.bib24, using naive factorization for three-body matrix elements, which involve generalized form factor of B→π​π→𝐵𝜋𝜋Btopipi transition and dimeson light cone distribution amplitudes incitebib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28. The calculation of the nonleptonic three-body B𝐵B decays with heavy-to-heavy transitions, up to the precision of next-next-to-leading order in αssubscript𝛼𝑠alphas has also been done in QCD factorization citebib.bib29. In all these quasi-two-body calculations of the three-body B𝐵B decays, the theoretical calculations rely heavily on the precision of two-body theoretical calculation methods. In the case of two-body nonleptonic B𝐵B decays, power corrections in the heavy-quark expansion, such as weak annihilation effects and chirally enhanced power corrections, are difficult to estimate and remain the sources of uncertainty in QCD factorization approachcitebib.bib30. A complete next-to-leading order calculation of two-body B𝐵B decays in perturbative QCD factorization approach has not been finished citebib.bib31. The problem about precision predictions in the calculation of two-body nonperturbative matrix elements also exist in three-body case. As discussed in Refcitebib.bib8, the predicted branch ratios of quasi-two-body decays such as B→ϕ​(K+​K−)​K,K∗​(K−​π+)​π,ρ​(π+​π−)​K→𝐵italic-ϕsuperscript𝐾superscript𝐾𝐾superscript𝐾superscript𝐾superscript𝜋𝜋𝜌superscript𝜋superscript𝜋𝐾Btophi(K+K-)K,K*(K-pi+)pi,rho(pi+pi-)K are smaller than experimental data due to the absence of 1/mb1subscript𝑚𝑏1/mb power corrections. To include the nonperturbative and nonfactorization contribution in two-body B𝐵B decays, a model independent framework of topological diagram approach citebib.bib32 has been introduced. The equivalence of the topological decay amplitudes and the S​U​𝑆𝑈3SU irreducible amplitudes has been approved citebib.bib33, bib.bib34. In this approach, one classifies the decay amplitudes of two-body charmless B𝐵B decays into different electroweak topological Feynman diagrams under SU symmetry. The topological amplitudes including the nonfactorizable QCD contributions were extracted through a global fit from all experimental data of these decays. The precision of this topological diagram approach is limited to the size of SU breaking effect. In order to include the SU breaking effects, the so-called factorization-assisted topological-amplitude (FAT) approachcitebib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib41, bib.bib42 has been introduced. They can give the most precise decay amplitudes of the two-body charmless B𝐵B-meson decays. For instance, the amplitude of the color-suppressed topological diagram dominated by the nonfactorizable QCD effect, was larger in the FAT approach than that in other perturbative approaches so that the long-standing B→π​π→𝐵𝜋𝜋Btopipi branching ratio puzzle and B→π​K→𝐵𝜋𝐾Btopi K 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry puzzle were solved simultaneously. Encouraged by the success in two-body B𝐵B decays, we will systematically analyze three-body charmless B𝐵B decays using the FAT approach at the edges of the Dalitz distribution with only vector resonance contribution, i. e. , quasi-two-body decays with two of the pseudoscalar mesons decaying from the vector resonance. The vector resonant effects are described in terms of the usual Breit-Wigner formalism, and a strong coupling accounts for the subsequent vector meson two-body decay. We will study the branching ratios and 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP violations of these three-body B𝐵B decays, and discuss the virtual effects of intermediate resonance ρ,K∗,ω𝜌superscript𝐾𝜔rho,K*,omega , and ϕitalic-ϕphi on quasi-two-body decays. This paper is organized as follows. In Sec. , the theoretical framework is introduced. The numerical results and discussions are collected in Sec. . Section is a summary.
content_sections.II Factorization Amplitudes for Topologi: As a quasi-two-body process, the B(s)→P1​P2​P3→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2subscript𝑃3B(s)to P1P2P3 decay (with P𝑃P denoting a light pseudoscalar meson) is divided into two stages. First, B(s)subscript𝐵𝑠B(s) meson decays to P1​Vsubscript𝑃1𝑉P1V, and the intermediate vector resonance V𝑉V decays to P2​P3subscript𝑃2subscript𝑃3P2P3 subsequently. The first decay B(s)→P1​V→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1𝑉B(s)to P1V is a weak decay induced by b→u​u¯​d​(s)→𝑏𝑢¯𝑢𝑑𝑠brightarrow,u,,baru,d(s), at quark level in leading order (tree diagram) and b→d​(s)​q​q¯​(q=u,d,s)→𝑏𝑑𝑠𝑞¯𝑞𝑞𝑢𝑑𝑠brightarrow,d(s),,q,barq,,(q=u,d,s) in next-to-leading order (penguin loop diagram). The secondary decay V→P2​P3→𝑉subscript𝑃2subscript𝑃3Vto P2P3 proceeds via strong interaction. In Fig. we show the topological diagrams of B(s)→P1​P2​P3→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2subscript𝑃3B(s)to P1P2P3 under the framework of quasi-two-body decay at tree level, including (i) color-favored tree emission diagram T𝑇T, (ii) color-suppressed tree emission diagram C𝐶C, (iii) W𝑊W-exchange tree diagram E𝐸E, and (iv) W𝑊W-annihilation tree diagram A𝐴A, which are specified by topological structures of the weak interaction. Likewise, one loop penguin diagram can also be grouped into four categories: (i) QCD-penguin emission diagram P𝑃P, (ii) flavor-singlet QCD-penguin diagram PCsubscript𝑃𝐶PC or EW-penguin emission diagram PE​Wsubscript𝑃𝐸𝑊PEW, (iii) time-like QCD-penguin diagram PEsubscript𝑃𝐸PE, and (iv) space-like QCD-penguin annihilation diagram PAsubscript𝑃𝐴PA, which are shown in Fig. . In the two kinds of figures, we only show one case that the intermediate resonance (labeled by gray ovals) is produced as a recoiling particle in emission diagrams. The electroweak B𝐵B decays at tree level have been proven to be factorizable at high precision citebib.bib43. Therefore, we can calculate the decay amplitude of color-favored tree diagram T𝑇T in perturbative QCD order by order. Large nonfactorizable contributions, such as soft corrections to the color-suppressed tree amplitude and 1/mb1subscript𝑚𝑏1/mb power corrections from annihilation type diagram, are found to be non-negligible citebib.bib38. Similar to the FAT approach for two-body B𝐵B decayscitebib.bib38, we have to introduce unknown parameters for nonfactorizable contributions for color-suppressed tree diagram C𝐶C, and W𝑊W-exchange tree diagram E𝐸E (W𝑊W-annihilation tree diagramA𝐴A is negligible) to be fitted from experimental data. content_sections.III Numerical Results of Branching Ratio: In the numerical calculations, we need input parameters, such as mVsubscript𝑚𝑉mV, Γ0subscriptΓ0Gamma0, and gV​P1​P2subscript𝑔𝑉subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2gVP1P2 involved in strong interaction decay of vector mesons, which are listed in the previous section. For the CKM matrix elements needed in the weak transition calculation, we use Wolfenstein parametrization with parameters from global fitcitebib.bib50 as follows: λ=0.22650±0.00048,A=0.790−0.012+0.017,ρ¯=0.141−0.017+0.016,η¯=0.357±0.01. formulae-sequence𝜆plus-or-minus0.226500.00048formulae-sequence𝐴subscriptsuperscript0.7900.0170.012formulae-sequence¯𝜌subscriptsuperscript0.1410.0160.017¯𝜂plus-or-minus0.3570.01lambda=0.22650pm 0.00048,A=0.790+0.017-0.012,barrho=0.141+0.016-0.017,bareta=0.357pm 0.01. The decay constants of light pseudoscalar mesons and vector mesons, and transition form factors of B𝐵B-meson decays at recoil momentum square Q2=0superscript𝑄20Q2=0 are listed in Tables and , respectively. The decay constants of pseudoscalar mesons π,K𝜋𝐾pi,K, and B𝐵B are from Particle Data Groupcitebib.bib50. The decay constants of Bssubscript𝐵𝑠Bs and the vector mesons, and all form factors are not measured by experiments and only calculated theoretically, such as in constitute quark model and light cone quark model citebib.bib52, bib.bib53, bib.bib54, bib.bib55, covariant light front approach citebib.bib56, bib.bib57, bib.bib58, light-cone sum rules citebib.bib59, bib.bib60, bib.bib61, bib.bib62, bib.bib63, bib.bib64, bib.bib65, bib.bib66, bib.bib67, bib.bib68, bib.bib69, bib.bib70, bib.bib71, bib.bib72, bib.bib73, bib.bib74, bib.bib75, bib.bib76, bib.bib77, bib.bib78, bib.bib79, PQCD citebib.bib80, bib.bib81, bib.bib82, bib.bib83, bib.bib84, bib.bib85, bib.bib86, bib.bib87, bib.bib88, and lattice QCD citebib.bib89, bib.bib90, bib.bib91. We will use the same theoretical values as in the charmless two-body decayscitebib.bib38, with 5% uncertainty kept for decay constants and 10% for form factors. The dipole model parameters α1,α2subscript𝛼1subscript𝛼2alpha1,alpha2 applied to describe the Q2superscript𝑄2Q2 dependence of form factors are also listed in Tablecitebib.bib54, bib.bib81. Our numerical results for the branching ratios of B(s)subscript𝐵𝑠B(s) decays are collected in Tables -, for decays B(s)→P1​ρ→P1​π​π→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1𝜌→subscript𝑃1𝜋𝜋B(s)to P1rhoto P1pipi, B(s)→P1​K∗→P1​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1superscript𝐾→subscript𝑃1𝐾𝜋B(s)to P1K*to P1Kpi, B(s)→P1​ϕ→P1​K​K¯→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1italic-ϕ→subscript𝑃1𝐾¯𝐾B(s)to P1phito P1KbarK, and B(s)→P1​ρ,P1​ω→P1​K​K¯formulae-sequence→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1𝜌→subscript𝑃1𝜔subscript𝑃1𝐾¯𝐾B(s)to P1rho,P1omegato P1KbarK, respectively. In these tables, we also list the intermediate resonances decays as well as the topological contributions represented by the corresponding symbols T,C(′)𝑇superscript𝐶′T,C(prime) and so on. In our results, the first uncertainty is from the nonpertubative parameters in Eq. , and the other two uncertainties are estimated with 10%percent1010% variations of form factors, 5%percent55% variations of decay constants. One can see that the dominant source of uncertainties are from form factors.

Comparing the results shown in Table with the corresponding ones calculated in the previous work citebib.bib38, we find that the branching ratios of quasi-two-body decay B(s)→P1​ρ→P1​π​π→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1𝜌→subscript𝑃1𝜋𝜋B(s)to P1rhoto P1pipi are a little smaller than that of the direct two-body decays B(s)→P1​ρ→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1𝜌B(s)to P1rho. It can be attributed to the not narrow resonance width ΓρsubscriptΓ𝜌Gammarho. We cannot apply the narrow width approximation to factorize quasi-two-body decay formula together with ℬ​(ρ→π​π)∼100%similar-toℬ→𝜌𝜋𝜋percent100B(rhotopipi)sim 100% to obtain the precise results of two-body decays. From Table , it is easy to see that our results are well consistent with the experimental data shown in the last column of the Table. For the decay modes B→π(ρ→)ππBtopi(rhoto)pipi, B→η(′)(ρ→)ππBtoeta(prime)(rhoto)pipi with color favored T𝑇T diagram dominated contribution, and the penguin diagram P𝑃P dominated modes B(s)→K(ρ→)ππB(s)to K(rhoto)pipi, the perturbation calculation is reliable, since these kinds of diagrams are proved to be factorizable to all orders of αssubscript𝛼𝑠alphas. Our results are in good agreement with those calculated under the QCD factorization citebib.bib8 and that in PQCD approachcitebib.bib13 within errors. For the decay modes with dominated color suppressed diagrams C(′)superscript𝐶′C(prime) contributions, such as B¯0→π0(ρ0→)ππbarB0topi0(rho0to)pipi and B¯0→η(′)(ρ0→)ππbarB0toeta(prime)(rho0to)pipi, the theoretical results are sensitive to the power corrections and next-to-leading order contributions. With the magnitude and phase of this C(′)superscript𝐶′C(prime) diagram given in Eq. , our results of branching ratios are consistent with experimental data and results calculated under the QCD factorization approach citebib.bib8. On the contrary, the calculations of these decay modes are only done to the leading order in the perturbative QCD approach with one order magnitude smaller branching ratiocitebib.bib13.

In Table , we only list quasi-two-body decays B(s)→P1​K∗→P1​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1superscript𝐾→subscript𝑃1𝐾𝜋B(s)to P1K*to P1Kpi through strong decays K∗0→K+​π−→superscript𝐾absent0superscript𝐾superscript𝜋K*0to K+pi-, K¯∗0→K−​π+→superscript¯𝐾absent0superscript𝐾superscript𝜋barK*0to K-pi+, and K∗−→K−​π0→superscript𝐾absentsuperscript𝐾superscript𝜋0K*-to K-pi0 with a pair of u​u¯𝑢¯𝑢ubaru from the sea. The branching ratios for B(s)→P1​K∗→P1​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1superscript𝐾→subscript𝑃1𝐾𝜋B(s)to P1K*to P1Kpi via sea quark pair d​d¯𝑑¯𝑑dbard strong decay K∗0→K0​π0→superscript𝐾absent0superscript𝐾0superscript𝜋0K*0to K0pi0, K¯∗0→K¯0​π0→superscript¯𝐾absent0superscript¯𝐾0superscript𝜋0barK*0tobarK0pi0, and K∗−→K¯0​π−→superscript𝐾absentsuperscript¯𝐾0superscript𝜋K*-tobarK0pi- can be calculated under the narrow width approximation according to the isospin conservation relationship for strong interaction, ℬ​(K¯∗0→K−​π+)ℬ→superscript¯𝐾absent0superscript𝐾superscript𝜋displaystyleB(barK*0to K-pi+) =displaystyle= 2​ℬ​(K¯∗0→K¯0​π0),2ℬ→superscript¯𝐾absent0superscript¯𝐾0superscript𝜋0displaystyle 2,B(barK*0tobarK0pi0), ℬ​(K∗−→K¯0​π−)ℬ→superscript𝐾absentsuperscript¯𝐾0superscript𝜋displaystyleB(K*-tobarK0pi-) =displaystyle= 2​ℬ​(K∗−→K−​π0).2ℬ→superscript𝐾absentsuperscript𝐾superscript𝜋0displaystyle 2,B(K*-to K-pi0). The experimental data listed in the last column are measured by BABAR citebib.bib94, bib.bib95, bib.bib96, bib.bib44 and Bellecitebib.bib97, bib.bib98 experiments. As we have considered the power corrections from “chiral enhanced” term, penguin annihilation contribution and EW-penguin diagram for the penguin-dominated modes Bu,d→π​K∗→π​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑢𝑑𝜋superscript𝐾→𝜋𝐾𝜋Bu,dtopi K*topi Kpi, our results are well consistent with experimental data, and larger by a factor of 2∼4similar-to242sim 4 than QCD factorization approachcitebib.bib8. The decay channels Bu,d→K​K∗→K​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑢𝑑𝐾superscript𝐾→𝐾𝐾𝜋Bu,dto KK*to KKpi listed in Table are CKM suppressed (|Vt​d/Vt​s|subscript𝑉𝑡𝑑subscript𝑉𝑡𝑠|Vtd/Vts|) comparing with decay Bu,d→π​K∗→π​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑢𝑑𝜋superscript𝐾→𝜋𝐾𝜋Bu,dtopi K*topi Kpi, so that the branching ratios of the former are one order magnitude smaller than the latter. Since there are not experimental measurements of these decays and Bs→K​K∗→K​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑠𝐾superscript𝐾→𝐾𝐾𝜋Bsto KK*to KKpi, we show in the last column the results of QCD factorization approachcitebib.bib8, bib.bib93 for comparison. Similarly with the case happened in Bu,d→π​K∗→π​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑢𝑑𝜋superscript𝐾→𝜋𝐾𝜋Bu,dtopi K*topi Kpi decays, the results of Bu,d→K​K∗→K​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑢𝑑𝐾superscript𝐾→𝐾𝐾𝜋Bu,dto KK*to KKpi as well as penguin-dominated decays Bs→K​K∗→K​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑠𝐾superscript𝐾→𝐾𝐾𝜋Bsto KK*to KKpi calculated in FAT approach are larger by a factor of 2∼3similar-to232sim 3 than those in QCD factorization approachcitebib.bib8. We also give the numerical results of branching ratios of decay modes B(s)→η​K∗→η​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑠𝜂superscript𝐾→𝜂𝐾𝜋B(s)toeta K*toeta Kpi, shown in Table . These decays have not been measured by experiments from the three-body Dalitz plot analyses. However the corresponding two-body decays have been measured experimentally. Applying the narrow width approximation for the K∗superscript𝐾K* resonance, we have Γ​(B(s)→η​K∗→η​K​π)=Γ​(B→η​K∗)​ℬ​(K∗→K​π)Γ→subscript𝐵𝑠𝜂superscript𝐾→𝜂𝐾𝜋Γ→𝐵𝜂superscript𝐾ℬ→superscript𝐾𝐾𝜋Gammaleft(B(s)toeta K*toeta Kpiright)=Gamma(Brightarroweta K*)Bleft(K*to Kpiright). Utilizing the isospin symmetry relationship ℬ​(K∗0→K+​π−)=ℬ​(K∗−→K¯0​π−)=2​ℬ​(K∗−→K−​π0),ℬ→superscript𝐾absent0superscript𝐾superscript𝜋ℬ→superscript𝐾absentsuperscript¯𝐾0superscript𝜋2ℬ→superscript𝐾absentsuperscript𝐾superscript𝜋0displaystyleBleft(K*0rightarrow K+pi-right)=Bleft(K*-rightarrowbarK0pi-right)=2,Bleft(K*-rightarrow K-pi0right), we can obtain the branching ratios for two-body decays B→η​K∗→𝐵𝜂superscript𝐾Btoeta K*, ℬ​(B−→η​K∗−)ℬ→superscript𝐵𝜂superscript𝐾absentdisplaystyleBleft(B-toeta K*-right) =displaystyle= (16.05±1.11±2.22±0.57)×10−6,plus-or-minus16.051.112.220.57superscript106displaystyle(16.05pm 1.11pm 2.22pm 0.57)times 10-6, ℬ​(B−→η′​K∗−)ℬ→superscript𝐵superscript𝜂′superscript𝐾absentdisplaystyleBleft(B-toetaprimeK*-right) =displaystyle= (3.06±1.05±0.36±0.18)×10−6,plus-or-minus3.061.050.360.18superscript106displaystyle(3.06pm 1.05pm 0.36pm 0.18)times 10-6, ℬ​(B¯0→η​K¯∗0)ℬ→superscript¯𝐵0𝜂superscript¯𝐾absent0displaystyleBleft(barB0toetabarK*0right) =displaystyle= (16.95±1.13±2.30±0.62)×10−6,plus-or-minus16.951.132.300.62superscript106displaystyle(16.95pm 1.13pm 2.30pm 0.62)times 10-6, ℬ​(B¯0→η′​K¯∗0)ℬ→superscript¯𝐵0superscript𝜂′superscript¯𝐾absent0displaystyleBleft(barB0toetaprimebarK*0right) =displaystyle= (3.02±1.11±0.32±0.18)×10−6. plus-or-minus3.021.110.320.18superscript106displaystyle(3.02pm 1.11pm 0.32pm 0.18)times 10-6. These results agree well with the experimental measurements of these two-body decays and the ones given in FAT approachcitebib.bib38.

We show the branching fraction results of B(s)→P1(ϕ→)KK¯B(s)to P1(phito)KbarK decays in Table together with the results from PQCD approachcitebib.bib16 in the last column. For penguin diagram P𝑃P dominated decay modes, Bu,d→K(ϕ→)KK¯Bu,dto K(phito)KbarK, the results in FAT approach are consistent with the ones in PQCD approach. As discussed in the previous section, this kind of decays are dominated by reliable perturbation contributions, which agree well with the experimental data ℬ(B−→K−(ϕ→)K+K−)=(4.48±0.22−0.24+0.33)×10−6B(B-to K-(phito)K+K-)=(4.48pm 0.22+0.33-0.24)times 10-6 citebib.bib99 and ℬ(B−→K−(ϕ→)K+K−)=(4.72±0.45±0.35−0.22+0.39)×10−6B(B-to K-(phito)K+K-)=(4.72pm 0.45pm 0.35-0.22+0.39)times 10-6 citebib.bib100. The decay modes Bs→K(ϕ→)KK¯Bsto K(phito)KbarK are CKM suppressed (|Vt​d/Vt​s|subscript𝑉𝑡𝑑subscript𝑉𝑡𝑠|Vtd/Vts|) comparing with Bu,d→K(ϕ→)KK¯Bu,dto K(phito)KbarK decays, so that the branching ratios of the former are one order smaller than the latter. It is easy to see from Table that branching ratios of all the CKM suppressed decay modes from the FAT approach are larger that that from PQCD approach. It can be attribute to the missing power corrections and next-to-leading order corrections to the color suppressed penguin diagram PC′superscriptsubscript𝑃𝐶′PCprime in PQCD approach. Summing over the two quasi-two-body decay modes with same weak decay but different subsequent strong decay, i. e. B(s)→P1(ϕ→)K+K−B(s)to P1(phi,to)K+K- and B(s)→P1(ϕ→)K0K¯0B(s)to P1(phi,to)K0barK0, we should obtain results of the corresponding two-body decays B(s)→P1​ϕ→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1italic-ϕB(s)to P1phi, by applying the narrow width approximation. For instance, the summation of the results of ℬ(B−→π−(ϕ→)K+K−)B(B-topi-(phi,to)K+K-) and ℬ(B−→π−(ϕ→)K0K¯0)B(B-topi-(phi,to)K0barK0) in Table is in agreement with the two-body decay value ℬ​(B−→π−​ϕ)=(2.80±0.04±0.55±0.03)×10−7ℬ→superscript𝐵superscript𝜋italic-ϕplus-or-minus2.800.040.550.03superscript107B(B-topi-phi)=(2.80pm 0.04pm 0.55pm 0.03)times 10-7 incitebib.bib38 within the error bar.

The decay modes B(s)→π,K(ρ,ω→)KK¯B(s)topi,K(rho,omegato)KbarK represent a category of decays, whose pole masses of the resonances ρ,ω𝜌𝜔rho,omega smaller than the threshold mass of producing K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK pair. They can only happen with the virtual effect, which is also called the Breit-Wigner tail effect. In Table , we collect these virtual effects in FAT approach, together with the results in PQCD approach citebib.bib17 for comparison. Here we only show subprocesses ρ0,ω→K+​K−→superscript𝜌0𝜔superscript𝐾superscript𝐾rho0,omegato K+K- without ρ0,ω→K0​K¯0→superscript𝜌0𝜔superscript𝐾0superscript¯𝐾0rho0,omegato K0barK0 due to the tiny mass difference between K+​K−superscript𝐾superscript𝐾K+K- and K0​K¯0superscript𝐾0superscript¯𝐾0K0barK0. The virtual effects of B(s)→π,K(ρ→)KK¯B(s)topi,K(rhoto)KbarK are approximately 1∼2similar-to121sim 2 order smaller than the dominated contribution of B(s)→π,K(ρ→)ππB(s)topi,K(rhoto)pipi in Table. Unlike the on-shell resonance contributions that mostly give the similar contributions between the FAT and PQCD approaches, the virtual contributions give quite different results between the two approaches. Since the color suppressed tree diagram C(′)superscript𝐶′C(prime) is calculated in the PQCD approach only to the leading order, its size is significantly smaller that that fitted from experimental data in the FAT approachcitebib.bib38. As a result, the off-shell effects in C(′)superscript𝐶′C(prime) dominated decay modes, B¯0→π0(ρ0,ω→)K+K−barB0topi0;(rho0,omega,to)K+K-, B¯s0→K0(ρ0,ω→)K+K−barBs0to K0;(rho0,omega;to)K+K- using FAT approach are nearly one order magnitude larger than that of PQCD approach. For another example, the hierarchy between the magnitude of virtual contributions for quasi-two-body decays B−→π0(ρ−→)K−K0B-topi0(rho-to)K-K0 and B−→π−(ρ0→)K+K−B-topi-(rho0to)K+K- is opposite in the PQCD and the FAT approaches, which need further experimental measurements to check. Comparing the results between Tables and , one can see that the off-shell effects in B→π,K(ρ→)KK¯Btopi,K(rhoto)KbarK are small, that is only about 1% of the on-resonance contribution. However from Table , one can see that the off-shell effect from the ground state ρ​0𝜌superscript7700rho0 in B−→π−(ρ0→)K+K−B-topi-(rho0to)K+K- decay in FAT approach is at the same order as the branching ratio ℬ(B−→π−(ρ0→)K+K−)displaystyleB(B-topi-(rho0to)K+K-) =displaystyle= (9.46−1.65−1.14−0.69−0.38−1.82+1.79+1.16+0.72+0.49+1.82)×10−8,superscriptsubscript9.461.651.140.690.381.821.791.160.720.491.82superscript108displaystyle(9.46-1.65-1.14-0.69-0.38-1.82+1.79+1.16+0.72+0.49+1.82)times 10-8, calculated by PQCD approachcitebib.bib17. The latter branching fraction of B−→π−(ρ0→)K+K−B-topi-(rho0to)K+K- was measured by LHCbcitebib.bib101, Bellecitebib.bib102, and BABARcitebib.bib103. Therefore the virtual contribution of B−→π−(ρ0→)K+K−B-topi-(rho0to)K+K- should also be considered in experimental analysis. Comparing the virtual effect contributions of ρ𝜌rho with ω𝜔omega resonance in Table , for instance, B−→π−(ρ0→)K+K−B-topi-(rho0to)K+K- and B−→π−(ω→)K+K−B-topi-(omegato)K+K-, we find that the virtual effects are at the same order magnitude, even though the decay widths of ρ𝜌rho and ω𝜔omega meson are very different, shown in Table. It means that the virtual contributions of these decays are not very sensitive to the full decay width of ρ𝜌rho and ω𝜔omega meson, which is also confirmed by the PQCD approachcitebib.bib17. This can be explained by the behavior of the Breit-Wigner propagators in Eq. in the kinematics regions of these decays, where the imaginary part i​mρ,ω​Γρ,ω​(s)isubscript𝑚𝜌𝜔subscriptΓ𝜌𝜔𝑠i,mrho,,omegaGammarho,,omega(s) becomes unimportant when the invariant mass square s𝑠s is larger than 1​G​e​V21GesuperscriptV21GeV2.
content_sections.IV 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃\\it{CP} Asymmetry: The study of three-body B𝐵B decays attracts a lot of interests because of its large 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry. In this work, we will concentrate on direct 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP violation and will not discuss mixing-induced 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP violation. We also do not consider integrated 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry of the whole phase space of three-body B𝐵B decays, but we only consider the direct 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry of quasi-two-body decay with one resonant as the intermediate state. Consequently this 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry will arise from the interference between tree and penguin amplitudes of the two-body resonances but not from the interference between three-body nonresonant and resonant contribution or between different resonant states. It is well known that direct 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetries are induced by the interference between difference of strong phases and different CKM phases of different contributions. The strong phase is also the major source of direct 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP violation uncertainty as it is mostly from nonperturbative QCD dynamics. As illustrated in Ref. citebib.bib38 for the two-body B𝐵B decays, the strong phases extracted from experimental data are sufficient to induce correct direct 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry, while QCD factorization approaches and soft-collinear effect theory usually make wrong predictions or no prediction for the direct 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetries due to absence of nonperturbative strong phases, such as the well-known K​π𝐾𝜋Kpi puzzle. The problem in two-body decays will also exist in three-body decays. For example, the signs of 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry of B−→π−​K+​K−→superscript𝐵superscript𝜋superscript𝐾superscript𝐾B-rightarrowpi-K+K- and B−→K−​π+​π−→superscript𝐵superscript𝐾superscript𝜋superscript𝜋B-rightarrow K-pi+pi- decay were conflict with experimental data under the QCD factorization approach citebib.bib8. The authors need to consider final-state rescattering effect by introducing an unknown strong phase δ𝛿delta to account for the sign flip of 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry. Therefore possible 1/mb1subscript𝑚𝑏1/mb power corrections, such as final-state interactions, or other nonperturbative contributions are necessary to correctly describe 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry. For the quasi-two-body decays under the framework of FAT, the strong phases result in from the nonperturbative contribution parameters in Eq. and the Breit-Wigner formalism for resonance in Eq. . These strong phases have already contained all perturbative and nonperturbative QCD effects, for instance, final-state interactions, color suppressed contribution, penguin annihilation contribution, and so on. They explain well the measured branching ratios, and thus are expected to give the right prediction of 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry parameters in three-body B𝐵B decays. We show the 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetries of B(s)→P1​ρ→P1​π​π→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1𝜌→subscript𝑃1𝜋𝜋B(s)to P1rhoto P1pipi and B(s)→P1​K∗→P1​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1superscript𝐾→subscript𝑃1𝐾𝜋B(s)to P1K*to P1Kpi in Tables and , respectively. As the theoretical uncertainty from hadronic parameters (form factors and decay constants ) mostly cancel, the major theoretical uncertainties for 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry parameters are from the nonperturbative contribution parameters in Eq. and weak phases. Here we only list the uncertainty arise from the strong phase in Eq. due to small uncertainties of CKM matrix elements. We also list the experimental data in the last column of the two tables. Actually only three decay modes with the intermediate process K−​ρ0superscript𝐾superscript𝜌0K-rho0, π+​K∗−superscript𝜋superscript𝐾absentpi+K*-, and η​K¯∗0𝜂superscript¯𝐾absent0etabarK*0 are well measured in experiments with more than 3​σ3𝜎3sigma signal significance. Most 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetries listed here are predictions to be tested by the future experiments. For example, the 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry of B−→π+​ρ0→π+​π+​π−→superscript𝐵superscript𝜋superscript𝜌0→superscript𝜋superscript𝜋superscript𝜋B-topi+rho0topi+pi+pi- is predicted quite large in this work, which is consistent with the theoretical predictions using QCDFcitebib.bib30 and the PQCD approachcitebib.bib13 but not yet measured by the experiments. We did not list the 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetries of B→P1​ϕ→P1​K​K¯→𝐵subscript𝑃1italic-ϕ→subscript𝑃1𝐾¯𝐾Bto P1phito P1KbarK decays in our table, since most of them are from pure penguin diagram contributions, PCsubscript𝑃𝐶PC, PAsubscript𝑃𝐴PA, whose 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry is expected to be zero with only one single CKM phase, at leading order approximation. We also do not list the 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetries of B(s)→P1​ρ​(ω)→P1​K​K¯→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1𝜌𝜔→subscript𝑃1𝐾¯𝐾B(s)to P1rho(omega)to P1KbarK decays contributed by the virtual effects. Most of the quasi-two-body decays are extracted from the Dalitz-plot analysis of three-body ones in experimental analysis. For example, B−→K−​ρ0→superscript𝐵superscript𝐾superscript𝜌0B-to K-rho0 and B−→π−​K¯∗0→superscript𝐵superscript𝜋superscript¯𝐾absent0B-topi-barK*0 decays are extracted from B−→K−​π+​π−→superscript𝐵superscript𝐾superscript𝜋superscript𝜋B-to K-pi+pi- decays. Comparing the 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetries of quasi-two-body decay by integrating out the invariant mass square (s𝑠s) distribution of 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry over the kinematics region in the Dalitz plot with the corresponding ones of two-body decay with s𝑠s fixed as mV2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑉2mV2 as in Ref citebib.bib38, we find that the size of 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetries of B(s)→P1​ρ→P1​π​π→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1𝜌→subscript𝑃1𝜋𝜋B(s)to P1rhoto P1pipi in Table are slightly smaller than that of the B(s)→P1​ρ→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1𝜌B(s)to P1rho. The 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry of B(s)→P1​K∗→P1​K​π→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1superscript𝐾→subscript𝑃1𝐾𝜋B(s)to P1K*to P1Kpi in Table are slightly larger than that of B(s)→P1​K∗→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1superscript𝐾B(s)to P1K* decay. These very small changes of 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry between two-body and quasi-two-body decays arise from the finite decay width of vector resonances.
content_sections.V Conclusion: We systematically analyze the three-body charmless B𝐵B meson decays through the intermediate resonance, i. e. , they proceed via quasi-two-body decays as B(s)→P1​V→P1​P2​P3→subscript𝐵𝑠subscript𝑃1𝑉→subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2subscript𝑃3B(s)to P1Vto P1P2P3, with the vector resonant state V𝑉V including all ground states ρ,K∗,ω,ϕ𝜌superscript𝐾𝜔italic-ϕrho,K*,omega,phi. The first step of two-body B𝐵B decays to P1​Vsubscript𝑃1𝑉P1V intermediate states is induced by flavor changing weak decays b→u​u¯​d​(s)→𝑏𝑢¯𝑢𝑑𝑠brightarrow,u,,baru,d(s), at leading order and b→d​(s)​q​q¯​(q=u,d,s)→𝑏𝑑𝑠𝑞¯𝑞𝑞𝑢𝑑𝑠brightarrow,d(s),,q,barq,,(q=u,d,s) at one loop level. The second step is that the intermediate vector resonant state V𝑉V, described by the Breit-Wigner propagator, decays into two light pseudoscalar mesons via strong interaction. In order to include all possible perturbative and nonperturbative QCD corrections, the two-body weak B𝐵B decays are described by the FAT approach with the decay amplitudes extracted from experimental data. We compare results of the branching fractions from FAT approach with the PQCD approach’s predictions and the ones of QCD factorization, as well as the experimental data. For a color suppressed tree diagram and color suppressed or annihilation penguin diagram dominated decay modes, the branching ratios in FAT approach are larger than that of the PQCD approach and the QCD factorization approach. The reason is that the decay magnitudes and phases extracted from experimental data in FAT approach are larger than that in the other two approaches due to the shortage of nonperturbative contribution and 1/mb1subscript𝑚𝑏1/mb power corrections. We have also considered the virtual effects from ρ𝜌rho and ω𝜔omega resonance tail, which are usually ignored by the experimental analysis. These virtual effects from ground state ρ​0𝜌superscript7700rho0 are about 1∼2similar-to121sim 2 order magnitude smaller than the dominated contribution of B(s)→π,K(ρ→)ππB(s)topi,K(rhoto)pipi decays. However, these virtual effects contributions are at the same order as the higher resonance contributions, such as ρ​𝜌1450rho. We also find that the virtual contributions of these decays are not very sensitive to the decay widths of ρ𝜌rho and ω𝜔omega. The branching ratios of B(s)→P1(ρ→)ππB(s)to P1(rhoto)pipi, B(s)→P1(K∗→)KπB(s)to P1(K*to)Kpi are consistent with experimental data, and the others are predictions waiting for future experiments to test. For 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry, theoretical predictions of three-body decays are mostly based on quasi-two-body decays via intermediate resonance, where the strong phases extracted from experimental data in FAT approach are sufficient to induce correct direct 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetries. In this work, we utilize the same strong phases of two-body decays together with the phases from Breit-Wigner formalism to predict the direct 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetries of quasi-two-body decays, B(s)→P1(ρ→)ππB(s)to P1(rhoto)pipi and B(s)→P1(K∗→)KπB(s)to P1(K*to)Kpi. Our results agree well with the existing experimental measurements. But many of the 𝐶𝑃𝐶𝑃itCP asymmetry predictions are waiting for future experiments.

all_papers.title:
Enhanced Next-to-Leading-Order Corrections to Weak Annihilation
  $B$-Meson Decays
all_papers.abstract:
We accomplish the analytical computation of the pure weak annihilation
non-leptonic $B$-meson decay amplitudes at leading power in the heavy quark
expansion. The novel observation regarding such fundamental hadronic quantities
is that adding the missing hard-collinear contribution on top of the hard gluon
exchange effect eliminates rapidity divergences entering the convolution
integrals of factorization formulae. Subsequently we identify the perturbative
enhancement mechanism due to the penguin contractions of the current-current
operators from the weak effective Hamiltonian, which yields the significant
impacts on the CP violating observables.

qualitative: The weak annihilation contributions in exclusive charmless B-meson decays are crucial for understanding CP violation and strong interaction dynamics, but their theoretical treatment has been hindered by rapidity divergences in convolution integrals within QCD factorization.

The inclusion of hard-collinear gluon exchange (with invariant mass of order √(mbΛQCD)) provides an analytical regularization of rapidity divergences, resolving a long-standing issue in factorization-based calculations of weak annihilation amplitudes.

Penguin contractions of current-current operators in the weak effective Hamiltonian lead to a perturbative enhancement mechanism that significantly impacts CP-violating observables, particularly through the generation of sizable strong phases.

Previous calculations neglected the hard-collinear contribution, leading to inconsistent regularization and unreliable predictions for annihilation-dominated decays; this work rectifies that by incorporating the missing hard-collinear topology.

The improved formalism enables systematic computation of next-to-leading-order (NLO) corrections to weak annihilation amplitudes, which is essential for precise predictions of direct CP asymmetries and other strong-phase-sensitive observables.

The validity of the QCD factorization framework for B→PP, B→PV, and B→VV decays is strengthened by this analytical regularization, especially in the presence of finite bottom-quark mass and end-point singularities.

The topological amplitude analysis reveals that NLO Feynman diagrams enhanced by large Wilson coefficients and CKM factors are key to generating significant strong phases, which are critical for CP violation studies.
quantitative: The hard-collinear gluon exchange contribution has an invariant mass scale of 𝒪(√(mbΛQCD)), where mb is the bottom quark mass and ΛQCD ≈ 300 MeV.

The perturbative enhancement arises from penguin contractions of current-current operators in the weak effective Hamiltonian, which are enhanced by large Wilson coefficients and CKM factors (e.g., |Vtd/Vts| ≈ 0.2), leading to non-negligible contributions to decay amplitudes.
The paper focuses on pure annihilation channels: B¯s→ππ and B¯d→ϕϕ, which are dominated by weak annihilation topologies and sensitive to strong phases.
The analysis is performed at next-to-leading order (NLO) in αs, with the inclusion of NLO Feynman diagrams that are enhanced by large Wilson coefficients and CKM factors.

The formalism relies on leading-twist light-cone distribution amplitudes for the final-state mesons and the B-meson, with acceptable models used to evaluate the convolution integrals (e.g., Gaussian or asymptotic forms).
The regularization of rapidity divergences is achieved analytically through the hard-collinear contribution, eliminating the need for ad hoc parametrizations of divergent integrals.

authors: Cai-Dian L\"u, Yue-Long Shen, Chao Wang, Yu-Ming Wang

content_sections.I Introduction: It is generally accepted that the exclusive two-body charmless bottom-meson decays are of fundamental importance for advancing our understanding towards diverse facets of the strong interaction dynamics governing the flavour-changing heavy-quark decay processes and for exploring the peculiar implications of the Cabibbo-Kobayashi-Maskawa (CKM) mechanism for CP violation in electroweak interactions. The primary challenge of predicting such hadronic B𝐵B-meson decay observables consists in constructing a field-theoretic framework to disentangle the calculable perturbative QCD fluctuations from the non-perturbative soft and collinear physics systematically. In this respect, the QCD factorization formalism based upon expansions in the small parameter ΛQCD/mbsubscriptΛQCDsubscript𝑚𝑏Lambdarm QCD/mb citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6 has proven to provide the precise prescriptions for evaluating the appeared non-leptonic matrix elements of the effective weak Hamiltonian operators. However, the long-standing obstacle to improve the factorization calculations of the two-body B𝐵B-meson decay amplitudes arises from the disturbing end-point divergences in the convolution integrals describing the power-suppressed but phenomenologically pronounced weak annihilation corrections. In particular, the inadequacy of achieving the factorization-compatible regularization of the annihilation amplitudes apparently does not allow to provide quantitative predictions for the annihilation-dominated decay processes. Additionally, the abiding lack of higher-order perturbative corrections to the weak annihilation topologies prevents us from obtaining reliable estimates of the strong-phase sensitive quantities (for instance, the direct CP asymmetry) due to a variety of potential enhancements, which do not manifest themselves at leading-order (LO) in the strong coupling. The process-independent parametrizations for logarithmically and linearly divergent integrals in the basic building blocks for the exclusive hadronic B¯q→P​P→subscript¯𝐵𝑞𝑃𝑃barBqto PP, B¯q→P​V→subscript¯𝐵𝑞𝑃𝑉barBqto PV and B¯q→V​V→subscript¯𝐵𝑞𝑉𝑉barBqto VV (with q=d,s𝑞𝑑𝑠q=d,,s) decay amplitudes citebib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10 (see also citebib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13 and references therein) introduce further assumption for the current QCD factorization predictions, especially with the finite bottom-quark mass in practice, which has been examined exploratorily by employing the data-driven strategy citebib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19. The striking importance of the weak annihilation mechanism in investigating the non-leptonic B𝐵B-meson decay amplitudes also motivated the interesting dynamical anatomy citebib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22 on the basis of distinct QCD techniques (see citebib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31 for an alternative treatment taking into account the non-vanishing transverse momenta of the active partons). As a consequence, accomplishing the systematic computation of the weak annihilation topologies with the non-trivial strong phases will be of the top priority for developing the conceptual framework of the charmless two-body B¯q→M1​M2→subscript¯𝐵𝑞subscript𝑀1subscript𝑀2barBqto M1M2 decays and for better confronting the anticipated precision measurements of a large number of CP violating observables at the Belle II experiment citebib.bib32. In order to probe factorization properties of the weak annihilation contributions immaculately, we report on a novel observation of the hard-collinear gluon exchange (the short-distance configuration with the invariant mass of 𝒪(mb​ΛQCD))cal O(sqrtmb,Lambdarm QCD)) in regularizing the unwanted rapidity divergences analytically, which has been unfortunately neglected in all previous calculations of the two-body hadronic B𝐵B-meson decays, by concentrating on the pure annihilation channels such as B¯s→π​π→subscript¯𝐵𝑠𝜋𝜋barBstopipi and B¯d→ϕ​ϕ→subscript¯𝐵𝑑italic-ϕitalic-ϕbarBdtophiphi. The significance of this essential insight for consolidating the theory foundation of QCD factorization in B𝐵B-meson decaying into two light mesons will be further strengthened by inspecting a sample set of the next-to-leading order (NLO) Feynman diagrams, which are enhanced by the large Wilson coefficients and/or the multiplication CKM factors numerically and are promising to yield the sizeable strong phases. General implications of the improved formalism on the accessible decay observables will be then discussed with the acceptable models for the leading-twist light-cone distribution amplitudes of both the bottom meson and the final-state hadrons.

It proves convenient to cast the exclusive transition amplitude governing the weak-annihilation bottom-meson decay in the form 𝒜¯​(B¯q→M1​M2)=−⟨M1​(p1)​M2​(p2)|ℋeff|B¯q​(pB)⟩¯𝒜→subscript¯𝐵𝑞subscript𝑀1subscript𝑀2quantum-operator-productsubscript𝑀1subscript𝑝1subscript𝑀2subscript𝑝2subscriptℋeffsubscript¯𝐵𝑞subscript𝑝𝐵displaystylebarcal A(barBqto M1M2)=- M1(p1),M2(p2)|cal Hrm eff|barBq(pB) =∓i​4​GF2​fB​fM1​fM2​∑p=u,cVp​b​Vp​q∗​(π​αs)​CFNc2absentminus-or-plus𝑖4subscript𝐺𝐹2subscript𝑓𝐵subscript𝑓subscript𝑀1subscript𝑓subscript𝑀2subscript𝑝𝑢𝑐subscript𝑉𝑝𝑏superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑝𝑞∗𝜋subscript𝛼𝑠subscript𝐶𝐹superscriptsubscript𝑁𝑐2displaystyle=mp,i,4,GFoversqrt2,fB,fM1,fM2,sump=u,cVpb,Vpqast,left(pi,alphasright),CFover Nc2, ×[𝒯p,+(αs4​π)​𝒯p,+𝒪​(αs2)],absentdelimited-superscript𝒯𝑝0subscript𝛼𝑠4𝜋superscript𝒯𝑝1𝒪superscriptsubscript𝛼𝑠2displaystylehskip 14.22636pttimesleft[cal Tp,,+left(alphasover 4,piright),cal Tp,,+cal O(alphas2)right],,hskip 14.22636pt where the upper sign in applies for M1​M2=P​P,VL​VLsubscript𝑀1subscript𝑀2𝑃𝑃subscript𝑉𝐿subscript𝑉𝐿M1,M2=PP,,VLVL, while the lower sign when M1​M2=P​V,V​Psubscript𝑀1subscript𝑀2𝑃𝑉𝑉𝑃M1,M2=PV,,VP. The yielding contribution due to the gluon emission from the bottom quark (i. e. , the LO diagram (b)) has been demonstrated to be calculable self-consistently at twist-two order citebib.bib39, bib.bib2. We are then led to anatomize the infrared behaviour of the effective matrix element represented by the first diagram in Figure (with an insertion of P2usuperscriptsubscript𝑃2𝑢P2u for the illustration purpose) ⟨P2u⟩(a)subscriptsuperscriptdelimited-⟨⟩superscriptsubscript𝑃2𝑢0𝑎displaystyle P2u(a) =displaystyle= (π​αs)​CFNc2​∫0∞𝑑ω​∫01𝑑x​∫01𝑑y𝜋subscript𝛼𝑠subscript𝐶𝐹superscriptsubscript𝑁𝑐2superscriptsubscript0differential-d𝜔superscriptsubscript01differential-d𝑥superscriptsubscript01differential-d𝑦displaystyleleft(pi,alphasright),fracCFNc2,int0inftydomega,int01dx,int01dy, where the explicit expressions of the momentum-space projection operators ℳB,ℳM1superscriptℳ𝐵superscriptℳsubscript𝑀1cal MB,,cal MM1 and ℳM2superscriptℳsubscript𝑀2cal MM2 have been derived in citebib.bib40, bib.bib7, bib.bib41, bib.bib9 (see citebib.bib42 for the original discussion on B𝐵B-meson distribution amplitudes in heavy quark effective theory (HQET) and citebib.bib43 for an alternative construction of the on-shell light-cone projectors). To this end, we will employ the three-parameter ansätz of the leading-twist bottom-meson distribution amplitude as proposed in citebib.bib55 (see citebib.bib59, bib.bib60, bib.bib61, bib.bib62, bib.bib63, bib.bib64, bib.bib65, bib.bib66, bib.bib67, bib.bib68 for additional discussions on the higher-twist distribution amplitudes) ϕB+​(ω,μ0)=Γ​(β)Γ​(α)​U​(β−α,3−α,ωω0)​ωω02​exp​(−ωω0)superscriptsubscriptitalic-ϕ𝐵𝜔subscript𝜇0Γ𝛽Γ𝛼𝑈𝛽𝛼3𝛼𝜔subscript𝜔0𝜔superscriptsubscript𝜔02exp𝜔subscript𝜔0phiB+(omega,,mu0)=Gamma(beta)overGamma(alpha),Uleft(beta-alpha,3-alpha,omegaoveromega0right)omegaoveromega02rm expleft(-omegaoveromega0right) at the reference scale μ0=1​GeVsubscript𝜇01GeVmu0=1,rm GeV, where U​(a,b,z)𝑈𝑎𝑏𝑧U(a,b,z) represents the confluent hypergeometric function of the second kind and the allowed intervals of the shape parameters ω0subscript𝜔0omega0, α𝛼alpha and β𝛽beta are determined by reproducing the numerical values of the inverse-logarithmic moments citebib.bib48, bib.bib56 λBd=350±150​MeVsubscript𝜆subscript𝐵𝑑plus-or-minus350150MeVlambdaBd=350pm 150,rm MeV, λBs=400±150​MeVsubscript𝜆subscript𝐵𝑠plus-or-minus400150MeVlambdaBs=400pm 150,rm MeV, σBd,s=0.0±0.7superscriptsubscript𝜎subscript𝐵𝑑𝑠1plus-or-minus0.00.7widehatsigmaBd,s=0.0pm 0.7, σBd,s=0.0±6.0superscriptsubscript𝜎subscript𝐵𝑑𝑠2plus-or-minus0.06.0widehatsigmaBd,s=0.0pm 6.0. We proceed to adopt the lattice predictions for the pseudoscalar-meson decay constants (fBdsubscript𝑓subscript𝐵𝑑fBd, fBssubscript𝑓subscript𝐵𝑠fBs, fπsubscript𝑓𝜋fpi, fKsubscript𝑓𝐾fK) as summarized in citebib.bib69 and take the improved extractions of the decay constants of the longitudinally polarized vector mesons (fρsubscript𝑓𝜌frho, fωsubscript𝑓𝜔fomega, fϕsubscript𝑓italic-ϕfphi) from citebib.bib70. The default values and uncertainties for the remaining SM parameters follow Table 1 of citebib.bib56. In order to develop a transparent understanding of the numerical feature for the identified enhancement mechanism, we present in Table the obtained results of 𝒯p,superscript𝒯𝑝0cal Tp,, and 𝒯p,superscript𝒯𝑝1cal Tp,, for the weak annihilation decay B¯s→π+​π−→subscript¯𝐵𝑠superscript𝜋superscript𝜋barBstopi+pi- with three distinct values of the renormalization scale ν=mb/2,mb, 2​mb𝜈subscript𝑚𝑏2subscript𝑚𝑏2subscript𝑚𝑏nu=mb/2,,mb,,2,mb, while employing the central values of the remaining theory inputs. Interestingly, the dominant NLO contribution from the charm-loop diagrams displayed in Figure (c) and (d) (plus the two diagrams due to the crossing symmetry) will result in the significant increase of the real part of 𝒯c,superscript𝒯𝑐0cal Tc,, in magnitude and simultaneously generates the considerable cancellation of its imaginary part, keeping in mind the CKM hierarchy Vu​b​Vu​s∗/Vc​b​Vc​s∗=0.021​e−1.20​isubscript𝑉𝑢𝑏superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑢𝑠∗subscript𝑉𝑐𝑏superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑐𝑠∗0.021superscript𝑒1.20𝑖VubVusast/VcbVcsast=0.021,e-1.20,i. In consequence, the perturbative rescattering effect under discussion provides an important source of the strong phases of the weak-annihilation non-leptonic B¯qsubscript¯𝐵𝑞barBq-meson decay amplitudes, which can then be probed feasibly by investigating the CP violating observables. It is straightforward to derive the time-dependent CP asymmetries for the neutral B¯qsubscript¯𝐵𝑞barBq-meson decaying into CP eigenstates 𝒜CP​(t)subscript𝒜CP𝑡displaystylecal Arm CP(t) =displaystyle= Γ​(B¯q→M1​M2)−Γ​(Bq→M1​M2)Γ​(B¯q→M1​M2)+Γ​(Bq→M1​M2)Γ→subscript¯𝐵𝑞subscript𝑀1subscript𝑀2Γ→subscript𝐵𝑞subscript𝑀1subscript𝑀2Γ→subscript¯𝐵𝑞subscript𝑀1subscript𝑀2Γ→subscript𝐵𝑞subscript𝑀1subscript𝑀2displaystylefracGamma(barBqto M1M2)-Gamma(Bqto M1M2)Gamma(barBqto M1M2)+Gamma(Bqto M1M2) where the general definitions of 𝒜CPdirsuperscriptsubscript𝒜CPdircal Arm CPrm dir, 𝒜CPmixsuperscriptsubscript𝒜CPmixcal Arm CPrm mix and 𝒜Δ​Γsubscript𝒜ΔΓcal ADeltaGamma can be found, for instance, in citebib.bib72, bib.bib73, bib.bib74, satisfying the exact algebra relation |𝒜CPdir|2+|𝒜CPmix|2+|𝒜Δ​Γ|2=1superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝒜CPdir2superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝒜CPmix2superscriptsubscript𝒜ΔΓ21|cal Arm CPrm dir|2+|cal Arm CPrm mix|2+|cal ADeltaGamma|2=1. The resulting predictions for the two independent CP asymmetries displayed in Table evidently reveal the striking numerical impacts of the enhanced NLO QCD correction to the weak annihilation amplitudes described in this Letter, without which the direct CP violation for the entire pure annihilation channels would vanish due to the absence of at least two separate terms with different strong phases involved in 𝒜¯​(B¯q→M1​M2)¯𝒜→subscript¯𝐵𝑞subscript𝑀1subscript𝑀2barcal A(barBqto M1M2). By contrast, the time-dependent CP asymmetries of B¯s→ρL​ωL→subscript¯𝐵𝑠subscript𝜌𝐿subscript𝜔𝐿barBstorhoL,omegaL turn out to be insensitive to this particular perturbative contribution in question, on account of the isopsin non-conservation of the QCD scattering amplitude ⟨ρL​(p)​ωL​(q)|g∗​(pg,α)​g∗​(pg,β)⟩inner-productsubscript𝜌𝐿𝑝subscript𝜔𝐿𝑞superscript𝑔∗subscript𝑝𝑔𝛼superscript𝑔∗subscript𝑝𝑔𝛽rhoL(p),omegaL(q)|gast(pg,alpha),gast(tildepg,beta). We further mention in passing that the newly computed NLO quantities 𝒯p,superscript𝒯𝑝1cal Tp,, generate the subdominant corrections to the CP-averaged branching fractions of the pure annihilation decay channels (numerically at the level of 𝒪​(10%)𝒪percent10cal O(10,%)).
content_sections.V Conclusions: To summarize, we have endeavored to achieve the analytical regularization of end-point divergences in the factorization formulae of the peculiar weak annihilation non-leptonic bottom-meson decay amplitudes, due to the gluon radiation off the initial-state partons, by adding the missing hard-collinear contribution on top of the hard gluon exchange effect, both of which led to the leading power contributions in the heavy quark expansion. Applying the improved factorization formalism, we then identified the novel perturbative mechanism generated by the penguin contractions of the current-current operators, yielding the significant numerical impacts on the strong phases of the pure annihilation amplitudes. Our results are of importance for enhancing the predictive power of the perturbative factorization approach in addressing the entire spectrum of CP violating observables from exclusive heavy hadron decays, accessible at the LHCb and Belle II experiments. all_papers.title:
Estimating QCD-factorization amplitudes through $SU(3)$ symmetry in
  $B\rightarrow P P$ decays
all_papers.abstract:
We estimate the potential size of the weak annihilation amplitudes in QCD
factorization as allowed by experimental data. To achieve this goal, we
establish a connection between the amplitudes in the QCD factorization and the
so-called topological and $SU(3)$-invariant descriptions. Our approach is based
purely on the analysis of the tensor structure of the decay amplitudes. By
focusing on the processes $B\rightarrow P P$ and considering data from CP
asymmetries and branching fractions, we perform a global fit to the
$SU(3)$-irreducible quantities assuming minimal theoretical bias. Subsequently
we translate the outcome to the QCD factorization decomposition, and find that
the most constrained weak annihilation amplitudes are below $4\%$. However, in
view of the large uncertainties in several of the experimental input
parameters, values up to $30\%$ are allowed in certain cases.
