all_papers.title: Hadronic three-body D decays mediated by scalar resonances
all_papers.abstract:
We study the quasi-two-body $D\to SP$ decays and the three-body $D$ decays
proceeding through intermediate scalar resonances, where $S$ and $P$ denote
scalar and pseudoscalar mesons, respectively. Our main results are: (i) Certain
external and internal $W$-emission diagrams with the emitted meson being a
scalar meson are na{\"i}vely expected to vanish, but they actually receive
contributions from vertex and hard spectator-scattering corrections beyond the
factorization approximation. (ii) For light scalars with masses below or close
to 1~GeV, it is more sensible to study three-body decays directly and compare
with experiment as the two-body branching fractions are either unavailable or
subject to large finite-width effects of the scalar meson. (iii) We consider
the two-quark (scheme I) and four-quark (scheme II) descriptions of the light
scalar mesons, and find the latter generally in better agreement with
experiment. This is in line with recent BESIII measurements of semileptonic
charm decays that prefer the tetraquark description of light scalars produced
in charmed meson decays. (iv) The topological amplitude approach fails here as
the $D\to SP$ decay branching fractions cannot be reliably inferred from the
measurements of three-body decays, mainly because the decay rates cannot be
factorized into the topological amplitude squared and the phase space factor.
(v) The predicted rates for $D^0\to f_0 P, a_0 P$ are generally smaller than
experimental data by one order of magnitude, presumably implying the
significance of $W$-exchange amplitudes. (vi) The $W$-annihilation amplitude is
found to be very sizable in the $SP$ sector with $|A/T|_{SP}\sim 1/2$, contrary
to its suppression in the $PP$ sector with $|A/T|_{PP}\sim 0.18$.

sections.16: Barnes, “Two Photon Decays Support the (K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾K\bar{K}) Molecule Picture of the S∗​(975)superscript𝑆975S^{*}(975) and δ​(980)𝛿980\delta(980),” Phys

qualitative: Certain external and internal W-emission diagrams with a scalar meson as the emitted particle are naively expected to vanish but receive non-zero contributions from vertex and hard spectator-scattering corrections beyond the factorization approximation.

For light scalar mesons with masses below or close to 1 GeV, direct study of three-body decays is more appropriate than two-body decays due to large finite-width effects and the unavailability of reliable two-body branching fractions.

The four-quark (tetraquark) description of light scalar mesons (scheme II) provides better agreement with experimental data compared to the two-quark (q¯q) description (scheme I), consistent with recent BESIII measurements of semileptonic charm decays.

The topological amplitude approach fails in the SP sector because decay rates cannot be reliably factorized into topological amplitude squared and phase space factor, making it impossible to infer D→SP branching fractions from three-body decay measurements.
The predicted branching fractions for D⁰→f₀P and D⁰→a₀P are generally smaller than experimental data by about one order of magnitude, suggesting a significant contribution from W-exchange amplitudes.
The W-annihilation amplitude in the SP sector is sizable, with |A/T|_{SP} ≈ 1/2, in contrast to its suppression in the PP sector where |A/T|_{PP} ≈ 0.18.
quantitative: Predicted D⁰→f₀P and D⁰→a₀P branching fractions are smaller than experimental data by one order of magnitude
Light scalar mesons below 1 GeV include σ/f₀(500), f₀(980), a₀(980), and κ/K₀*(700)
Scalar mesons above 1 GeV include a₀(1450), K₀*(1430), f₀(1370), f₀(1500), and f₀(1710)
Finite-width effects are particularly important for decays involving σ/f₀(500) and κ/K₀*(700) due to their broad widths relative to their masses

authors: Hai-Yang Cheng, Cheng-Wei Chiang, Zhi-Qing Zhang

content_sections.I Introduction: In recent years many measurements of hadronic three-body and four-body decays of charmed mesons have been performed with Dalitz-plot amplitude analyses. Amplitudes describing D𝐷D meson decays into multibody final states are dominated by quasi-two-body processes, such as D→P​P,V​P,S​P,A​P→𝐷𝑃𝑃𝑉𝑃𝑆𝑃𝐴𝑃Dto PP,VP,SP,AP and T​P𝑇𝑃TP, where P,V,S,A𝑃𝑉𝑆𝐴P,V,S,A and T𝑇T denote pseudoscalar, vector, scalar, axial-vector and tensor mesons, respectively. Among various S𝑆S-, P𝑃P- and D𝐷D-wave intermediate resonances, the identification of the scalar mesons is rather difficult due to their broad widths and flat angular distributions. Scalar mesons with masses lower than 2 GeV can be classified into two nonets: one nonet with masses below or close to 1 GeV, including σ/f0​𝜎subscript𝑓0500sigma/f0, f0​subscript𝑓0980f0, a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 and κ/K0∗​𝜅superscriptsubscript𝐾0700kappa/K0*; and the other nonet with masses above 1 GeV, including a0​subscript𝑎01450a0, K0∗​subscriptsuperscript𝐾01430K*0, f0​subscript𝑓01370f0, f0​subscript𝑓01500f0 and f0​subscript𝑓01710f0. Since the last three are all isosinglet scalars and only two of them can be accommodated in the quark model, implying a dominant scalar glueball content in one of the three isosinglets. In this work, we shall study the quasi-two-body D→S​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃Dto SP decays and the three-body D𝐷D decays proceeding through intermediate scalar resonances. In Tables and we collect all the measured branching fractions of D→S​P→P1​P2​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃→subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2𝑃Dto SPto P1P2P decays available in the Particle Data Group (PDG) citebib.bib1. It is clear that f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and the f0subscript𝑓0f0 family such as f0​subscript𝑓01370f0, f0​subscript𝑓01500f0 and f0​subscript𝑓01710f0 are observed in the three-body decays of D+,D0superscript𝐷superscript𝐷0D+,D0 and Ds+superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠Ds+, while a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 is seen exclusively in three-body D0superscript𝐷0D0 decays (except for Ds+→a0+,0​π0,+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑎00superscript𝜋0Ds+to a0+,0pi0,+). Contrary to f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 which are relatively easy to identify experimentally, the establishment of σ𝜎sigma and κ𝜅kappa is very difficult and controversial because their widths are so broad that their shapes are not clearly resonant. Nevertheless, their signals in three-body D𝐷D decays have been identified in D+,0→σ​π+,0→π+​π−​π+,0→superscript𝐷0𝜎superscript𝜋0→superscript𝜋superscript𝜋superscript𝜋0D+,0tosigmapi+,0topi+pi-pi+,0, D+→κ¯0​π+→KS​π0​π+→superscript𝐷superscript¯𝜅0superscript𝜋→subscript𝐾𝑆superscript𝜋0superscript𝜋D+tobarkappa0pi+to KSpi0pi+ and D+→κ¯0​K+→π+​K−​K+→superscript𝐷superscript¯𝜅0superscript𝐾→superscript𝜋superscript𝐾superscript𝐾D+tobarkappa0K+topi+K-K+, respectively. Because of threshold and coupled-channel effects for f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 and the very broad widths for σ𝜎sigma and κ𝜅kappa, it is no longer pertinent to use the conventional Breit-Wigner parametrization to describe their line shapes. The D→S​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃Dto SP decays and related three-body D𝐷D decays have been studied previously in Refs. citebib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib5, bib.bib4, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib11, bib.bib10, bib.bib12, bib.bib13. In the D→S​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃Dto SP decays, the flavor diagram of each topology has two possibilities: one with the spectator quark in the charmed meson going to the pseudoscalar meson in the final state, and the other with the spectator quark ending up in the scalar meson. We thus need two copies of each topological diagram to describe the decay processes. Many of these decays have been observed in recent years through dedicated experiments and powerful Dalitz plot analysis of multi-body decays. We will investigate whether an extraction of the sizes and relative strong phases of these amplitudes is possible. One purpose of studying these decays is to check our understanding in the structures and properties of light even-parity scalar mesons. Another goal is to learn the final-state interaction pattern in view of the rich resonance spectrum around the D𝐷D meson mass range. Not only does this work update our previous study citebib.bib9, we also study the finite-width effect in the three-body decays mediated by the scalar mesons. Such an effect is observed to be particularly important for decays involving σ/f0​𝜎subscript𝑓0500sigma/f0 and κ/K0∗​𝜅superscriptsubscript𝐾0700kappa/K0* in the intermediate state because of their broad widths compared to their masses, respectively. Therefore, one should be careful in the use of the narrow width approximation (NWA) to extract the D→S​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃Dto SP two-body decays from the three-body decay rates. This paper is organized as follows. In Section, we review the current experimental status about how various D→S​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃Dto SP decay branching fractions are extracted using the NWA from three-body decay rates. In Section, we discuss the two-quark q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq and tetraquark pictures of the scalar nonet near or below 1GeV along with the associated conundrums. The decay constants and form factors required for subsequent numerical calculations are given in this section, too. Section sets up the notation and formalism of flavor amplitude analysis, for both quark-antiquark and tetraquark pictures. In Section, we take the factorization approach as an alternative toward analyzing these decays. We also introduce line shapes for the scalar resonances when describing various three-body decays. Section gives the results obtained based upon the approaches in the previous two sections for a comparison. Section is devoted to the study of finite-width effect and how the NWA should be modified. We summarize our findings in Section.
content_sections.II Experimental status: It is known that three- and four-body decays of heavy mesons provide a rich laboratory for studying the intermediate-state resonances. The Dalitz plot analysis of three-body or four-body decays of charmed mesons is a very useful technique for this purpose. We are interested in D→S​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃Dto SP decays followed by S→P1​P2→𝑆subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2Sto P1P2. The results of various experiments are summarized in Tables and . To extract the branching fraction for a D→S​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃Dto SP decay, it is the usual practice to use the NWA: Γ​(D→S​P→P1​P2​P)=Γ​(D→S​P)NWA​ℬ​(S→P1​P2). content_sections.III Physical properties of scalar mesons: It is known that the underlying structure of scalar mesons is not well established theoretically (see, e. g. , Refs. citebib.bib19, bib.bib20 for a review). Scalar mesons with masses lower than 2GeV can be classified into two nonets: one nonet with masses below or close to 1GeV, including the isoscalars f0​subscript𝑓0500f0 (or σ𝜎sigma), f0​subscript𝑓0980f0, the isodoublet K0∗​superscriptsubscript𝐾0700K0* (or κ𝜅kappa) and the isovector a0​subscript𝑎0980a0; and the other nonet with masses above 1GeV, including f0​subscript𝑓01370f0, a0​subscript𝑎01450a0, K0∗​subscriptsuperscript𝐾01430K*0 and f0​/f0​subscript𝑓01500subscript𝑓01710f0/f0. If the scalar meson states below or near 1GeV are identified as the conventional low-lying 0+superscript00+ q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq nonet, then the nonet states above 1GeV could be excited q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq states. In the naïve quark model, the flavor wave functions of the light scalars read σ=12​(u​u¯+d​d¯),f0=s​s¯,formulae-sequence𝜎12𝑢¯𝑢𝑑¯𝑑subscript𝑓0𝑠¯𝑠displaystylesigma=1oversqrt2(ubaru+dbard),qquadqquadf0=sbars, a00=12​(u​u¯−d​d¯),a0+=u​d¯,a0−=d​u¯,formulae-sequencesuperscriptsubscript𝑎0012𝑢¯𝑢𝑑¯𝑑formulae-sequencesuperscriptsubscript𝑎0𝑢¯𝑑superscriptsubscript𝑎0𝑑¯𝑢displaystyle a00=1oversqrt2(ubaru-dbard),qquadqquad a0+=ubard,qquad a0-=dbaru, κ+=u​s¯,κ0=d​s¯,κ¯0=s​d¯,κ−=s​u¯,formulae-sequencesuperscript𝜅𝑢¯𝑠formulae-sequencesuperscript𝜅0𝑑¯𝑠formulae-sequencesuperscript¯𝜅0𝑠¯𝑑superscript𝜅𝑠¯𝑢displaystylekappa+=ubars,qquadkappa0=dbars,qquadbarkappa0=sbard,qquadkappa-=sbaru, where an ideal mixing for f0subscript𝑓0f0 and σ𝜎sigma is assumed as f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 is the heaviest one and σ𝜎sigma the lightest one in the light scalar nonet. However, as summarized in Ref. citebib.bib9, this simple picture encounters several serious problems: 1. It is impossible to understand the mass degeneracy between f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0, which is the so-called “inverted spectrum problem. ” 2. The P𝑃P-wave 0+superscript00+ meson has one unit of orbital angular momentum which costs an energy around 500MeV. Hence, it should have a mass lying above rather than below 1GeV. 3. It is difficult to explain why σ𝜎sigma and κ𝜅kappa are much broader than f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 in width. 4. The γ​γ𝛾𝛾gammagamma widths of a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 and f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 are much smaller than naïvely expected for a q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq statecitebib.bib21. 5. The radiative decay ϕ→a0​​γ→italic-ϕsubscript𝑎0980𝛾phito a0gamma, which cannot proceed if a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 is a pure q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq state, can be nicely described by the four-quark nature of a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 citebib.bib22, bib.bib23 or the kaon loop mechanismcitebib.bib24. Likewise, the observation of the radiative decay ϕ→f0​​γ→π​π​γ→italic-ϕsubscript𝑓0980𝛾→𝜋𝜋𝛾phito f0gammatopipigamma is also accounted for by the four-quark state of f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 citebib.bib23. It turns out that these difficulties can be readily resolved in the tetraquark scenario where the four-quark flavor wave functions of light scalar mesons are symbolically given by citebib.bib25 σ=u​u¯​d​d¯,f0=12​(u​u¯+d​d¯)​s​s¯,formulae-sequence𝜎𝑢¯𝑢𝑑¯𝑑subscript𝑓012𝑢¯𝑢𝑑¯𝑑𝑠¯𝑠displaystylesigma=ubarudbard,qquadqquadqquadf0=frac1sqrt2(ubaru+dbard)sbars, a00=12​(u​u¯−d​d¯)​s​s¯,a0+=u​d¯​s​s¯,a0−=d​u¯​s​s¯,formulae-sequencesuperscriptsubscript𝑎0012𝑢¯𝑢𝑑¯𝑑𝑠¯𝑠formulae-sequencesuperscriptsubscript𝑎0𝑢¯𝑑𝑠¯𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑎0𝑑¯𝑢𝑠¯𝑠displaystyle a00=frac1sqrt2(ubaru-dbard)sbars,qquad a0+=ubardsbars,qquad a0-=dbarusbars, κ+=u​s¯​d​d¯,κ0=d​s¯​u​u¯,κ¯0=s​d¯​u​u¯,κ−=s​u¯​d​d¯. formulae-sequencesuperscript𝜅𝑢¯𝑠𝑑¯𝑑formulae-sequencesuperscript𝜅0𝑑¯𝑠𝑢¯𝑢formulae-sequencesuperscript¯𝜅0𝑠¯𝑑𝑢¯𝑢superscript𝜅𝑠¯𝑢𝑑¯𝑑displaystylekappa+=ubarsdbard,qquadkappa0=dbarsubaru,qquadbarkappa0=sbardubaru,qquadkappa-=sbarudbard. The four quarks q2​q¯2superscript𝑞2superscript¯𝑞2q2barq2 can form an S𝑆S-wave (rather than P𝑃P-wave) 0+superscript00+ meson without introducing one unit of orbital angular momentum. This four-quark description explains naturally the inverted mass spectrum of the light nonet, 1However, it has been claimed recently in Ref. citebib.bib26 that the inverse mass hierarchy can be realized in the q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq picture through a U​𝑈1U axial anomaly including explicit S​U​F𝑆𝑈subscript3𝐹SUF breaking. The anomaly term contributes to a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 with the strange quark mass and to κ/K0∗​𝜅superscriptsubscript𝐾0700kappa/K0* with the up or down quark mass due to its flavor singlet nature. The current mass of the strange quark makes the a0subscript𝑎0a0 meson heavier than the κ𝜅kappa meson. especially the mass degeneracy between f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0, and accounts for the broad widths of σ𝜎sigma and κ𝜅kappa while f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 are narrow because of the suppressed phase space for their decays to the kaon pairs. Lattice calculations have confirmed that a0​subscript𝑎01450a0 and K0∗​superscriptsubscript𝐾01430K0* are q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq mesons, and suggested that σ𝜎sigma, κ𝜅kappa and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 are tetraquark mesonia citebib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib31, bib.bib29, bib.bib30. The inverted spectrum problem can also be alleviated in the scenario where the light scalars are dynamically generated from the meson-meson interaction, with the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 coupling strongly to the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KoverlineK channel with isospin 0 and 1, respectively. Indeed, the whole light scalar nonet appears naturally from properly unitarized chiral amplitudes for pseudoscalar-pseudoscalar scatteringscitebib.bib32, bib.bib33. Consequently, both f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 are good candidates of K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KoverlineK molecular statescitebib.bib34, while σ𝜎sigma and κ𝜅kappa can be considered as the bound states of π​π𝜋𝜋pipi and K​π𝐾𝜋Kpi, respectively. In the naïve two-quark model with ideal mixing for f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and σ​𝜎500sigma, f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 is purely an s​s¯𝑠¯𝑠sbars state, while σ​𝜎500sigma is an n​n¯𝑛¯𝑛nbarn state with n​n¯≡(u¯​u+d¯​d)/2𝑛¯𝑛¯𝑢𝑢¯𝑑𝑑2nbarnequiv(baruu+bardd)/sqrt2. However, there also exists some experimental evidence indicating that f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 is not a purely s​s¯𝑠¯𝑠sbars state. Ref. citebib.bib51). content_sections.IV Diagrammatic amplitudes: A least model-dependent analysis of heavy meson decays can be carried out in the so-called topological diagram approach. In this diagrammatic scenario, all two-body nonleptonic weak decays of heavy mesons can be expressed in terms of six distinct quark diagrams citebib.bib53, bib.bib54, bib.bib52: T𝑇T, the external W𝑊W-emission tree diagram; C𝐶C, the internal W𝑊W-emission; E𝐸E, the W𝑊W-exchange; A𝐴A, the W𝑊W-annihilation; H𝐻H, the horizontal W𝑊W-loop; and V𝑉V, the vertical W𝑊W-loop. The one-gluon exchange approximation of the H𝐻H graph is the so-called “penguin diagram. ” These diagrams are classified according to the topologies of weak interactions with all strong interaction effects encoded. The topological amplitudes for D→S​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃Dto SP decays have been discussed in citebib.bib6, bib.bib9. Just as D→V​P→𝐷𝑉𝑃Dto V!P decays, one generally has two sets of distinct diagrams for each topology. For example, there are two external W𝑊W-emission and two internal W𝑊W-emission diagrams, depending on whether the emitted particle is an even-party meson or an odd-parity one. Following the convention in citebib.bib6, bib.bib9, we shall denote the primed amplitudes T′superscript𝑇′Tprime and C′superscript𝐶′Cprime for the case when the emitted meson is a scalar one. For the W𝑊W-exchange and W𝑊W-annihilation diagrams with the final state q1​q¯2subscript𝑞1subscript¯𝑞2q1barq2, the primed amplitude denotes that the even-parity meson contains the quark q1subscript𝑞1q1. Since K0∗subscriptsuperscript𝐾0K*0, a0​subscript𝑎01450a0 and the light scalars σ,κ,f0​,a0​𝜎𝜅subscript𝑓0980subscript𝑎0980sigma,kappa,f0,a0 fall into two different SU flavor nonets, in principle one cannot apply SU symmetry to relate the topological amplitudes in D+→f0​​π+→superscript𝐷subscript𝑓0980superscript𝜋D+to f0pi+ to, for example, those in D+→K¯0∗0​π+→superscript𝐷subscriptsuperscript¯𝐾absent00superscript𝜋D+tooverlineK*00pi+. In Ref. citebib.bib9 we have presented the topological amplitude decomposition in D→S​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃Dto SP decays in two different schemes. In scheme I, light scalar mesons σ,κ,a0​𝜎𝜅subscript𝑎0980sigma,kappa,a0 and f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 are described by the ground-state q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq states, while K0∗subscriptsuperscript𝐾0K*0 and a0​subscript𝑎01450a0 as excited q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq states. In scheme II, light scalars are tetraquark states, while K0∗subscriptsuperscript𝐾0K*0 and a0​subscript𝑎01450a0 are ground-state q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq. The topological amplitudes for D→S​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃Dto SP decays are listed in Table. The expressions of topological amplitudes are the same in both schemes I and II except for the channels involving f0subscript𝑓0f0 and σ𝜎sigma. For example, A​(D+→f0​π+)𝐴→superscript𝐷subscript𝑓0superscript𝜋displaystyle A(D+to f0pi+) =displaystyle= 12​Vc​d∗​Vu​d​(T+C′+A+A′)​sin⁡θ+Vc​s∗​Vu​s​C′​cos⁡θSchemeI,12​Vc​d∗​Vu​d​(T+C′+A+A′)+2​Vc​s∗​Vu​s​C′SchemeII,cases12superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑐𝑑subscript𝑉𝑢𝑑𝑇superscript𝐶′𝐴superscript𝐴′𝜃superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑐𝑠subscript𝑉𝑢𝑠superscript𝐶′𝜃SchemeI12superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑐𝑑subscript𝑉𝑢𝑑𝑇superscript𝐶′𝐴superscript𝐴′2superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑐𝑠subscript𝑉𝑢𝑠superscript𝐶′SchemeIIdisplaystyleleftbeginarraycl1oversqrt2Vcd*Vud(T+Cprime+A+Aprime)sintheta+Vcs*VusCprimecosthetaquadmboxSchemeI , 1oversqrt2Vcd*Vud(T+Cprime+A+Aprime)+sqrt2Vcs*VusCprimequadmboxSchemeII ,endarrayright. A​(D+→σ​π+)𝐴→superscript𝐷𝜎superscript𝜋displaystyle A(D+tosigmapi+) =displaystyle= 12​Vc​d∗​Vu​d​(T+C′+A+A′)​cos⁡θ−Vc​s∗​Vu​s​C′​sin⁡θSchemeI,Vc​d∗​Vu​d​(T+C′+A+A′)SchemeII. cases12superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑐𝑑subscript𝑉𝑢𝑑𝑇superscript𝐶′𝐴superscript𝐴′𝜃superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑐𝑠subscript𝑉𝑢𝑠superscript𝐶′𝜃SchemeIsuperscriptsubscript𝑉𝑐𝑑subscript𝑉𝑢𝑑𝑇superscript𝐶′𝐴superscript𝐴′SchemeIIdisplaystyleleftbeginarraycl1oversqrt2Vcd*Vud(T+Cprime+A+Aprime)costheta-Vcs*VusCprimesinthetaquadmboxSchemeI , Vcd*Vud(T+Cprime+A+Aprime)quadmboxSchemeII . endarrayright. In our numerical estimates, we will take θ=30∘𝜃superscript30theta=30circ, saturating the measured upper bound mentioned earlier. In Table the upper part involves only light scalar mesons (f0subscript𝑓0f0, a0subscript𝑎0a0, σ𝜎sigma, and κ𝜅kappa), whereas the lower part involves the a0​subscript𝑎01450a0 and K0∗​superscriptsubscript𝐾01430K0* mesons in the heavier nonet representation. This division is made because the amplitudes of the same topology in these two groups have no a priori relations. In each group we have 15 unknown parameters for the 8 topological amplitudes T,C,E,A𝑇𝐶𝐸𝐴T,C,E,A and T′,C′,E′,A′superscript𝑇′superscript𝐶′superscript𝐸′superscript𝐴′Tprime,Cprime,Eprime,Aprime. For neutral scalar mesons σ,f0𝜎subscript𝑓0sigma,f0 and a00superscriptsubscript𝑎00a00, we cannot set T′=C′=0superscript𝑇′superscript𝐶′0Tprime=Cprime=0 even though their vector decay constants vanish. As will be discussed in Sec. V. A, T′superscript𝑇′Tprime and C′superscript𝐶′Cprime do receive nonfactorizable contributions through vertex and spectator-scattering corrections citebib.bib55, bib.bib56. Nevertheless, it is naïvely expected that, for example, |T′|≪|T|much-less-thansuperscript𝑇′𝑇|Tprime|ll|T| and |C′|≪|C|much-less-thansuperscript𝐶′𝐶|Cprime|ll|C| for charged a0subscript𝑎0a0. However, as we shall see in Sec. V. C, a realistic calculation yields |C′|>|C|superscript𝐶′𝐶|Cprime|>|C| instead. At any rate, we have more theory parameters than observables (6 in the upper part and 5 in the lower part of the table), barring a fit. Since the branching fractions of f0→π​π→subscript𝑓0𝜋𝜋f0topipi and (f0,a0)→K​K¯→subscript𝑓0subscript𝑎0𝐾¯𝐾(f0,a0)to KoverlineK are unknown, many of the two-body decays in Table cannot be extracted from the data of three-body decays. Nevertheless, the strong couplings such as gf0→π​π,gf0→K​K¯,ga0→K​K¯subscript𝑔→subscript𝑓0𝜋𝜋subscript𝑔→subscript𝑓0𝐾¯𝐾subscript𝑔→subscript𝑎0𝐾¯𝐾gf0topipi,gf0to KbarK,ga0to KbarK and ga0→η​πsubscript𝑔→subscript𝑎0𝜂𝜋ga0toetapi have been inferred from a fit to the data. There are 17 available D→S​P→P1​P2​P2→𝐷𝑆𝑃→subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2subscript𝑃2Dto SPto P1P2P2 modes, but there are only 14 data related to D→S​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃Dto SP and we have 15 parameters to fit. Moreover, since we need to introduce appropriate energy-dependent line shapes for the scalar mesons, it is not conceivable to extract the topological amplitudes from three-body decays as the decay rate cannot be factorized into the topological amplitude squared and the phase space factor. We will come back to this point later. It is interesting to notice that the current data already imply the importance of W𝑊W-exchange and W𝑊W-annihilation amplitudes. Consider the decays: D0→a0+​π−→K+​K¯0​π−→superscript𝐷0superscriptsubscript𝑎0superscript𝜋→superscript𝐾superscript¯𝐾0superscript𝜋D0to a0+pi-to K+overlineK0pi- and D0→a0−​π+→K−​K0​π+→superscript𝐷0superscriptsubscript𝑎0superscript𝜋→superscript𝐾superscript𝐾0superscript𝜋D0to a0-pi+to K-K0pi+ with the two-body decay amplitudes proportional to (T′+E)superscript𝑇′𝐸(Tprime+E) and (T+E′)𝑇superscript𝐸′(T+Eprime), respectively (see Table). If the W𝑊W-exchange contributions are negligible, the former mode governed by the amplitude T′superscript𝑇′Tprime is expected to have a rate smaller than the latter (cf. Table ). Experimentally, it is the other way around. This is an indication that E𝐸E and E′superscript𝐸′Eprime play some role.
content_sections.V Factorization Approach: The diagrammatic approach has been applied quite successfully to hadronic decays of charmed mesons into P​P𝑃𝑃PP and V​P𝑉𝑃V!P final states citebib.bib58, bib.bib59, bib.bib60, bib.bib57, bib.bib61, bib.bib62, bib.bib63, bib.bib64, bib.bib65, bib.bib66. When generalized to the decay modes involving a scalar meson in the final state, it appears that the current data are still insufficient for us to fully extract the information of all amplitudes. Therefore, we take the naïve factorization formalism as a complementary approach to estimate the rates of these decay modes. In this framework, the W𝑊W-exchange and -annihilation type of contributions will be neglected.

content_sections.V Factorization Approach V.4 Flatté line: To describe three-body decays we need to introduce a line shape of the scalar resonance. Normally we use the relativistic Breit-Wigner line shape to describe the scalar resonance contributions to three-body decays D→S​P→P1​P2​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃→subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2𝑃Dto SPto P1P2P: TBW​(s)=1s−mR2+i​mR​ΓR​(s),superscript𝑇BW𝑠1𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑅2𝑖subscript𝑚𝑅subscriptΓ𝑅𝑠displaystyle Trm BW(s)=1over s-mR2+imRGammaR(s), with ΓR​(s)=ΓR0​(qq0)​mRs,subscriptΓ𝑅𝑠superscriptsubscriptΓ𝑅0𝑞subscript𝑞0subscript𝑚𝑅𝑠displaystyleGammaR(s)=GammaR0left(qover q0right)mRoversqrts, where q=|p→1|=|p→2|𝑞subscript→𝑝1subscript→𝑝2q=|vecp1|=|vecp2| is the c. m. momentum in the rest frame of R𝑅R, q0subscript𝑞0q0 the value of q𝑞q when s𝑠s is equal to mR2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑅2mR2. However, this parametrization is not suitable to describe the decay of f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 or a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 into K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KoverlineK as m​(K+)+m​(K−)=987.4𝑚superscript𝐾𝑚superscript𝐾987.4m(K+)+m(K-)=987.4 MeV and m​(K0)+m​(K¯0)=995.2𝑚superscript𝐾0𝑚superscript¯𝐾0995.2m(K0)+m(barK0)=995.2 MeV are near threshold. In other words, one has to take the threshold effect into account. content_sections.V Factorization Approach V.5 Line shape: As stressed in Ref. citebib.bib74, the scalar resonance σ/f0​𝜎subscript𝑓0500sigma/f0 is very broad and cannot be described by the usual Breit-Wigner line shape. Its partial wave amplitude does not resemble a Breit-Wigner shape with a clear peak and a simultaneous steep rise in the phase. The mass and width of the σ𝜎sigma resonance are identified from the associated pole position sσsubscript𝑠𝜎sqrtssigma of the partial wave amplitude in the second Riemann sheet as sσ=mσ−i​Γσ/2subscript𝑠𝜎subscript𝑚𝜎𝑖subscriptΓ𝜎2sqrtssigma=msigma-iGammasigma/2citebib.bib74. content_sections.VI Results and Discussion: In Tables and we have calculated two-body D→S​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃Dto SP and three-body D→S​P→P1​P2​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃→subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2𝑃Dto SPto P1P2P decays, respectively, in schemes I and II using the factorization approach with W𝑊W-exchange and W𝑊W-annihilation being neglected. We see from Table that the decay modes D+→a0+​K¯0,κ¯​π+→superscript𝐷superscriptsubscript𝑎0superscript¯𝐾0¯𝜅superscript𝜋D+to a0+overlineK0,barkappapi+ and K¯0∗​π+superscriptsubscript¯𝐾0superscript𝜋overlineK0*pi+ are free of W𝑊W-annihilation contributions and they are ideal for testing the validity of the factorization approach. From Table it is evident that the calculated rates of D+→κ¯​π+→KS​π0​π+→superscript𝐷¯𝜅superscript𝜋→subscript𝐾𝑆superscript𝜋0superscript𝜋D+tobarkappapi+to KSpi0pi+ and D+→K¯0∗0​π+→(K​π)0​π+→superscript𝐷subscriptsuperscript¯𝐾absent00superscript𝜋→superscript𝐾𝜋0superscript𝜋D+tooverlineK*00pi+to(Kpi)0pi+ in scheme II are in agreement with experiment. These modes are governed by the topologies T+C′𝑇superscript𝐶′T+Cprime which interfere constructively. This is in contrast to the Cabibbo-favored (CF) D+→K¯0​π+→superscript𝐷superscript¯𝐾0superscript𝜋D+tooverlineK0pi+ decay in the P​P𝑃𝑃P!P sector where T𝑇T and C𝐶C contribute destructively. For (D+,D0,Ds+)→f0​P;f0→P1​P2formulae-sequence→superscript𝐷subscript𝐷0superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑓0𝑃→subscript𝑓0subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2(D+,D0,Ds+)to f0P;f0to P1P2, predictions in scheme II are improved over that in scheme I and the discrepancies presumably arise from the W𝑊W-exchange or W𝑊W-annihilation amplitude. This implies that the tetraquark picture for light scalars works better than the quark-antiquark scenario. Upon an inspection of Table , the reader may wonder (i) why the branching fractions for D→(f0,σ)​P→𝐷subscript𝑓0𝜎𝑃Dto(f0,sigma)P decays in schemeII are always larger than that in schemeI except for D0→f0​π0→superscript𝐷0subscript𝑓0superscript𝜋0D0to f0pi0, and (ii) why the predicted branching fractions of D+→σ​π+→superscript𝐷𝜎superscript𝜋D+tosigmapi+ and D+→κ¯0​π+→superscript𝐷superscript¯𝜅0superscript𝜋D+tobarkappa0pi+ are larger than experimental data, while the corresponding three-body decays agree with the measurements. For (i), we see from TableIV and also Eq. that the W𝑊W-emission decay amplitude involving σ𝜎sigma is suppressed by a factor of cos⁡θ/2𝜃2costheta/sqrt2 in schemeI relative to that in schemeII, while it is suppressed by a factor of sin⁡θ𝜃sintheta for the W𝑊W-emission decay amplitude involving f0​subscript𝑓0980f0. As a consequence our choice of θ=30∘𝜃superscript30theta=30circ, the branching fractions for D→(f0,σ)​P→𝐷subscript𝑓0𝜎𝑃Dto(f0,sigma)P in schemeII are always larger than schemeI except for D0→f0​π0→superscript𝐷0subscript𝑓0superscript𝜋0D0to f0pi0. For (ii), it has something to do with the finite-width effects of σ𝜎sigma and κ𝜅kappa as they are both very broad. We shall see in Sec. that the extraction of ℬ​(D→S​P)ℬ→𝐷𝑆𝑃cal B(Dto SP) from the data is affected by the broad widths of both σ𝜎sigma and κ𝜅kappa.
content_sections.VI Results and Discussion VI.1 W𝑊W-annih: In the factorization calculations presented in Tables and , we have neglected both W𝑊W-exchange and W𝑊W-annihilation amplitudes. The Ds+→a0+​π0+a00​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑎0superscript𝜋0superscriptsubscript𝑎00superscript𝜋Ds+to a0+pi0+a00pi+ mode recently observed by BESIII citebib.bib17 proceeds only through the W𝑊W-annihilation amplitudes. However, its branching fraction at a percent level is much larger than the other two W𝑊W-annihilation channels Ds+→ω​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠𝜔superscript𝜋Ds+toomegapi+ and ρ0​π+superscript𝜌0superscript𝜋rho0pi+ whose branching fractions are (1.92±0.30)×10−3plus-or-minus1.920.30superscript103(1.92pm 0.30)times 10-3 and (1.9±1.2)×10−4plus-or-minus1.91.2superscript104(1.9pm 1.2)times 10-4, respectively citebib.bib1. This implies that |A​(S​P)|>|A​(V​P)|𝐴𝑆𝑃𝐴𝑉𝑃|A(SP)|>|A(V!P)|. In other words, the W𝑊W-annihilation amplitude plays a more significant role in the S​P𝑆𝑃SP sector than in the V​P𝑉𝑃V!P one. Consider the decay amplitude of Ds+→a00​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑎00superscript𝜋Ds+to a00pi+ and the W𝑊W-annihilation contribution to Ds+→f0​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑓0superscript𝜋Ds+to f0pi+ (in scheme II) 𝒜​(Ds+→a00​π+)=12​Vc​s∗​Vu​d​(−A+A′),𝒜​(Ds+→f0​π+)ann=12​Vc​s∗​Vu​d​(A+A′). formulae-sequence𝒜→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑎00superscript𝜋12superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑐𝑠subscript𝑉𝑢𝑑𝐴superscript𝐴′𝒜subscript→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑓0superscript𝜋ann12superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑐𝑠subscript𝑉𝑢𝑑𝐴superscript𝐴′displaystylecal A(Ds+to a00pi+)=1oversqrt2Vcs*Vud(-A+Aprime),qquadcal A(Ds+to f0pi+)rm ann=1oversqrt2Vcs*Vud(A+Aprime). Following the G𝐺G-parity argument given in Ref. citebib.bib57, it is obvious that the direct W𝑊W-annihilation process through c​s¯→W→u​d¯→𝑐¯𝑠𝑊→𝑢¯𝑑cbarsto Wto ubard is allowed in Ds+→f0​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑓0superscript𝜋Ds+to f0pi+ decay but not in Ds+→a00​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑎00superscript𝜋Ds+to a00pi+ decay as G​(u​d¯)=−𝐺𝑢¯𝑑G(ubard)=-, G​(a0​π)=+𝐺subscript𝑎0𝜋G(a0pi)=+ and G​(f0​π)=−𝐺subscript𝑓0𝜋G(f0pi)=-. This means that short-distance W𝑊W-annihilation contributions respect the relation A′=Asuperscript𝐴′𝐴Aprime=A, contrary to the naïve expectation. Hence, one needs large long-distance W𝑊W-annihilation which yields A′=−Asuperscript𝐴′𝐴Aprime=-A. Since Ds+→ρ+​η→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜌𝜂Ds+torho+eta has the largest branching fraction of (8.9±0.8)%percentplus-or-minus8.90.8(8.9pm 0.8)% among the CF Ds+→V​P→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠𝑉𝑃Ds+to VP decays citebib.bib1 , it is conceivable that long-distance contribution from the weak decays Ds+→ρ+​η→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜌𝜂Ds+torho+eta followed by the resonantlike final-state rescattering of ρ+​η→a00​π+→superscript𝜌𝜂superscriptsubscript𝑎00superscript𝜋rho+etato a00pi+ (see Fig. ), which has the same topology as W𝑊W-annihilation, may explain the large W𝑊W-annihilation rate. 6The hadronic weak decays Ds+→ρ+​η′,K¯∗0​K+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜌superscript𝜂′superscript¯𝐾absent0superscript𝐾Ds+torho+etaprime,overlineK*0K+ and K¯0​K∗+superscript¯𝐾0superscript𝐾absentoverlineK0K*+ followed by final-state rescattering will also contribute to Ds+→a00​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑎00superscript𝜋Ds+to a00pi+. It is customary to evaluate the final-state rescattering contribution, Fig. , at the hadron level manifested in Fig. . One of the diagrams, namely, the triangle graph in Fig. (b) has been evaluated recently in citebib.bib78, bib.bib79. It yields a major contribution to Ds+→a00​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑎00superscript𝜋Ds+to a00pi+ owing to the large coupling constants for ρ+→π+​π0→superscript𝜌superscript𝜋superscript𝜋0rho+topi+pi0 and a00→π0​η→superscriptsubscript𝑎00superscript𝜋0𝜂a00topi0eta. The graph in Fig. (a) shows the resonant final-state interactions manifested by the nearby resonance π​𝜋1800pi whose strong decay to a0​πsubscript𝑎0𝜋a0pi has been seen experimentally citebib.bib1. However, we are not able to have a quantitative statement owing to the lack of information on its partial width. Assuming A′≈−Asuperscript𝐴′𝐴Aprimeapprox-A, the annihilation amplitude extracted from the data of Ds+→a0+​π0+a00​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑎0superscript𝜋0superscriptsubscript𝑎00superscript𝜋Ds+to a0+pi0+a00pi+ is (in units of 10−6superscript10610-6 GeV), |A|=0.91±0.12. content_sections.VI Results and Discussion VI.2 Finite Wi: The finite-width effect is accounted for by the quantity ηRsubscript𝜂𝑅etaR defined by citebib.bib82, bib.bib83 ηR≡Γ​(D→R​P3→P1​P2​P3)ΓR→0Γ​(D→R​P3→P1​P2​P3)=Γ​(D→R​P3)​ℬ​(R→P1​P2)Γ​(D→R​P3→P1​P2​P3)=1+δ,subscript𝜂𝑅Γsubscript→𝐷𝑅subscript𝑃3→subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2subscript𝑃3→subscriptΓ𝑅0Γ→𝐷𝑅subscript𝑃3→subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2subscript𝑃3Γ→𝐷𝑅subscript𝑃3ℬ→𝑅subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2Γ→𝐷𝑅subscript𝑃3→subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2subscript𝑃31𝛿displaystyleetaRequivfracGamma(Dto RP3to P1P2P3)GammaRto 0Gamma(Dto RP3to P1P2P3)=fracGamma(Dto RP3)cal B(Rto P1P2)Gamma(Dto RP3to P1P2P3)=1+delta, so that the deviation of ηRsubscript𝜂𝑅etaR from unity measures the degree of departure from the NWA when the resonance width is finite. It is naïvely expected that the correction δ𝛿delta will be of order ΓR/mRsubscriptΓ𝑅subscript𝑚𝑅GammaR/mR. It is calculable theoretically but depends on the line shape of the resonance and the approach of describing weak hadronic decays such as QCD factorization and perturbative QCD. Using the branching fractions of two-body and three-body D𝐷D decays calculated in Tables and , respectively, in scheme II, the resultant ηRsubscript𝜂𝑅etaR parameters for scalar resonances σ,κ𝜎𝜅sigma,kappa and K0∗superscriptsubscript𝐾0K0* produced in the three-body D𝐷D decays are summarized in Table. We only consider the D+superscript𝐷D+ decays as the three-body modes listed in Table are not contaminated by the W𝑊W-annihilation amplitude and hence the calculated finite width effects are more trustworth. We have also checked explicitly that ηR→1→subscript𝜂𝑅1etaRto 1 in the narrow width limit as it should be. The ηRsubscript𝜂𝑅etaR parameters for various resonances produced in the three-body B𝐵B decays have been evaluated in citebib.bib82, bib.bib83. Our results for ηRsubscript𝜂𝑅etaR’s in Table have similar features as the values ησ/f0​=2.15±0.05subscript𝜂𝜎subscript𝑓0500plus-or-minus2.150.05etasigma/f0=2.15pm 0.05 and ηK0∗​=0.83±0.04subscript𝜂superscriptsubscript𝐾01430plus-or-minus0.830.04etaK0*=0.83pm 0.04 obtained in B𝐵B decays. Note that a priori we do not know if the deviation of ηRsubscript𝜂𝑅etaR from unity is positive or negative. In general, it depends on the line shape, mass and width of the resonance. As alluded to above, the mass and width have a more dominant effect than the line shape in the case of κ​𝜅700kappa. As another example, we found in Ref. that ηρ>1subscript𝜂𝜌1etarho>1 for the Breit-Wigner line shape and ηρ<1subscript𝜂𝜌1etarho<1 when the Gounaris-Sakurai model citebib.bib84 is used to describe the line shape of the broad ρ​𝜌770rho resonance. To our knowledge, there is no good argument favoring one line shape over the other. Therefore, ηK0∗​=0.985<1subscript𝜂superscriptsubscript𝐾014300.9851etaK0*=0.985<1, for example, is the result of our particular line shape choice. When the resonance is sufficiently broad, it is necessary to take into account the finite-width effects characterized by the parameter ηRsubscript𝜂𝑅etaR. Explicitly citebib.bib82, bib.bib83, ℬ​(D→R​P)=ηR​ℬ​(D→R​P)NWA=ηR​ℬ​(D→R​P3→P1​P2​P3)exptℬ​(R→P1​P2)expt,ℬ→𝐷𝑅𝑃subscript𝜂𝑅ℬsubscript→𝐷𝑅𝑃NWAsubscript𝜂𝑅ℬsubscript→𝐷𝑅subscript𝑃3→subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2subscript𝑃3exptℬsubscript→𝑅subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2exptdisplaystylecal B(Dto RP)=etaRcal B(Dto RP)rm NWA=etaRcal B(Dto RP3to P1P2P3)rm exptovercal B(Rto P1P2)rm expt, Therefore, the experimental branching fractions ℬ​(D→R​P)NWAℬsubscript→𝐷𝑅𝑃NWAcal B(Dto RP)rm NWA for D+→σ​π+,κ¯0​π+→superscript𝐷𝜎superscript𝜋superscript¯𝜅0superscript𝜋D+tosigmapi+,barkappa0pi+ and K¯0∗0​π+superscriptsubscript¯𝐾0absent0superscript𝜋overlineK0*0pi+ decays in Tables and should have the following corrections: ℬ​(D+→σ​π+)::ℬ→superscript𝐷𝜎superscript𝜋absentdisplaystylecal B(D+tosigmapi+): (2.1±0.2)×10−3→(3.8±0.3)×10−3,→plus-or-minus2.10.2superscript103plus-or-minus3.80.3superscript103displaystyle(2.1pm 0.2)times 10-3to(3.8pm 0.3)times 10-3, ℬ​(D+→κ¯0​π+)::ℬ→superscript𝐷superscript¯𝜅0superscript𝜋absentdisplaystylecal B(D+tobarkappa0pi+): (3.6−2.4+3.0)%→(6.7−4.5+5.6)%,displaystyle(3.6+3.0-2.4)%qquadquadto(6.7+5.6-4.5)%, ℬ​(D+→K¯0∗0​π+)::ℬ→superscript𝐷superscriptsubscript¯𝐾0absent0superscript𝜋absentdisplaystylecal B(D+tooverlineK0*0pi+): (1.98±0.22)%→(1.94±0.22)%. displaystyle(1.98pm 0.22)%quadto(1.94pm 0.22)%. From Table , it is evident that the agreement between theory and experiment is substantially improved for D+→σ​π+→superscript𝐷𝜎superscript𝜋D+tosigmapi+ and D+→κ¯0​π+→superscript𝐷superscript¯𝜅0superscript𝜋D+tobarkappa0pi+. If we employ the pole mass and width, mκ=648±7subscript𝑚𝜅plus-or-minus6487mkappa=648pm 7 MeV and Γκ=560±32subscriptΓ𝜅plus-or-minus56032Gammakappa=560pm 32 MeV, respectively, for κ/K0∗​𝜅superscriptsubscript𝐾0700kappa/K0* and the pole line shape given in Eq. , we will be led to the results ℬ​(D+→κ¯0​π+)=8.10%ℬ→superscript𝐷superscript¯𝜅0superscript𝜋percent8.10cal B(D+tobarkappa0pi+)=8.10%, ℬ​(D+→κ¯0​π+→KS0​π0​π+)=1.62×10−3ℬ→superscript𝐷superscript¯𝜅0superscript𝜋→superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0superscript𝜋0superscript𝜋1.62superscript103cal B(D+tobarkappa0pi+to KS0pi0pi+)=1.62times 10-3 and ηκ=8.34subscript𝜂𝜅8.34etakappa=8.34. This implies that the finite-width correction will be unreasonably too large and thus unlikely, as alluded to at the end of Sec. . However, if the Breit-Wigner mass and width are used instead, we get ηκ=1.92subscript𝜂𝜅1.92etakappa=1.92 for pole line shape, which is a more reasonable result. This implies that in this case, it is the mass and width rather than the line shape that governs the finite-width correction. For the case of f0​subscript𝑓0500f0, one may wonder what the correction will be if the Breit-Wigner line shape is used. According to PDG citebib.bib1, the Breit-Wigner mass and width of f0​subscript𝑓0500f0 lie in the wide ranges of 400-800MeV and 100-800MeV, respectively. As a result, it is quite difficult to pin down a specific set of parameters and thereby determine the finite-width correction. On the contrary, LHCb has determined its pole mass and width with reasonable accuracy using the pole line shape [see Eq. ]. It turns out that the pole mass and width fall within the above allowed ranges of the Breit-Wigner mass and width. Therefore, it is more sensible to use pole mass and width for calculations in either line shapes.
content_sections.VII Conclusions: In this work we have examined the quasi-two-body D→S​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃Dto SP decays and the three-body D𝐷D decays proceeding through intermediate scalar resonances. Our main results are: • In the D→S​P3→P1​P2​P3→𝐷𝑆subscript𝑃3→subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2subscript𝑃3Dto SP3to P1P2P3 decays, we cannot extract the two-body branching fractions ℬ​(D→S​P)ℬ→𝐷𝑆𝑃cal B(Dto SP) for S=f0​𝑆subscript𝑓0980S=f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 due to the lack of information of ℬ​(S→P1​P2)ℬ→𝑆subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2cal B(Sto P1P2) (except for a0​→π​η→subscript𝑎0980𝜋𝜂a0topieta). For S=κ/K0∗​𝑆𝜅superscriptsubscript𝐾0700S=kappa/K0* and σ/f0​𝜎subscript𝑓0500sigma/f0, the extracted two-body branching fractions are subject to large finite-width effects owing to their broad widths. Hence, for light scalars it is more sensible to study ℬ​(D→S​P→P1​P2​P)ℬ→𝐷𝑆𝑃→subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2𝑃cal B(Dto SPto P1P2P) directly and compare with experiment. • We have considered the two-quark (scheme I) and four-quark (scheme II) descriptions of the light scalar mesons with masses below or close to 1 GeV. Recent BESIII measurements of semileptonic charm decays favor the SU nonet tetraquark description of the f0​subscript𝑓0500f0, f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 produced in charmed meson decay. In Table we have calculated D→S​P3→P1​P2​P3→𝐷𝑆subscript𝑃3→subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2subscript𝑃3Dto SP3to P1P2P3 in schemes I and II. It is evident that scheme II agrees better with experiment for decays such as D+→f0​π+→superscript𝐷subscript𝑓0superscript𝜋D+to f0pi+ followed by f0→π+​π−→subscript𝑓0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋f0topi+pi- and D+→f0​K+→superscript𝐷subscript𝑓0superscript𝐾D+to f0K+ followed by f0→π+​π−→subscript𝑓0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋f0topi+pi- or f0→K+​K−→subscript𝑓0superscript𝐾superscript𝐾f0to K+K-. This again favors the tetraquark structure for light scalars. The predicted rates for D0→f0​P,a0​P→superscript𝐷0subscript𝑓0𝑃subscript𝑎0𝑃D0to f0P,a0P are generally smaller than experimental data by one order of magnitude, presumably implying the importance of W𝑊W-exchange. • The three-body decay modes D+→κ¯0(→KSπ0)π+D+tobarkappa0(to KSpi0)pi+, D+→K¯0∗(→K−π+)π+D+tooverlineK0*(to K-pi+)pi+ and D+→K¯0∗(→KSπ0)π+D+tooverlineK0*(to KSpi0)pi+ are ideal for testing the validity of the factorization approach as they are free of W𝑊W-annihilation contributions. T𝑇T and C′superscript𝐶′Cprime amplitudes contribute constructively, contrary to the Cabibbo-allowed D+→K¯0​π+→superscript𝐷superscript¯𝐾0superscript𝜋D+tooverlineK0pi+ decay where the interference between external and internal W𝑊W-emission is destructive. • Denoting the primed amplitudes T′superscript𝑇′Tprime and C′superscript𝐶′Cprime for the case when the emitted meson is a scalar meson, it is naïvely expected that T′=C′=0superscript𝑇′superscript𝐶′0Tprime=Cprime=0 for the neutral scalars σ,f0𝜎subscript𝑓0sigma,f0 and a00superscriptsubscript𝑎00a00, |T′|≪|T|much-less-thansuperscript𝑇′𝑇|Tprime|ll|T| and |C′|≪|C|much-less-thansuperscript𝐶′𝐶|Cprime|ll|C| for the charged a0subscript𝑎0a0 and |T′|<|T|superscript𝑇′𝑇|Tprime|<|T| and |C′|<|C|superscript𝐶′𝐶|Cprime|<|C| for the κ𝜅kappa and K0∗​superscriptsubscript𝐾01430K0*. Beyond the factorization approximation, contributions proportional to the scalar decay constant f¯Ssubscript¯𝑓𝑆barfS can be produced from vertex and hard spectator-scattering corrections for the above-mentioned neutral scalars. • We have studied the flavor operators a1,2​(M1​M2)subscript𝑎12subscript𝑀1subscript𝑀2a1,2(M1M2) for M1​M2=S​Psubscript𝑀1subscript𝑀2𝑆𝑃M1M2=SP and P​S𝑃𝑆PS within the framework of QCD factorization. Notice that ai​(P​S)subscript𝑎𝑖𝑃𝑆ai(PS) and ai​(S​P)subscript𝑎𝑖𝑆𝑃ai(SP) are very different as the former does not receive factorizable contributions. While a1,2​(S​P)subscript𝑎12𝑆𝑃a1,2(SP) are similar for any light and heavy scalar mesons, a1​(P​S)subscript𝑎1𝑃𝑆a1(PS) and a2​(P​S)subscript𝑎2𝑃𝑆a2(PS) vary from neutral to the charged ones as shown in Table . The flavor operators a1,2​(π​a0±)subscript𝑎12𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑎0plus-or-minusa1,2(pi a0pm) are much greater than a1,2​(π​a00)subscript𝑎12𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑎00a1,2(pi a00). In general, a1,2​(P​S)subscript𝑎12𝑃𝑆a1,2(PS) become larger when the vector decay constants become smaller. • For f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0, we use the Flatté line shape to describe both of them to take into account the threshold and coupled channel effects. For the very broad σ/f0​𝜎subscript𝑓0500sigma/f0 , we follow LHCb to employ a simple pole description. • The annihilation amplitude inferred from the measurement of Ds+→a0+,0​π0,+→η​π+,0​π0,+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑎00superscript𝜋0→𝜂superscript𝜋0superscript𝜋0Ds+to a0+,0pi0,+toetapi+,0pi0,+ is given by |A|=(0.91±0.12)×10−6​GeV𝐴plus-or-minus0.910.12superscript106GeV|A|=(0.91pm 0.12)times 10-6,rm GeV. It is very sizable in the S​P𝑆𝑃SP sector, |A/T|S​P∼1/2similar-tosubscript𝐴𝑇𝑆𝑃12|A/T|SPsim 1/2, contrary to its suppression in the P​P𝑃𝑃P!P sector with |A/T|P​P∼0.18similar-tosubscript𝐴𝑇𝑃𝑃0.18|A/T|PPsim 0.18. • Since σ𝜎sigma and κ𝜅kappa are very broad, we have considered their finite-width effects characterized by the parameter ηSsubscript𝜂𝑆etaS, whose deviation from unity measures the degree of departure from the NWA when the resonance width is finite. We find ησsubscript𝜂𝜎etasigma and ηκsubscript𝜂𝜅etakappa to be of order 1.85−1.871.851.871.85-1.87. The experimental branching fractions ℬ​(D+→σ​π+)ℬ→superscript𝐷𝜎superscript𝜋cal B(D+tosigmapi+) and ℬ​(D+→κ¯0​π+)ℬ→superscript𝐷superscript¯𝜅0superscript𝜋cal B(D+tobarkappa0pi+) should then read (3.8±0.3)×10−3plus-or-minus3.80.3superscript103(3.8pm 0.3)times 10-3 and (6.7−4.5+5.6)%percentsubscriptsuperscript6.75.64.5(6.7+5.6-4.5)%, respectively. • For each scalar nonet (lighter and heavier one) we have 15 unknown parameters for the 8 topological amplitudes T,C,E,A𝑇𝐶𝐸𝐴T,C,E,A and T′,C′,E′,A′superscript𝑇′superscript𝐶′superscript𝐸′superscript𝐴′Tprime,Cprime,Eprime,Aprime. However, there are only 14 independent data to fit. Moreover, since we need to introduce appropriate energy-dependent line shapes for the scalar mesons, it is not conceivable to extract the topological amplitudes from three-body decays as the decay rates cannot be factorized into the topological amplitude squared and the phase space factor.

all_papers.title: Tetraquark nature of the $a_0(980)$ meson in hadronic $D$ decays
all_papers.abstract:
The internal structure of the light scalar meson $a_0(980)$ is explored in
the three-body $D$ decays of $D\to a_0(980)P\to P_1P_2P$ through the
intermediate state $a_0(980)$, where $P$ denotes a pseudoscalar meson. The
quasi-two-body $D\to a_0(980)^+P$ decays are governed by the external
$W$-emission diagram in which $a_0(980)^+$ is emitted. The predicted branching
fractions in the $q\bar q$ model of $a_0(980)$ are too small by one to two
orders of magnitude compared to experiment as the amplitude is suppressed by
the smallness of the $a_0(980)^+$ decay constant, while those for $D^+\to
a_0(980)^0 P$ and $D^0\to a_0(980)^{-}P$ are usually too large. These
discrepancies can be resolved provided that $a_0(980)$ is a tetraquark state.
In this case, there exist two additional $T$-like topological amplitudes,
denoted by $\overline{T}$ and $\tilde T$ which readily account for the
discrepancies. An important implication of the tetraquark model is that the
$D_s^+\to a_0(980)^+\pi^0+a_0(980)^0\pi^+$ decay is not a purely
$W$-annihilation process as in the diquark model of $a_0(980)$; it receives
dominant contributions from $\overline{T}$ newly noticed in this work.
Therefore, measurements of $(D,D_s^+)\to a_0(980)P$ decays lend strong support
to the tetraquark picture of $a_0(980)$.

qualitative: The internal structure of the $a_0(980)$ meson is investigated in the context of three-body $D$ decays, particularly $D \to a_0(980)P \to P_1P_2P$, where $a_0(980)$ acts as an intermediate state.

In the $q\bar{q}$ quark model, the $a_0(980)$ meson has a small decay constant, leading to a suppression of the $W$-emission amplitude in $D \to a_0(980)^+P$ decays, resulting in predicted branching fractions that are too small by one to two orders of magnitude compared to experimental observations.
For $D^+ \to a_0(980)^0 P$ and $D^0 \to a_0(980)^- P$ decays, the $q\bar{q}$ model predicts branching fractions that are too large, indicating a significant discrepancy with experiment.

These discrepancies are resolved if the $a_0(980)$ is interpreted as a tetraquark state, which introduces two additional topological amplitudes, $\overline{T}$ and $\tilde{T}$, not present in the $q\bar{q}$ model.
The $\overline{T}$ and $\tilde{T}$ amplitudes are $T$-like and arise due to the tetraquark structure, enabling a more consistent description of the observed decay rates.

The decay $D_s^+ \to a_0(980)^+\pi^0 + a_0(980)^0\pi^+$ is not purely a $W$-annihilation process as assumed in the diquark model; instead, it is dominated by the newly identified $\overline{T}$ amplitude in the tetraquark picture.
Measurements of $(D, D_s^+) \to a_0(980)P$ decays provide strong empirical support for the tetraquark nature of the $a_0(980)$ meson.
quantitative: Predicted branching fractions in the $q\bar{q}$ model are too small by one to two orders of magnitude compared to experiment for $D \to a_0(980)^+P$ decays.
Branching fractions for $D^+ \to a_0(980)^0 P$ and $D^0 \to a_0(980)^- P$ in the $q\bar{q}$ model are too large compared to experimental data.
The presence of two additional topological amplitudes $\overline{T}$ and $\tilde{T}$ in the tetraquark model accounts for the observed discrepancies in branching fractions.
The decay $D_s^+ \to a_0(980)^+\pi^0 + a_0(980)^0\pi^+$ receives dominant contributions from the $\overline{T}$ amplitude, not from $W$-annihilation as in the diquark model.

论文紧扣弱衰变因子化近似与拓扑振幅分析主题，明确讨论 $D$ 衰变中 $W$-发射与 $T$-型振幅，验证 $a_0(980)$ 的四夸克结构，与查询核心一致。  

约束项检查：  
- C1：研究弱衰变因子化近似 — 满足。证据：“quasi-two-body $D\to a_0(980)^+P$ decays are governed by the external $W$-emission diagram” 对比 “弱衰变因子化近似”  
- C2：分析拓扑振幅 — 满足。证据：“two additional $T$-like topological amplitudes, denoted by $\overline{T}$ and $\tilde T$” 对比 “拓扑振幅分析”  
- C3：聚焦 $D$ 衰变过程 — 满足。证据：“$D\to a_0(980)P\to P_1P_2P$” 对比 “弱衰变因子化近似和拓扑振幅分析”  

不相关性 / 风险点 / 缺口：  
- 无。论文完全覆盖查询核心要求，无偏离。

B(D0to a0+pi-)overcal B(D0to a0-pi+)=7.5+2.5-0.8pm 1.7, r+/0equivcal B(D+to a0+pi0)overcal B(D+to a00pi+)=2.6pm 0.6pm 0.3. endsplit Naively, the ratios are expected to be much smaller than unity because the quasi-two-body decays D→a0​+​P→𝐷subscript𝑎0superscript980𝑃Dto a0+P, governed by the external W𝑊W-emission diagram with a0​+subscript𝑎0superscript980a0+ being an emitted meson, are suppressed by the smallness of the decay constant of a0​+subscript𝑎0superscript980a0+. The predicted branching fractions of D→a0​+​P→𝐷subscript𝑎0superscript980𝑃Dto a0+P in the q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq model of light scalar mesons are too small by one to two orders of magnitude compared to the experiment (for details, see Table below). On the contrary, the calculated rates of D+→a0​0​P→superscript𝐷subscript𝑎0superscript9800𝑃D+to a00P and D0→a0​−​P→superscript𝐷0subscript𝑎0superscript980𝑃D0to a0-P are usually too large compared to the data. For example, realistic calculations in Ref. citebib.bib2 indicate that r+⁣/−=0.045subscript𝑟absent0.045r+/-=0.045 which is too small by two orders of magnitude compared to the BESIII measurement. This strongly implies that the diquark scenario for a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 fails to explain the data. In the tetraquark model, we shall see that for the above-mentioned decays there exist two additional T𝑇T-like topological diagrams which we call T𝑇tildeT and T¯¯𝑇overlineT (the corresponding diagrams are shown in Fig. ). Unlike the external W𝑊W-emission diagram T′superscript𝑇′Tprime in which a0​+subscript𝑎0superscript980a0+ is the emitted meson, they are not suppressed by the smallness of the decay constant of the charged a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 meson. Although these amplitudes are non-factorizable and thus pose difficulty in perturbative calculations, their information can be extracted from the data. We shall see that these T𝑇T-like topologies will enable us to resolve the large discrepancy between theory and experiment occurring in the q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq model of a0​subscript𝑎0980a0. In the Ds+superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠Ds+ sector, the decay Ds+→a0+​π0+a00​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑎0superscript𝜋0superscriptsubscript𝑎00superscript𝜋Ds+to a0+pi0+a00pi+ followed by a0​+,0→π+,0​η→subscript𝑎0superscript9800superscript𝜋0𝜂a0+,0topi+,0eta has been observed by BESIII with a branching fraction of (1.46±0.27)%percentplus-or-minus1.460.27(1.46pm 0.27)%citebib.bib3. If a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 is a q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq state, this decay proceeds only through the W𝑊W-annihilation diagram. However, its branching fraction at a percent level is much larger than the other two W𝑊W-annihilation channels Ds+→ω​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠𝜔superscript𝜋Ds+toomegapi+ and ρ0​π+superscript𝜌0superscript𝜋rho0pi+ whose branching fractions are (1.92±0.30)×10−3plus-or-minus1.920.30superscript103(1.92pm 0.30)times 10-3 and (1.9±1.2)×10−4plus-or-minus1.91.2superscript104(1.9pm 1.2)times 10-4, respectivelycitebib.bib4. Naïvely, these data would imply that the annihilation amplitude is mysteriously sizable in the S​P𝑆𝑃SP sector, |A/T|S​P∼1/2similar-tosubscript𝐴𝑇𝑆𝑃12|A/T|SPsim 1/2citebib.bib2, in stark contrast to the suppressed |A/T|P​P∼0.18similar-tosubscript𝐴𝑇𝑃𝑃0.18|A/T|PPsim 0.18 in the P​P𝑃𝑃P!P sector and |AV/TP|V​P∼0.07similar-tosubscriptsubscript𝐴𝑉subscript𝑇𝑃𝑉𝑃0.07|AV/TP|VPsim 0.07 in the V​P𝑉𝑃V!P sectorcitebib.bib5. This is a puzzle as a priori there is no reason to expect a large enhancement of the W𝑊W-annihilation amplitude in the D→S​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃Dto SP decays. We find that if a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 is a tetraquark state instead, Ds+→a0+​π0+a00​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑎0superscript𝜋0superscriptsubscript𝑎00superscript𝜋Ds+to a0+pi0+a00pi+ will receive a contribution from T¯¯𝑇overlineT and the predicted rate agrees with experiment even in the absence of the W𝑊W-annihilation amplitude. This has an important implication that Ds+→a0+​π0+a00​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑎0superscript𝜋0superscriptsubscript𝑎00superscript𝜋Ds+to a0+pi0+a00pi+ is not a purely W𝑊W-annihilation process and thus the aforementioned puzzle is readily resolved. Very recently, BESIII made an observation of Λc+→Λ​a0​+→Λ​π+​η→superscriptsubscriptΛ𝑐Λsubscript𝑎0superscript980→Λsuperscript𝜋𝜂Lambdac+toLambda a0+toLambdapi+eta with a branching fraction of (1.05±0.16±0.05±0.07)%percentplus-or-minus1.050.160.050.07(1.05pm 0.16pm 0.05pm 0.07)%citebib.bib6, which is unexpectedly large in the diquark model. The factorizable contribution is rather suppressed due to the tiny a0+superscriptsubscript𝑎0a0+ decay constant. An early model calculation based on factorization and the pole model gave a branching fraction of order 1.9×10−41.9superscript1041.9times 10-4citebib.bib7. It had been improved by one order of magnitude after including final-state rescattering, yielding the result of (1.7−1.0+2.8±0.3)×10−3plus-or-minussubscriptsuperscript1.72.81.00.3superscript103(1.7+2.8-1.0pm 0.3)times 10-3citebib.bib8. In the tetraquark model of a0​subscript𝑎0980a0, however, this decay also proceeds through the diagram T𝑇tildeT to be introduced below, which allows to reach the branching fraction at a percent level. The layout of the present paper is as follows. In Section, we discuss the diquark q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq and tetraquark pictures of the scalar nonet near or below 1GeV and the discrimination of the two scenarios in semileptonic decays of charmed mesons. We turn to hadronic D→a0​​P→𝐷subscript𝑎0980𝑃Dto a0P decays in Section and emphasize the new topological diagrams unique to the four-quark nature of light scalars. We give the explicit expressions for the factorizable amplitudes and extract the new topologies from the available data. Section comes to our conclusions. A detailed calculation of the three-body decay rate is presented in the Appendix.
content_sections.II Physical properties of light scalar m: The underlying structure of light scalar mesons is not well established even until today. Scalar mesons with masses below or close to 1GeV include the isoscalars σ/f0​𝜎subscript𝑓0500sigma/f0, f0​subscript𝑓0980f0, the isodoublet κ/K0∗​𝜅superscriptsubscript𝐾0700kappa/K0* and the isovector a0​subscript𝑎0980a0. If these scalar meson states are identified as the conventional low-lying 0+superscript00+ q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq nonet, their flavor wave functions read σ=12​(u​u¯+d​d¯),f0=s​s¯,formulae-sequence𝜎12𝑢¯𝑢𝑑¯𝑑subscript𝑓0𝑠¯𝑠displaystylesigma=1oversqrt2(ubaru+dbard),qquadqquadf0=sbars, a00=12​(u​u¯−d​d¯),a0+=u​d¯,a0−=d​u¯,formulae-sequencesuperscriptsubscript𝑎0012𝑢¯𝑢𝑑¯𝑑formulae-sequencesuperscriptsubscript𝑎0𝑢¯𝑑superscriptsubscript𝑎0𝑑¯𝑢displaystyle a00=1oversqrt2(ubaru-dbard),qquadqquad a0+=ubard,qquad a0-=dbaru, κ+=u​s¯,κ0=d​s¯,κ¯0=s​d¯,κ−=s​u¯,formulae-sequencesuperscript𝜅𝑢¯𝑠formulae-sequencesuperscript𝜅0𝑑¯𝑠formulae-sequencesuperscript¯𝜅0𝑠¯𝑑superscript𝜅𝑠¯𝑢displaystylekappa+=ubars,qquadkappa0=dbars,qquadbarkappa0=sbard,qquadkappa-=sbaru, where an ideal mixing for f0subscript𝑓0f0 and σ𝜎sigma is assumed here as f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 is the heaviest one and σ𝜎sigma the lightest one in the light scalar nonet. However, this simple picture encounters several serious difficulties. Two of them are (i) it is impossible to understand the mass degeneracy between f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0, which is the so-called “inverted spectrum problem,” and (ii) it is difficult to explain why σ𝜎sigma and κ𝜅kappa are much broader than f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 in width. It turns out that these issues can be readily resolved in the tetraquark scenario where the four-quark flavor wave functions of light scalar mesons are symbolically given bycitebib.bib9 σ=u​u¯​d​d¯,f0=12​(u​u¯+d​d¯)​s​s¯,formulae-sequence𝜎𝑢¯𝑢𝑑¯𝑑subscript𝑓012𝑢¯𝑢𝑑¯𝑑𝑠¯𝑠displaystylesigma=ubarudbard,qquadqquadqquadf0=frac1sqrt2(ubaru+dbard)sbars, a00=12​(u​u¯−d​d¯)​s​s¯,a0+=u​d¯​s​s¯,a0−=d​u¯​s​s¯,formulae-sequencesuperscriptsubscript𝑎0012𝑢¯𝑢𝑑¯𝑑𝑠¯𝑠formulae-sequencesuperscriptsubscript𝑎0𝑢¯𝑑𝑠¯𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑎0𝑑¯𝑢𝑠¯𝑠displaystyle a00=frac1sqrt2(ubaru-dbard)sbars,qquad a0+=ubardsbars,qquad a0-=dbarusbars, κ+=u​s¯​d​d¯,κ0=d​s¯​u​u¯,κ¯0=s​d¯​u​u¯,κ−=s​u¯​d​d¯. formulae-sequencesuperscript𝜅𝑢¯𝑠𝑑¯𝑑formulae-sequencesuperscript𝜅0𝑑¯𝑠𝑢¯𝑢formulae-sequencesuperscript¯𝜅0𝑠¯𝑑𝑢¯𝑢superscript𝜅𝑠¯𝑢𝑑¯𝑑displaystylekappa+=ubarsdbard,qquadkappa0=dbarsubaru,qquadbarkappa0=sbardubaru,qquadkappa-=sbarudbard. This four-quark description explains naturally the inverted mass spectrum of the light nonet, especially the mass degeneracy between f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0, and accounts for the broad widths of σ𝜎sigma and κ𝜅kappa while f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 are narrow because of the suppressed phase space for their decays to the kaon pairs. In the naïve diquark model with ideal mixing for f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and σ​𝜎500sigma, f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 is purely an s​s¯𝑠¯𝑠sbars state, while σ​𝜎500sigma is an n​n¯𝑛¯𝑛nbarn state with n​n¯≡(u¯​u+d¯​d)/2𝑛¯𝑛¯𝑢𝑢¯𝑑𝑑2nbarnequiv(baruu+bardd)/sqrt2. However, there also exists some experimental evidence indicating that f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 is not a purely s​s¯𝑠¯𝑠sbars state. For example, the observation of Γ​(J/ψ→f0​ω)≈12​Γ​(J/ψ→f0​ϕ)Γ→𝐽𝜓subscript𝑓0𝜔12Γ→𝐽𝜓subscript𝑓0italic-ϕGamma(J/psito f0omega)approx1over 2Gamma(J/psito f0phi)citebib.bib4 clearly shows the existence of both non-strange and strange quark contents in f0​subscript𝑓0980f0. Therefore, isoscalars σ​𝜎500sigma and f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 must have a nontrivial mixing |f0​⟩=|s​s¯⟩​cos⁡θ+|n​n¯⟩​sin⁡θ,|σ​⟩=−|s​s¯⟩​sin⁡θ+|n​n¯⟩​cos⁡θ. formulae-sequenceketsubscript𝑓0980ket𝑠¯𝑠𝜃ket𝑛¯𝑛𝜃ket𝜎500ket𝑠¯𝑠𝜃ket𝑛¯𝑛𝜃displaystyle|f0=|sbarscostheta+|nbarnsintheta,qquad|sigma=-|sbarssintheta+|nbarncostheta. Various mixing angle measurements have been discussed in the literature and summarized in Refs. citebib.bib10, bib.bib11. A recent measurement of the upper limit on the branching fraction product ℬ​(B¯0→J/ψ​f0​)×ℬ​(f0​→π+​π−)ℬ→superscript¯𝐵0𝐽𝜓subscript𝑓0980ℬ→subscript𝑓0980superscript𝜋superscript𝜋cal B(overlineB0to J/psi f0)timescal B(f0topi+pi-) by LHCb leads to |θ|2.7𝑅2.7R>2.7 at 90% confidence level and hence favor the SU nonet tetraquark description of the f0​subscript𝑓0500f0, f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 produced in charmed meson decays. In the diquark model, the strange quark content of σ/f0​𝜎subscript𝑓0500sigma/f0 depends on the f0−σsubscript𝑓0𝜎f0-sigma mixing angle, which is of order 30∘superscript3030circ, while in the tetraquark scenario of σ𝜎sigma, its strange content is quite suppressed as the mixing angle defined in Eq. is close to π𝜋pi. As a result, the predicted branching fraction of Ds+→f0​​e+​νe→π+​π−​e+​νe→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscript𝑓0500superscript𝑒subscript𝜈𝑒→superscript𝜋superscript𝜋superscript𝑒subscript𝜈𝑒Ds+to f0e+nuetopi+pi-e+nue can be very different in the 2-quark and 4-quark models. content_sections.III Nonleptonic D→a0​(980)​P→P1​P2​P→𝐷su: We now turn to the hadronic decays of D→a0​​P→𝐷subscript𝑎0980𝑃Dto a0P followed by a0​→P1​P2→subscript𝑎0980subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2a0to P1P2 which can be used to discriminate the internal structure of a0​subscript𝑎0980a0. A suitable framework is the topological diagrammatic approach which we are going to describe below.

A model-independent analysis of heavy meson decays can be carried out in the so-called topological diagram approachcitebib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib19. For the purpose of discussing decay rates, it suffices to consider just the tree-type amplitudes: color-allowed tree amplitude T𝑇T, color-suppressed tree amplitude C𝐶C, W𝑊W-exchange amplitude E𝐸E, and W𝑊W-annihilation amplitude A𝐴A. Just as in D→V​P→𝐷𝑉𝑃Dto V!P decays, one generally has for the D→S​P→𝐷𝑆𝑃Dto SP decays two sets of distinct diagrams for each topology. For example, there are two external W𝑊W-emission and two internal W𝑊W-emission diagrams, depending on whether the spectator quark of the charmed meson stays in the pseudoscalar or scalar meson. Following the convention in Refs. citebib.bib22, bib.bib23, we shall denote the primed amplitudes T′superscript𝑇′Tprime and C′superscript𝐶′Cprime for the case when the emitted meson is a scalar one (see Fig. ). For the W𝑊W-exchange and W𝑊W-annihilation diagrams with the final state q1​q¯2subscript𝑞1subscript¯𝑞2q1barq2, the primed amplitude denotes that the even-parity meson contains the q1subscript𝑞1q1 quark. If the light scalar meson is a tetraquark state, there exist additional topological diagrams T𝑇tildeT, T¯¯𝑇overlineT and C𝐶tildeC as depicted in Fig. . It follows that the topology C𝐶tildeC is equivalent to T𝑇tildeT, and hence this redundant contribution will not be considered in this work. We shall see below that the diagrams T𝑇tildeT and T¯¯𝑇overlineT are crucial for understanding the D→a0​​P→𝐷subscript𝑎0980𝑃Dto a0P decays, whose topological amplitude decompositions are listed in Table in both diquark and tetraquark models for the light scalar mesons. The topological diagram T𝑇tildeT has been noticed before in Refs. citebib.bib24, bib.bib25. However, we find that the new contribution T¯¯𝑇overlineT is comparable to T𝑇T in size and plays a more dominant role than T𝑇tildeT in these decays.

For D+→a0​​π→superscript𝐷subscript𝑎0980𝜋D+to a0pi decays, their topological amplitudes are A​(D+→a0​+​π0)=12​Vc​d∗​Vu​d​(T′+C−T+T¯−A+A′),A​(D+→a0​0​π+)=12​Vc​d∗​Vu​d​(T+C′+T−T¯+A−A′). formulae-sequence𝐴→superscript𝐷subscript𝑎0superscript980superscript𝜋012superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑐𝑑subscript𝑉𝑢𝑑superscript𝑇′𝐶𝑇¯𝑇𝐴superscript𝐴′𝐴→superscript𝐷subscript𝑎0superscript9800superscript𝜋12superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑐𝑑subscript𝑉𝑢𝑑𝑇superscript𝐶′𝑇¯𝑇𝐴superscript𝐴′displaystylebeginsplitA(D+to a0+pi0)=1oversqrt2Vcd*Vud(Tprime+C-tildeT+overlineT-A+Aprime), A(D+to a00pi+)=1oversqrt2Vcd*Vud(T+Cprime+tildeT-overlineT+A-Aprime). endsplit We see from Table that the calculated branching fraction of D+→a0​0​π+→η​π0​π+→superscript𝐷subscript𝑎0superscript9800superscript𝜋→𝜂superscript𝜋0superscript𝜋D+to a00pi+toetapi0pi+ from the contributions of T+C′𝑇superscript𝐶′T+Cprime alone is too large by a factor of ∼3similar-toabsent3sim 3. This implies that a destructive contribution from the combination (T−T¯)𝑇¯𝑇(tildeT-overlineT) is needed. Hence, this favors the solution of T=−0.25​T𝑇0.25𝑇tildeT=-0.25T and rules out the other one with T=1.2​T𝑇1.2𝑇tildeT=1.2T.

To summarize, using the measurements of D+→a0+(→π+η)K¯0D+to a0+(topi+eta)overlineK0, D0→a0−(→π−η)π+D0to a0-(topi-eta)pi+ and D0→a00(→π0η)K¯0D0to a00(topi0eta)overlineK0 as the input, we obtain T=−(0.22−0.02+0.03)​T,T¯=(1.00−0.17+0.15)​T,E′/T=(0.725​ei​180∘∼0.961​ei​164∘). formulae-sequence𝑇subscriptsuperscript0.220.030.02𝑇formulae-sequence¯𝑇subscriptsuperscript1.000.150.17𝑇superscript𝐸′𝑇similar-to0.725superscript𝑒𝑖superscript1800.961superscript𝑒𝑖superscript164displaystyletildeT=-(0.22+0.03-0.02)T,qquadoverlineT=(1.00+0.15-0.17)T,qquad Eprime/T=(0.725ei180circsim 0.961ei164circ). The calculated branching fractions of various D→a0​​P→P1​P2​P→𝐷subscript𝑎0980𝑃→subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2𝑃Dto a0Pto P1P2P decays in the diquark and tetraquark models of a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 are shown in Table where we have used the value of LCSR(II) for the form factor F0D​a0superscriptsubscript𝐹0𝐷subscript𝑎0F0Da0 (see Table). We see that the calculated results are generally in good agreement with experiment except for D0→a0+(→K+K¯0)π−D0to a0+(to K+overlineK0)pi- and D0→a0−(→K−K0)π+D0to a0-(to K-K0)pi+ where the predicted branching fractions are too small by one order of magnitude. However, the current experimental measurements of these two modes have very large uncertainties and need to be improved in the future. As discussed in Section, if a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 is a q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq state, the Ds+→a0+​π0+a00​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑎0superscript𝜋0superscriptsubscript𝑎00superscript𝜋Ds+to a0+pi0+a00pi+ decay proceeds only through the W𝑊W-annihilation topology. However, its branching fraction observed at a percent level is much larger than the other two W𝑊W-annihilation channels Ds+→ω​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠𝜔superscript𝜋Ds+toomegapi+ and ρ0​π+superscript𝜌0superscript𝜋rho0pi+. Hence, the question is why the annihilation amplitude is so sizable in the S​P𝑆𝑃SP sector with |A/T|S​P∼1/2similar-tosubscript𝐴𝑇𝑆𝑃12|A/T|SPsim 1/2citebib.bib2, whereas it is rather suppressed in the P​P𝑃𝑃P!P and V​P𝑉𝑃V!P sector with |A/T|P​P∼0.18similar-tosubscript𝐴𝑇𝑃𝑃0.18|A/T|PPsim 0.18 and |AV/TP|V​P∼0.07similar-tosubscriptsubscript𝐴𝑉subscript𝑇𝑃𝑉𝑃0.07|AV/TP|VPsim 0.07citebib.bib5. Although potentially large long-distance contributions to the W𝑊W-annihilation topology through the triangle diagrams at the hadron level had been evaluated in the literaturecitebib.bib35, bib.bib36, the aforementioned question was not really addressed. If a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 is a tetraquark state, the decay Ds+→a0+​π0+a00​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑎0superscript𝜋0superscriptsubscript𝑎00superscript𝜋Ds+to a0+pi0+a00pi+ receives an additional contribution of T¯¯𝑇overlineT. This contribution alone is slightly larger than the observed branching fraction (see Table) and the destructive contribution from W𝑊W-annihilation will help explain the data. We thus conclude that Ds+→a0+​π0+a00​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑎0superscript𝜋0superscriptsubscript𝑎00superscript𝜋Ds+to a0+pi0+a00pi+ is not a purely W𝑊W-annihilation process and that the W𝑊W-annihilation process in the S​P𝑆𝑃SP sector is not greatly enhanced relative to the P​P𝑃𝑃PP or V​P𝑉𝑃V!P sector.
content_sections.IV Conclusions: In this work, we have shown that the hadronic decays D→a0​​π→𝐷subscript𝑎0980𝜋Dto a0pi serve a remarkable place for discriminating the internal structure of the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 meson. If a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 is a q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq state, the predicted branching fractions of D→a0​+​π→𝐷subscript𝑎0superscript980𝜋Dto a0+pi are too small by one to two orders of magnitude compared to the experiment as the corresponding amplitude is suppressed by the smallness of the a0+superscriptsubscript𝑎0a0+ decay constant, while the predicted rates for D+→a0​0​P→superscript𝐷subscript𝑎0superscript9800𝑃D+to a00P and D0→a0​−,0​P→superscript𝐷0subscript𝑎0superscript9800𝑃D0to a0-,0P are usually too large compared to the data. In particular, the ratios r+⁣/−subscript𝑟absentr+/- and r+⁣/0subscript𝑟absent0r+/0 naïvely expected to be at a few percent level are measured to be around 7.5 and 2.6, respectively. The above difficulties can be resolved in the tetraquark model of a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 as there exist additional T𝑇T-like topological diagrams, namely, T𝑇tildeT and T¯¯𝑇overlineT. Although both amplitudes are nonfactorizable and thus not calculable, their information can be extracted from data. We find T¯≈T¯𝑇𝑇overlineTapprox T and T≈−0.25​T𝑇0.25𝑇tildeTapprox-0.25T. A product of this study shows that the decay Ds+→a0+​π0+a00​π+→superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑎0superscript𝜋0superscriptsubscript𝑎00superscript𝜋Ds+to a0+pi0+a00pi+ is not a purely W𝑊W-annihilation process and it is governed by the tetraquark topology T¯¯𝑇overlineT. Therefore, measurements of D→a0​​P→𝐷subscript𝑎0980𝑃Dto a0P decays lend strong support to the tetraquark picture of a0​subscript𝑎0980a0.

all_papers.title:
Searching a systematics for nonfactorizable contribution to B-and B0
  mesons
all_papers.abstract:
Two-body weak decays / and are examined under isospin analysis to study
nonfactorizable contributions. After extracting the strong phases and obtaining
the factorizable contributions from spectator-quark diagrams for Nc=3, we
determine nonfactorizable isospin amplitudes from the experimental data for
these modes. Our results support the universality of ratio of nonfactorizable
isospin reduced amplitudes for these decays within experimental errors. In
order to show that this systematics is not coincidental, we also plot our
results w. r. t. this ratio.

all_papers.title:
Analysis of Hadronic Weak Decays of Charmed Baryons in the Topological
  Diagrammatic Approach
all_papers.abstract:
We perform a global fit to the experimental data of two-body charmed baryon
decays based on the topological diagrammatic approach (TDA) and take into
account the phase shifts between $S$- and $P$-wave amplitudes as inspired by
the recent BESIII measurement of the decay asymmetry in the decay
$\Lambda_c^+\to \Xi^0K^+$. The TDA has the advantage that it is more intuitive,
graphic and easier to implement model calculations. The measured branching
fractions and decay asymmetries are well accommodated in the TDA except for a
few modes, in particular, the predicted ${\cal B}(\Xi_c^0\to
\Xi^-\pi^+)=(2.83\pm0.10)\%$ is larger than its current value. The equivalence
of the TDA and the irreducible SU(3) approach (IRA) is established. We show
that the number of the minimum set of tensor invariants in the IRA and the
topological amplitudes in the TDA is the same and present their relations. The
predicted magnitudes of $S$- and $P$-wave amplitudes and their phase shifts are
presented for measured and yet-to-be-measured modes in both the TDA and IRA
which can be tested in the near future. Besides the decay $\Lambda_c^+\to \Xi^0
K^+$, there exist several modes which proceed only through $W$-exchange. In
particular, the observed channel $\Xi_c^0\to \Sigma^+ K^-$ should have phase
shifts similar to that in $\Lambda_c^+\to \Xi^0 K^+$ and its decay asymmetry is
predicted to be $-0.21\pm0.17$ which can be used to test our theoretical
framework. In contrast, the TDA leads to a large $\alpha$ of order $-0.93$ for
the decay $\Xi_c^+\to \Xi^0\pi^+$ even after the phase-shift effect is
incorporated in the fit.

sections.4: Kohara, “Quark diagram analysis of charmed baryon decays,” Phys
sections.15: Kohara, “Quark diagram analysis of charmed baryon decays,” Phys
sections.17: Kohara, “Comments on ’Analysis of two-body decays of charmed baryons using the quark diagram scheme’,” [arXiv:hep-ph/9701287 [hep-ph]].

qualitative: The topological diagrammatic approach (TDA) provides an intuitive, graphical, and implementable framework for analyzing two-body charmed baryon weak decays, offering advantages over traditional model-independent approaches.

The TDA incorporates phase shifts between S- and P-wave amplitudes, inspired by recent BESIII measurements of decay asymmetry in $\Lambda_c^+ \to \Xi^0 K^+$, which improves the description of experimental data.

The TDA and the irreducible SU(3) approach (IRA) are equivalent in their underlying structure, with the same minimum number of tensor invariants in IRA matching the number of topological amplitudes in TDA, and explicit relations between them are established.
Several decay modes proceed exclusively through W-exchange diagrams, making them sensitive probes of non-factorizable contributions and offering unique tests of theoretical frameworks.

The decay $\Xi_c^0 \to \Sigma^+ K^-$ is predicted to have a decay asymmetry similar to that of $\Lambda_c^+ \to \Xi^0 K^+$, with a predicted asymmetry of $-0.21 \pm 0.17$, providing a key test of the theoretical model.
The TDA predicts a large decay asymmetry $\alpha \approx -0.93$ for $\Xi_c^+ \to \Xi^0 \pi^+$, even after including phase-shift effects, indicating a potential tension with future measurements.

The TDA successfully describes most measured branching fractions and decay asymmetries, except for $\Xi_c^0 \to \Xi^- \pi^+$, where the predicted branching fraction $(2.83 \pm 0.10)\%$ exceeds the current experimental value.
quantitative: Predicted branching fraction: $\mathcal{B}(\Xi_c^0 \to \Xi^- \pi^+) = (2.83 \pm 0.10)\%$
Predicted decay asymmetry: $\alpha(\Xi_c^0 \to \Sigma^+ K^-) = -0.21 \pm 0.17$
Predicted decay asymmetry: $\alpha(\Xi_c^+ \to \Xi^0 \pi^+) \approx -0.93$ (after phase-shift inclusion)
Phase shift effects between S- and P-wave amplitudes are incorporated based on BESIII measurement in $\Lambda_c^+ \to \Xi^0 K^+$
The number of minimum tensor invariants in IRA equals the number of topological amplitudes in TDA, establishing their equivalence
